context:
Course context:
This course explores how to design and manage the physical, information, and financial flows that connect organizations across the supply chain. Using analytical frameworks, mathematical models, and real-world applications, you will learn to make strategic decisions related to supply chain network design, supply chain finance, supplier management and procurement, production and material requirements planning, distribution, Sales & Operations Planning (S&OP), and process and organizational design. By the end of the course you will be equipped to optimize supply chain networks, apply financial analysis to supply chain decisions, and lead cross-functional initiatives that balance operational excellence with strategic business objectives. This course is part of the MITx MicroMasters® Program in Supply Chain Management.

Syllabus:
CTL.SC2x Supply Chain Design

Program:
Part of the MITx MicroMasters Program in Supply Chain Management.

Course Description:
Supply Chain Design: SC2x explores how to design and manage the physical, information, and financial flows that connect organizations across the supply chain. Using analytical frameworks, mathematical models, and real-world applications, you will learn how to make strategic decisions related to network design, supply chain finance, supplier management, production planning, distribution, and organizational design.

Learning Objectives:
- Design and optimize supply chain networks
- Apply financial analysis to supply chain decisions
- Develop supplier management and procurement strategies
- Plan production and material requirements
- Coordinate Sales & Operations Planning (S&OP) processes
- Improve cross-functional communication and organizational performance

Prerequisites:
There are no formal prerequisites. However, because SC2x is a quantitative course, learners benefit from familiarity with algebra, spreadsheets, and introductory concepts in statistics, probability, and optimization. Learners seeking additional preparation may wish to complete Supply Chain Analytics: SC0x and Supply Chain Fundamentals: SC1x first.

Estimated Effort:
8–12 hours per week for 12 weeks.

Intended Audience:
Professionals and lifelong learners interested in supply chain management, operations, logistics, and business analytics.

Course Schedule:
New material for each week is released on Wednesdays at 15:00 UTC. Graded assignments are due on Wednesdays at 15:00 UTC on the specified deadline.
- Week 0 — Course Overview & Logistics (starts Jun 17, 2026)
- Week 1 — Basic Supply Chain Network Design (Jun 24 – Jul 8, 2026)
- Week 2 — Advanced Supply Chain Network Design (Jul 1 – Jul 15, 2026)
- Week 3 — Production Planning (Jul 8 – Jul 22, 2026)
- Week 4 — Sales & Operations Planning and Distribution Strategies (Jul 15 – Jul 29, 2026)
- Week 5 — Prep Week (Jul 22, 2026) — no graded assignment; review for the Midterm
- Week 6 — Midterm Exam (Jul 29 – Jul 31, 2026)
- Week 7 — Supplier Management (Aug 5 – Aug 19, 2026)
- Week 8 — Supply Chain Finance I (Aug 12 – Aug 26, 2026)
- Week 9 — Supply Chain Finance II (Aug 19 – Sep 2, 2026)
- Week 10 — Supply Chain Finance III and Organizational Design (Aug 26 – Sep 9, 2026)
- Week 11 — Prep Week (Sep 2, 2026)
- Week 12 — Final Exam (Sep 9 – Sep 11, 2026)

Key Dates:
Course starts Jun 17, 2026; Financial assistance application deadline Jul 8, 2026; Enrollment / Verification upgrade deadline (Certificate Track) Jul 22, 2026; Course ends Sep 11, 2026; Certificates available Sep 18, 2026.

Weekly Topics:
- Week 1 — Basic Supply Chain Network Design: Introduces supply chain design and reviews key concepts from SC1x. Formulate and solve network flow models using spreadsheets, then explore the Facility Location Problem, including its formulation and solution as a Mixed Integer Linear Program (MILP).
- Week 2 — Advanced Supply Chain Network Design: Combine the Facility Location problem with network flow models to formulate and solve larger network design problems including facility capacity, level of service, and inbound/outbound transportation. Cover modeling multiple products, echelons, and periods; discuss flexibility and robustness.
- Week 3 — Production Planning: Move from network design to working with internal and external manufacturing partners. Present the Fixed Planning Horizon model; introduce Bill of Materials (BOM), Material Requirements Planning (MRP), and Distribution Requirements Planning (DRP) systems.
- Week 4 — Sales & Operations Planning and Distribution Strategies: The S&OP process is presented and discussed, along with different distribution strategies and how e-commerce has affected the design of distribution networks.
- Week 5 — Prep Week: Catch up and review; addresses practical concerns of running a network design project, in preparation for the Midterm.
- Week 6 — Midterm Exam: Assesses understanding of concepts and methods covered in Weeks 1–4.
- Weeks 7–10 — Supplier Management and Supply Chain Finance: Procurement strategy and optimization-based procurement; accounting fundamentals and cost accounting; cash flows and discounted cash flows; supply chain finance, financial performance, and organizational/metric design.
- Week 12 — Final Exam.

Assessment & Grading:
The course separates a learning space (videos, quick questions, and practice problems with immediate feedback; weekly Graded Assignments as formative assessments, with solutions released after the due date) from an assessment space (the Midterm and Final Exams, which evaluate individual mastery; no solutions or individualized feedback are provided for exam questions). To pass the course and earn a Certificate, you must achieve a final course grade of at least 60%. Grade weights:
- Weekly Graded Assignments: 20%
- Midterm Exam: 35%
- Final Exam: 45%

Enrollment Track Access:
Graded Assignments for Weeks 1, 2, and 3 are available to both Audit and Certificate Track learners. Graded Assignments for Weeks 4, 7, 8, 9, and 10, the Midterm Exam, and the Final Exam are available only to Certificate Track learners.

Graded Assignments are released with each content week and may be completed any time before the deadline (deadlines are firm; no extensions). Point values are indicated per question; most questions allow two submission attempts; only the last submission is graded; you must click Submit for each answer. A green/red indicator shows correctness after submission, and official solutions and explanations are released after the deadline. Assignments remain available for review afterward.

Exams: the Midterm and Final together account for 80% of the final grade. They are open-book (you may consult course videos, slides, Key Concept Documents, and your own notes) and timed. Each exam is available during a 48-hour testing window; once started you have 2.5 hours, and the timer cannot be paused, stopped, or reset (start at least 2.5 hours before the deadline for full time). The Discussion Forum and Progress Tab are disabled while an exam is open. Unlike Graded Assignments, exams provide no correctness feedback and no solutions after the deadline. Timed exams must be completed in a web browser.

Policies & Support:
Academic integrity: All graded work must comply with MIT Learn's Honor Code and Academic Integrity Policy — original work; no unauthorized collaboration; no use of generative AI tools unless explicitly permitted by the teaching team; a single account; fair competition; and protecting assessment integrity (do not post, share, or distribute answers). Violations may result in invalidation of graded work, certificate revocation, or removal from the course.
Communication: The Discussion Forum (monitored by Community Teaching Assistants and staff) is for course concepts, videos, quick questions, practice problems, and general problem-solving approaches; do not use it for Weekly Graded Assignments, the Midterm, or the Final. Assessment-related questions go to the email help desk at sc2xhelp@mit.edu (include the week, problem number, and a description); staff will not review calculations, verify answers, or provide hints for graded work.
Accessibility: For disability-related accommodations, contact mitx-accessibility@mit.edu as early as possible (at least two weeks before an exam opens for exam accommodations).

Course Team:
Instructors: Dr. Chris Caplice; Prof. Yossi Sheffi; Dr. Jarrod Goentzel; Mr. Jim Rice. Course Lead: Ms. Laura Allegue. Teaching Assistant: Mr. Matt Toy. MicroMasters in SCM Team: Dr. Eva Ponce (Director); Dr. Inma Borrella (Academic Lead); Ms. Cara Greaney; Mr. Kellen Betts; Mr. Paulo de Sousa; Mr. Jeff Baker; Mr. Robert Cummings; Mr. Connor Makowski; Ms. Elise Yonika. Course help / assessment questions: sc2xhelp@mit.edu

Lecture transcripts:
[lecture_00_00_course_overview]
Welcome to CTL.SC2x, Supply Chain Design. Now it's preferred, but it's not required, that you've already taken SC1x, Supply Chain and Logistics Fundamentals. In that course, we covered a lot of the building blocks that we will utilize in this course. If you haven't, that's OK. We'll help you along, and I'll point you to references. So let's get started. We should all know by now what a supply chain is. And the definition that we like to use is that it's two or more parties, companies, organizations, some entity, linked by a flow of resources. Typically, this is material, physical material, information, and money, that ultimately fulfill a customer's request. So we think of it as managing flows. There's the information flow, and the primary information flow is the order. I place an order for something. Hopefully, it comes to me delivered. It's physical. And there's some kind of financial payment back. And there's also other information flows in between, usually the status of the delivery. So we really like to think about supply chains as you're managing these different types of flows. So we know what a supply chain is, and we know what they involve multiple parties, not just a customer and supplier, but usually that supplier's supplier, and their supplier, all the way down to the raw materials. This is a linear relationship, but that's not that common actually. Most supply chains are very complex with non-linear relationships. Something like this, very ugly, where I have my customers over here, my raw material, but they're different manufacturers of components. And I have many cases where my main customer is also one of my primary suppliers. So it's very complex and very nonlinear. And the nice thing about supply chains, what makes them so interesting is they take many different forms. So one classic form is that of a manufacturer. So let's say in this simple example, I'm a bicycle manufacturer. And I like to think of this as tier zero. So the things that I do or the starting point. I might have two suppliers, and I would call these tier one suppliers. They're my primary suppliers. They supply the hardware and the frame for my bicycles that I manufacture. They, in turn, have primary suppliers, but for me as a bike manufacturer, they're tier two. So there are suppliers to my supplier. And you can keep going back tier three, and in some supply chains, you might go to double digits, tier 20, whatever. A lot of times, you'll say tier n because you're going so far back. And so one of the challenges that we face in supply chain management is maybe I can control and manage these guys, my tier ones. But as I move further back, I lose more and more control. And you can see a case here where I have three separate suppliers here for my tier two, for my casting, my pedal, and my gear, but it turns out they're very contingent on one supplier here in my third tier. I might not know about them. But if they go down, it knocks out a lot of my material. So this is one type of supply chain form, but this is only half. So this is how the manufacturing is done, but you could think of the other side, the distribution down stream to the customers. So we talked about going upstream, now we're talking about the downstream portion. So my bicycle manufacturer might sell to wholesalers, who sell to different retailers, and then to different customers. But, maybe I have my own series of stores. And so I, as a manufacturer, might also be a retailer, and I have my own outlets, or different niche stores, or maybe I have a website, and that's how people mainly buy their bicycles. So I have all these different channels for distribution, different ways of manufacturing on the upstream and distributing on the downstream. So there are many different forms, but when we talk about supply chain design, most people think of something like this. They think about the globe. And where should I locate my manufacturing? Where should I locate my distribution? So they tend to focus on the physical. Right? We focus on the physical because that's where we have to make those big investments. We're going to take a more expansive view here. When I talk about supply chain design, I'm talking about designing it all. So you need to know how information flows because it can flow very differently between the different parties in your supply chain. We'll talk about the physical design, but also the financial. You need to translate what we're doing in supply chain world into language that the CFO, the chief financial officer can understand. So we'll look at all of these things when we talk about supply chain design. So what is a design though? So let's go back to basic definitions. It's always a good place to start. So if I go to Merriam Webster and look at the definition of design, it's pretty obvious. It's a-- the definition is to plan or make decisions about something, to create the plans, drawings to show how something will be made, to plan and make something for a specific user purpose. We all know. This is kind of common sense, but it's not a really helpful definition. So I went to the software design literature, and I found a better definition. I really like this definition. It's very dense but let me talk you through it. So this is from Ralph and Wand. And they say a design of anything is a specification of an object or a system. We're going to focus on a system. A supply chain is a system of getting product to satisfy an end customer's demand. It's manifested by an agent. What does that mean? Well, you're an agent. You are doing it. Someone designs it. It doesn't just suddenly design itself. So someone is designing the system. It's intended to accomplish goals. You determine what goals it's going to accomplish, and we'll talk more. Different supply chains have different goals. It operates and is designed to operate in a particular environment. So you need to know the situation that you're involved in. We use a set of primary or fundamental components. That's everything we learned in SC1x. So the fundamental ways of how you forecast, how you manage inventory, how you move product, all of those are building blocks that you used to accomplish your design. Now you do this satisfying a set of requirements. You have to meet that objective, and it's subject to constraints because we live in the real world. You don't have infinite budgets, an infinite time. So you have constraints to satisfy requirements using your basic building blocks, and this is what you do in a design. I love this definition because it really encapsulates everything that we're doing, and we're going to see more of this. We're going to talk a lot about how you accomplish these goals because the primary hallmark of supply chain design is making those optimal trade offs, mainly between costs and service, but there are a lot of other little trade offs that are made along the way. OK. So now we've defined it. We've said what a supply chain design is. Let's talk about why it's important at all.

[lecture_00_01_review_of_supply_chain_fundamentals_sc1x]
So why is supply chain design so important? Well, basically, it's because we have so many choices. We've talked about this in SC1x, and we went over many different ways to forecast product demand. There's not one way to forecast demand. There are many different ways that depend on the situation at hand. Same thing with how much inventory to position and where. Same thing with how to move product between facilities. Do I use fast transportation, do I use slow? These are all a function of the situation. We also talked about different ways of segmenting customers and how you can collaborate with customers, how to work with suppliers-- even the whole idea of how you organize your supply chain function itself. Is it decentralized? Is it centralized? So all of these things you have these different choices, and to include selecting locations, which we'll spend a lot of time on in this next lesson. So we have all these different choices, but we also know there's no single best way for all situations. It's not like there's this one holy grail supply chain that everyone should and will eventually get to. They all differ based on the situation. The situation of the market, these customers you're serving, the product characteristics-- all of these things. And it's not just different between firms. It's different within a firm because a single firm will typically sell to multiple, different types of customers and they'll segment them accordingly. And they might have very different types of products and they'll segment those accordingly. So I'm going to take this opportunity to do a really quick review of some of the options and the choices that we talked about in SC1x that you're going to be using for supply chain design. And I'm going to focus in on forecasting, inventory, and transportation. So this is a very, very quick review of SC1x, and so feel free to skip this if you're very familiar with these concepts. So let's start with demand forecasting. The thing that determines the type of forecasting method to use really is all about history. Do I have it? Do I have history in terms of the market where I'm trying to sell to, as well as the product that I'm trying to sell? If you have both of those, if you have history, and you think it's going to repeat itself, that's all about time series and exponential smoothing. I'm going to say that what happened in the past is probably going to happen going forward. And so, I identify and recognize the underlying patterns of level, trend, seasonality, and I use that to project my demand going forward. There are many, many products. Most industrial products fall in this quadrant because there's a lot of history out there. What if you have no history, though? So you know the market, but you don't-- it's a new product to that market. Well, this is where you use a "looks like" type approach where you look at similar products that you've launched in the past and see, OK, they did such-and-such. So therefore, I think this product is very similar to this product. So you use that approach and you think their demand is going to be similar. Same thing if you have new market, but you know the product. So this is something new to market, but you already know the existing technology. And here, you're going to get experimental input, and kind of more qualitative input than quantitative because you don't have as much history of the market so you get expert opinions. And then finally, if you're down here where you have no history of the product, no history of the market, you're kind of off in the deep, blue waters. This is where you use forecasting approaches to really help understand-- to get better sense of the whole market. And this is where we talk about bass diffusion models, scenario planning, Delphi approaches, or expert panels. Again, it's somewhat more qualitative, but you're going to try to find something to help guide you. Now, regardless of the type of forecasting method used in the approach, there are three things that are always going to be constant. One-- forecasts are always wrong, but don't stop there. Do something about it. You want to use ranges and you want to track your forecast error. I'm always shocked at the number of companies that do not track their forecast error, either accuracy or bias-- it's amazing. Always track that. We know that aggregated forecasts are more accurate. This is why we use risk pooling-- to reduce the coefficient of variation. And shorter time horizon forecasts are more accurate. This is why postponement can often make sense because we're making that last forecast very close to when the product is going to be used. We postpone the customization. So regardless of the method of forecasting, we're always going to look at that error term, which is the actual demand minus the forecasted, and there's three metrics that you should always keep in mind. One is the mean deviation. This is a great metric to show bias. So this will help me determine if I'm over forecasting or under forecasting over time. Then you've got MAPE. MAPE is widely used to help compare different forecasting systems and is simply the absolute value of the error divided by the actual amount, and you take an average of that. So it put things into perspective as a percentage. For use in inventory models, we use the root mean square error-- the RMSE-- which is simply the sum of the squares of those errors, and then you take the average of that and take the square root. So we use this when we start figuring out our safety stock. So all three of these metrics are valid and they have a separate purpose, so you should really always track those. Then we go into the inventory management, and just as a history of demand is the primary driver of the approach for forecasting, the primary driver for inventory management is your demand variability and your horizon-- your planning horizon. So if I have something that has no or very little planning horizon-- excuse me-- very little demand variability but a long planning horizon, then I'm probably going to use something like an economic order quantity-- EOQ. And so, this is where we're trading off fixed costs and variable costs. And so, the demand variability is next to nothing, and you have a lot of items that fall into this, especially in industrial settings. So I'm going to order Q* every T* time periods, and I'm going to order Q* when the inventory position-- that's the inventory on hand plus what's on order-- is equal to my expected demand over lead time. Now you have an opposite situation where I have a very short horizon-- that's one single period. And this could be selling Christmas trees, sushi, ice cream cones, newspapers-- things that I can only sell during that one period. No one wants to buy yesterday's newspaper. So I have a single period and my variability can be quite high-- my variability of demand. And in this case we showed how to make a trade off again, but this time between shortage costs and excess costs. Well, the excess cost is obvious. If I'm selling Christmas trees, the price I can charge for selling Christmas trees the day after Christmas is pretty low. I'm not going to make much money. In fact, it might be zero. But the shortage cost is another side that we don't always think about. If I run out of selling, for example, Christmas trees a week ahead of time, that's a week of sales that I missed. So you've got shortage costs and excess costs. And what this model does-- the news vendor or news boy or single period model does-- is it makes a trade off between having too much and having too little. And we found that ordering the amount-- the Q* at the start of the period to cover the amount of the demand distribution-- the probability that my demand will be less than what I ordered-- is equal to what we call the critical ratio. And we showed how to develop this critical ratio as a ratio of the shortage cost over the sum of the shortage and excess costs. So the real differentiator here is that for EOQ, we assume very, very low demand variability, but a long time frame. And for the news vendor problem, or the model, we assume a very short time period and a very high variability. But there's a lot of cases obviously in the middle, and they kind of combine both approaches. And we have two main classes where we can set up an inventory systems for each of these. And so, for the replenishment period where I'm trying to again order to determine how much to replenish and when, I've got two major classes-- continuous review and periodic review. So for the continuous review, we talked about the (s,Q) system, which is order point, order quantity-- (s,Q). And the policy is I order Q-- the amount Q-- if the inventory position is less than this trigger point, little s. OK, how much is Q? My old friend, the economic order quantity. This is Q* the EOQ that we just talked about when we assume that demand variability was relatively stable. It's a good place to start. Now, how do I set this s? What I'm saying here is my demand, or my inventory position, I track that. And if it crosses this threshold, I'm going to order Q. I'm going to order that amount up, and then inventory goes. It shows up, and the same thing happens when I cross that threshold-- I place an order. So I place an order while I'm continuously monitoring the inventory position. So how do I set s? Well, it's going to be the expected demand over lead time because if I place the order here, it's not going to show up until L time periods later. So I want to have the average, plus I want to throw in safety stock. This is Safety Stock. It's going to be some safety factor, K, that we can take off of whatever the distribution looks like, multiplied by the standard deviation of the forecast error of that lead time. So that's a continuous review policy. Pretty widely used, but even more widely used is the periodic review. This is where I will only review my inventory every R time periods. That could be once a week, every two weeks, once a month, every third day-- whatever. I have this review period every R days, and I'm going to look at that time period, look at my inventory position, and I'm going to order up to this big S. That's what's called an order up to level. Sometimes is called a par level. So I order up to, so I'd order this amount. For here, I'd order this amount, and it's whenever my review period comes up. So how much do I order? How do I set this S-- this order up to? What's going to be equal to my expected demand over my review time plus the lead time, because that's the next time I'll actually get the stuff in. Plus my safety stock-- again, my safety factor and then the sigma over the same time period, the replenishment, and lead time, and that's my variance of my forecast error. So two different methods-- continuous review, periodic review, and they're very similar. Then we went into transportation. Transportation gives you many different options to get product from A to B. So, three basic approaches. One is the direct, or one-to-one, point-to-point moves. If I have sufficient volume in a move-- full truckload, full container-- then it might be worthwhile for me to move it directly from one point to the other point. As the shipment size decreases though, it might not be economically justifiable to ship something direct. I might have to do a one-to-many, or a multi-stop. So instead of a full truckload-- let's say, going for here-- maybe each one of these has a quarter of a truckload. And now it would make it economically justifiable to do a multi-stop. As my shipment size gets even smaller, I need this many-to-many, where I want to combine as many things going to each one of these customer locations. And so, that requires a mixing center, cross docking, or a hub operation. So you have all these different ways to distribute product from a distribution center in this case to a customer. At the end of the day, we showed that you have these options. This is the strategic network. So moving product from A to B for each of the options, I can summarise them in terms of how much it costs, how long it takes L in terms of lead time, and then the variability of that lead time, sigma. So I have all of these. How do I combine those all together? Well, this is where the total cost equation comes in because I look for the solution that minimizes my total cost if I'm looking at designing a cost-based system. So my total cost is my purchase cost for each item plus my ordering cost plus my inventory holding costs, which consists of three components. My cycle stock-- the inventory expect to use during my typical replenishment cycle. My safety stock-- kind of my buffer in case a demand peaks or something happens. And the amount of inventory in my pipeline-- the stuff that's in the boat, in the truck as it's being delivered. And then I've got this final term, which is a cost-- it's kind of a holder-- holding for the stock out cost. If I'm out for each individual item or I'm short for the event, there are different ways you cost this out, but it's all the stock out types. So what we can see hopefully is that the forecasting and transportation impact my total cost. As my forecasting error gets worse, what happens? Well, my sigma DL gets bigger. So that means my safety stock goes up. If my transportation gets slower, then what does it do? It increases my pipeline inventory. It also increases the length of time that I have to hold inventory, so my safety stock goes up. And it can also impact my cost for each order as well as for each item. So forecasting and transportation all impact things, and it really comes down to setting that safety stock. In a lot of cases, the inventory is the prime driver. That's where a lot of the money is. And so, we talked about how we can set these based on service metrics, we set the K value based on a level of service we want to hit. 95% of the time we don't have a stock out event, or my item fill rate is at least 99%. Or we can find a K that minimizes a total cost if I put a dollar value on each stock out event. The last thing we did is just combine the variability of lead time with the variability of demand. And I just have the equations here just for reference. But what we did in SC1x then is we went through each of the primary functions-- forecasting, inventory, and transportation-- and showed different options and when to use what. And what we're going to do for the rest of this course is apply those in different ways and figure out when to use which type of approach.

[lecture_00_02_selecting_the_right_design]
So how do you select the right design? Well, it's a combination of both art and science. The science is all the stuff we're going to hit in the first couple of lessons. We're going to show you ways to mathematically quantify the different impact of the choices you might make. And these are all being made to find that optimal tradeoff between cost and service as well as between different types of costs. And using these models, you'll be able to select the best approach given the characteristics of the product, the supplier, the customers, the markets, and other thing that might have a factor. But it's also an art, because the future is uncertain. We don't know where things are going to go. And this is especially true if I'm making decisions about large investments over a long period of time, such as facilities or large pieces of equipment. These assumptions that we make that feed into the models rarely hold true completely. They're never going to be perfect. They're assumptions. The data are never going to be completely accurate, regardless of the precision that you go to. So simply having 13 decimal places doesn't make a piece of data any more accurate. It might make it more precise, but it might actually make it overly precise for the level of accuracy that you have. So it could give a false sense of security. And we'll talk more about this. And finally, situations change over time, sometimes abruptly. So there will always be an essence of art to this. It's not a pure mathematical program. We're going to spend a lot of time on developing these math programs, but they're used to feed in and help support the decision. So let's look at some classic ways of doing this. This is from a paper by Marshall Fisher, classic in the field, that looks at how you should select the right supply chain for the product. And what Fisher did, he said, if you look at it, there's really two types of products that serve two types of markets. There are functional products-- and these are things like soup, like the example here. Where it's predictable demand, long life cycle. The margins are OK. Not that much variety. There's not that much of stock out. And you have a fairly long lead time. And for these products, you want to drive down costs. You want to make them as efficient as possible. But then you've got these other products. And he called them innovative products. And for these ones, the demand is unpredictable. It's a short life cycle. Things happen quickly. Margins are much higher. You have higher variety. Because they're innovative, you don't know exactly what the market is going to be yet. That's why you have such high variety. There's errors at production, because it's new products. It hasn't been fixed totally yet. High stock out rates. And for these products, you want to match supply and demand. Two different types of supply chains to support two different types of products going to two different types of markets. So why do we care about this? Well, this changes the way that we approach our supply chain design and how we perform our supply chain functions. So for demand forecasting for these functional products, I have history. So I'm going to use exponential smoothing, time series. I'm going to have detailed analysis of trends, seasonality, maybe geographic factors, because I have history I can lean on. For innovative products, not so much. I'll have to use more qualitative methods, like looks like analysis. So I'll find a product that was launched, say, last year that's kind of similar, and use that as a proxy. I might use the bass diffusion model. So it's a very different type of forecasting. For inventory management, I'm going to be probably Periodic Review, because I'm not going to spend a lot of time on it, so I want to minimize that time. I might build up inventory for peaks. For soup, I probably have a bigger peak in the winter. So I'll pre-build, because I know it's probably not going to go out of style. It's not as perishable. Very different over here in innovative products. These are more like the Newsvendor problem-- single period. I've got to push my product first, because if I miss that first wave, I might miss the whole market. Very different ways of managing inventory. Transportation. For over here in functional, it's full truckload. It's ocean. Cost dominates speed. I'll go for a slower mode if it lowers my cost. I might require minimum order quantities to DCs to get that economies of scale. Over here in the innovative products, not so much. Just the opposite. Speed dominates cost. I don't want things sitting in a boat. It might miss my whole market. My market window is very small in these types of products. So speed dominates cost. However, this is just a two by two matrix. You have two types of products and two types of supply chains. The real world is more complex. These are not hard and fast rules. These are more directional. So not all products will be purely functional or purely innovative. But this gives a nice sense that there are two vastly different extreme point solutions for supply chains. And the reality is going to be somewhere in the middle. And most firms aren't going to have just one or the other. They're going to have a portfolio of different supply chains that can meet the needs and requirements of all their products. Because every company will have some functional products and some innovative products. And so they need the supply chains to be able to adapt and be flexible to handle all the different products. Also, functional products can be innovative. So you look at soup, and the can of soup looks pretty traditional. Hasn't changed for about 100 years. But they also have another things that come out, like soup in a bag, soup for a microwave, different innovative products. So some of those products are more like the smartphones over here, the innovative ones. You don't know what the demand is going to look like. It's a short life cycle. You might not know how long you'll keep it. So some very functional-looking products, especially if they're offered under new product promotions, will behave like an innovative product. Over time, the same thing can happen in reverse. Innovative products can become functional. And so why can that happen? Well, let me give you some examples. So let's look in the high tech world. And this where it happens a lot. Look at Intel. The market for computer chips have shifted dramatically in the last 20 years. It has shifted from mostly PCs to laptops and notebooks to tablets and smartphones. In fact, in 2014, while about 200 million PCs were sold across the world, 1.8 billion smartphones were sold. So this looks like good news to Intel, because they put chips in everything. But the downside is the cost of chips in a smartphone is about 1/5 of that in the PCs. So what that means is the profit margin shrinks dramatically. So you're going to treat it very differently. You're no longer going to fly a chip across the world six times to go to a different production plant. You need to start being more concerned about the costs. So it becomes more efficient driven. Same thing for memory storage. The prices drop from about $1.5 per megabyte in 2000 to less than half of a penny per megabyte in 2015. That's incredible what the drop is doing. So you no longer have that profit margin. So now, instead of being innovative, I have to be more functional. I have to treat that product as a functional product and really get a handle on my cost. And that changes the way you'll set up your supply chain. Totally different industry-- pharmaceuticals. When most pharmaceutical drugs are introduced, they're protected by patents. Patents allow exclusivity. But after a certain time, in return for getting awarded this patent, it'll go off patent in a certain time. And at that point when it moves off that cliff, then generic manufacturers can produce them. So what's the effect there? Well, let's look at Lipitor. Lipitor is the best-selling drug of all time for Pfizer. It was introduced in the mid 1990s. And for about 10, 15 years, it was earning about $11 billion a year. It was Pfizer's largest source of revenue. In 2011, it came off patent. Immediately, even Pfizer started selling it for about 80% of its price. The price dropped so much that they have to now start protecting from generic manufacturers. At the same time, their profit dropped by over 50%. So what was once a drug which had high margin because it was protected under this patent, had exclusivity, suddenly you have competition. Your margins go down. So now I have to treat that very differently. This is a classic example of moving from an innovative product, where I always want to meet demand-- I don't care about the cost. Meet demand, meet demand. To now where I have to be functional. I have to be very efficient, because the margin has dropped. So these things can happen all the time. Another case study, and this is a great one. This about HP and their Inkjets and their other printers. And this is from a paper by Olavson, Lee, and DeNyse. And what they did is they traced how their supply chain has changed over the last 15, 20 years. And so if you look at the supply chain for the printers, you can decide that the different design elements you have is where do you manufacture the core products, how do you get the things from where it's located to where it's going to be distributed. If there's any final assembly, where do you do that? Where do you fulfill, and what kind of inventory model do you use? And so when the HP Inkjet was first introduced in the early 1990s, they were manufactured right where the market was-- within the United States and Europe. They used rail or truck, an intermediate speed, to get to their markets. Everything was done close to market. However, in the late 1990s, they changed things a little bit. They found moving manufacturing to Singapore in this case-- wherever it is offshore. In this case, it was Singapore initially, and then eventually moved to China. You can really reduce the manufacturing cost, and you can bring in the core components by ocean. However, what they did is they used postponement. And so what they created was the vanilla printer, and they did the customization for each country close to market. So that way, they decoupled the core component, which had very stable demand, and they did the customization much later. They postponed it until it hit the market. And they did all that in the more expensive areas. But still, they achieved a lot of benefits by manufacturing from a very low cost location. Another inventory stocking now means they configure to order. So they get the raw material, the vanilla printer, and then they customize it close to shore. But this didn't last as long. In the 2000s, they've shifted again, which proves the point that supply chains are never stable and constant. They're constantly changing. So what they did here now, they stayed with the manufacturing. And in fact, they moved from Singapore to China to even lower cost. They found the benefits there were so dramatic that they decide to build the entire supply chain, the finished product supply chain, off site. And then they move the finished product to the final market where they fulfill, and they do build to stock. Now, why was that done? Well, there's five reasons. One, the market is much more predictable and inventory rates are much lower now than they were in the early 1900s. There are many fewer SKUs now. The market is much more mature for the printer market. Therefore, risk pooling is not as beneficial. The product quality-- it's a very mature product-- is very stable. Minimal rework, so it's not as much of a concern. Printers are smaller. They're about a third the size of what they were when they were first introduced. So shipping the finished product all the way over ocean is much cheaper. And finally, the manufacturing costs offshore are so much lower. They dwarf any season, any improvements or any benefit they had from doing postponement at the close to market in a more expensive environment. So this is a great example. You have the paper attached to this video, so you can read that as well. But what it illustrates is that a company needs a portfolio, the supply chain design portfolio. There are three-- or really more options, but in this case, just three-- options that are listed for each type of supply chain function that you need to design. You have a high cost or very fast option. At the opposite end, you have a lower cost, a slower option. And then usually have something in the middle-- the Goldilocks option just in the middle. And so for each of these-- manufacturing, the way you ship, final assembly, order fulfillment, and inventory stocking-- you can see that there's different options. And depending on your situation, what they talk about in the paper is how they decided to select which option which made sense for that product at that time. And so you can use this as a template. And this is an example for what they did at HP. But it shows the concept that you need to constantly be trading off cost versus service and always looking for innovation in how you design your supply chain.

[lecture_00_03_roadmap_for_supply_chain_design_sc2x]
To finish up this lesson, I want to give you a roadmap of SC2x, let you see what's coming down the pike, what you'll see for the rest of the course. So there's really four things we're doing here in SC2x. We're all talking about design, and we're going to start with the design of the physical flow, then the information flows, the financial flows, and then we'll talk about design of the supply chain organization and the metric system itself. So let me talk about each of these in turn. So for the physical flow, this is all about answering the question, how should my material flow through a supply chain? We'll approach purchase in three ways. We'll first start with modeling the problem. And you've seen these types of problems before if you've taken SC0x, which hopefully you have. We'll talk about transportation and transhipment problems, facility location problems. And then we'll combine them together in what's known as a supply chain network design problem. So we'll talk about how to formulate the problems, and then we'll talk about how to solve them. And all this solving is doing is finding a trade-off between the costs and the level of service, or different types of costs-- fixed and variable. And so we'll be using mixed integer linear programming here. And so we'll be using this a lot during this course because it's a very powerful tool. So we've modeled the problem. We'll solve the problems. And then we'll interpret them, and this is the most important part. Surprisingly, to many students, the math is the least important part. What's really important is being able to understand what it is that you've come up with, being able to relate how sensitive it is to different inputs changing, so you understand how to do sensitivity analysis. We'll make sure that you understand to use this as a decision support tool, with the accent on the word "support," because models don't make decisions; people do. So it really helps you make a decision. You shouldn't just take whatever it spits out as the thing to do. And then we'll talk about some lessons learned and things you might run into when you do network design projects in the real world. So that's the physical flow. Then we'll move over to information flow. And as you should know by now, information is the thing that connects us from suppliers to customers, from initial supplier to final consumer and even back in a closed-loop supply chain. So the three big functions that we've talked about before are the source, the make, and the deliver, and we'll talk about each of these. We'll do them in a slightly different order. We'll start with the make decisions. This is all about coordinating the production or the manufacturing with other demands. And so we'll talk about production planning, and we'll use the exact same tools that we did in the network design for physical flows. We'll be using mixed integer linear programs. We'll talk about bills of material, fixed rise in plannings, and introduce you to material resource planning and distribution resource planning systems. Then we'll move further downstream to deliver, and this is all about collaborating with customers. And again, we'll use some of the same tools when we do aggregate planning. This is for a shorter time frame, and you're trying to tie-in some other things with your customers, as well as planning for production. So it kind of extends those models that we talked about. And we'll also talk about design distribution channels, how you should flow product, depending on the situations that you're in. And we'll finish up with Sales and Operations Planning, or S&OP, which is a very widely used practice in supply chains that tie together the operations side of the business with the marketing or sales side, how do you tie those together. Then we'll go back to sourcing. Now, I'm separating these for a couple of reasons. One, this will be after the midterm, and this is where we start introducing some more qualitative aspects. So we'll talk about sourcing strategies and procurement methods, but we will also use the same mixed integer linear program and illustrate how that is sometimes used for optimization-based procurement. So we'll follow information flow from supplier, how you procure, how you make, and how you deliver and coordinate with your customer. We'll just do it slightly out of order because it makes sense thematically. Then we'll move to financial flow. And all we're doing here is helping you understand how to translate all these supply chain concepts that you've learned in previous courses and in this course-- inventory levels, facilities you have, in-sourcing versus outsourcing-- and how to translate that into language that the CFO, the Chief Financial Officer, uses. These are income statements, balance sheets, cash flows. And so we'll hit five main topics-- activity-based costing, how do you work, or working capital, the cash-to-cash cycle, discounted cash flow analysis, the idea of supply chain finance, which is more about financing supply chain operations, not just the general topic. And then different types of financial analysis do include the DuPont model and some other widely used models. But the whole objective of this financial flow section is so you can understand how the CFO looks at your business, how the CEO looks at your operations. Then we'll finish up with designing the organization, and it's simply how should a supply chain be organized. This is more of a function for larger companies. If you're a small startup, you don't have many options. But if you're a large company, you have questions about how should it be organized? Should it be by business unit? Should it be centralized for the whole company? Should I centralize or decentralize by function, by business unit, by geography, all? So there's all these questions here. And then process and performance metrics, how do I measure the performance of these different processes that I do? How do I establish a balanced scorecard? So the roadmap then, these four elements, and you can see the amount of time you'll spend in each, the most will be spent on the information flows. But we'll try to balance these all together. And the nice thing at the end of the whole course, you'll have a case study that hopefully wraps a lot of this together. So that's it. Hopefully you're going to have fun. The first week starts next week. So please use the quick questions. Try the practice problems. Use the discussion forum. Have fun.

3.1 Introduction to Supply Chain Design
3.1.1 Summary
Supply Chain Design is as much an art as it is science. Science because we can quantify the impact of
diﬀerent choices and ﬁnd the optimal trade-oﬀs between diﬀerent choice parameters. But also, an art
because the assumptions we make are never going to completely match reality, and because the data we
use for our models is never going to be completely accurate. There is no single right way of making these
assumptions – doing this in a “good” way for a particular problem is more art than science!
Still, we learned that there are some frameworks, tools, and methods to aid the design process. The
course examines the ﬂows in a supply chain, with each design issue discussed in a separate module:
• Design of Physical Flows. How should materials ﬂow through the supply chain? We will model
physical ﬂows taking into account the costs of transportation and facilities. We will balance costs and
service using Mixed Integer Programs. However, key is in not just solving the models, but in interpreting
the results. Remember, the tools are decision -support tools, they are intended to support decisions, not
make them for us!
• Design of Financial Flows. How to translate supply chain concepts and actions into the language of
the CFO (Chief Financial Oﬃcer)? We will go through activity-based costing, cash ﬂow analysis and
capital budgeting to better understand how supply chain design decisions translate to changes in the
income statement and the balance sheet.
• Design of Information Flows. For this module, we will follow the SCOR-model’s three phases of
Source, Make, and Deliver. How should you work with suppliers? How should information be coordinated
between diﬀerent manufacturers, internal and external? And how should you coordinate and collaborate
with customers? In this module strategies and procedures for this is discussed.
• Designing the organization. How should a supply chain be organized? We will investigate supply
chain processes and how the measuring of the performance of the processes create diﬀerent incentives.
We will also discuss organizational structure.
3.1.2 Key Concepts
• Supply Chains – Two or more parties linked by a ﬂow of resources that ultimately fulﬁll a customer
request.
• Supply Chain Flows – physical ﬂows, ﬁnancial ﬂows, and information ﬂows. All are important to
consider when designing a supply chain.
3.1.3 Review of SC1x Concepts
Demand forecasting
Forecasting truisms:
• Forecast are always wrong – use ranges and track forecast error
• Aggregate forecasts are more accurate – risk pooling reduces coeﬃcient of variation
• Shorter time forecasts are more accurate – postpone customization as late as possible
Supply Chain Design 129
FIGURE 3.1: Product forecasting
Adapted from Kahn & Kennet (2006) New Product Forecasting
Inventory management
Inventory is generally managed using one of two key types of policies: continuous review or periodic
review.
• Continuous review means that the inventory position is continuously supervised (presumably by soft-
ware). As soon as the inventory position reaches a pre-determined level s a replenishment order of Q
units is placed. Consequently, the time between orders is uncertain.
• Periodic review means that the inventory position is reviewed at certain reoccurring points in time, such
as every evening after a retailer closes the store. Based on the current inventory position, a replenishment
order is placed to bring the inventory position up to a pre-determined level S. Consequently, the order
quantity is uncertain.
Both policies are used when decisions are made for a long horizon, where the items can be stored
between periods of replenishment, but demand in each replenishment period is uncertain.
Transportation
There are several ways of organizing the transportation in a supply chain. Among them:
• One-to-one: direct point-to-point movements from origin to destination, e.g. daily full van loads to each
customer
• One-to-many: multi-stop moves from a single origin to many destinations
• Many-to-many: moving from multiple origins to multiple destinations usually with a hub or terminal –
this decouples line haul (from e.g. a supplier to a terminal) from local delivery operations (from e.g. a
terminal to stores)
Total cost equation
The total cost equation speciﬁes the total logistics cost for a system for an arbitrary time period:
Total cost = Purchase Cost + Order (Set Up) Cost + Holding (Carrying) Cost + Shortage Cost
• Purchase: Cost per item or total landed cost for acquiring product.
130 MITx MicroMasters Program in SCM Key Concepts
• Ordering: It is a ﬁxed cost and contains cost to place, receive and process a batch of goods including
processing invoicing, auditing, labor, etc. In manufacturing this is the set up cost for a run.
• Holding: Costs required to hold inventory such as storage cost (warehouse space), service costs (in-
surance, taxes), risk costs (lost, stolen, damaged, obsolete), and capital costs, both for units in-transit
(pipeline inventory) and in warehouse (cycle stock + safety stock)
• Shortage: Costs of not having an item in stock including backorder, lost sales, lost customers, and
disruption costs.
Notation
• D: Demand rate (units/time)
• Q: Order quantity (units)
• L: Lead time (time)
• σDL: Standard deviation of demand during the lead time
• k: Safety stock factor
• c: Purchase cost ( $/unit)
• ct: Ordering Costs ( $/order)
• h: Holding rate – usually expressed as a percentage ( $/$ value/time)
• ce: Excess holding Costs ( $/unit-time); also equal to ch
• cs: Shortage costs ( $/unit)
• TC : Total Costs – the sum of all four cost elements

From the formula we see that transport speed as well as forecast accuracy has an impact on total cost

Fisher’s model: Innovative versus functional products
When designing a supply chain, the design needs to be adapted to the type of products it is intended for.
In Fisher’s model, products can be classiﬁed as being either more Functional or more Innovative.
• Functional: predictable demand, long life cycle, low margin, low error in production, low stock-out rates
• Innovative: unpredictable demand, short life cycle, high margin, high error in production, high stock-out
rates
As a rule of thumb, functional products should have a design focusing more on eﬃciency, whereas
innovative products should have a supply chain design focusing more on matching supply with demand.
Remember that these are not hard and fast rules. In practice there are, of course, many products
that share characteristics with both segments. Most ﬁrms are going to have a portfolio of supply chains.
Further, innovative products often move into becoming more functional as their markets become more
mature. This requires adaptation of the supply chain design. For instance, when the patent protection
for the cholesterol lowering drug Lipitor went out, Pﬁzer had to reduce the price to protect the drug
from generic competition. With a lower margin, the supply chain must be design with a higher focus on
eﬃciency.

customers’ demand.

3.2 Supply Chain Network Design Models
3.2.1 Summary
We review supply chain network design. Sometimes it is referred to as network modeling because you
need to build a mathematical model of the supply chain. This model is then solved using optimization
techniques and then analyzed to pick the best solution. Speciﬁcally, we will focus on modeling the supply
chain to determine the optimal location of facilities (plants, warehouses, etc.) and the best ﬂow of products
through this facility network structure.
132 MITx MicroMasters Program in SCM Key Concepts
3.2.2 Key Concepts
3.2.3 Network Models
Network models are a useful class of models that can be utilized to aid many types of supply chain
decisions. We review the basic notations of these models and show how to set up a simple spreadsheet
model in Excel or Libre Oﬃce to solve the model. We focus on two types of models that are relatively
simple yet powerful in deriving insights to support supply chain design decisions: the Transportation
Problem and the Transshipment Problem.
Both the transportation problem and the transshipment problem consider an underlying network of
nodes (facilities) and arcs (transport ﬂows between facilities). The objective is to minimize costs for a
given period (day, week, year) by choosing the number of units to be shipped/transported on diﬀerent
arcs during the period to fulﬁll demand, while meeting the capacity constraints. Both problems can be
formulated as linear programs (see below) and solved using your spreadsheet software of choice (e.g. Excel
or Libre Oﬃce). For details on how to run the solver, please refer to the separate guide for how to setup
and run the solver, which is found in the course material.
We discuss some of the limitations to these network models. First, in the models review, we limited
ourselves to variable costs for the arcs (i.e. transport costs per unit). Clearly, the cost structure for a
network is, in practice, often more complex, involving costs that are both ﬁxed or vary over diﬀerent
parameters. We also considered a single commodity – all plants produced perfectly substitutable goods.
The demand was deterministic, that is, we assumed that demand was perfectly known for the entire
period. Finally, we also assumed there was no capacity limits on the arcs, so that any amount could be
transported. Clearly, all of these assumptions limit our results, so we have to be careful what inferences
we make from the models. In the coming lessons we will relax some of these and also discuss how to make
inference while still using a relatively simple model.
Also – we noted that transportation and transshipment problems may have several optima (the same
value of the objective function is found by diﬀerent value on the decision variables).
Depending on the algorithm and software, diﬀerent optima may be the “ﬁrst choice” of the algorithm.
Network terminology
• Node or vertices – a point (facility, DC, plant, region)
• Arc or edge – link between two nodes (roads, ﬂows, etc.)
• Network or graph – a collection of nodes and arcs
The transportation problem
The transportation problem considers transports from i supply nodes to j demand nodes over arcs ij.
With each arc is associated a cost cij. The number of units transported on each arc ij is denoted xij.
These xij’s are our decision variables – we want to ﬁnd the amounts for each arc that minimizes total
cost.
Let z be the objective function (i.e., the function expressing the total cost we want to minimize).
Notation
• Indices
– Supply nodes i
– Demand nodes j
• Input data
– Si = Available supply from supply node i (units) ∀i∈S
– Dj = Demand in demand node j (units) ∀j∈D
– cij = Cost for sending one unit from supply node i to demand node j ($/unit) ∀i,j

– xij = Flow on arc from supply node i to demand node j ∀i,j

The transshipment problem
The transshipment problem is similar to the transportation problem. The diﬀerence is that we now
introduce a set of nodes that are neither supply nodes nor demand nodes, but transshipment nodes,
meaning that over a period, anything that goes in to the transshipment node needs to come out.

Note that we have the same formulation as for the transportation problem but with one diﬀerence:
Equations 3.8 have been added. This constraint is the constraint forcing the transshipment node to be
“empty” – the conservation of ﬂow constraint. Consider transshipment node j. The ﬁrst sum in the
constraint is then the total number of units shipped to node j from all other nodes i. The second sum is
the total number of units shipped from nodej to all other nodesi. The constraint says that this diﬀerence
must be zero.
3.2.4 Facility Location Models
In the previously introduce models we assumed that every facility in the network was used. We now relax
that assumption. First, we review where to locate a facility, given that we need a single facility. We then
investigate how many (and which) facilities to use, given a set of candidates. Lastly, we review how to
incorporate explicit Level Of Service (LOS) constraints in our models, to identify certain cost and service
trade-oﬀs in more detail.
For the single facility case we look at two fundamentally diﬀerent ways of approaching the decision.
The ﬁrst was by considering all points in the Euclidian space as potential candidates, and search for an
optimal location for a facility anywhere in this space. For this we use both the center-of-gravity- method
and the Weber method. While the former has an intuitive appeal, the Weber method is more appropriate
as it minimizes the actual transportation costs. The fundamental problem of relying on either of these
methods, though, is that the optimal solution may end up in places that are not feasible for a wide
number of reasons: lack of infrastructure (we could end up in a lake!), high construction costs, diﬃculty
of getting permits etc. So while these methods are useful to get a ball park ﬁgure of where to locate the
facility, they provide only a region to target.
We review another approach where, instead of considering the full Euclidian space, there is only a
ﬁnite set of candidate locations. This problem can be approached using a Mixed Integer Linear Program
(MILP), for which we can use a spreadsheet solver to ﬁnd the solution.
MILP used to solve the single facility problem could be easily extended to investigate the case of
multiple facilities. The model could then be used to answer both how many facilities to use, and which
facilities to use.
Finally, we incorporate explicit LOS constraints in our models. Two types of service performance
were considered: the average weighted distance to customers, and the amount of demand within a certain
distance from a DC. We can specify bounds for these performance measures and include them in the
MILP, to ensure that the optimal solution met the LOS requirements.
Continuous single facility problem
With a continuous single facility model, the aim is to ﬁnd one optimal point in the Euclidian space.
Center of gravity . With a center of gravity model, we let the optimal point’s coordinates be given
by weighted x and y coordinates, where the weight given to each node k is given by the demand in
that node. For instance, if we want to ﬁnd the optimal location for a DC that will support 3 stores, the
optimal location will be given by the weighted coordinates of the stores, where each store’s coordinates
are weighted by the demand at the store.

Network facility location problem
When we have a number of candidate locations to choose between, we can create a network ﬂow model
to ﬁnd which of the candidate locations provide the lowest cost. To do this, we formulate a Mixed Integer
Linear Program (MILP). Our problem is MILP because it has a linear objective function and linear
constraints, but with some variables being integers instead of continuous. The integer values are the Yi’s
that describe whether facility i is used (Yi = 1) or not (Yi = 0). Associated with each facility is a ﬁxed
cost for the time period, fi. As with the transportation problem, the number of units shipped between
two nodes are given by the xij’s.

• Pmax : Maximum number of facilities

Note that two constraints were added 3.27 and 3.28.
• A constraint on the average weighted distance: This constraint takes the (demand)-weighted average
distance and ensures that it is less than or equal to some critical level MaxAvgDist, the maximum
allowed average weighted distance. Consequently, the model will make sure that the average customer
is not “too far away”.
• A constraint on the amount of demand within a certain distance: The previous constraint considers
only the average, which means that we do not know how much of our demand has an LOS below a
certain threshold. For instance, even if we know that the average distance is less than 50 miles, we
may be interested in ensuring that at least 75% of our demand is less than 50 miles from a DC. This
constraint ensures that. For this we need to specify a distance (say 50 miles). The binary variable aij
then denotes whether or not a certain link is shorter ( aij = 1) than 50 miles or not ( aij = 0). That is,
the constraint ensures that not “too many” customers are far away.
It is also important to note that when we introduce service constraints, we may need to introduce
binding constraints on the demand. If not, the model may try to enforce the service constraint by delivering
more than demanded to certain demand nodes. This will however be artiﬁcial (and unsold) demand. To
ensure this is not the case, let the demand constraint be given by equality, instead of the computationally
more eﬃcient inequality.
3.2.5 Supply Chain Network Design
We now combine the diﬀerent techniques learned into one model. We take network optimization and
combine it with the facility location models together into what’s known as a supply chain network design
model.
Data collection: Transportation Data
The ﬁrst step in building the model is to collect data. For transports, we want to know the costs and
capacities associated with both inbound and outbound transportation.
With the Supply Chain Network Design Model, transportation costs are assumed linear in transport
volumes. Clearly, this is not always the case: there may be minimum charges, ﬁxed costs, or many other
types of ﬁxed and variable cost components. However, in order to build the model, a linear approximation
must be found. This could be done in several diﬀerent ways:
• Take average transport cost from historical data
• Use list prices
• Use regression analysis to ﬁnd the costs
• Use benchmark rates from other sources
Whichever way you use to uncover these costs, you need to be aware of how your linear approximation
aﬀects the reliability of the model.
Data collection: Facility Data
The Supply Chain Network Design Model allows facility costs to be both ﬁxed and variable in volume.
There will always be people arguing that “all costs are ﬁxed” or “all costs are variable”, but what you
need to consider is how ﬁxed and variable cost components aﬀect the solution your model searches for .
Fixed costs will make it expensive to have many facilities, so the model will try to reduce the number
138 MITx MicroMasters Program in SCM Key Concepts
of facilities. Variable costs will make it costly to have long distances, and the model will therefore try to
increase the number of facilities (or at least reduce average distances).
If facilities are owned and/or operated by a third-party, ﬁnding the ﬁxed and variable costs is normally
straight-forward – they are given in the contract. If a facility is operated in-house, other techniques have
to be used. Among them:
• Activity-based costing to ﬁnd variable costs
• Regression analysis over volume (note that the intercept is the ﬁxed cost component)
For facilities we also need to understand the capacity. The capacity is the maximum throughput over
a speciﬁed unit of time (e.g. a week). Note that this can be very diﬃcult to measure in practice, since
shifts can be added, while capacity also depends on planning and scheduling.
Network Design Baselines
You should be careful in which baseline you use for comparing your results. Three baselines are important
to consider:
• Baseline 1 – Actual costs: what cost does the model give us if we use the actual, current ﬂows? We
want to know how well the model matches reality. You use this baseline to calibrate the model.
• Baseline 2 – Adhere to policy: there may be a policy in place that you are simply not adhering to in
your operations. With this baseline you want to know how other solutions compare to what you ought
to be doing according to the policy in place.
• Baseline 3 – Optimal DC assignment: if you were to use the optimal assignment for the DC – what
would the solution look like? With this baseline you can isolate the eﬀect of number of DCs since you
optimize allocation.
It is important to keep in mind that you compare design changes to the right baseline. For instance,
if you use the model to ﬁgure out how much you could reduce costs by reducing the number of DCs, you
need to be aware the model will tell you this while, at the same time, optimizing the allocation given the
number of DCs. Hence you should compare your solution - the “optimal number of DCs under optimal
allocation” - to Baseline 3, where you have the “current number of DCs under optimal allocation”. Why?
Because otherwise you compare solutions that have optimal allocations to solutions that do not have
optimal allocations – you do not isolate the eﬀect of changing the number of DCs.
Running Scenarios
One of the key beneﬁts of the Supply Chain Network Design Model is that it is easy and fast to run
diﬀerent scenarios. Scenarios can investigate uncertain parameters (sensitivity analysis) or explore how
diﬀerent constraints aﬀect the optimal solution.
While this is a great beneﬁt, it is important not to get “analysis paralysis” – just because it is possible
to run many scenarios does not mean it is the way to go. It is important to understand which scenarios
are relevant and, most importantly, how to interpret the results. For instance, which baseline should be
used for comparing the results of the scenario?
3.2.6 Advanced Supply Chain Network Design Topics
There are many advanced supply chain network design topics and we introduce several here. The ﬁrst is
robust optimization using simulation along with optimization. This is a relatively simple and quick way
to get a handle on the impact of the variability of demand (or other factors) on facility selection. We also
cover multi-commodity ﬂow problems, which signiﬁcantly increases both the complexity and size of the
models. While more complicated, multi-commodity ﬂow models are still very similar to the transshipment
Supply Chain Design 139
and facility location models explored previously. The concept of Flexibility was introduced following the
approach developed by Jordan and Graves (1995). Forming chains between plants increases the overall
ﬂexibility at a much lower cost than if we provided full ﬂexibility. The ﬁnal expansion of the model covers
multiple time periods. This is meant for more tactical planning periods – but again, the models were very
similar to the simpler single commodity transshipment models with the addition of inventory balance
equations. The ﬁnal section reviews how pipeline inventory, safety stock, and cycle stock can be included
in strategic network design models.
Robustness – demand uncertainty
Supply Chain Network Design (SCND) models are deterministic. They assume that each input value is
known with certainty and exhibits no variability. We know this is not the case in reality. So, while there
are many more sophisticated mathematical techniques that address this shortcoming, we can use simple
Monte-Carlo simulation to try to understand how robust our solutions are.
Essentially, the method involves re-solving the model with new input information each time. The new
input data is randomly selected using estimates of the distribution of the variables. The easiest way to
simulate random variables in spreadsheets is to use the RAND() function. It returns a number between
0 and 1.00 following a uniform distribution.
• To simulate a uniform distribution with mean of X, plus or minus Y%, we would set the value to
=X∗ (1 + (RAND ()− 0.50)∗ (2∗Y ))
So that if we wanted 250 +/- 20%, we’d use =250*(1+(RAND()-0.50)*0.40) returning values from ∼
200 to 300.
• To simulate a Normal Distribution with a mean of X and a standard deviation of Y, we would use:
=NORM.INV (RAND (),X,Y )
So for mean of 300 with standard deviation of 45 we’d use =NORM.INV(RAND(),300,45) returning
values∼ N(300,45).
After each new simulation of input values, the model is run and the results are stored. The analysis of
the resulting runs can help determine which facilities, for example, are more likely to stay open under
a variety of demand outcomes. This is not an exhaustive method – simply one approach to gauge the
robustness of the design.
Multi-commodity ﬂow model
Multi-commodity ﬂow (MCF) models introduce multiple products. Each product will have its own de-
mand, supply and other characteristics. If each product is independent and there are no interactions,
then we can simply model each commodity individually. Whenever there is a shared resource (common
capacity constraint, for example), then a multi-commodity ﬂow model is needed.
The formulation is shown below. The primary addition to the original formulation is the additional
subscript of k for the diﬀerent commodities for the costs, decision variables, demand, and supply. We
added, in this formulation, two types of supply capacity constraints: one that is location-commodity
speciﬁc and one that is location speciﬁc for all commodities there. These are quite common in practice.

Solving the MCF models are the same as the single commodity models – they are simply larger and
more diﬃcult to interpret. The multiple layers of constraints make the calculation of individual costs
diﬃcult – this is covered in activity based costing and managerial accounting.
Chain approach to ﬂexibility
Supply chain network design (SCND) models do not consider variability of demand. A MILP solution,
while optimal, can often be quite fragile. This means, that if any of the input values change, the total
costs could change dramatically. We might want to ﬁnd a SCND that is more ﬂexible in its ability to
handle changes in demand – in particular sudden surges or peaks in demand for certain products or
commodities.
Full ﬂexibility can be achieved by forcing each plant to manufacture every product. This provides
complete demand ﬂexibility as capacity can be diverted from any plant to handle surges of a certain
product. This is very expensive, however. The opposite extreme is to create dedicated plants that only
manufacture a single product. Dedicated plants are able to leverage economies of scale and tend to
increase the level of expertise, however, they also increase risk exposure and limit ﬂexibility to respond to
surges. A clever design called chaining allows manufacturing networks to achieve most of the ﬂexibility
allowed in fully ﬂexible designs, but at a fraction of the cost.
Multiple time period models
Up to this point, each model assumed a single bucket of time. We have expanded this now to consider
multiple time periods. This is more common with tactical time frame (weeks to months) models.
To model multiple time periods, we need to introduce the idea of the inventory level at a location, j,
at time t, Ijt. This is the quantity available at location j at the end of time t. This allows us to charge
an inventory holding charge (h) for each time period.
We also need to include an inventory balance constraint – similar to the conservation of ﬂow constraints
in transshipment models. This is the 3rd constraint in the formulation below, which states, the sum of all
ﬂow into node j during time t, minus the sum of all ﬂow out of node j during time t, plus the inventory
available at node j at the end of time t-1, minus the inventory available at node j at the end of time t is
zero.
