Ratio Analysis Advice
There are not any “correct” values for ratios. Ratio values need to be understood in context such as:
compare with industry averages, compare with speciﬁc competitors; and/or observe trends over time.
You will need to develop a framework of several ratios to monitor. In combination, these clues may tell
an interesting story.
DuPont Analysis
DuPont analysis is based on a return-on-investment formula developed in 1914 by a DuPont explosives
salesman named Donaldson Brown and used by the company. Mr. Brown later used it as CFO at General
Motors, but it was already known as the DuPont formula.
R =T×P
Where:
R = rate of return on capital invested
T = rate of turnover of invested capital
P = percentage of proﬁt on sales
In essence, it is a simple combination of two ratios:
ROA = Net Margin× Asset Turnover
Net Margin = Net Income/Sales
Asset Turnover = Sales/Total Assets
The formula commonly used today focuses on ROE for Dupont analysis incorporates ﬁnancial leverage:
ROE = Net Margin× Asset Turnover× Financial Leverage
Net Margin = Net Income/Sales
Asset Turnover = Sales/Total Assets
Financial Leverage = Total Assets/Equity
Supply Chain Design 185
GMROI: Gross Margin Return on Investment
GMROI has a narrower focus than DuPont and is useful in evaluating inventory decisions, and their
impact on proﬁtability.
GMROI = Gross Margin× Inventory Turnover
Gross Margin = Gross Proﬁt/Sales
Inventory Turnover = Sales/Inventory
ROIC
ROIC (Return on Invested Capital) is an overall measure of ﬁnancial performance. This includes returns
on all capital for investors seeking a return, not only on equity. ROIC is a good measure for supply chain
performance. It is not confounded by ﬁnancing strategies. It shows the fundamental earning power of the
ﬁrm, i.e. created by operations.
ROA = Net Income / Total Assets
ROE = Net Income / Equity
Both measures can be distorted by ﬁnancial leverage, i.e. more debt.
ROIC = NOPAT / Invested Capital
Net Operating Proﬁt After Tax (NOPAT)
• NOPAT = EBIT (1 – Tax rate)
• Earnings after tax as if it were all equity ﬁnanced (i.e., not considering interest expense or tax books)
Invested Capital (IC)
• IC = Interest-Bearing Debt + Equity
• Sum of all sources of cash on which a return must be earned (i.e., not including accounts payable)
• You may want to subtract excess cash
• Prefer the book value of Invested Capital (i.e. the value invested) rather than the market value
RONA
RONA (Return on Net Assets) is similar to ROIC and similar to ROCE (Return on Capital Employed).
It is a return on assets that are expected to produce proﬁt.
RONA = NOPAT/ Net Assets
Net Assets
• NA = Fixed Assets + Non-cash Working Capital
• NA = Total Assets – Current Liabilities – Cash

3.7 Organizational, Process, and Performance Metric Design
3.7.1 Summary
We also review three areas of “soft” design: organizations, processes, and metrics.
Organizational design for supply chains has evolved as the profession has changed. Supply chain or-
ganizations started as separate silo-ed functions or activities spread out across diﬀerent larger divisions,
such as ﬁnance, manufacturing, and marketing. The areas started combining into materials management
(covering the ﬂow of inbound materials) and physical distribution (covering the movement of ﬁnal prod-
ucts to customers). Logistics groups emerged later to bring these two functions together in order to work
out trade-oﬀs between the sometimes conﬂicting goals of inbound and outbound management. Currently,
there are various forms of organizations used across diﬀerent companies. The main perspectives diﬀer in
terms of viewing supply chains or logistics as a set of functions, as a program, or within a matrix. The
matrix form is most common in larger global ﬁrms. In these cases, logistics is a horizontal function that
interacts directly with each vertical business unit providing shared services.
The key trade-oﬀ involved with supply chain organizational design is whether to centralize or de-
centralize diﬀerent activities. Centralization implies that all decisions are made at a headquarters while
decentralization moves this to the regions or individual business units. Each has a role with the general
rule of thumb shifting most procurement, long-range planning, and new product activities to central and
keeping daily operations decentralized.
Processes within supply chains are essentially transformations of inputs into outputs. Procurement
transforms an order into product. Transportation converts material at a distribution center to product
at the customer location. Each of these processes can be managed individually or looked at as within a
greater system. The diﬀerent processes within the supply chain should be designed to complement each
other.
Metrics follow the process structure. All metrics can be boiled down to utilization, productivity,
or eﬀectiveness metrics. Utilization measures inputs compared to some norm (capacity, standard, etc.).
Productivity or eﬃciency compares inputs to outputs. Eﬀectiveness compares output to some norm value.
The diﬀerent metrics can be evaluated for robustness, integration, usefulness, and validity. There is no
single best metric, however, they will all have trade-oﬀs between these criteria. This is why systems of
metrics should be used in a balanced scorecard framework.
3.7.2 Key Concepts
3.7.3 Supply Chain Organizational Design
Centralization vs. Decentralization
Organizational design centers on whether to centralize or decentralize an activity. Each has a place and
there are basic trade-oﬀs involved.
Centralized
• Leverages economies of scale
• Harmonizes policies and practices
• Allows for optimal (global) solutions
Decentralized
• Allows decision making to be closer to customer
• Allows decisions to reﬂect local cultures and customs
• Allows for optimal solutions within a region
• Allows business units to act autonomously

3.7.4 Supply Chain Metric Design
Performance Metrics
Metrics can be divided into Utilization, Productivity, and Eﬀectiveness measures.
FIGURE 3.25: Performance dimensions
Performance Metric Criteria and Trade-oﬀs
Metrics can be evaluated according to several metrics – but they will always have trade-oﬀs.
• Robust: The metric is interpreted similarly by all users, is comparable across time, location and
organizations, and is repeatable.
• Valid:The metric accurately captures events and activities measured and controls for exogenous factors.
• Integrative: The metric includes all relevant aspects of the process and promotes coordination across
functions and divisions (and even enterprises).
Supply Chain Design 189
• Useful: The metric is readily understandable by decision makers and provides a guide to action.
FIGURE 3.26: Performance criteria trade-oﬀs
Balanced Metrics
Metric systems should be designed to balance across three key areas: Asset utilization (utilization metrics),
Eﬃciency (productivity metrics), and Customer Response (eﬀectiveness metrics). Diﬀerent industries will
emphasize diﬀerent aspects of process performance.

[lecture_01_00_introduction_to_network_models]
We're going to start our discussion in designing the physical flow in the supply chain by introducing network models. So let's start with some common terms. There are many different types of networks that we're going to see. But they all consist of the same two things-- nodes and arcs. Nodes are simply points. And they also sometimes are called vertices. And so these could be distribution centers, customers, regions, plants, whatever. For our purposes, they'll be probably actual facilities. But they can be more abstract things and other network problems. Then you're going to have arcs, or edges, or links. And these are the things that connect the different nodes. And so we can see, this could be a road network. This could represent flows. This could represent how cash is used on different projects. You can make it represent so many different things. That's the beauty of networks. Now, a network then or a graph-- sometimes, you see graph theory and network theory-- it's just a collection of nodes and arcs. And there's a whole large domain here. If you're really interested, you can look into network models. We're going to focus on them specifically in the supply chain space. So there's some problems that you're going to see more often than others in supply chain that use networks. Let's talk about them briefly. And you've seen some of these before. First one, shortest path, so this is the problem if I want to go from node i to node j-- let's say that's New York and the j is Los Angeles-- so I have a road network. So the shortest path problem is finding what is the shortest, least cost, usually distance, through the network. What's my path from i to j? And so this is pretty much-- we use this every day. If we use any kind of mapping tool or software, either on our phone or in our car. So this is a classic algorithm. It's kind of the root of almost every other algorithm that we're going to talk about. Then you've got the traveling salesman problem. And what the traveling salesman problem does-- let me just clean this up just a little bit, there we go-- so I've got these nodes still and these arcs. And what the traveling salesman problem says, let's start at one node. Let's call that the home. And what I want to do is find a path-- or a tour rather that visits each node once and only once and then returns home. So let's say I'm going to go here. Then I'm going to go there. And I want to find the tour that is the shortest distance. And this is a problem that's been around for hundreds of years, seems trivial, but can be quite complicated. And so this is known as the traveling salesman problem. Then you have another problem called vehicle routing. Let me clean this little network up again, erase this. And so what this is, it's a little bit different, but it's very much related to the traveling salesman problem. Let's say I have a distribution center or a source node. So this is where a bunch of supply or physical product is. And then I have a bunch of locations I need to deliver to, whether these are stores, customers, whatever. So I've got this source node, the square, and I want to visit each of the demand nodes. And so I want to find the shortest tours-- and this is each vehicle, because I have multiple vehicles-- and what tour will they take to visit all the different sites to deliver their product and return. So what you have is a bunch of vehicle tours, or vehicle routing problem. So it's the least cost, usually least distance, tours for vehicles to distribute all the product that's demanded from a single source node. Now, the problem we're going to focus on the most in this lesson and the next several lessons is known as the min cost flow problem. And so what that consists of is a little bit different. So we're trying to find the least cost flows of a certain product or a commodity through our network that satisfies demand at certain nodes. So I have certain demand nodes, call these customers. And I want to flow it-- maybe I have some distribution centers here, and I have some plants or sources. So I have product that goes from the plants to the DCs. And then each DC can go to each separate customer. This plant can also go here. And this DC could also distribute here. This plant could also go here. You see, I have all these different flows. And so I want to figure out the lowest cost solution from my source nodes to my ultimate demand nodes in the least cost manner. So we'll talk a lot more about this problem. But as you see, as you get into this deeper, you'll see they all are very much related, and they're all tied to networks. So let's talk about our approach in this module. Here are some things we're going to do. Three things we will do when we talk about network modeling. First, our whole focus here is on the insights. I want you to understand what are the trade-offs involved with design, and especially, in this case, design of the physical flow. What are the trade-offs? How do I quantify them? What are the decisions that I'm making? I want you to understand the common approaches that are used in practice and each of their strengths and weaknesses. Second thing we're going to do is we're going to formulate math programs for optimization. We're going to solve these design problems using optimization tools. I could use "seat of pants," for example, which means my experience. I know that I always want to have a distribution center here in Atlanta. You can also use other techniques, like heuristics. So if you know your domain is of a certain volume, I know I need that over x number of distribution centers. And like I said, it's using optimization. So I'm going to show you how to formulate a math program, not to solve an individual problem, but solve a class of problems. I'm still minimizing the sum of all the costs on all the arcs times the flow on each of those arcs. My supplies constraints hasn't changed. I still have this for all i's in S, which there's only two of them. So there will be two supply constraints. Demand, this is making sure demand is met for all j's within D. And there's three of those. Hasn't changed. And then let me jump down here to the non-negativity. All our flow still has to be in the real world, has to be zero or greater. The only thing that's new is this constraint. This is known as a constraint for the conservation of flow. So let me talk a little bit about this because it's a little complicated, but it's critical for any network problem that we have. Because what we've done so far, we had two types of nodes. We had supply nodes at these plants where stuff comes from. And then we had demand or sink nodes where stuff when to. Now we're introducing these transshipment or intermediate nodes where whatever flows in has to flow out. You can't accumulate product. How do we enforce that? Well let's take an example. Let's say I have a node j. Pick whatever your intermediate node is. And I'm going to look at everything coming into it, all the x_i,j's. So everything going into j, I'm going to sum those. Sum for all is of x_i,j. So x_1 to node j. x_2 to node j. x_3 to node j. All the things coming in. And then I'm going to look at everything going out. And all I did was flip the subscripts. So it's x_j,i, and I'm going to sum this over i again. And so for node j, it's everything going from node j to node 1, to node 2, to node 3, to node 4, whatever. So what's coming in and what's going out has to be equal. Right? So all I've done is this x_i,j. The first term is this. The second term is this. So if they're equal to each other, if I subtract one from the other, I should get 0. So my constraint is simply saying the sum of the input minus the sum of the output has to equal 0 for all nodes that are not in demand nodes and that are not supply nodes. In other words, only those intermediate nodes have to have conservation of flow. What goes in needs to go out. Very straightforward. And it's a nice easy, elegant way to enforce that. So we'll enforce this for any node that is a transshipment point, a cross dock, a mixing center. Hubs sometimes are called this. So the idea is I've got three types of nodes now-- source nodes where stuff comes from, demand nodes where stuff goes to, and transshipment nodes where stuff goes in and out and doesn't accumulate. So how do I formulate this? Should look very similar. I've got my objective function up here in cell B3. And it's simply the sum product of my decision variables times my cost on each arc, because that's the only cost I have so far. You see I broke the arcs up between the ones in columns C through F are the plants to the centers. And then from G to L, there are the flows from the center to the regions. Pretty straightforward. And then I have the same supply constraints in rows nine and 10. The same demand constraints in rows 13 through 15. And then my conservation of flow. So let's look at row 18 and see what it's doing. This is going for center A. So I'm saying, OK, because this is just the sum product here in cell M18, sum product of this row, this array, times my decision variables up here in row five. It's going to be this variable in cell C5 times 1 plus what's in x in cell E5 times 1 minus what's in x A1 which is in cell G5 five minus what's in cell H5 minus what's in cell I5. So it's what's the inbound is in columns C through F minus the outbound, which is columns G through L. And again, the reason why I love this formulation, you see the whole matrix. So you can get a sense of what this is saying. These are all the inbounds to A. And here's all the outbounds to A. Here are all the outbounds from B. And here are all the inbounds to B. The logic just hits you right in the face. The other beautiful thing, the equations that you use to solve this are really straightforward. All the constraints here are really the same thing. It's a sum product of its own array times the decision variables up here in row five. So you really only have two equations in this whole math program. Now the downside of this formulation-- and we'll talk about this later-- is that it gets big. It gets quite big. But I love it when you're trying to figure out a problem because you can see the structure of the problem. All right, we have it in the spreadsheet. You can play it around and put different numbers in and see what it does. But let's solve it. So here's a spreadsheet. I'm in Excel. I open up the solver. You can see I put the objective function in, my changing cells or decision variables. And then I've got the constraints. First, the non-negativity constraints which again are redundant because I can just do this. Then you've got the region demand constraints in rows 13 to 15. Then I've got the supply constraints in rows 18-- oops, the supply constraints in nine and 10. There we go. And then in 18 and 19 are my flow conservation constraints. That's the only new things you've added here really. I make sure I'm solving Simplex. And then I solve it. And this is what you get. It's a solution. And let's look at what it's saying. It's saying shipping 75 tons from plant one to center B. I can just read my decision variables across here and I can see what the solution is for $69,200 a week. Now, let's look at in a picture because sometimes it's easier to see. So here's my solution. And there's my input data. So let's look at this for a second. Now what we have, look at plant one is not operating at capacity. So it has a capacity of 100 tons a week. And so it's only shipping out 75. Plant two now is operating at full capacity. That's interesting. If I look at the distribution centers or the packing centers rather, I can see that B gets served by both plant one and plant two. And plant two only goes to A. And then A is the only one who serves region one. So if I look at region one I'm only served by one plant. Look at plant three and plant two. The sand, I don't whatever sand I receive here at region two, it could come from plant one or plant two. I assume it's fungible, it's a commodity. So that's actually a nice thing, a little more resilient. I have a little more backup, a little more flexibility. Region one out here, if its demand suddenly goes up, that's a little bit of a problem because I'm only being single sourced. We'll talk about this in later lessons. But what I want to make sure you start doing is when you look at a solution, don't just say OK, there's my answer, done. Does it make sense? Does it fit everything? Are there any issues that are missed? As we talk more about this, that's the more important component of using one of these models. Not just being able to formulate it, get the math and get a number, we can do that. But the real thing is how do I interpret that result? What does it mean? Where are the strengths? Where are the weaknesses? Because these models are all decision support. They are not the final solutions.

[lecture_01_04_key_take_aways]
All right, let's take a big, deep breath and focus on what we've learned in this lesson, because it was a big lesson. We did a lot of different things. What are the key takeaways? Well, we introduced two basic network flow models. The first model was the transportation model. So this is where I'm going from origins to destinations, and I'm flowing in. I'm trying to find the minimum cost. We introduced the idea of a math formulation. We have an objective function, which is what I'm circling right now, subject to a series of constraints. And in this case, the transportation problem has supply constraints that everything flowing out of an i to all the different j's has to be less than or equal to the supply that's there. And then you have demand constraints. And these simply say that everything flowing into a certain demand node has to at least be equal to the demand that's required there, the D's. And all my flow has to be 0 or greater. So this is the granddaddy of flow problems, the transportation problem. We then made it a little more complicated, because that's what we like to do. And I added what's known as hubs, or transshipment points. Let me just circle those. So now, we have sources. We have sinks, or demand nodes. And I have transshipment hubs, or mixing centers. And the only difference between the transportation problem and the transshipment problem is that I have to add in these flow conservation constraints for any of the transshipment hubs. So this is simply saying what goes in must be what goes out. So what goes in for A has to be equal to what goes out. What goes in to B has to be equal to what goes out. And it's very logical. You need this to enforce a network structure. Now, a lot of limitations-- these are the simplest problems in the world, even though they're complicated the first time you go through. But we had very simple cost structures. We only had variable cost on the arcs. There were no fixed arc costs. There no node costs. In fact, I assumed that you're going to use every single one of these. I didn't have you decide which plant to use, or which DC, or give you the choice of whether to open one or not. We ignored that and said, you're going to use everything that's there. The question is, how much are you going to use it. We had a single commodity. I assumed that the sand you produced here at plant one was the same one desired at each of the customer locations. There isn't specific commodities or product types. I assumed that they're all fungible. What I produce in one can flow to anywhere-- single commodity. I also assumed a deterministic solution. My demand is a constant. It's an input. I didn't allow any variability of the demand, of the supply, or the flow. This gets more complicated-- and we'll talk about this later on-- but for the first cut, we assumed very deterministic solutions. Also we assumed everything was uncapacitated. I had no capacity constraints here at my distribution centers or at my plants. I just had a supply limitation. But you could think, it'd be a little more complicated if I said, you know what, this distribution center or packaging center A can only handle a certain level. And then that would force things to happen. And we'll get into that. Same thing with arcs, it might be that this arc can only take a certain amount of volume. We assumed total uncapacitation. There is no capacity limits. Infinite can flow. And so you can kind of see the path we're going to do. We're going to reduce some of these limitations, loosen some of these assumptions. And the models get a little more complex, but they get a little more realistic. We also did something that's really fundamental. And you'll use this throughout the course and probably beyond in your career. And that's the whole idea of formulating a model. And there is a classic process for this. The first thing you have to do-- and these are kind of common sense-- read and understand the problem thoroughly. 90% of the time, if you have a problem, you didn't read the problem that carefully. And in this class, and in other ones, if English is not your first language, read it twice, because a lot of times, the wording is critical. Once you understand that, figure out, OK, what are the things I'm trying to decide? What are my decision variables-- what the spreadsheets call changing variables? What are the things I'm trying to decide? Then you say, OK, what is the thing I'm trying to minimize or maximize? What is the objective function? Am I minimizing cost? Am I maximizing profit? What am I trying to do? Then you say, OK, what am I subject to? What are my constraints? I need to make sure everything coming out of a supply node doesn't exceed supply. Everything going into a demand node has at least be the required demand. I need to make sure that for transshipment hubs, what goes in, what goes out-- all the different constraints. And then you identify upper and lower bounds. For this, we only had a lower bound on the decision variable, zero. You couldn't be below zero. But there might be cases where you need to have a certain level, a minimum. And then you solve it. It's very important that you understand your assumptions and limitations. There is often a naive assumption that these complex models are perfect. And they are not. They all have assumptions. They all have limitations. They're models. They are never reality. They're abstractions of reality. And as I move closer and closer to reality, it gets more complicated. And we're building in more assumptions. So the most important takeaway from this is be cognizant and recognize the assumptions you're making in any model that you put together. All right, that's it for this lesson. So please use the comments for any questions. If you have suggestions, let us know. Use a discussion forum. And I'll let you look at Dexter, wondering how he can optimize his way to his own ball through the fence.

[lecture_01_05_facility_location_models]
OK so we've discussed network flow models and we've shown how to formulate them and solve them. In all those models though, we assumed that every facility that was there was used. Now we're going to restrict that problem a little bit and focus in on the decision of which facilities to use, which facilities to open. So this whole lesson is about facility location models. So we're going to start very simple in this first segment-- in this lesson-- and we're going to start talking about continuous single facility problems. By continuous, what I mean is you're on a Euclidean Space and I can place a facility anywhere within that space. So I just want to find the optimal space and it's a single facility, which means I'm only looking for one location and we're going to talk are two different ways to do this. One is known as the Weber problem and that's finding the minimum weighted distance from each of the points that I have and the other is known as center of gravity, which comes from physics. And we'll explain these and we'll talk about the strengths and weaknesses of each of them. Then we're going to go and look at a slightly different approach. Instead of having a continuous set of options to pick from, where you can have any kind of location, you're going to have a set of candidate locations and sometimes this is called a network facility location problem. And we're going to solve this using our old friend the mixed integer linear program which we started talking about in previous lessons, and we're going to show how to formulate it and we're going to show how to solve it in spreadsheets. And then we're going to expand it to multiple locations because we want to talk about the trade-offs-- because there are a lot of trade-offs involved with having a single facility, two facilities, three-- and you're going to have trade offs between the costs-- different types of costs-- as well as level of service and we'll express those and show those, formulate the problems and solve them. And then finally we'll wrap it up, talking about sensitivity analysis using these models because as we've tried to get across, the benefit of these models is not necessarily just to come up with an answer, you know, the answer's six, the real benefit is that it allows you to explore the options in the robustness of a solution. You can explore other potential solutions so it really gives you an idea of understanding the problem better-- that's the real benefit of using these models. All right so let's get started.

[lecture_01_06_continuous_location_models]
Let's start with the simplest location model, the single location model on a continuous surface. So the problem objective of this is to find one location that minimizes the transportation costs from this point to K different destination points. This is also known as the Weber problem, named after Alfred Weber, who first formulated this problem in the early 1900s. So the idea here is, I'm on a Euclidean Space. So it's a tabletop. I can go directly from point to point. There's no underlying network. The center location could be anywhere on the space. So think of a tabletop, and I'm putting one cup anywhere on that table. And the transportation cost is calculated as the distance multiplied by the volume or the weight. So if I have something that's 10 units going 2 miles, that would be 20 unit miles-- same thing as 20 units going 1 mile. So it's the transportation cost, distance times the weight. All right, so here's what the formulation would look like. It should look kind of familiar. I'm minimizing something, and I have my locations. Each location is subscripted k. So I'm saying I'm trying to minimize this function, and the minimized distance is going to be z. So z is equal to the summation for all the points k within this larger set K. And each of those is multiplied by its weight, w_k, times the distance. And that's what this function is, D_k of x, y. So x and y are my decision variables. And this is the x-coordinate, or horizontal coordinate, and the y-coordinate for my center point. So I'm trying to minimize this distance. And my two decision variables are x and y. Those are the variables that I'm trying to find the answer to. So because I'm in Euclidean space, this distance is simply the square root of the sum of the squared differences in the x and the y directions. So this is, x_k is, for the k-th location, or the point, it's its x-coordinate. And here's its y-coordinate. And I'm subtracting off from the center location, squaring that, adding it to that squared difference of the y location-- square root. And that gives me the distance from the k-th point to the center point. So I want to find and select the x and y that minimizes this function. All right, so we can solve this in Excel. And let me give you an example. And we'll use this example for the rest of this week. The New England Root Beer Distributors-- NERD. And at NERD, we serve root beer in New England. And we sell our products currently in just three cities-- Boston, Springfield, and Providence, Rhode Island. So here we are up in Boston. Springfield is out west. And Providence is down south just a little bit. So this is our market. And each one of the cities sells a certain amount of root beer and marketing is giving that Boston sells 425 units. Call it per week, or whatever time period: Providence sells 320, and Springfield sells 220. And the question is, where should I set up my distribution center to service these three markets? Now, I've given each of the cities a coordinate. So they have an x and a y coordinate. So Providence is at x-coordinate 86, y-coordinate 40. Boston is at 100, 80. And Springfield, over to the west, is 20, 60. So these are x, y coordinates. I think it's centered down here on New Haven down here, further to the left. It'll make sense a little later. And so what we're trying to do is find where is the location that would minimize the weighted distance. So where in this triangle is the distance that minimizes the distance traveled for each one of the units, for 425 units from Boston, 320 units Providence, and for Springfield, 220 units. So where will I locate that? If you think about it, you know that it's going to be somewhere within this triangle. There's no way that you'll have a shorter distance that falls outside. And I'll let you think about that, and prove that to yourself. So let's see how we solve this in a spreadsheet. This is restating the problem. And here's my function I'm trying to minimize. And so all I've done is taken the table, and put it into a spreadsheet. You can see that column B shows the weight for each city. Column C is the x-coordinate. Column D is the y-coordinate. And column E, what I've got is the distance. Now, it's finding the distance from what? Well, it's from these x, y coordinates that I put down here in cells C6 and D6. So what this is saying is that Boston is 17.54 miles away from this location. So if I typed in different values down here in cell C6 and D6, that location would change. Column F is simply the weighted distance. I multiply it by the weight for each of the cities. And then down here in cell F6, I'm simply summing them up. Because what do I want to do? I want to find the x and y-coordinates that minimize this value, that's currently 31,179. I can do this using Solver. So what's my objective function? It's F6. It's this cell right here. I'm trying to minimize that. And what cells am I going to change? The cells that are right here in the x and y, that I have highlighted in yellow. Now, for constraints, it's a little different, because I'm solving this with a non-linear solver, because this is not a linear equation. I have to put max and mins. So I set the x-coordinate max to 200, the y-coordinate max to 200. And I'm making sure that they're not going to be negative. And so that's all that these constraints are doing. They're just putting bounds on my decision variables-- have to be greater than 0 and less than 200. But since the furthest I can be-- I'll never be more than 180 away-- so it's pretty safe. Now I'm using a non-linear solving function. Make sure you pick that. If I press Solve, it'll tell you where the answer is. It'll be 89, 66. So this is the location that minimizes, like I said, the weighted distance from each of these cities. Now we're going to talk about a different approach called center of gravity. Center of gravity is something that comes to us very intuitively-- comes from physics. And the way to think about it is, suppose that this triangle was made out of a very rigid material like plywood. And I had each of the cities, instead of the weight being units of root beer, let's say they were pounds or kilograms. So I had 420 kilograms here at Boston. I had 320 kilograms here at this location, and I have 220 kilograms here. What center of gravity is trying to do is say, OK, where would I put a center point to balance all of these? If I had this rigid board, where would I put my finger to balance them all? It's the point of balance, or the centroid. This is what center of gravity means. So how would I find that? Well, all you're going to do is take the weighted average of each of the x and y coordinates. So let's look at the x one. So I would say that the x for the centroid-- that's what this is-- going to be equal to 425, which is the weight of Boston, divided by 965, which is simply the sum of these weights, just summing them all up. So this is the percent of the volume that Boston represents times its x-coordinate of 100. I'll do the same thing for Providence. 320 divided by 965 times its x coordinate, and the same thing for Springfield. And I get a weighted average of 77. Do the same thing for the y coordinates, and I get a y coordinate of 62. So this tells me my center of gravity is that blue dot, right there at x coordinate 77, y coordinate 62. Why are they different? I bet some of you thought they'd be the same. They're different because they're doing very different things. The Weber one, the single location up here, the red dot, minimized the distance times the weight. The centroid-- what its actually doing if you look at it-- it's minimizing the distance squared times the weight. So in other words it's implicitly over-weighting the further locations. So the further it's away, it's like a seesaw. The further I am on on that seesaw, the more my weight is going to count, and not just in a linear fashion, but in a squared fashion. This is how physics works. This is what a centroid is. It's not, however, minimizing the distance times the weight. So when we use this, we want to use the Weber method. We want to use the method that minimizes distance and volume, not distance squared volume. That makes sense in physics, in the physical world. Doesn't make sense when I'm designing a network. So picking between using center of gravity and Weber method for the minimum distance weight, we want to use that Weber point. However, the Weber point's not perfect. And so here I'm showing what the average distances are. And you'll see that the center of gravity, the average weighted distance for location is higher than it is for the Weber one. However, even the Weber one's not perfect. So let's think about this. So the Weber method would say, OK, locate this right outside of Walpole, the little red dot. The problem is we don't live in Euclidean space. So we don't know what's there. There might be a swamp located there. Every location is neither feasible nor equally desirable. Also, there is an underlying network of highways and rails. If anyone's been up in this area, you know there are these certain roads that you want to be on, because they have the better access. This method ignores that. So in practice, in large-scale models, we don't really do it this way. We don't look at the continuous location. We do that to get insights, but when push comes to shove and you're actually selecting a location, you use candidates. You have candidate locations. You'd say something like, OK, I'm going to consider locating it in Woonsocket, Webster, or Hopkinton. Which one is the best selection? Rather than saying, OK, if I could locate anywhere, where should I go? That's always an insightful, kind of interesting thing to do. But in practice, most of these models use location candidates. And that's what we'll do next.

[lecture_01_07_network_facility_location]
Now we're going to look at the network facility location problem, where instead of looking on a continuous space and finding one location out of any possible location, we're going to have candidates to select from. So the objective is very similar still. We want to find the location or locations from a list of candidates, rather than a continuous space, that minimizes the total costs of delivering from this point to the n or k different destination points over a given network. So this is almost identical to the problem statement we had before. The difference is we have a list of candidates now. So how are we going to solve this? We're going to use the mixed integer linear program. And so what I'm showing you here is the formulation. Calm down. It's not that complicated. Let's break it down. We know it's a math program, because we see the objective function, and we see that we're minimizing something. We see we're subject to a series of constraints. So OK, I know where I am. So now let's look at a little more, because it shouldn't be that unfamiliar to you. Because in fact, this is the transportation problem with some extra constraints. We've still got the objective function that's minimizing the cost of the flows, the cij's times the xij's, which is what we did last time. We still have the supply constraints that's making sure that everything from the source nodes doesn't exceed what I have available there for all the I's within the supply set. I'm doing the same thing on the demand side, right? Where I'm looking for all the flow going into each of the j locations that are in the demand set, that they have to be at least as much as what was demanded. So this is the exact same formulation as we had for the transportation problem, along with our non-negativity constraints for the flows. I can't flow negative volume. The only thing we're doing is adding some constraints. So let's talk about those, and we'll start from the bottom. I'm introducing a new decision variable, this y. y_i is a binary variable. That means it can only take two values, either 0 or 1. It's not between 0 and 1. It's either 0 or 1. We use this to indicate whether to open or not open a facility. So y_i is equal to 1 if distribution center i is opened. Otherwise, it's equal to 0. And this is for all the i's that are in the supply set. OK, so that's the first one. Now, we might want to have a set of constraints that limit the number of facilities I open. And so I'm introducing two new constraints here. One is setting a minimum value p_min, which is an input value I give, and then an upper bound, p_max. So p is the number of facilities I can open, min is the lower bound, max is the upper bound. How do I enforce that? Well, I'm simply going to sum all of the y_i's. So if I sum those up, since it's 1 if it's open, 0 if it's closed, if I sum all the y_i's up, that's the number of facilities I'm opening. And so these two constraints just set the min and the max, make sure I fall within those two numbers. Pretty straightforward. All right. Now, let me go to the top, because we've added something to the objective function. And what is this? Well, I've got this y_i, right? That's the decision whether I open a facility or not. It's a binary variable. And I'm multiplying it by this f_i. And that's simply the fixed cost for opening, DC_i. So this problem that we did, that we're looking at, this could be for flow over the course of a week, a month, a year. And so this fixed cost is the cost for opening this facility for that period of time. So it could be labor. It could be lease. It could be any kind of thing that doesn't change with the amount of volume going through. So the f_i is independent of how much volume is going through. If I have one unit going through, I pay the same amount. If I have 1,000 units going through, I pay the same amount. So it's a fixed cost with respect to the volume flowing through it. So I've got two elements to my objective function now. I have these variable transportation costs that I'm circling, and then I've got these fixed facility location costs. So variable costs are based on the flow. The facility costs are based on the locations that I open. All right. We're almost there. We have one other constraint that I need to talk about, and that's this one. Because up until this point, I have two decision variables, or two types of decision variables-- the flow and the locations. And they're totally independent. If I didn't include this logical, or linking, constraint, what would happen? Well, the x's would do whatever they need to do, just like in the transportation problem. They'd solve the demand, make sure I met that and didn't exceed supply, but the y's would stay at 0. In other words, if I didn't have something connecting them together, then this would just be the transportation problem all over again. It would say, OK, all facilities are open. They're all free. So we need something to tie together the x's and the y's. So here's the same formulation. I just made it a little smaller. And I boxed the constraint we're going to talk about, this linking constraint. And so what does this mean? Well, what this is really doing is saying, we can't deliver from a distribution center unless that distribution center is open. Because if we don't do this, then the objective function, right here, is going to force it closed. It's going to say, hey, it cost me money to open it? If I don't have to, I won't. What if it's not included? Well, we talked about this. It becomes a transportation problem. If this constraint is not here, right, if we got rid of it, you essentially have the transportation problem, and there's nothing different. But it is here, and so let's talk about why it works. OK. So let's think about it, because all that it's saying is that any flows I have from i to j-- and so let's draw. I have node i, and it's going to node j. If there's any flow on here, then we want to make sure that i is turned on, that y_i is turned on. So what if xij is 0? What value will y take if xij is 0? Well, if it's 0, then I'd have 0 minus this M, and we'll talk about it, but just assume right now it's a really big number. So 0 minus a big number times y less than or equal to 0. So y could actually be any number at all, and then it would satisfy this constraint, which means that y would be forced down to 0. So if there's no flow, then it's going to go to 0. However, what happens if I have one unit of flow going through, just one unit? So now, it would be 1 minus big number M times y has to be less than or equal to 0. What value does y has to take? It has to become 1. It can't be 0 any more. It has to be turned on. So what this constraint is doing, it's forcing, by keeping it negative, it's saying if xij is positive, then yi has to be 1. Or if not, then it won't be less than 0. Play with this and see how it works. It's a nice, it's a very clever constraint. What's the best value for these M's? Well, as a general rule, you want it to be big. But technically, you don't want to make it too big, because then it slows down some of the solution time, and it takes a little more effort. So a good rule of thumb, you set the Mij greater than any value that the xij, the flow, can possibly take. In fact, it's OK, it's perfectly acceptable, to set all the Mij's to the same number. You set it equal to the sum of all the demand, right? Because the flow on each individual arc can never be greater than the total demand required. So that's one way to do it, to set a really big M. And that's why we make it just a big M. I'll talk later about how you can make that a little more finer, because it makes the solution a little faster. But it's perfectly acceptable to set the M, just to make it a really big number. Because what you really want to do is connect the x's and the y's. So what we're going to do next is put this into a spreadsheet, and we're going to solve it.

[lecture_01_08_solving_the_network_facility_problem]
OK. We've formulated the network facility location problem. Now let's solve it. So I'm going to have to extend the problem a little bit to make it realistic. We'll stay with New England Root Beer Distributors, NERD. We'll call this problem NERD 2. And we'll see that they've expanded their market from just three cities-- Boston, Springfield, and Providence-- to a series of cities, 12 cities across New England. And they have five candidate DC locations-- Boston; Nashua, New Hampshire; Worcester, Massachusetts-- it looks like it's pronounced "wor-chester." It's "wuss-ter"-- Springfield; and Providence. OK. So I've got these 12 locations. And I've got five candidate DCs. And they want to know where should you locate the single DC. So I've got in this table, I've got the demand or the weight for each of the locations, and I've got the x- and y-coordinates. You can see that I set it up for New Haven, which is down here, is at 0, 0. So that's my bottom leftmost city. Now for fun, and I'll let you do this on your side, if you do the center of gravity, or the Weber method, you can find the location assuming continuous location of all these. And I'll let you do that on your own. But for candidates, what you really need is different information than x,y coordinates. What you really need is in what's known as the distance matrix. And that's what I have here. And so it shows for each city. And here are the 12 cities. Right? Boston to Worcester. And each of the candidate DCs is a column. Right? Boston, Nashua, Providence, Springfield, and Worcester. And I've got the distance. For example, from Hartford to Providence is 73 miles. From Portsmouth to Worcester is 85 miles, and so on. And you'll see it it's not quite symmetric because I didn't list it for all cities. It's not a city to city matrix because we will never distribute root beer from, for example, Brattleboro to Manchester. We just won't do that. Everything comes from a DC and goes to one of the cities. I also have the weight here. So that's all there. OK. What else do we know? We're going to say that to open a facility, it costs $10,000 a week. This could be labor. This could be rental. Whatever. The fixed cost is for that facility and it's independent of how much root beer I push through it. It's a fixed cost. It has nothing to do with the amount of product that I push through. My cost for going from i to j is simply going to be $1 a mile times the distance. I'm just keep it really simple here. So we assume every mile cost $1. Then my total supply is simply just that sum of the demands that are coming out and go into d_j. That's a total supply going through. And I'm going to set-- because I only want one location. My P_min is equal to my P_max. Remember, P_min is the minimum number of DCs that I can open. And P_max is the maximum number. If they're the same, then I'm forcing the solution. So I'm forcing the model to say, find the single location that maximizes or minimizes my total cost. And my cost is both my flows, my transportation cost, and my fixed cost for the facilities. All right. So let me show you what the spreadsheet looks like. And just calm down. It looks complicated because it's big. And this is that extended formulation that I like. And I'll explain why in a second. But let's talk through it. So let me talk you off the ledge because it hasn't changed that much. In cell B3 up here, I've got my objective function. And that's my z. Hasn't changed. In these cells, I've got my binary variables, my y_i's, and their fixed cost, the f_i's. And then all across here I've got my xijs. That's the flow decision variables and the cost for flowing on each of those. And you can see up here in cell B3, it's just the same sum product that I've had before, the decision variables multiplied by their costs. OK. Then I've got a bunch of constraints. In the first set are the exact same ones as the transportation, its maximum supply at each DC. Then I've got a bunch of demand constraints. And again, these are exactly the same. Now I've had to add these ones for minimum maximum number of DCs. And you can see that all I'm doing is summing up the yijs and making sure that they are less than my P_min or greater than my P_min and less than my P_max. And then all these big ones down here, these the linking constraints. And so all it's saying is that the xijs, if there's any flow, then you've got to turn your yi on. You've got to turn your DC on if you're going to flow anything out of it. I've got my left-hand constraints. These are just sum products for each of these. It's simply the sum product of the row that it's adjacent to multiplied by the decision variable row. That's the same function that's through all of these constraints in this column. It's the exact same function. So we really haven't changed anything. And then over here I've got my right hand sides. So why do I like this formulation? I love it because it shows the structure of the problem. If I look at this, I can see all these linking constraints. Here's the one for all of Boston. Right? That's those negative m values. And you can see what's known. This is known as an identity matrix. So I know that this is linking all the Boston constraints. So anything coming out of Boston, if there's any flow on any of these xij variables, right, because these are the ones that's saying there's flow out of Boston, then it's going to turn on my y_i. These ones are turning it on for the next one, which is Nashua. And so the nice-- one of the reasons why I like doing it like this, it shows me the structure of the problem. Now, the downside is, as your problem gets larger, you will never be able to do this because it gets too big. But it's very helpful. I love setting it up this way because you see the structure of the problem. And you understand that this is just matrix algebra. All that I have for functions here, I've got some products here in this column. And I've got a sum product up here to sum things up for the cost. Those are the only two functions I have in this entire spreadsheet. As I make this more compact, it gets a little more opaque and less transparent. OK. Enough about that. Now we're going to solve it. So how do I solve it? I bring up my solver. And I'll put in my objective function, which is just that cell B3, which is simply the sum product of the decision variables and their relevant costs. I'm going to minimize it. My decision variables, or changing variables, are highlighted there. Then I've got a series of constraints. I've got my capacity constraints. Then I've got my supply constraints. These are the demand constraints, rather. Then I've got the plant minimums and maximums. And they're both equal to 1 for this because I only want to find one facility location. Then all my linking constraints. And you see that the right-hand side is all 0 because it's a negative m times y plus the x is less than 0. And then I've got something that's saying that these y variables are binary. And I want to make sure that I check this box to make all the xijs non-negative. And I'm going to use a simplex method. And I'm going to solve it. Press the big solve button. And I get this. And here are the answers. So all I'm doing is showing the top left side of the spreadsheet just to blow it up a little bit. Remember this is that cell B3 that tells my objective function. So my solution is $89,478 per week. And what DC am I opening? I'm opening up Worcester because that's a 1. The other ones are 0. In this slice of the spreadsheet I'm showing the flows from Worcester. And so you're seeing that I flow from Worcester to Boston 425 units, 12 units from Worcester to Brattleboro, 12 units, 43 Worcester to Concord, and so forth. So what the model has done, it's shown me which facility to open and how much is flowing. So this is my solution for this model. Now the question is I just opened one DC. Worcester is the optimal location amongst the five candidates that gives me the lowest total cost, fixed cost, and flow cost that I forced it to be one. Is one the right number? Should open more facilities? So that's what we're going to look at next.

[lecture_01_09_tradeoffs_with_multiple_locations]
So if we want to decide whether to open multiple facilities, we need to think about the trade-offs that are involved. So in any kind of supply chain design, what you're trying to do is find a balance between competing interests, and it's mainly costs and service. And so if I look at costs, they're not all the same. There's a whole bunch of different types of costs that I incur. Inventory costs, there could be cycle stock, safety stock, pipeline stock, all these things change depending on the number of locations I have. Facility costs obviously increase as I increase the number of facilities. It could be the amount I'm paying for property, the energy to open it, the insurance, the labor, the fixed cost involved with that. Transportation costs for moving product both into the DC and out to the customers, and the inbound and the outbound cost don't always move in the same direction. The operating or labor cost, obviously, as I increase the number of facilities, my labor cost probably increased because I have more places to staff. And the asset cost, the cost of the facilities themselves. If I have a private fleet, those assets. But then I also have different service characteristics. And so if you think of service, I think about customer satisfaction. Right? I want to make sure the customer is happy and satisfied. When they order something, they get it. I want to look at sales growth. I want to make sure that it's growing. I want to make sure the profit is growing. Increased market share, all these kind of things. The challenge is in supply chain design, this is a strategic model. And so I'm looking things one, two, three years out. Very hard to measure those. So we can also think of something like response time. Am I able to respond quicker? And that whole idea of a level of service. And that's what we're really going to focus on here. And we'll talk more in depth. But in general, what we're trying to do with the multiple locations is find the balance that improves profitability, which means keeping an eye on our costs, then that increases our customer satisfaction or our service. So let's focus in on the cost first because they don't always move in the same direction. So I've a number of costs here, and as I increase the number of DCs, as that increases, obviously the cost will increase. The warehousing or facility costs because I've got more facilities. It's almost a step function, but it actually is an increasing step function because as I open more distribution centers, I'm locating them closer to customers, which means the property value be more expensive because you're moving into denser areas. So it's an actually increasing step function. Then you also have inventory costs. Now we know that inventory cost will increase as I have more distribution centers, and it doesn't increase in a linear fashion. It's actually a square root. And so that's based on mainly the impact of the safety stock. As we add more distribution centers, we assume we're adding them closer to the customers. Right? So the outbound, the yellow, this outbound delivery cost will decrease because I'm closer. So as I have more and more, I'll be closer and closer. You see a lot of online retailers opening up more DCs closer to urban areas so they can reduce this cost and actually increase their-- improve their response time as well. But it decreases the outbound delivery cost. Interestingly, as I have more of these distribution centers, my inbound, the cost for transportation from the plants into these DC's increases. Why? One, there's more of them, but the other is I have less volume going to each of them. So it's a lower concentration. So what maybe I could have done for one or two DC's, to ship by rail or by full truckload, which is the lowest per unit cost modes, I now might have to use LTL or multi-stop truckload. So as I increase the number of DC's my inbound costs will actually increase. So you'll see that I have both increasing and decreasing costs here. So we can see where there will be an optimal location. And this is an optimal number of DCs. And so that's what we're going to do in terms of cost. But now, we've got that whole other side of service. So when I talk about level of service, it means different things to different people. It's know, it's-- I don't know any company where it's just one thing. It's usually whole array of different things. And the problem is, which levels of service can we include in the strategic model? Now previously, we've looked at tactical levels of service such as Cycle Service Level, which if you remember back from previous classes, is looking at the probability that you stock out during a replenishment time, or the probability you don't stock out, rather, in a replenishment cycle, the Item Fill Rate, the percent of the items you ordered that actually come in, and then the cost penalties for each of those. So these are well-established tactical level service indicators, but they don't fit for a strategic design because they're influenced by stocking level not the location of facilities. You'll notice in the models that we've done so far, that we'll continue doing, inventory doesn't play a role. I'm just making my decisions based on mainly facility costs and transportation costs. So we can't use these levels, these metrics explicitly, but in strategic design, what we'll try to do is use distance to customer as a proxy. And so this is the best way we can get at in measuring the opportunity that you'll be able to meet these tactical service objectives. So the closer I am to the customers, the higher the probability, the easier it is for someone to be able to meet these CSL or IFR metrics. So we're going to use the distance to customers as a proxy here. Now this seems easy, but there are many different ways to measure this. So one is, I say allowable distance. So I say no customer is allowed to be more than x, 75 miles from the DC. So I make sure that there's a maximum level. Or I could say that at least 30% of all customers need to be within 20 miles. So you have some kind of absolute allowable distances. You might also have an average distance so that the average distance for customer to DCs has to be less than 22.6 miles. Whatever. Or the weighted average, so that you favor customers or regions that have higher volumes, the weighted average has to be less than some distance. So these are different ways to do it, and these are just two ways to do it. There are many different metrics. These are very commonly used. And so how would I model these? Well, one way is to not include it in the model but report it after each run. So if I do a run, then I say, OK, what is the average distance? What is the percent of customers that are within a certain distance? So I report it. The next level would be add a constraint to enforce it to happen. And so you'd say the average distance shall not be more than 20 miles. And I'll show you how to add that in as a constraint. And then the highest level, as you go from really passive to very active, I can change the objective function. There's nothing that says I can't make one of these distance equations the objective function. Minimize the average weighted distance from DCs to customers. If I make that my objective function, then it will minimize. It'll open as many facilities as possible close to customers, and then you'll be able to find out what the cost is, buy you're no longer minimizing the cost. So what we're going to do is start with this, and then we'll go and look at the constraints. So let's get started.

[lecture_01_10_selecting_optimal_dcs]
OK, let's get started looking at selecting the optimal DCs using these trade-offs. So I've got my NERD 2 problem, my 12 cities, and five candidate solutions. And I want to find the single DC location that minimizes facility and transportation costs. We already solved this, and we found that it was Worcester was the location and the total cost was $89,000 and change. And we can also look at some metrics. So for this solution I can, after the fact, look and see, OK what is the average customer distance? And that's simply the distance from each distribution center that it's serving. This average distance for each of the cities. Then I look at the average weighted, what I call, demand distance. And that's where you weight that distance by the amount of volume that's going through each of the cities. Then we look at percent demand within the distance brackets. So I want to say, what percent of the demand is within 25 miles, 50 miles, 50 to 75, 75 to 100, and over 100. So what we see is, here's my optimal location. If I can only pick one, it's going to be Worcester. And here are my distances. And 10% of all the demand is within 25 miles. The majority is between 25 and 50. So this raises the question, well maybe I should open two DCs. What if I open two? Where should I locate them? So we're going to do-- and you can do this in the spreadsheet that you have-- is we're going to set the P_min and the P_max-- which were equal to 1-- set them both equal to 2. So you change the right-hand side from 1 to 2 for both. Solve it. And now what are we doing? We're finding the optimal two DC locations. We're forcing it. You've got to open two. If you have to open two, which one of the five candidates do you open? And you'll see it'll open Springfield and Boston. Now some of you might be surprised that it didn't keep Worcester and open something else. This happens a lot when you have binary variables. A slight change can have a dramatically different solution. It's something to keep in the back of your mind. And we'll talk more about this when we talk about realistic practices. All right. So we can also look at the level of service. We can see what our customer distance-- and we notice it went down as I opened more DCs. Same thing with my average demand distance. And now instead of 10%, I've got 37% of my customers within 25 miles. That's pretty good. Let's keep going. Now we're going force P_min and P_max equal to 3. So if I open up three, I'm suddenly going to open up my Providence DC. And what it's going to do is, it's going to serve its own demand as well as New London, which it's taking from what Springfield used to service. And so I can see what happens to my distance and all my other metrics and my costs. We'll summarize these at the end. But let me just keep going through. And now I'm going to force four DCs to be open. And it opens up Nashua, which makes sense because you had all this long haul from Boston up to the northern part of the region. And now I set up a northernmost DC. And so it takes off that whole line haul distance. So it makes sense to open up that facility if I have to open up four. All right. So now, I want to do one more. I'm going to forced it to five, which really isn't much of a decision as I only have five candidates. But what it's doing-- go back-- it's taking the Worcester domain and saying you know what, open Worcester DC. Serve your own demand. You're not going to have to serve it from Boston anymore. So it gets rid of that. That's the real change from going from four to five. We see what the costs are, service level metrics. And now let's look at them all on one table. So some observations. What I've got here each column forcing the number of distribution centers from one to five. The first row is my total cost, which is the sum of the transportation costs and the facility costs. Then I've got a bunch of metrics for the average distance, the average customer distance from each city. Then I weight that by the volume in each city. Then I list the maximum customer distance. Which city is the furthest away from any distribution center that's serving it? So I see that it's New Haven. Initially it's 98 miles away when I'm servicing out of Worcester with just one plant-- or one DC, rather. And it reduces pretty dramatically down when I introduced three, four, and five. And then I also have the percent of the demand that's within 25 mile bands. Within 25 miles, 25 to 50, 50 to 75 and so on. So what do I see? Increasing the number of DCs reduces the distance to customers. You see all of these numbers decrease, right? As I get more distribution centers open, the average customer distance is cut in half, from 56 to 25, and cut to quarter for the average demand weighted distance. You can see the maximum, the worst located city, goes from 98 miles away to 63. That's a big improvement. And you can see the percent of the population that's within 25 miles increases from 10% to 77%. So it makes sense as I increase the number of DCs, my service level is going to increase. Costs don't always move in the same direction, though, right? The facility costs obviously move up lockstep. Every new facility I open adds $10,000. And the outbound transportation decreases. Now, why isn't inbound transportation increasing here? Well, it's because I didn't capture it in the model. All that I'm capturing is the transportation costs from the DC to the customer. We'll introduce models later that have that component. But what I want to show here is the fundamental trade-off between opening more distribution centers. It will improve all of your service metrics. Right? It will also improve your transportation-- your outbound transportation costs at the detriment of higher facility costs. It's a basic trade-off. And we can see this in a number of charts. So what I'm showing here is on the horizontal axis the number of distribution centers we open and we're forcing them open. I'm just seeing what the effect is. And we see what the average demand distance does. As it goes-- this y-axis over here for the red is reversed. So this is 60 miles, and this is 20 miles. So as I increase the number of distribution centers my distance to customers decreases. But my cost, it decreases. But then it starts increasing again as the facility costs start dominating. I can do the same thing with looking at a different metric. Same total cost here on the left axis. But on the right axis now, I'm going to do percent of demand within 25 miles. It goes from around 10% to over almost 80% here. So as I increase the number of DCs, your level of service will almost always increase because you're closer to customers. So what if we want to find just the optimal solution? So far we've forced one DC, two DCs, three DCs, four DCs, five DCs. Now I can use the exact same model. I'm just going to sit P_min to 1, P_max to 5 and our re-solve for lowest cost. I'm going to let the model decide how many locations to open. And as you can guess since we kind of saw where the min was, it's going to open up three. It's going to open up in Boston, Providence, and Springfield. We've actually seen these metrics. So what I've done in this little video segment, hopefully, is shown you a couple things. One, we went and forced a bunch of solutions. These are runs I want to see what happens if we forced two DCs, if we force three DCs. And we saw what the effect was both in terms of the costs, facility, and transportation as well as the impact on all of these service metrics, these proximity to customers. Because in the model you're not going to capture everything that's important in real life. There might be something that's very important about having something open in a certain region. And you can see, OK what is the effect of doing that? So by using this model, I can do a lot of what-if scenarios. And that's what we did. The other thing we get at the very end, we said OK, forget the what-if scenarios. Just find the optimal. And that's where we set the P_min the P_max at different levels. And we wanted the model to give us an idea of, OK, where is the optimal location? So what we're going to do next is say OK, instead of just reporting these after the fact, what if I include those in the model? I include those in constraints. And that's what we'll do in the next video segment.

[lecture_01_11_enforcing_level_of_service_constraints]
OK, now we're going to solve the exact same problem, the NERD 2 problem, but instead of just reporting the distance metrics passively, we're going to enforce them as constraints. So I'm adding level of service constraints, and I'm going to add two of them. So what I've got here is the formulation. Nothing has changed except for these two new constraints that I'm about to explain. OK, so I'm going to add a new constraint for the max average distance and the minimum percent within 50 miles. So let me talk about the first one. What I've got here is I'm saying for every x_ij I want to multiply it by the distance, because that's the weighted distance. And I'm going to divide it by the total demand because I want to know the percent. And so this is telling me the average distance. So if I sum that up for all the x_ij's I'll get an average weighted distance. So to do this I need the distance matrix, and that's what I've got down here in the lower left. So the distance going from Nashua to Providence is 72 miles, right? This is the same distance matrix that we showed in previous slides. So that's the average weighted distance, and it's going to be less than equal to some value. That's an input value. That's a number that you put in. Notice that this isn't saying for all i's or for all j's. This sums across for all of the x_ij's. So this is only one constraint. It's not like a class of constraints, it's a single value. So it's saying across all of the flows going to customers, what's the maximum average distance? It has to be less than some value that I'm giving it. The next constraint we added is this percent of demand that's within 50 miles of a DC. It's got to be above a certain amount. So the right-hand side of this constraint is an input that you put in there. I need to make sure it's at least 30%, 40%, whatever. The left-hand side-- it's a little trickier. I'm going to multiply each of the flows. So this is the number of units through x_ij, and I'm multiplying that by this new input data, which is a constant, a_ij. And a_ij is something that I input. a_ij is equal to 1 if customer j served by DCi is within 50 miles. It's 0 otherwise. So what I have here in the lower right-hand corner is the a_ij matrix. And I just created this by looking at the distance matrix. So for example, if-- let's pick one-- let's say if I want to pick Nashua. If Nashua is served out of Boston, is it within 50 miles? Why yes, it is. So therefore I make that 1. If Nashua is served out of Nashua is it less than 50 miles? Yeah, of course. It's 0. It's the same city. If Nashua was served from Providence it's 72, so that's why that's a 0, and so on. I did this-- it's a one time. I just used an if statement and you create this matrix. So what it's saying is if there's a 1, then if I'm serving that destination from the selected distribution center, it's either in 50 miles or it's not. So what I'm doing is I'm multiplying again, and it only counts the ones that are within 50 miles, and again I divide it by the total demand. So it's taking the percentage, the percentage of the total demand that's within 50 miles. So these are how I set it up in the formulation. It's a little tricky. Think about it. This sum of the d_j is not a variable. I put this in-- these are inputs. So this is just simply the sum of all the demand. I just want to find the weighted average for these two distances. So what does it look like in our spreadsheet? Well, let me just show you some of the rows. What I did here, and you have the spreadsheet available for you-- I added a new a_ij values in row 8, and it's simply equal to 1 if the distance is less than 50 miles, 0 otherwise. I just did that once, so that's now input data. So it tells me if I have this DC serving this customer, it either is or is not within this 50 miles that I have deemed is important. Then I've got some things here in this part of the spreadsheet. Let me just blow it up a little bit. Remember, this is my objective function. And so I've got these two new constraints, and these are in rows 97 and 98. So there's a lot of other stuff in between here, between rows 4, 90-- all the other constraints. I just wanted to show you the new constraints that we're adding. So for the minimum average distance on row 97, each of these cells is simply the distance divided by the sum of the demand-- the total demand, which in this case, from Boston to Brattleboro-- BO to BR-- is 93/1746, or 0.05326. Now my spreadsheet's just truncating it to two decimals. The value is there. It's just not displaying them all. Then for the min, or the max percent within 50, same thing for cell H98 here for the max percent within 50 miles constraint. It's just that a value, which in this case, if I serve Boston from Boston, it's within 50 miles, so that's why it's a 1 divided by the total demand. It's 0.0005727. It's only displaying two decimal places. The actual value is in there. So you see we populate-- that's what's populating all of this part of the spreadsheet. For each x_ij, we give what the weighted average and the mean average distance would be, and also whether it's within 50 miles. And on the right-hand side, you can see this is what will happen. Let me just clean this up just a tad. I have on the right-hand side for my left-hand side of the equation, of the spreadsheet, I have the exact same function that I've used before. It's just a sum product. It's a sum product of its own row times the decision variables. That's it. It hasn't changed. That's why I love this format for showing problems. I'm using the exact same function, and it's going to be less than equal to, for the first one-- 60, because that's my max average distance. And I say 80% here, for I want to have at least 80% of the population, or of the demand, within 50 miles. So that's how I set it up on my spreadsheet, and I can solve it as open solver. And the only thing I've done is I've added these two new constraints. The first one is saying that my average distance has to be less than a certain value. The second one is saying that the percent within 50 miles has to be above a certain value. And I can just solve this pressing the solve button. Make sure I'm on Simplex, nothing else has changed. And it'll open four distribution centers, and the average distance will be 15 miles. 90% of the customers will be less than 50 miles, total cost $66,781. So what have I done? The level of service constraints drove the problem. Remember, optimally, if I didn't have to consider this at all, I only open three of the distribution centers for a lower cost. But what this showed me is that for a little more money I can get a better service level. So depending, now you can see how I can use this. I could go back and say, well, maybe 80% isn't needed. Maybe I could do 75%, 74%. And as the problem gets more complex, I can play with these numbers and I can say OK, what does the cost really increase? Does it increase dramatically? Because if I look back, my solution for having three distribution centers open isn't that much more expensive. In fact, if I look at it, it was only $66,285. So it was $66,285. So this really, really quick analysis shows, you know what, for less than $500 a week I can improve my service level dramatically. So maybe I don't want to do that optimal lowest cost solution. Maybe I want to enforce some of these constraints. This is the beauty of a network model. I can make these analysis and I can test the sensitivity, because one of the things you need to remember-- an optimization model will do anything to save $1. Anything. So you need to test the solution around the edges and see what works, and see what makes realistic sense.

[lecture_01_12_key_take_aways]
OK, that was a big lesson. We covered a lot of material here. So what are the key takeaways? Well, we actually looked at solving one problem-- facility location problems. Where should I open my facilities? And we looked at in in two ways. We solved them two different ways. The first, we made an assumption that it was a continuous selection. Any place was equally likely. And they will do anything to save a dollar. If cost is in your objective function, you're asking this model to do anything to save that one additional dollar. So you need to do sensitivity analysis to understand, well, what did it give up to find that last dollar? Maybe it's something that doesn't make sense in reality. So they're deterministic.
