Of course not, because I didn't take into account any of these setup costs or the holding costs. I just assumed, when you want it, that's when I'm going to order it. So there's a lot of things I can do here. I could make it a little smarter. I could optimize it. So let's see what happens when I do that. Now these are separate companies. So this is known as sequential optimization. So the end item manufacturer will optimize their situation, feed it to their vendor, who will then optimize theirs. So by sequential, what I mean is the end item manufacturer optimizes. Then the component manufacturer optimizes based on the output from the first firm. So what I have is the same gross requirements for months six to 12. See, I'm going to go back. These are the same numbers right in here. And instead of producing them just as they're being ordered, it's going to find an optimal solution, which is making 220 in month 11, making 300 in month eight, and making 80 in month six. So now this is what it feeds down to its component manufacture. So it goes over, one two three, because we're-- two, rather two months, because that's its lead time. And each of these will feed down to the manufacturer. So I need 220 end items in month nine. 220 times 3, because I have three in the bill of materials, is 660. And so you can see that this just ripples down exactly so. And now I'm going to optimize down here as a composite manufacturer. And it's pretty straightforward. It's going to be very lumping. It's going to produce exactly that amount each time. And so you see the lead time is two months. So this is what the production plan will be. So this is a simple, simple example of sequential optimization. Now let's make it a little more complicated. The capacity has been a 1,000 units here, a 1,000 units a month. What if I restrict it from 1,000-- oops, there we go-- to 300. So now I'm down to 300 units. And that's all I can do. Well this hasn't changed up here, because that doesn't change what the end item manufacturer does. They're still producing exactly as they did before. If you look through, 80, 300, 220, everything's the same. But the tier one manufacturer now has some constraints. What are the constraints? Well, they can only produce 300 a month. So optimizing with that constraint changes my production plan. You can see I'm going to produce 300 in period-- I need 300 in period three, 300 in period four, and so on. And you can see, based on the lead time, what the production plan will be for the planned order response-- order release rather. All right, so this is sequential optimization. First these guys optimize. They feed it down. And then each of the vendors optimize based on the output from the end item manufacturer. What if we did it a little differently though? What if they all work together? And what if actually the tier one manufacturer, this component manufacturer, was part of the same company as the end item manufacturer, they're the same thing. What if they could make decisions jointly? Well, it would look something like this. So in the sequential case, for the end item, I'm minimizing this cost for the blue, for the end item, and I'm minimizing this cost when I'm optimizing here. If I want to do it simultaneously, I'm minimizing this cost, which is simply the sum of these two. And I'm keeping the same capacity. They have 300. So I'm going to solve it simultaneously. And look what happens. I'm going to flip back and forth a little bit, because right now, this is saying that I need 220 in month 11, 200 in month nine, 200 in month eight, and 80 in month six-- very different from the previous run. And so you can see that both will change slightly by doing it simultaneously. So let's throw all three of these solutions up. And what I have is the MRP with no optimization. And I actually did it with a constrained amount. It's in the spreadsheet that you can play with, the sequential optimization, and then the simultaneous optimization that we just saw, with all the common, same demand, and the same capacity constraints. You can see the component manufacturing cost as well as the end item manufacturing cost and the total costs. So which one makes the most sense? Well, some things jump out. MRP with no optimization is clearly the most expensive option, because I'm not making my decisions based on tradeoffs of setup and holding costs. The simultaneous optimization is always going to be lower cost, or the lowest cost, because I'm removing a constraint. If I look at sequential optimization, I'm essentially putting a constraint in. I'm doing suboptimal optimization. I'm optimizing one and then the other. I'm not looking at how they can trade off. So you would think this is the best solution. It's cheaper. It's the lowest cost solution. But it'd be very hard to implement. Why? Well let's look at the detail a little more. I'll just clean it up. So if I do a sequential optimization, the component manufacturer-- remember these are separate firms-- their cost is 4,800. By simultaneous, their price goes down by $900. They're saving money. What happens to the end item manufacturer? They go up by $500. So overall, I save $400. So overall it goes down. But it's not uniform. In other words, the end item manufacturer is paying the price for the system to be optimal. This happens all the time. You'll see it in procurement. You'll see it in network design. Whenever you're looking for a system optimal-- and that's what the sequential optimization or simultaneous optimization is doing-- then you're going to have disparate benefits and costs. Everyone won't benefit equally. Some will benefit a lot more. Some will get penalized. In this case, the end item manufacturer is paying the price so that the component manufacturer can save even more money. So would this ever happen? What would you do? Well, you can think about cost sharing, and there can be a way of maybe tying something together where the benefit, this total savings is saved at some sort. But if you don't have this mechanism, then it could be that it never happens. And that's why it's really interesting to see what would actually get implemented versus what you think would get implemented. And you might think, well, this is easy to do. Why don't I just do simultaneous? Well this was a very simple example with one vendor, three items that go to one end item. Think about the car. Think about the electronics. Think about that Adirondack chair, or even the huevos rancheros. There's thousands of parts you have here. You might have hundreds of vendors. So just doing everything simultaneously, it sounds good, but it really is not feasible. So you have to pick and choose what you think you can solve jointly, and what you need to do sequentially. And a lot of times, people ask, well why don't we just reoptimize whenever there's a change? Well, think back to what we did when we talked about the available to promise. It takes a lot of effort sometimes to change a production plant when it's ready to go. So think of your time fences. And you've got a frozen time when there is no change to the plan. You've got a slush time where you can make some changes, but you have to make the tradeoffs. And then you've got this water where any change is OK. So this all fits together, because you're not going to be optimizing every time a new order comes in. You want to only do that when you have enough time to react. So as my time gets closer to now, time gets shorter, I'm going to be more restrictive on what I can and can't change in terms of my production plan. So this shows how the MRP can be used to coordinate between different firms, suppliers, vendors, and their manufacturers.

[lecture_03_14_benefits_and_limitations_of_mrp]
So we spent a lot of time talking about the mechanics of material requirements planning systems and how they work, and how they ripple the demand for the end items down to each of the components that have indentured demand. Let's talk about some of the benefits and limitations of the systems. So the benefits are pretty self-evident. They definitely lead to lower inventory levels. The MRP system allows me to better manage components. There's increased visibility. So the inventory levels should be reduced. So I don't need to just keep a bunch of stuff there just in case. It shows me when I need it, and how much. This should lead to fewer stock outs and less expediting, getting things there faster due to that increased visibility. Also, the relationships are clearly defined through the bill of materials. So it really helps in that aspect. And we expect, because the needs are explicitly modeled, there should be fewer production disruptions. There are some limitations though. Remember, this is a scheduling algorithm. It tells me when to place that order. It doesn't tell me how much to stock. It doesn't take into account holding costs as well as setup costs. It doesn't determine lot size. That's why we've talked about the fixed planning horizon problem. You have to do that in addition to the standard MRP logic. It doesn't deal with uncertainty. Everything was assumed to be deterministic. In fact, we never even mentioned safety stock really. And so I could kind of lump safety stock in. But what's commonly used, or is sometimes used, is something called-- I introduce safety time. And what I'm doing, instead of buffering the amount of inventory I have, I buffer the amount of lead time. So instead of taking seven days lead time let's say, I might add an extra day, make it eight days. And so I can treat that the same as what I do for safety stock, figure out how much of the uncertainty I want to cover, and just increase my buffer time accordingly. But MRP by itself does not handle uncertainty. It's assuming constant and known lead times. And we know in practice that just isn't the case, especially when I'm bringing things in a long distance. I might have multiple sources. I might have multiple means of transportation bringing them in. So I could have different lead times. It also doesn't provide incentives for improvement-- this one's kind of interesting-- because the MRP systems are so complex. You saw the number of components that are going to-- and most the products we take advantage of every day, that we have around us. And so they are very complex, and people are very hesitant to fix something that isn't broken. The old saying, don't fix it if it ain't broke, that applies to MRP systems. So you have something that's working. Maybe it's not perfect, but you don't want to break it. So there are some limitations. But it is widely, widely used. The other thing to remember, it's not static. It isn't like the system was created 50 years ago and hasn't changed. It's evolving. And so what started out as a very simple MRP focused on order launching, it's now evolved into something that covers the whole enterprise. And so it really takes a lot more into consideration, because it is very, very data intensive. It's making those tradeoffs. So it needs to have that enterprise resource visibility. And so what you're seeing is MRPs are becoming more ingrained in larger business decisions.

[lecture_03_15_distribution_requirements_planning_drp]
What I want to do to finish up this lesson is talk briefly about DRP, or distribution requirements planning, because it's essentially a mirror image of the MRP system. Now it's not used by all firms, but sometimes you hear it. And I want to make sure you understood what it is. So remember, we talked about the master production schedule. And that takes input from sales. And they can use it for figuring out their availability to promise. We looked at MRP systems, which go upstream and determine how my individual components can be brought into creating the end item. And now we're going to look at DRP going downstream, going down to the customer. And we're going to use the same type of logic as we did for MRP. So the premise is, you've got inventory control in a distribution environment. I'm trying to distribute things. I have many products, many stockage location. I have a multi-echelon distribution network, which means I have different levels or layers of warehouses, DCs, and stocking points. So these layers of inventory create lumpy demand. We'll see that in a second. We've seen that in the past. Whenever you have batching of orders, whether it's due to economies of scale or whatever, you're going to create a lumpy kind of demand. So the concept is, we're going to treat the demand as dependent rather than independent. We're going to look at requirements calculation versus forecasting demand. And we're going to schedule the flow versus stockpiling assets like we've done in the past when we figured out safety stock, cycle stock. So this is kind of a different approach. Again, it's using those MRP techniques to flow things down, and replacing information with inventory. And the system really came about from Andre Martin, who was kind of the father of MRP. And again, it makes sense for certain environments. So here's our example. I've got a manufacturing plant that feeds into a central warehouse that goes to three regional warehouses, one, two, and three. And they each fit and feed into these different retailers. So what I've got is this DRP plan. And we'll go in detail for this. But essentially what we're doing is we're rolling up from the demand at each level down here, at each of the warehouses. So we're going to take the demand from the stores, the retailers, up to here. And then this is going to roll up into the central warehouse. That's going to roll up into the plant. So when we looked at the MRP system, I started with the demand here. And then I went down and figured out, OK, and how do I schedule things for each of the components? Here I'm doing just the opposite. I'm starting at the end items, and I'm rolling up to what the plant demand is going to be. So let's go in detail. And so let's pick one of the regions. This is warehouse one. And this should look very familiar here. It's how much my period usage is, what my forecast is. And for the next eight weeks or whatever the time period is, I'm expecting 25 per time period. Now my gross requirement is going to be equal to my forecast plus my safety stock. So I'm including some safety stock here. So 25 plus 15 is 40. So I want to have 40 units here for each time period. My demand, it looks like it's being fairly consistent. Now my beginning inventory for time period one is my end inventory from the previous time. And so I have nothing scheduled in. And so my net requirement is zero. My end requirement is going to be 25. I know I'm going to need 25 the next time period. Since my lead time is one, I'm going to need inventory for time period two. So I'm going to place an order here for 50 units at time period one. And you see I'm going to have the same order being placed, 50 units. And this is where you see the lumpiness comes through. Because while the demand is very consistent, 25 per period, because I have an EOQ, or however I figure that out, minimum order quantity, whatever, it's going to be lumpy going forward. So we can go to the next warehouse. And they have slightly different demand. And based on their characteristics, they'll have some economic order quantity. They have safety stock was determined somehow. And they have a lead time. And you can see the same type of thing. They have a demand of 10, safety stock of 10. So therefore, they need 20 units here. Demand increases in period five. So you're increasing 20 plus the safety stock of 10 for 30. And everything carries out similarly. You can see the ordering pattern gets very lumpy. It's based on when you need those units. So because I have to order in quantities of 30, this is very similar to the fixed order quantity that we did when we were looking at the fixed planning horizon problems. So let's go to the third warehouse. Again, slightly different. We have more spotty demand, five units, 15 units, 10 units. I have an order quantity that was determined somehow, some safety stock, and some lead time. Those are inputs to me. And out of that I can determine my gross requirements. And again, it all ripples down to when I'm going to have my planned order releases, or when I'm going to place my orders. So you can see that the demand at the period usage translates into very lumpy orders for all three of these warehouses. So what I want to do now is I'm going to roll that up from period one where the planned order release was 50, 30 for region two, 20 for region three. That turns into an order of 100 units to the central DC. And you can see that follows out across as I go across all the periods. And so while the end demand might be very consistent at the individual levels, it gets very lumpy in what gets ordered. And it gets really lumpy here in what they order up from the central up to the plant. So this now shows what the central warehouse orders to its source, which is the manufacturing plant. Now it will naturally have a bigger order quantity, full truckload requirements. It might have something-- we put here a safety stock of zero. We could put something in here, a slightly longer lead time. And the same logic goes forward in that you figure what you need, your gross requirements, adding a safety stock. In this case, it's zero. And so you see what you have, beginning inventory. And then you determine when you place your orders. So very lumpy. Orders going up. So what we've done is essentially just rippled up. And we go up this way in determining how I should distribute down. Similar to how from here, when I did MRP, I went this way to determine how much of each component to make and when. So what we're really doing is pushing the information from the market upstream to the final, through the manufacturing plant, and even into the components. So what are the results and insights? DRP, it's a mirror of MRP. It's a scheduling and stockage algorithm. It replaces the forecasting mechanism above what my expected inventory level is going to be, everything we did with an (S,Q) or a (R,S) system. It doesn't determine lot size or safety stock. That's an input. It doesn't consider any cost. It's really all about the scheduling. And then it can deal with uncertainty somewhat, but that's doing that safety time that I talked about. I can have an input of safety stock. But DRP is a way to plan for that distribution out based on what I think is going to be happening in the market. You can see this used a lot when you have initial launches of products, because you have an expectation of what the demand is going to be. I need to know how to push it down there. So what you can see sometimes is DRP used to push product out, and then sensing the market, and changing and adjusting your inventory policies as the market changes and adjusts.

[lecture_03_16_key_take_aways]
So what are the key takeaways from this lesson? Well I think the main takeaway is picture. If you can keep this picture in your mind, you'll understand how the master production schedule interfaces with the MRP and the DRP systems, and how the MPS is really the communication tool between sales, what we think we're going to sell-- sales and operations, planning-- and what we're allowing the sales guys to go out for availability to promise. So some of the takeaways. The NPS, again, it's a communication tool. And it works both ways. It allows the sales and marketing guys to communicate to production, and allows production to communicate back up to sales. The whole idea of time fencing I think is really important too. And this is a way to reduce nervousness. And simply, as things get closer to my time of production, I don't allow as much change. So I might, within three months let's say, have no changes to production. Within three to six months, I allow some changes, maybe allow the order quantity to change, but not the SKU selection. And then above a certain time, I don't care. I allow free reign. And so as I get closer to my time of production, then my range of flexibility reduces. It's a powerful concept. We talked about MRP, material requirements planning. And what we're really doing is determining when and how much to order for components. Our inputs are the MPS and the bills of material, just a list of ingredients. And we showed how, with using a simultaneous and sequential optimization, how you can use the MRP to coordinate between different firms. Then we finished up. We talked briefly about the DRP, or distribution requirements planning. And again, it's a mirror image of the MRP logic. And so it assumes deterministic demand, all those kind of things. But you can modify it for uncertainty. But it's a powerful tool to kind of understand how product should flow from your production down to your market through a multi-echelon network. So that's all I wanted to cover. If you have any questions, comments, or suggestions, please use the discussion forum. And I hope you enjoyed the lecture.

[lecture_04_00_introduction]
In this lesson, we'll continue our discussion of designing the information flow and supply chain by focusing in on how sales and operations connect. Now remember our overarching question is how do we use information to coordinate the different activities of all the different players and stakeholders in the supply chain? And so previously, we've talked about procurement. We've talked about how you work with your vendors through better sourcing, sharing of risks. We've also talked about how you can tie this together with production, and we mainly talked about master production scheduling, also MRP, Material Requirements Planning. And now we're going to focus in on the interface here. And this is how operations, or production, that's really this is sometimes just called operations, ties in with the demand side, or the sales side. And this is mainly going to be the sales and operations planning process S&OP. We'll finish off this discussion in a later lesson, where we'll focus on the interface with the customer and how you collaborate. But for this lesson, we're going to focus right in here. So traditionally, this process has been very siloed. This isn't a real word, but it's used very often. If things are in a silo, that means they're kept separate from each other. So it used to be the business planning would be set up by the senior executives, they come up with their strategy. This would get fed to the sales team, and they would do their demand planning, their forecasting, and management to determine what they think they could actually sell. They would come up with a sales plan that would fit and be handed to the production planning team. They would take this as input, and then they would then determine what their capacity plan, their scheduling, and their management. You notice that this arrow is typically a one-way street. So you'd be handed the sales plan, and your job was to make sure you can produce what they think they can sell. This would then turn into a massive production schedule that everyone would tend to use to make those decisions of available to promise, or how much I can commit, things like that. And what we're talking about when we talk about an integrative process, a lot of the players are the same. We still have capacity planning and scheduling. We still have demand planning and forecasting. There's still some strategic planning for the business side, and there's still going to be an MPS, a Master Production Schedule. The big difference, though, is when the strategy comes down, it doesn't go to sales then production, or sales then operations. It's tied together, and they're met jointly. And so the plan comes down in sales and operations jointly try to understand what it is that can be done. So these feed out to those processes, and, again, we get the master production schedule. Now this might look totally trivial to you, if you haven't been an organization that's of a significant size. You don't need an S&OP process if you're your own company. Because you are sales, you are operations, you could meet with yourself, you don't need a process. But when companies get bigger, they get very much siloed, so you have a whole team here working on operations, the production, the transportation, supply chain, and then the whole marketing, sales team over here. And you have to force them to get together. And so what this S&OP, the sales and operations planning process, is a formal way to make that happen. And it provides visibility. So sales knows what operations is doing, what their capabilities are, and operations knows what sales expectations and what their capabilities are. So it's really a communication process. So what are we going to talk about in this lesson? We're going to cover three big things. I'm going to talk about aggregate planning, because this is the main tool that you want to use for communicating some of these decisions between sales and operations. We'll develop the model. We'll do an example with Dire Wolf. And then we're going to go into fundamentals of sales and operations planning and focus in on how you manage supply, how you manage demand, again, using some this aggregate planning models that we're going to develop. Finally, I'll talk a little bit about the process for actually implementing a sales and operations process within your firm. This is a huge literature, and there's a lot of other references out there that I could point you to as well, but we'll go over the basic steps. So let's get started.

[lecture_04_01_aggregate_planning]
So let's talk about aggregate planning. Whenever we talk any type of planning-- whether it's demand planning, or production planning, or anything in between-- we need to know what the horizon is. And so for aggregate planning, we're dealing with a three-month to 18-month planning horizon. However, we've already talked about a shorter planning horizon, and that's between now and roughly three months. Now the numbers that I'm giving for the timelines and for the time fences in the planning horizons are general, rough guidelines. Every company's going to be a little bit different, and I'll talk more about that in a second. But generally as I get closer to now, my schedule gets much more rigid-- I have fewer things that I can change. And so we've talked in the past about these three time fences that we have. From now to, say, 3 or four weeks out I might have a frozen time period, where for my master production schedule I can change nothing, it's frozen-- I'm going to produce exactly what's been demanded, what customers have reserved. There might be a period out, say, five to eight weeks where it's slushy, where I'm allowed to make some changes to the MPS-- say plus or minus 10%. And then there's a period after that where I'm able to make even wider range of changes. Now in this time frame I'm looking at planning SKUs or families of SKUs. So it's pretty detailed. And as I get closer, then I get more and more frozen. What we're going to talk about in this lesson is aggregate planning. And this is where it's from the roughly three-month to 18-month time period. And instead of modeling individual SKUs I'm going to look at aggregated volumes. I'm going to combine all those SKUs because I don't care about the mix yet, I'm trying to get some big planning things done. Why is this different from what we do for the master production scheduling? Well, I have other things that I can change-- I have different levers. For here, my only real lever is the number-- how much do I produce, does it go up, does it go down-- and shifting it a little bit between time periods. In this three to 18-month period I have a lot more levers that take more time to implement. I can hire people, I can fire people, I can decide to outsource-- so all these other things that we'll talk about in more detail in a second. But those are the things that are in this three to 18-month bucket. Then you can think of longer term-- and I arbitrarily said 18 months here, but it can be much longer or much shorter, depending on the company. And these are more long range-- or really longer range-- planning. And we had some examples of this earlier in this course when we talked about network design. We talk about network design, these are things you don't do for tomorrow-- I'm not going to do network design for tomorrow's flow because I've got to build facilities, they're dramatic changes. So as I'm further out I can make bigger changes because they take longer to implement. So I mentioned that these are all just general guidelines. And what companies want to do, they constantly want to shorten these time cycles. So for example, if I could move my frozen time fence for the master production schedule from four weeks to two weeks, that makes me much more agile. So while we have these fences, there should always be an effort to try to shrink them. Why? Because that makes me more agile-- I can respond to the market much faster. Now the other thing that changes is the units that I'm planning. For this shorter time frame it's very disaggregated and I'm looking at individual SKUs or families of SKUs. For aggregate planning, that 3 to 18 months, I'm looking at this aggregated volume-- I'm going to convert them into a unit that makes sense. And for longer range you use flow units. And we talked about this a long time ago-- whatever makes sense for your certain operation, whether it's tons, cubic feet, units, barrels, whatever makes sense, because you're making different decisions. So let's focus in on aggregate planning problem. So we're going to be given a forecasted demand over this planning horizon-- again, 3 to 18 months roughly. We're going to determine the production, the inventory levels, the workforce, how much do I outsource if I do it all, and the capacity levels. And I'm going to do this in order to maximize the firm's profit over that 3 to 18-month planning horizon. Now I have certain levers that I can change. So these are levers that I can adjust to maximize that profit. So I can change the production rate-- how much do I produce? Do I increase that or decrease that? My workforce-- do I make it bigger or smaller? Do I bring in seasonal workers? Overtime-- do I have my existing workers work longer? I'm going to have to pay them more, but that might be easier than adding or subtracting workers. Machine capacity-- can I improve the capacity of my equipment by having more go through it? Should I outsource to a contract manufacturer? Should I allow backlogs? Can I allow it to go short so that I catch up later on? Or on the other hand, should I build up pre-inventory ahead of the time when I need it? So these are the levers that we're going to focus on to determine how to maximize a profit over the planning horizon.

[lecture_04_02_aggregate_planning_model_formulation]
So let's see how we formulate the Aggregate Planning Model. It's going to be a linear program. I don't have any binary or integer variables. And it looks complicated, mainly because we have a lot of data. But it's actually not that big. There's only four classes of constraint, so it's not that bad. So let's talk through it. First, the decision variables. So what are the things we're trying to decide? Well, these are all the levers that we talked about-- the number of employees we hire, the number we fire, the total number we have each month. I is the inventory I have on hand each month. O_t is the amount of overtime hours I have planned that my workers will work. B_t is the amount of units I plan to put on backorder each month that's available to me. P_t is the amount that I'm going to produce or manufacture each month internally with my own resources. And C_t is going to be the amount that I outsource to contract manufacturing each month. Now, the objective function, I'm minimizing here-- I know we talked about maximizing profit, but let's start with just minimizing cost over the planning horizon, and we'll get to the maximization in a few minutes. But all I'm going to do is minimize the sum of those costs. So it's the working, the amount of employees I have, multiplied by the cost of employing each person on average. The amount that I hire each period, there's going to be some cost for hiring someone. Whether it's training or just recruiting, there is going to be some kind of cost. The same goes for the opposite, if I fire someone. If I lay someone off, there's probably severance or some kind of penalty that's paid. I can think overtime. If I have overtime hours, you're going to be paid more for overtime for those existing employees. I have a holding cost with the inventory, so in the dollars per unit per month. I can think of backorder. If I have any items on backorder, I will be charged a certain amount per month, like a shortage cost. And then for production, it's going to be multiplied by the cost of the materials. So what's the cost of the raw materials going into the unit times the number of units that I'm making-- again, with my internal resources? And then finally, the contract-- so this is the number of units that I outsource to a contract manufacturer, and that's multiplied by the c_c right here. And so we made an assumption here-- and you can modify this if you want, this is a very simple model-- that if I use a contract manufacturer, the materials are included in that cost. If it wasn't, then I would just add that in. But we're assuming that if I go to a contractor, that's the total cost-- materials, labor for them to do it. So that's the objective function. Now let's look at the constraints. There's only four. The first one is production capacity. So P_t is the amount that I'm producing each month, and that can't exceed the term H, which is the number of hours each employee can work per month, times the number of employees I have plus amount of overtime hours, and that's divided by this l. So this l is the number of hours required to make each item. So this sets the maximum amount that I can produce, and it's a function of labor. How much labor do I have available? Now, I might be able to do contract. This is just the limit of what I produce internally. So this is what I'm manufacturing. I also have an overtime hours constraint where the amount of overtime hours I plan for-- remember, this is a planning model-- can't exceed the amount of workers I have multiplied by the maximum number of overtime hours I'm allowing. So again, we're setting up a plan here. It might be if things are really hectic at the last minute, we go for unplanned overtime. But this is for planned overtime hours. Then I've got two constraints that should look kind of familiar to you. They're conservation constraints. So this first one is for workers. And essentially, it's saying whatever workers I have today, they have to be equal to what I had last month plus what I've hired minus what I fired. Now, I put everything over on the left-hand side so I can set the constraint equal to 0. But essentially, it's the same conservation type of constraint we've seen since the first week of this course. I have the same thing for inventory. So it's saying the inventory I have on hand today is equal to what I had last month plus what was produced plus what was produced with the contract manufacturer minus what was demanded and then minus what was back backordered the previous month plus what's being backordered now. Now, again, I put everything to the left-hand side so I could set it equal to 0, but it's saying the exact same thing. Now, you might want to think for a second about how I'm making those backorders work. Essentially, what it's saying is you need to take care of your back orders. You can't just forget about them. They're not lost sales, and so you have to keep track of those. And this is how we keep track. Now, you notice what we're doing here-- a lot of these decision variables, they're calculated. So for example, if I tell you how many I hire and fire each period, that's going to determine what my number of workers are, because I'm going to set my initial conditions. That's what these inputs are. But that's OK. So we'll talk about this when we show how we're actually solving this. But I wanted to point that out. So some of these decision variables are calculated based on the other ones. So we're going to work this problem. Here's the example. It's called the Dire Wolf Company, and they manufacture various pieces of equipment for winter sports-- snowshoeing, dog sledding, whatever. They have a single manufacturing facility located in Haight Ashbury in San Francisco. It supplies products across the world. While they have many different SKUs, for the purposes of aggregate planning, they've converted all these items into a common unit. And so this is going to be just units or items. And this is common practice. You don't want to plan for each individual SKU. We're getting rough aggregate planning here. The costs have also been standardized on that unit. And they want to set up an aggregate production plan for the next 3 to 18 months. We'll get into the details in a second. But the levers that we have to work is size of the workforce-- How much do I hire, how much do I fire each month? Overtime hours-- how much do I plan on employees working overtime? Do I allow backorders? What production level do I have with my internal resources, and how much do I outsource? So this is the example we're going to use, and we're going to solve this problem and use this for the rest of the lesson.

[lecture_04_05_fundamentals_of_connecting_sales_and_operations]
So now let's talk about the fundamentals of connecting the sales and operations. We've seen this chart before and we've kind of focused over on this side so far. We have the business strategy comes in then we talked about how do you have the sales and operations planning process. But everything we've talked about so far has been all about the levers that opps have, whether they determine how much inventory, how much production, all those kind of things. What we're gonna to do now is shift to the other side and talk about some of those demand side levers as well. But first, how does it all fit together? What are we trying to solve? Well there's really three big questions we're trying to figure out. We're trying to understand how I should change supply, how I can modify or meet demand by changing how much I produce, and what the mix is going to be. And interestingly the priority is matching supply and demand first, then that helps dictate the volume they're producing, and then finally determine what the mix is going to be between the SKUs. How much to make of what. The interesting thing when you talk about supply and demand, we talk about it being together, but you're really going to decouple them, because if you have supply problems or challenges, your levers match. They're different. Same thing with demand. Now it's very important to know what the impact is. So if I have a change in what I can produce for my supply, what is the impact on the demand, and vice versa. But the levers are done separately so you should almost decouple those to understand what you can and can't change. So we talk about balancing supply and demand. You can think about managing supply and managing demand. So let's review what we've done for supply so far. You really have two big categories. You can manage the capacity, which you are capable of producing, and then inventory, what I've already produced. And for this list for capacity, these should all look very familiar because these are all the levers that we just talked about in the Dire Wolf problem. I have workforce flexibility on hiring, firing, and using overtime. One example you see in practice a lot is seasonal workforces in retail and transportation, such as UPS and FedEx. They will hire a lot of seasonal workers to handle that holiday rush at the end of the year. Subcontracting an outsourcing is widely used and this is something that allows you to respond quicker to changes in demand and also, sometimes, the outsourced manufacturing could be cheaper than what you can do internally. Now we didn't talk about dual facilities but it's worth mentioning for a second. You might want to have two sets of facilities. One is specialized in making a certain product or certain category and it handles a stable flow and you make that as efficient as possible, but then, you've got a separate facility that handles peaks in different categories or different families, and it has to be flexible. Now it's more expensive per unit, probably, but you use this to handle those peaks across the different product categories. You can also think about product flexibility. If all my products are very modular, in that they use the same, say, base chassis, if it's auto manufacturing. Then that actually increases my capacity that I have to produce. Now the other major lever I have is inventory. We talked about pre-building but you can also think along the lines of common components or modularity here as well. If I have the same engine block going in many of my automobiles that I'm manufacturing, then I'm using risk pooling to reduce the volatility, because I have fewer parts going to more end items. So you can see how we've talked a little bit about these different levers that you have on the supply side. And the demand side, we haven't talked too much here so let's introduce some things. First, what are the objectives? What is the demand side, the sales marketing team doing? There are really three big things. First they want to grow the market. They'd love to make the whole market bigger because that would increase sales. Second, they want to steal market share from a competitor. So maybe the market size doesn't get bigger but the percentage that your company serves increases. And the third is maybe you want to shift the buying patterns. So in our example of Dire Wolf, the demand in the summer was very low. So maybe I want to do something to incentivize customers to behave differently. Buying in the summer months. Buying a higher quantity. Things along those lines. So the levers that they have are really related and it all comes down, essentially, to pricing. So if I have pricing, this is the way I can incentivize my customers. We've seen this before when we talk about discounts for order size. So I might have minimum order quantities, MOQs, and these are all things that incentivize me to buy more, to shift my buying pattern. I might increase advertising. So as I advertise more, not only does that help me grow my market share, that might also help me steal market share from other competitors. And these kind of come together in what's known as promotions. And promotions are the primary tool that a lot of manufacturers use, especially if I'm selling to consumers. So consumer packaged goods here, whether it's laundry detergent, anything along those lines, you see a lot of promotions. Now, what happens here, is I'm doing a price reduction over a shorter period of time. And so the question we're going to look at: If this is the levers that I have on the demand side, how does this impact my supply? Because that's what the S&OP process is really all about. How does what I do on one side impact what I'm doing on the other? So what we're going to do next is use the same problem, the Dire Wolf problem, and see how these demand levers can be integrated into the same process.

Now let's add in some of those demand levers, instead of just the operational or supply side levers that we've seen so far. So what I have here is the same problem-- Dire Wolf 2. So if I wanted to optimize this, I would be optimizing this objective function in cell C3, which is the profit. So therefore, I'm going to maximize it. Everything else pretty much stays the same. So the constraints-- the decision variables are still the ones in yellow. So not much has changed. And if I solve this, you'll see that I get the exact same solution that I had if I minimized cost. So my costs are the things I'm going to minimize, because my revenue is pretty much set. So even if I said the price is $200 per, then the solution wouldn't change and the profit would get better. Now, what we're going to do next though is allow the demand side to change some things, and specifically we're going to look at price elasticities. So suppose that sales and marketing can estimate how the demand for these products will change if they change the price. So recall back from your economics days that the elasticity is essentially the change in the quantity as a percentage. That's my delta Q percentage divided by the change in the price as a percentage. So my number could be something-- might change could be a 5% over a 1%, right. So we would say if the price dropped by 1% then this would be saying that my volume would go up by 5%. So my elasticity, in this case, would be negative 5. And so all it's saying is if I drop the price a certain amount, or increase the price, how will the volume change the quantity. So if I just rearrange this, I can set up that Q_new equals q-old times 1 minus the elasticity times the delta of the price as a percentage. Remember delta simply means the change, right. So that would be Q_new minus Q_old over Q_old, right. Same thing for price-- it'd be the price of the new minus price of the old over the price of the old. And so this just standard form. And so all we're doing-- elasticity is pretty straightforward. It's just saying what the change is. And so for an example, in the data that I have, suppose my elasticity is negative 3. Why is it negative? Well, if I drop my price, my volume should go up. Does that make sense? So if it was all in the same direction, it'd be saying that if my price dropped, my volume would drop. And that doesn't make sense. That's very, very rare. So price elasticity with respect to the mean is usually negative. So suppose the elasticity is 3, and in period one my price drops from 180 to 162. That's a 10% decrease. And then I want to figure out what demand is going to do. So I'm just going to use this formula, which is just a restatement of the definition of elasticity. It's going to be the demand old, which is 2,800-- what the base demand is for that month-- times 1 minus, or actually plus, negative 3, that elasticity that I gave you, times negative 0.1. That's a 10% drop, and that gives you 3,640. So a 10% decrease in price increases demand by 30%. And so this is a way that you can use to estimate what the effect of a certain promotion or change in pricing will have on demand. Now, in my spreadsheets, I'm going to keep it simple. All my elasticities-- enter them as positive numbers, because my percent discounts are entering them as positive numbers. I could enter these as negative, enter these as negative. It's the same thing. But just to keep it simple, I'm going to enter these as positive. And I keep a little note here. And this is in the spreadsheet that we've attached for you. So what I want to do next-- well, let me show you are having a spreadsheet actually. So let's clean this up just a tad. There we go.
