Outsourcing is basically the movement of a set of activities or functional services to a contractor instead of doing it internally within the company. So the question is why outsource? There are several reasons for thinking about outsourcing. The external supply may have better capability than we have. The external supplier simply has more appropriate capacity. We need our capacity to do something else. We want to free resources for other purposes. In some cases, the supplier's simply better and can reduce the operating cost. We may be in need of cash and by selling an asset to a provider and letting they provider operator this asset for us gives us a certain cash and then we pay the provider over time for the use of the asset. You can do it in order to spread risk. So you are worried about the certain risk that have to do with a certain operation and you want the supplier to be the owner of the operation or asset. Of course, you may not have internal resource. In this case, it's obvious why you do it. Many companies outsource because simply they want to focus more tightly on the core business. They want to focus on what they're good it, rather than peripheral function. Finally, the supplier may have better economies of scale. They may be servicing several companies like you, and because of economies of scale, the costs are lower, the service is better, what have you. Now the main contributor to success of outsourcing is first of all when the activity is well defined. You don't want to outsource a process that is not well defined because there is no reason to think that the supplier will be better than you in this case. You want to make sure that you have your role and responsibilities of all the parties are very clear. Who is doing what and when and how much they're getting paid for it. You want to have a good relationship with the supplier. The supplier's actually now doing a function that you used to be doing in house, so you want to have a good relationship with the supplier. Finally, you want a high quality supplier. You want somebody who is good at what they do. And the last point is that you want effective contract management. What I mean by that? You want a single point of contact. So you want somebody in your organization and somebody from the supplier organization, maybe even the CEO if this is an important outsourcing arrangement. This has to be the single point for contact between the companies. You want continuously business reviews to find out what's going on, what's going well, what's not going well, and what should be improved. You always want to have regular site visits to see with your own eyes how the suppliers conducting the business. You want to keep performance indicator current. They may change from time to time as the needs change from time to time. This has to be included in the contract and actually has to be changed as needs change. And you always communicate, communicate, communicate. Over communication is not a problem. Under communication is a problem. Now let's go over some key contractual elements. First of all, the definition of what's required. It has to be comprehensive, exactly what is required. What do you want to supplier to do? Then you have to define the process of service evolution. There will be changes in the environment. You want continuous improvement on the part of the supplier. You want to put down the audit and reporting procedure. You want the ability to change services, to add service and delete service. You want to have a process of doing it that is fair to all sides. You want the ability to change volumes, if something, suddenly your business goes up and the supplier has to supply more, you want to make sure that they maybe charge you less because they now have economies of scale. Or they don't have capacity, and they have to have new capacity and they may charge you more, but you have to think about all of this ahead of time. Finally, or the next point, is the service never. There have to be meaningful key performance indicator and targets. So you know if you want 90% on time, you actually measure it and it's something that's important to you. In some contracts, when there is shared risk and reward, you want to think about how you're going to measure this. How you're going to measure the service, for example. You say, I expect 90% on time service, but if it's 95%, I'll give you a little more money. On the other hand if it's 85%, you're going to get penalized for this. Finally, you want the objectives of both sides and the acceptance process of these objectives to be set. Let's continue. Change control process. What happens if the supply gets bought? Do you need approval process over this? What happens if a competitor buys a supplier? How do you protect your intellectual property. Maybe a competitor is buying the supplier. You want to make sure that you avoid lock-in. Lock-in means that you are tied to a particular technology, particular solution, even particular suppliers because-- although outsourcing contracts are longer term by their very nature, you want to make sure there's some out if something fundamentally changes. Of course, you always want to do ongoing market testing and benchmarking, find out if there are others in the marketplace who can provide even better service, and you want to take it back to your partner and find out why that supplier is not as good. You want the whole process of dispute resolution. When there's a key employee change, you may rely on a team of engineers in the supplier are very, very good, world class, and suddenly, several members of the team leave to start their own start up in a totally different business. So you want also to review key employees, and you also want to make sure you have a say in the hiring of key employees. You also want to think about termination process. Force majeure is a contractual term when a supplier says I cannot supply something because something happened. There's a force of God. There's a flood. There's an earthquake. I just cannot supply something. You want to limit those to make sure the supplier has an incentive to have extra inventory, extra plants, and processes to get out of a disruption without just resorting to force majeure. You want to include support for the new contractor, and if you have to terminate the process, if you have to terminate the outsourcing agreement, you need to train a new outsourcing supplier. Records have to be destroyed and returned. And now the question is who owns the capital assets? Who pays for the cost of moving the equipment or plant to a new location. Maybe they share it. Maybe one party is responsible, or another. A very important issue is the usual of intellectual property ownership. Who owns the knowledge base, the data work product, the engineer design, the vendor list, the price data, and on and on and on. The software, the test databases, the engineering analysis, all of this. The question is who owns them when you have to terminate the contract. Most importantly, do you retain the knowledge to monitor the next contractor performance, to take the work back in house or what are the effects of this on engineering talent? When you go to outsourcing, you have to think that-- and I've known several companies who had this problem when they outsourced product design to China-- suddenly they saw that many of their engineers were leaving the company, because they didn't want to just fly to China and be responsible for monitoring Chinese manufacturers. They became engineers because they like to do the design and they like to do the engineering. So you have to think about the effect of total outsourcing on your engineering talent in house.

[lecture_07_09_outsourcing_part_ii]
We talked before during the first segment, on the River Rouge plant. This was the ultimate in no outsourcing. This is the ultimate in vertical integration. Ford owned all factors of production, the wood, the rubber, the iron ore, everything that was used to make steel and wood, and power the plant, and out came cars. The exact opposite is the Volkswagen Resende truck plant in Brazil. There it works as follows. There are certain items that are being done on the production line and each item is being performed by a different company. So there's a company that provides chassis, other company provides the axles, other company provides the wheel, other company provides the engine, the cabin, the body, the paint job. Everything is done by suppliers. Suppliers even providing the internal logistics and Volkswagen is only responsible for the overhead functions. So this is always the ultimate in outsourcing. Now, the advantages of outsourcing are that it can convert fixed cost to variable cost. So it provides some flexibility to adjust capacity and balance work force requirement. It reduces capital investment requirements. You don't have to buy, you can simply outsource. You can accelerate new product development, because the partner to whom you outsource may have capabilities to innovate that you don't have and may accelerate new product development. We mentioned supplier economics and lower weight structure possibly that reduce your cost. And you get access to invention innovation from supplier through this type of partnership. You also can gain access to new markets. For example, Boeing originally outsourced the making of the wings of several of its planes to Mitsubishi, in order to be able to sell jets to Japanese airlines who insisted that some of the manufacturing will be done in their country. Now, you want to focus your own resources on high-value additivity, what's called your core competency. The things that you are good at. The things that give you a competitive advantage. So you don't want to be involved in managing and running operations that are not high-value added. Now, of course, like every other issue, there's the plus side and the minus side. You think about the main example here is the outsourcing to China, which originally was done because of low labor rates. But labor rate is now going up and the advantage of labor shrink as countries mature. And direct labor also becomes a smaller and smaller part of the total cost, because of robotics, and automation, and provision of services. So manufacturing labor is really becoming a very small part of the equation. And in fact, in many countries, labor costs are going up. Supply chain management is difficult when you have offshore suppliers. When you have people who have to fly to China all the time and it takes a long time before the product gets on the ship and gets all the way back to Europe or to the United States, supply chain management becomes more challenging. Lead time may be a problem with suppliers. It may take several months until the product gets into your shop and your customer. So this may be a problem, because during the time that-- during the month or long weeks that the product is on the ocean, requirements may change, technology may change, and you are stuck with certain products. Then there are security considerations. With world-wide terrorism, and Somali pirates, and other issues, the whole idea of offshore procurement is questionable. Now, of course, not all outsourcing is offshore. But offshore outsourcing is a major issue in outsourcing. And usually the main reason for outsourcing is to go offshore and to low labor rate countries. So this is where all these questions come up. There's also a strategic risk. You can create a competitor. In 1914, the Dodge brother turned from a Ford engine supplier to a competitor. The Japanese consumer electronics industry started with contracting for a US firm for radio receiver, for RCA. But then they adopted the transistor faster, and today, there are no TV makers in the United States. Japanese industry and later Korea and others decimated the US consumer electronic industry. We mentioned before that Boeing contracted with Mitsubishi to provide wings for its aircraft. Guess what? Mitsubishi is now making aircrafts competing directly with Boeing. So you can lose the control of the channel to a supplier. More than just a competitor, supplier can just take control of the channel. IBM in 1980, designed the PC, designed the manufacturing process, and had the entire value chain. And then it contracted two small companies at the time, Microsoft and Intel, to provide the operating system and the chip. What became out of it is a machine that was characterized by being a Windows machine and Intel inside. The operating system and the chip were the most important component. And many, many companies could make the box. In fact, IBM left that business. By the way, you can also lose control of a channel to a customer. If you think about the relationship between Procter & Gamble and Walmart. Walmart is now making detergents, which are bread and butter of Procter & Gamble's product, because unlike Intel inside, it now may be Walmart outside. People may come to Walmart to shop at Walmart, not because they want to buy a certain product, but because they like to shop at Walmart. Another example of a complete cost consideration was not taking into account. Marks and Spencer, the British retailer, started in 1998 to move purchasing overseas. It severed relationships with long-time suppliers who would not agree to procure in the Far East. Even though Marks and Spencer was known for British-procured goods, it decided to change. It could not compete anymore, so it forced long-term suppliers to go overseas. And those that didn't go, it severed their relationship with them and started buying from Chinese and other Far East suppliers. It did not take into account all the costs and what happened is the stock price took a dive and went down substantially. And Marks and Spencer was on the verge of going bankrupt. Similarly, the Boeing 787 manufacturing was a case study in how not to outsource. Boeing decided to reduce the risk of developing a completely new aircraft, because it will use new technologies rather than aluminum, which is all kinds of epoxies for the body. So it decided to use not only new technologies, but new supply chain. It wanted suppliers to take on risk and develop a large component of the aircraft. And then they'll put up the capital to do it and then sell the part to Boeing. So these are some of the main suppliers of Boeing. You see they are all over the world. Every supplier was responsible for a certain part of the airplane. And this is just a general view. There are many more suppliers all over the world. The result was delays for the 787. They were years and years for delay and they had to pay compensation for customers. This was the stock price of Boeing at the time. It had a lot of problems, couldn't get the supplier to build the part as needed. Finally, several suppliers have delays of their own. And the example here is the Rolls-Royce engines. It's a good supplier, like Rolls-Royce, even it had problems building the specialized engine needed for the 787. So with this, after looking at the benefits and risk of outsourcing, let's stop with this segment.

[lecture_07_10_purchasing_social_responsibility]
In the last segment of this lesson, we will talk about corporate social responsibility or CSR as it manifests itself in the purchasing of products and services. So in general, one can think that there are three hurdles to success. A company must meet or exceed marketplace demand. It has to be ahead of the competition. A company must meet or exceed regulatory demand. It has to operate legally within the framework of the law. And it has to meet or exceed the social demand of civil society. It cannot do something that is against what civil society thinks are the right things to do. So there's a range of activities that we can include in corporate social responsibilities. These may include philanthropy, include activities that are done to enhance the brand, include activities that are aimed at attracting people, include activities that support the main mission of the company, include social business, and include activity that directly support the cost reduction or increase revenue, which mean increase the profits of the business. And let's give examples of each one of those. So an example of philanthropy is WalMart, which in 2007 gave over $100 million to all the communities where they do business. Example of brand image is Ben & Jerry's, the ice cream maker, and Starbucks coffee. Both of them are very environmentally responsible. And this environmental responsibility is just to enhance their brand. So Ben & Jerry's is known as a company that has a very small carbon footprint. And Starbucks is a company that makes sure that all its employees have health care. It actually now also allows employees to take a degree, a graduate degree at night, and they'll pay for it. So it treats its employees well, and it treats the farmer and the suppliers well. An example of HR attraction is T&T, the logistics company, which contract with the World Food Program to provide food to communities all over the world. This helped employees pride and satisfaction in being employees of T&T, and it helps retain employees and attract new employees into T&T. An example of mission support is Cisco Network Academy. Cisco allows people who are right now may be on minimum wage to take a course for free in the evening at their Network Academy. And they have academies like this all over the United States. Within several months, sometimes years, they train these people to be network technicians, to be able to fix the Cisco network. Now on the one hand, this is a corporate social responsibility or a social mission because they allow those people to move from minimum wage to middle class salaries. On the other hand, it also helps Cisco because there are dozens of those or hundreds of those in some cities, and they can go to a new customer and tell the customer look, if you buy our equipment, there will be 152 technicians within two miles of your place. And this is our website, and this is who they are, and you can get them. An example of social business is either Patagonia, what they call doing well by doing good, or micro-financing that took place in Bangladesh to begin with and then in several other countries, where money was given mostly to women, small amount of money to start businesses. Turned out to be a very profitable business, because there were almost no defaults on this money. Patagonia did something else. They had ads in the newspaper when they came out with a new product several years ago, and the ad said don't buy this jacket. They were trying to convince people to go and buy used jacket, used Patagonia jacket on eBay. Now, why is it good for Patagonia? Clearly it's good for the environment because if people don't throw away a jacket and don't buy a new one, but somebody buys a used one, then a new one doesn't have to be made. And there's smaller environmental footprint. But it also helped the business. That's why Patagonia was doing well by doing good, because think about it. It helped sell the new jacket because people now knew that even though the jackets are expensive, they can sell them after a few years. There's a market for them. There's also a message about how durable this jacket is, that they last a long time and you can sell them later on. So all of this helped Patagonia's business. And in fact, many of its competitors thought it was just a marketing trick, and not really done for corporate social responsibility, even though Patagonia is a company committed to corporate social responsibility. Finally, there's many, many examples of so-called eco efficiency, or efficiency, reducing cost, and at the same time reducing the carbon footprint. IBM operating now green data centers powered by sun power, by wind power, by hydro power. And many of these centers consume no fossil fuel energy, so they don't contribute anything to global warming. And at the same time, energy costs are lower. So they both are good for the environment and good for cost reductions. Many, many examples like these. Many companies change light bulbs or reduce the speed of trucks. They reduce the energy consumption, reduce costs, but also reduce the emission and the use of fossil fuel. So one can think about a range of procurement activities in order to make sure the suppliers are environmentally responsible. The first one is having a code of conduct. Many companies have a code of conduct for the supplier. For example, Ikea have what is called IWAY, or IKEA Way. This is a set of principles that govern who can be a supplier for IKEA. And suppliers, if they fall on one of them and don't comply with one of them, they have a certain time to make good and to improve, otherwise they stop being suppliers. Unless it's one of those very important items that there's no recovery from them. For example, if IKEA discovers that somebody is using slave labor or child labor, the supplier is cut off immediately. But this is all included in the code of conduct. Of course, the code of conduct requires regular auditing. For example, many companies, IKEA and many others are doing auditing of suppliers. Intel is trying to make sure, or has now made sure that it's not using any conflict minerals. These are minerals that are mined in the Democratic Republic of Congo through conditions of slave labor and coercion in conflict area by guerrilla group and gangs. So it's now enshrined in US law, but even before the law, Intel tried to make sure that it's not using any mineral mined in the Congo. For this, it had to go very deep in the supply chain and finally get to the smelters, which is the last point where one can audit where the conflict minerals are coming from. But audit was key to find out that it is free of conflict minerals. Many companies also train suppliers to become better. For example, Starbucks has over 100,000 suppliers of coffee. And it works with all of them to make sure that they use efficient processes, they use just enough water and not too much, and use the right minerals in the ground, and they work with the land in an efficient way. These things both reduce the environmental footprint of growing coffee, and they also actually reduce the cost or they making the farms a lot more productive, and thereby reducing the cost. Unilever does the same thing with their tea plantations and many of their other materials that they get into their products. Finally, there's supply chain transparency. There's the issue of you want to be very transparent, to show everybody that you're actually doing the right thing. So the example is Patagonia. If you go to the Patagonia site and click on a certain product, and then click on what's called the footprint chronicle, you will get a picture something like this. You will first get a picture of a map with a location of all the suppliers who contribute parts and material to the product, and where the product is actually assembled or made. And then when you click on each supplier, you'll get a lot more information on the supplier, including for example the ratio of females and males, including the language spoken, what they make here. But a whole history of their corporate social responsibility, how they treat their employees. Were there any problems that Patagonia found through the audit? Or the existing problem, how much time they have to fix this problem? So the good and the bad about each one of these suppliers. This type of transparency makes sure that Patagonia creates pressure on itself and its competitors to provide similar transparency and not to hide anything, making sure that everybody who is using Patagonia product can be sure that they're using a product responsibly produced. You can think by way of summary about the hierarchy of procurement, corporate social responsibility. We think about economic responsibility. As we mentioned at the beginning, one has to be ahead of the competition. One has to be within the confines of the law, and have to fulfill any legal responsibility. One has to have so-called license to operate, and fulfill any ethical responsibility. And finally, there are discretionary responsibility, where one might go beyond just standard ethical responsibility or provide some philanthropy on top of everything. So let's summarize this lesson. We started saying that procurement is based on more than cost. Remember the iceberg scenario, the iceberg metaphor. As you deal more and more into the item that you buy, you discover there are many hidden costs that should be taken into account. We can segment the buy into complexity versus spend. And the important quadrant there is the critical buy, that should be reduced either by substitution, simplification, or partnership. So you move the upper left corner into the lower left corner, the lower right corner, or the upper right corner through substitution, simplification, or partnership. The procurement process, we went through the process itself, but the most important part or one of the important part is that it should be followed by data collection and reporting for the next contract. So when you come to procure the same item a year or two years from now, you're not in the same position but you know more. It's important to be a good customer. You have to look internally. Ask your suppliers what you can do in order to simplify processes. Be a better customer, because this will be reflected in the price and in the service. We talk about volatility that can be handled by hedging, either financial hedging or actual hedging. We talked about options. And another way of handling volatility is to separate between the stable and volatile element, and to tie the volatile element to some index. We mentioned that capital goods require special attention, and we talked about certain issues with capital goods that one should pay attention to. We talked about the pros and cons in outsourcing, the fact that it may help you concentrate on the main business, on your core competency. It also may change fixed costs into variable costs. But on the other hand, you may create a competitor, and the business may be taken away from you. Finally, we talked about supplier CSR, and understand that supplier corporate social responsibility reflects on the company. In fact, suppliers by and large are not very susceptible to NGO attack on the corporate social responsibility processes. The companies who are susceptible are the brand holder, the OEM, because they sell to consumers and NGO campaigns usually are aimed at swaying consumers to avoid using the product of a company, even though the offenders may be deep in the supply chain. So let's stop here, and thank you for listening to this lesson.

[lecture_07_11_optimization_based_procurement]
Hi, everybody. This is Yossi Sheffi. In this lesson, we'll talk about procurement optimization. When buying products or services, companies are trying to make sure that they'll get the best deal. The question is, what constitutes best? Usually, it's the lowest cost. So let's talk about lowest cost in terms of best. But this means that the auctioneers choice of winner involves an optimization trying to get this best deal. However, in real life, the questions are much more complicated. Companies are trying to fulfill not only one objective like lowest cost, but in many cases, are trying to get the best service, and even they try to fulfill other objectives, such as social goals. And we'll talk about this. Throughout this lesson we'll talk about various conditions on the optimization, or on the choice, of the winning suppliers for various parts of the business. One of the characteristics of multiple item procurement, which in many cases companies are conducting multiple items at the once, is that the controlling economics could economies of scope, not economies of scale. What does it mean, economies of scope? The costs of the supplier are lower if it can provide several related items. For example, if the same supplier can provide us the navigation system and the radio, then its costs may be lower. And if we evaluate the radio suppliers and the navigation system suppliers separately, we may not take advantage of it. So in the last part of the lesson, we will explain how to conduct the bundled, or combinatorial, procurement auctions, and take advantage of such economies of scope when we can get a better price if we let the supplier provide us some related items. So let's start with this lesson. As I mentioned before, we'll talk about several conditions. We'll start with simple auctions. Then we will deal with capacity constraint. Then we will deal with various other constraint on the supplier. We'll deal with minimum volume requirements. We'll deal with volume discounts that a supplier offers. And we'll deal with combinatorial auction. These are the type of auction that take into account economies of scope. So recall the basic process of auction. Every procurement is an auction. And the procurement team usually estimate the requirement for the next performance period, maybe yeah, maybe two year, or maybe the life of the next model. And then their requirements are communicated to the supplier, to the bidder, usually at some supply conference or by email. And each suppliers then submits a bid. In this case, we're talking about first price sale bid auctions. In most procurement auctions, one does not conduct English auctions with many, many rounds, because it is complicated. There are many, many items, and we don't want to sell each item separately. We don't want to buy each item separately. So by and large, we do a first prize sale bid auction. And you'll see later why it's the right type of auction for procurement. In some cases, when the items are bought are separate, and there are many suppliers in a simple option, in that case, some companies have conducted internet-based, English-type auctions, when the prices go down, down, down. And suppliers are bidding on this. General Electric is famous for this. But in most procurement auctions, one use first pricing sale bid auctions. The procurement team looks, of course, at all the envelope, and chooses the bidder which is the best. Let's talk about an example which we will carry throughout this lesson. This is an example of procurement for transportation services. So what we have here is a simple network with four lanes, four origin destination pair, a to b, b to c, c back to b, and c to a. And we have two carriers bidding on it. So here are the bids. This is carrier one. And this is carrier two. And these are the various lanes. So carrier one from a to b is bidding $500. And carry two, form a to b, is b bidding $525. Now, this is a very simple situation. You can just look at it and see which carrier is winning what because we're looking here only at the lowest possible cost. But we will add hear something that will allow us to use it in a spreadsheet. What we will add here are the decision variable. So what we have here, inside these cells, are eight values which are simply the number of loads that carrier will carry on each lane. So we start with 10, just a starting value. It doesn't matter what we start with. This is the number of loads per week, let's assume, that each carrier will get. But we'll have later on a requirement that the sum of every row would be only 10. We don't have more than 10 loads on each lane. So the optimization will have to allocate the number of loads between the carriers, so that on each lane we'll have only 10 loads. This is done with a spreadsheet. And this is the spreadsheet. And what we have here are the decision variables, and the-- excuse me-- the prices, and the decision variable. This 40 is simply the sum of all the cost. So it's 500 times 10. 500 times 10. 500 times this 10. 475 times this 10. It's basically the multiplication of all these eight by the corresponding decision variables. So as I mentioned before, this is the sum of each row. So this is c11 to d11, c11 plus d11, basically. This is the sum of all the columns. So this is, then, total number of loads that carrier two is getting, in this case, is the sum along the column. And this is so-called the sum product, is the c3 to d6, which is these eight values times these eight values. And what we do here, we use the solver, the spreadsheet solver, in order to optimize here. And if we get to the solver, we use the data entry at the spreadsheet. And we get to the solver. And this is what the optimization looks at. And this is the solver screen. What we have here is the following. We optimize c19, which is the total cost. We're trying to minimize the total cost. So this is the first one. c19 is the objective, and we try to minimize the total cost, subject to certain constraint. So these are the decision variables. So we try to change these decision variables so that the total cost will be minimized and all these constraints will be met. So the total cost has to be-- this is the total cost has to be minimized by changing these variables and dealing with all of these constraints. And these constraints are-- the first one-- that each one of these entries, each one of these decision variables, is greater than zero, and has to be integer. We don't want to have 1.7 trucks. And the sum of every row is 10. So e12 to e15, which is each one of these variables, which is the sum of the row, equal 10. When we're done, we hit solve. And we get the solution for this simple case. The solution is that the minimum is $19,500. And we see that, as expected, the optimization allocated the loads to the least expensive carrier. So carrier two, because it offered $475 on b to c, as opposed to $500 that carrier one offered, got 10. But in every other lane, in every other origin destination pair, it said the higher cost of the optimization didn't assign anything to it. So this was, basically, what we did. We had to minimize the sum product, subject to integrality, non-negativity, and the sum of every row here equal to 10. And we look at the solution, and we see that the constraint was met. And we start with just randomly assigned ten to every carrier on everything lane. We could assign any other number. It doesn't matter. And the question, now, is what if we want a minimum of carriers on each lane? So we want to introduce a little complication, which is still very simply. And so we just want to set the minimum of 20% of the volume on each lane. You can actually solve this, too, by inspection, but just to make it clear, let's go to the spreadsheet. So this is the spreadsheet, and once you press solve, you get this type of screen. Again, recall that we are looking for c19, that is here. We want to minimize c19. It's right here. This is the cell. We still want to minimize it. And let's see what is the additional constraint that we put it. In addition to having every one of these variables integer, rather than greater than zero, we put it greater than 2. So this is the constraint here. Of course, we still need the constraint that the sum of each row is 10. This is the last constraint, and everything's integer. And once we do this, we can actually press solve, just like before. Here's the solve. And what we get is the solution. So here are the solution. You see that where we had 10-- where we had 10 before, we have 8. Where we had 0 before we have 2. Because we have a constraint. We have to give at least 2 to every one of the carriers on every one of the lanes. So the optimization tries, still, to push the big number towards the low cost. So for example, in a to b we get 8 at $500 and 2 at $525. And we get 2 at $525 because we impose a constraint on the optimization. As a result of this, the previous minimum was $19,500. The previous total cost here was $19,500. It is now $19,700. So in that sense, we got a worse solution. It's not as good as before. Remember, we are trying to minimize the total cost. Our solution is not as good as before. Is it really not as good as before? It really is as good as before, because it now fulfills another constraint. Let's go back and understand that if we minimize just cost, any constraint that we put in optimization can not improve the result. The result can be only the same or worse. So here we put a constraint on it. And the result is worst in terms of the total cost. It's not worse from the business point of view, because from the business point of view, you now have to fulfill more than minimize the cost. You want to protect yourself. There's some risk management here that you want at least two on each one of the lane. Let's now have another type of constraint. This is the same as before, the same data as before. Let's now assume that the carriers to the bidding process are not only giving the price, but they are also telling the auctioneer, the company, that there's only so much that they can give on each lane. It's a very large number. It has to be a very large number relative to the existing capacity. So we put 100 here. So basically this means that carrier one on b to c and carrier two on c to a and c to b have no capacity constraint, basically. And this has to be-- in the solution-- less than this. This has to be less than this, which it will be, of course, because it's a large number. This has to be this, and so on. So this brings us to this constraint. We simply say that, basically, this eight cells have to be each smaller than these eight cells. And now we actually run the optimization and we get the solution. The solution is now here, $19,925. It is also higher than the original solution that we have before. We now don't impose two on every lane. But we have capacity constraint. So a different type of constraint. So it's a different type of total cost. It's higher than the original for the same reason that we mentioned before. The carrier by the bidding process, introducing capacity constraint, constrained optimization in the solution cannot be as good as before. [lecture_07_13_but_what_about_service]
In this lesson, we will talk about service. We all know that there's more than price to choosing a supplier, especially if it's not a commodity business. If there are not hundreds of suppliers out there, they're all replaceable, they're all competing with each other. This is not a common situation. And in many cases, the service, innovation, reach, and other factors that we all call service are just as important, and sometimes even more important, than the price that we pay just for the item itself, or for the bare-knuckle service. So let's talk about how, in most cases, services are dealt with. So the current practice, by and large, is what's called screen and auction. For example, we're talking here throughout this lesson about tracking, so let's assume that we decide that we want to have only truck companies that are 95% or better on time. So out of all the bidders, we define a group of suppliers, just those whose service meets our minimum service requirement. And among those, we conduct an auction based on price. And this, is some sense, takes into account both service and price. However, it has an important drawback. It's arbitrary. The cutoff point does not allow for trade offs. For example, a 93% service supplier may be out. And a 94% service supplier may be in, regardless of the price. In other words, the 93% service supplier may be substantially lower in price than the 94% supplier. So is it really worth it, such big price difference for 1% level of service. So we want to do it more methodically. So how do we do it within an optimization framework? So let's go through it. Within the framework that we have used to day, what we do is we modified the prices based on service before the optimization. So we use the optimization, as we'll see in a minute, modified prices rather than the price is submitted by the bidders. Let me give you an example, it is much easier to explain to an American example than talk about it in abstract. So let's say there's a supplier who's bidding on one lane, $500. And this supplier is 97% on time supplier. And let's say the 97% is what we care about in this particular example. There's also another supply who is submitting a lower bid for $475, but he's only 94% on time. The question is, do we choose the high service, high cost supplier? Or the low service, low cost supplier? In order to determine, we have to determine how much level of service is worth. Let's assume that level of service is worth $10 per each 1% of service. Somehow we got into this determination, we found out. We'll talk in a minute how to do it. But we decided that each percent of level of service is worth $10. What we do now, we decide on some baseline. And let's standardize on 94%, even though we can standardize on any other baseline. So let's bring everything to 94%, assuming that each 1% of service is worth $10. So the 94% supplier is already had $475. We don't have to move it. But the 97% supplier bid is modified. We take $30 off because his service is 30% better than the standard service. So we look at this supplier as if his cost is $470. Now we conduct the optimization between the two. And we compare $470 to $475. And of course, the 97% supplier will be chosen. At the end, of course, we don't pay $470. We pay $500. We pay the bid that this supplier gave us. But since we decided that each 1% level of service was worth $10, we should be happy with this choice. Of course, the challenge here is to estimate the value of the level of service. And there is no easy way to do it. Usually, it's done by-- I could call it-- argument. You can call it discussion between sales people, marketing people, operating people, plant people. They all come together. And through this discussion, they have to decide on the value of level of service. So it's something that comes from the outside in the optimization. But once this level is decided, we can conduct optimization. And I'll show you in a minute how. So now we have the following. We have the original bids here, the original table here with the initial values. And here we have this table is the level of service of each carrier on each lane. And this is an auxiliary table that we are creating for the purposes of this optimization. So what do we do here? First of all, we define level of service, so each supply and each item in percent per lane. This is what was done right here. Now we define what a percentage level of service is worth. So using the same example, we decide that each 1% is worth $10. The next step is we define a standard level of service. In this case, because it doesn't matter which one we define. These are all linear, and the result will be the same. But we define a standard level of service as 92, in this case. So now we have to bring each one of these bids up or down to 92. To modify each bid according to the service, relative to the standard. So for example, why is this $450? Because carrier one on a and b is bidding $500. His level of service here is 97%, which means he's 5% better than 92. So we have to take down 50 points, 92 minus 97 times 10, and add the original bid. So we get $450. Why is this bid here $515? He bid $475. But we look at him as $515, because 88 is four points lower than 92. So we have to increase it by 40. We have to increase the $475 by 40 and we get $515. Now we take into the optimization, we do it a little differently. We don't multiply this value by the variables to get the objective function. We multiply this value, these modify prices, by these variables. And this gives us a modified objective function. See, this is the modified objective function that we get after multiplying these variables, these prices, these modified prices, by this. Now we go into the optimization. So the sum product here is l4 m7, which is here, times these eight values. Now we get into the optimization. And we conduct the optimization. Before-- there's actually no constraint here, just a set of modified prices. But something strange is happening here. We get that the objective function here value is lower, lower than the original. Unconstrained it was $19,500, totally unconstrained value. So how come the value now is lower than what we got initially? Well, let's think about it for a minute. What did we do here? We modified the prices. So actually, if we look, this is what we got, by multiplying this times this. But what the carriers will get, and what the company will pay, is not this times this. It's actually the original bids times the value. So the real objective function will be $20,000 here, which is higher than the original one. Because now we used modified prices. So it is different. The sum product-- this was the sum product of the l4 m7. This x value times the variables. While this is the sum product of these, c4 d7 here times the value that we have here. OK, let's go to optimization with item attributes, which is explain what we did here. A service is a supplier item attribute, which means it's tied to specific suppliers and specific lanes. We can associate each type of price or condition with individual item of a given supplier. Many examples of this, for example, supplier can be familiar with a certain name. Supplier can be [INAUDIBLE] readiness on certain lane. He has a certain percentage of damage and wrong delivery on a particular lane. He has on time performance on each lane. These are all lane attributes. They can associate with each lane of each item, separately. As opposed to what we are going to talk about in a minute, which are system attributes rather than item attributes or lane attributes. [lecture_07_14_system_attributes_constraints_part_i]
In this segment we will talk about system attributes or system constraint. So the first order of business is to explain what they are. And it can be explained through an example. Recall that the capacity constraint that we had before were item level conditions, or item level constraint. What does it mean? We had the condition that on a particularly lane, a particular carrier can carry only x loads. I think carrier one a to b could carry only five, or whatever. This is an item level constraint because it's a particular origin destination pair on a particular item up for bid. There could be also system level constraint. For example, the vendor may say, I can give you only so many trucks, or I can serve only so many lanes, or I can do only so much business with you. So you now you have to find the best solution, the cost-minimizing solution, subject to the fact that you meet the carrier demand, or you meet the carrier constraint. So this is now a system constraint. And a system constraint, there are many types. It's not the one-- and we'll give some example. We'll talk about it more as we go along here. By and large, they require the introduction of more elaborate mathematical constraints of the optimization. We always represent this condition through constraint, because we want to meet this condition or this attributes of the problem, so we introduce a constraint and make sure that they get the minimum cost subject to the fact that this condition is met. So we introduce this as a constraint into the optimization. Another example of system condition is vendor preference. Here, the vendor does not put up the condition, we as the auctioneer, or as the procurement department, put certain conditions. For example, we would like a certain carrier to get at least that much money because we like them. Or we like a certain carrier to be the carrier of choice of not more than 25 lanes, because we're not sure about them. Or whatever, whatever the reason is. But this is a condition that we introduce. But again, since we want to meet this condition, we introduce it through a constraint. And we let the optimization minimize cost subject to meeting this condition. In both cases, by the way, whether it's item constraint or system constraint, item or system attributes, the resulting optimization cannot have a better solution than before. It cannot have a lower cost than before. The cost can be only the same or worse. So this doesn't matter if it's item condition or system condition. So let's talk about the first example. In the first example, we introduce a fleet constraint. So each one of the carriers here can say, I can serve only 25 shipments. I say use the same number, just as an example. It doesn't matter. You can use any number here. Here are the tables are the same. The construct are exactly what we had before. But when we look at the spreadsheet, what we have to make sure, the extra constraint here, is that this number, which is actually the sum of the loads that this carrier, carrier number one, carries, has to be less than this number. So we have to make sure that the resulting number after the optimization is less than 25. Also, the same thing for the other carriers. So the total the number, the sum of the loads that this carrier is carrying, which is here, is less than its condition. So here is the extra constraint. We say that de16 is less than j8 k8. If we look at it in the solver screen, this is the extra solution. And here we get the solution. So we see here that the total cost is higher than $19,500 we had before. This is to be expected. We just add a constraint. And we see that the conditions are met. We see that none of the carriers has more than 25 loads. In order to meet the constraint, if you tried to compare the solution to the original solution, the original solution had 10 and zero. So this optimization moved five loads from carrier one to carry two on a to b, because it figured this is the best way to minimize the cost and still meet this constraint. Turns out it's not the only way. In this particular case, there's also in additional solution that will have exactly the same total cost, meet the constraint in exactly the same way. This happens, by and large, because of quirks by the optimization. We're not going to get into it. Let's now talk about other example of preferred supplier or condition that we may put in. [lecture_07_16_economies_of_scope_and_combinatorial_auctions_part_i]
As I mentioned in the introduction to this lesson, one of the challenges in procurement optimization is to take advantage of economies of scope. And this can be done with what's called combinatorial auction or combinatorial procurement, and this is the subject of this particular segment. So let's talk about economies of scope first. In many cases, the bid for one item depends on whether the supplier knows that they will get other items. They may have joint cost in the production of certain things, so it's not that this supplier will give us lower costs if we get more of the certain items. This is simply based on economies of scope, and suppliers, as we saw before, can give us some volume discount. This is not volume discount. This is getting discount or getting low price on both items if the supplier gets to supply both. It can be 2, 3, or 100 items, but they are related somehow, and the cost to produce them are related. So the supplier may get, for example, to sell two types of cereal that are made together or a navigation system and cruise control that can be made in one system. And in that case, the supplier will give us a better price because its costs are lower. So these are not false economics. This is not pressing the supplier or anything like this. This is the supplier costs are lower because the production cost depend on doing these two items together. It is particularly evident in trucking transportation when you think about truckloads. Truckloads is taking full loads from origin to destination. Here the transportation product is lane, origin destination. This is what we've talked about throughout this lecture. So the cost are of two types. There's direct cost and connection cost for the truckers, for the transportation carrier. The direct costs are the costs that are tied to the distance going from A to B. Its the drivers' salaries, it's the gas, it's the tire wear, all the cost that are directly tied to the movement. Connection costs are different. The connection costs are the cost of what happens at the destination. For example, does the driver have to wait there for three days until he get the next load? Does the truck have to move empty out of there, which costs money and nobody pays? So these are some of the connection costs. The connection costs create lane cost dependency or economies of scope. Because if I just bid on A to B not knowing what's going to happen in B, is different than if I get an A to B knowing that you also give me B to A or B to C. Because now I know the truck doesn't have to wait there, I don't have to go empty, and I can give you a better price because it costs the trucker or less, so the trucker can give a better price. So the problem is that the standard auction process or the standard procurement process for transportation is that the shippers, the auctioneers, evaluate each lane bid by itself, which means the carrier are bidding on hundreds or thousands or tens of thousands of lane. The shipper, which is the auctioneer, may look at all the bids from Boston to Detroit and choose the minimum subject to some constraints or the combination of the best service and best price. But to simplify this, let's not talk only about price. So basically the shipper ranks all the bids on a particular lane and chooses the best one or the least cost one. It doesn't work for the carriers because the carriers are trying to build a network. They need to get the equipment back home. They need to keep moving. So there's a disconnect here that we'll explain further. So the current practice in transportation, in most cases, it starts with information exchange. The shippers gives aggregated volume estimate by lane, by origin, region, system, based on last year and some forecast of what will happen last year and ask the carrier to bid on this. The carrier submit lane rate, which sometimes a per mile rate or per move between A to B, a charge that much or between A to B, a charge so much per mile, same idea. And then what happened? There's assign mechanism. It's what I mentioned before, lane-by-lane analysis. For each lane, the low bids win. It's basically done through a spreadsheet. So let's continue with an example that we use throughout this lesson. So this is the simple network with four lanes, A to B. We call A to B, B to C, C to A, and C to B, and C to A. And these are the bids of the carriers. That's actually the same table that we had before. And you look at this and it's clear that the carrier 1 will meet A to B, C to A, and C to B, and carrier 2 will win B to C. So this will be the winning bids, and if we look back on who is winning what, carrier 1 is winning A to B, B to C, and C to A. Carrier 2 winning B to C. That's a problem because there are two very good trips here. One trip is if one carrier can win A to B, B to C, and C to A, it can get the equipment back to maintenance, it can get the driver back home, or if a carrier win, B to C and C to A. In this situation, none of the carriers won this good tour, this good type of business. That's the disconnect between the carrier and the shipper here. To solve this, what we use is what's called combinatorial bid. And with combinatorial bid, the following takes place. We let each carrier bid not only on each lane separately, but on combinations. So in this particular network, possible package includes the following. The first one is bids on each lane separately. Package number 1 is A to B. Package number 2 is B to C. We call it the package just to keep the nomenclature. But we also let carriers bid on the combination of lanes. So carrier can say, I can give you a good price if you give me A to B and B to C and even better price if you give me A to B, B to C, and C to A. I can give you a good price B to C. So now we get lots of bids for all these combinations. Now, the problem is the complexity. The complexity is that there's a large number of bids. Each carrier, let's say we have several 100 lanes or 7,000 lanes. Instead of submitting just number of bids similar to the number of lanes or smaller than the number of lanes, it's submit that plus many of the combinations. So we can have million bids submitted by each carrier. Then we have also overlapping bid. What does it mean overlapping bid? Let me give you an example. Let's say we have a lane going from A to B. So one carrier bids A to B, the same carrier bids A, let's say C to A to B. Another carrier bids going to A going to B going to somewhere else coming back. That's just one carrier. There's another carrier submit I'm going from this point to here and here and there and then coming back. So which carrier gets what? There are so many overlapping bids, it gets really, really difficult. And the large number, of course, doesn't simplify anything. So how do we deal with it? Turns out there are certain mathematical structures that are very good for dealing with this. So let's arrange, let's show how it is done in a spreadsheet. In actuality, it's not done in spreadsheet. It's done in special software, but for this particular example, it can be done with a spreadsheet. So let's arrange all the bids in a table. How do we arrange it? So this is the package number here. These are the lane. And a 1 here says that this carrier is bidding on this lane. So package 1 includes a bid on A to B, and the price is $500. Package 5 include a bid on A to B and B to C, and the price is $975. You see the $975 is less than bidding separately for A to B and B to C because if carrier 1 is bidding separately for A to B and B to C, he does not know if he'll get both of them. But if he bids on a combination, he can provide a better price because he knows that he'll get both of them. By the same token, you see, bid number 8 includes B to C and C to B. This is the round robin, B to C and C to B. And bid number 9 is A to B, B to C, and C to A. This is a tour that goes back to the origin. And unlike bidding these three separately, which cost $1,500, the carrier, in this example, is bidding $1,325, so they're bidding less. Same thing. Carrier 2 is also, there's a 1. If there's a bid, the price is here at the bottom and we have the same number of bids. Add to this now the number of loads assigned to a particular package. So this is the variable that we have, the number of loads assigned to a particular package, and this is the cost data on the lanes. So what we have here is, if this 1, this means that there's a bid here. This is the number of loads. It's coming through here, and this is 10 times $500 will be the $5,000 here. So we have here the bid of this particular. So if carrier 1 gets package 1, they'll get 10 loads on this number of loads. If it will be 10 at a cost of $500 each, their bid is $5,000. So let's see how each one of these works. So just repeating what we said. So there's a 1 in the table, in the cell, in this case in package 5. So if A to B is included in package 5, there's a 1 here and 0 otherwise or nothing. The cost of package 5 by carrier 1 is $975. In addition, the number of loads assigned to carrier 1, package 5 is here. This is the variable that will change as part of the permutation, but we start with less then number of 10 as a starting condition. This is the loads that carrier 1 carries from A to B, and this is the total cost of assigning 10 loads to package 1 of carrier 1. Here we have actually the objective function. This is total cost. It's the sum of all the cost along here, along the bottom row. And of course, this is the sum of the loads. We have to have this column equal to this column. So we have to have at least 10. The constraint here is least 10 on every one of these lanes, just like before. So now we get to the optimization itself. So in the optimization, we are trying, this is the objective function. So this is the C11 times C5. So this is C5 times C11. It's simply the number of loads that carrier 1 is getting under package 1. This is the number of loads that carrier 1 is getting under package 7. This is the cost, the amount of money that carrier 1 is getting under package 1 if package 1 is assigned to it, and this is the sum which is the total cost which we are trying to minimize. And this, of course, again, is the constraint that having 10, we want to have 10 in each row. Now, we get to the optimization. So we optimize-- U19 is exactly here. And we want to change the C11 to T11 are here. Everything here is integer, and you see here that we contend here that U15, U18, which is this, is equal to this. So now we optimize this. You see this is just what I said. And these numbers are these numbers here are the decision variables, and we impose the constraint that we have 10 in each row. So now we look at a solution. We optimize it. We solve this, minimize this. And this is not easy to do by inspection. You can do it, but it's not as easy. You see that this is the solution. We see that carrier 1, the round robin here, was carrying A to B, B to C, and C to A. A to B, B to C, C to A. In carrier 2 and also carrier 1 was getting this one. We can have conditions on the combinatorial bid. For example, we want to use minimum two carriers and each carrier should get at least 10. We don't know where, but must get at least 10 throughout the system. What we add here is this variable here. This is the sum. 90 is the sum of all. And this is the sum of L11 to T11 from here to here. So each one of them is the sum of all the loads that the carrier is getting. And we can put it to any number, want to have some minimum number, so whatever. In this case I think we put it to 10. So we make sure that each of the sum is greater than or equal to 10. So this is simply an additional constraint. You see that K12, which is this, and T12, which is this here, are greater then 10. All the other conditions are the same. So this is what we just said. So we go ahead and optimizing it, and we get a different solution in this case. You see that still carrier one is getting its package that it wants, but carrier two is also getting some reasonably good packaging. For example, it gets B to C and C to B, what's called a continuous move. And the assignment is different. You see here, by the way, that the solution here is higher. We had 18,000 before. It's 18,125 because we just impose the constraint that each carrier has to get at least 10. And you see how the numbers here are distributed across the various packages. So the solution looks now something like this. Carrier one gets five on C to B and gets its package. Carrier two gets C to A and A to B, which is a continuous move, and also one of the good packages. So with this, let's explain the process. This is a more complex process than just regular auction or regular procurement. So you'll need to explain to the supplier. In many cases, companies that we dealt with are having a supplier conference, more than one sometimes, and explaining how the process works. And especially for small suppliers, you need to help them. In fact you may want to even identify the packages in their request for bids that you are submitting because large carriers will have operation research analysts who can find out through the data what are good bids and what good packages. Small carriers you may want to identify when you talk about transportation carriers. It does require a specialized software for the optimization. The problem structure is called set covering. And it can be solved for very large problems. But you need specialized software. This is something for a good-sized problem. It is something that is solved iteratively. It is not solved, of course, by a spreadsheet. And it takes a while to get to a good solution. You test various condition with various optimization runs. Just like we said before, you can test all kinds of conditions, add to the combinatorial nature of the problem anything that you want. You can add a level of service. You can add my brother-in-law has to get at least a million dollars, anything that you want. Or we can deal with only so many carriers or at least that many carriers or a constraint that the order sees from every facility, we want to have at least two carriers, one primary and one secondary. All kinds of conditions take place. And these conditions are imposed after the bidding data is in. So you first get the data. Then you impose all those conditions and run the optimization. Now as we said before, one of the issues that's not easily determined-- and determined as we said through negotiations. Remember when we talk about the level of service, we said that you had to determine how much level of service is worth to you. Turns out that in many cases it's not easy to determine. And by successive runs, you run with different types of level of service, different values. And people can say OK, this is what it means when the level of service is $10 per 1%. This is what it mean when it's $5 or $15 or whatever. And they start seeing the implication of different values on the level of service. By way of summary, let's say the following. A procurement auction is always an optimization process. You always try to find the best, the minimum, the lowest, the best service, what have you. The choice of the winner of course can be based on various criteria and can be subject to many conditions. Each one of these criteria and conditions can be modeled as a constraint. And you can have a combination of them. You don't need to test them one at a time. You may want to test what happens when I do a conspiratorial auction or when I introduce a level of service, take into account level of service, and also give a million dollars to my brother-in-law and also make sure that 30% of the carriers are women-owned businesses or whatever. So these are actual conditions that people are using in practice. And they usually use a combination of them. In all cases the solution will never be as good as running it with no conditions at all. When you introduce a constraint, the solution would be more expensive. But it means that it is worth for you. But you also know how much it is worth for you. By modeling it this way you know, for example, that having 25% minority carriers-- if you run the solution without any condition and then you run with 25% minority carriers, the difference in the value, the extra money that you have to pay for having 25% minority carriers is the extra that you pay for meeting this condition. So now you know how much this condition costs you. Now you know how much it may cost you to deal with high level of service carriers as opposed to any carrier. So all these introduce light and introduce more understanding into the process. You can model the level of service tradeoff explicitly. Do you want to have high cost, high service carrier? Do you want to have low cost, low service carrier? And finally combinatorial auctions that we did in the last segment can account for bidders' economics of scope. So you can take into account the actual economics of the supplier. Not only your economics are taken into account by the optimization, but you take into account the economics of the supplier who can give you better prices if they can be guaranteed that they are getting a combination of items that they're bidding on. So let's stop here. Thank you very much.

Hi, and welcome to the supply chain finance module. In this segment, we'll focus on components of the financial statements, specifically the income statement and the balance sheet. Now, the financial statements of the firm include many, many different types and documents. They include those that are presented to investors, those that are presented to the general public, and those that are used internally in the firm, generated many cases by the accounting department. Those are often used for helping the business to make decisions. The ones that we're going to be speaking about today are going to be the income statement and the balance sheet. These documents can be found in the 10k. They can be found in the annual report, and they can also be found-- or at least, components of that would be found in the statement of changes in financial position. Again, there are many reports. In this segment, we're focusing on the income statement and the balance sheet. Now, the income statement really lists all the flows of cash in and out of the firm, and it's quite simple. Because there are really three components of the income statement. We have revenues. That represents all the monies that come into the firm. You have expenses. Those are the monies that go out of the firm, and then you have profit. That's what's left over afterwards. Now, revenues goes by a number of different terms. You see them sometimes as gross sales, turnover proceeds. Expenses has many different components. The main ones that we'll talk about today will be the cost of goods sold and an operating expense. And then profit goes by a number of different terms, and of course, there are variations of different ways to think and look at profit. As we see listed here, sometimes it's called income. Another segment of that or one way to look at it is gross margin. There's gross profit, again, many of the others. EBIT and EBITDA, EBIT standing for Earnings Before Interest and Tax, and EBITDA standing for Earnings Before Interest Tax Depreciation and Amortization. You'll probably hear that if you're traveling around in the financial circles. The operating expense element that is most important that we will think about in the supply chain will be the cost of goods sold. This represents all the direct cost that contribute to the production of a particular product, which basically entails the materials and the labor that are used to produce the product. It does not include what are called indirect expenses, meaning expenses that cannot be directly attributed to the production of one particular product. These would be things like distribution costs or sales force costs. These are generally speaking not included in COGS, or Cost of Goods Sold. Now, there's a variation of cost of goods sold that's called the cost of revenue, and cost of revenue, it's slightly different. And the distinction here is that the cost of revenue includes those costs beyond the production of the product, beyond the materials and the labor, and includes distribution and marketing. You'll see it represented in various ways, but most of the time you'll see COGS. Another important element of the operating expense is what's called SG&A, or Sales General Administration. There's really two components there. Those are the sales expenses. Those are the expenses that are directly linked to the sale of the product, and of course, you could imagine that would be the cost of the sales personnel, the cost for the vehicle that they might be driving, the cost for travel expenses, the cost for them using a phone, cost for promotion, advertising, all things associated with sales. The other element of SG&A is general administration. All right, those are going to be the expenses that would be the managerial expenses or any kind of cost that the firm incurs that are not directly attributable to the production of the product or the sale and distribution of the product. This would include things like utilities or include cost for insurance, or the attorneys, or the finance department. That's what's included in SG&A. There's another group of expenses called other, and this can include a lot of different components, really depending on what's important within the firm. Sometimes you'll see it as depreciation. Sometimes you'll see amortization, and sometimes you'll see R&D. It's really up to the firm what they include in other, and how they represent that, and for that matter, how it's shown in the income statement. Depreciation very directly an expense that accounts for the use of a tangible asset. Amortization is accounting for the use of an intangible asset, particularly goodwill, and goodwill is nothing more than the monies that are spent in excess of the book value of a particular asset that the firm purchases. So if a firm pays $1 billion to acquire a company and its book value is $100 million, well, they just earned themselves $900 million worth of goodwill. We can talk about that at another time, but again, this is just another potential list of an expense. Let's look at an example or two to give you an idea of what an income statement might look like. Income statement for Intel 2014, starts with net revenue, top line. We often hear the term top line. It means the revenue. It's the monies that you bring in before any expenses. We see here it's $55 billion, and of course, the other components are just as we've spoke about. In their case, they're showing the cost of goods sold, the cost of sales, and you deduct the cost of sales. Remember you would get gross margin. Below that there are a series of other expenses that represent the operating expenses. In this case, they show R&D. They show marketing general administration, and then they include amortization. They don't show depreciation in this particular case. The result is you have an income before taxes, and then the bottom line-- what's left over at the end-- is their net income. We could see something similar for General Motors. In their case, they show the cost of revenue. Remember we talked about a different way to represent the cost of goods sold. This includes distribution costs, but here we see they list their expenses. And in their expenses they similarly include R&D. In their case, they have SG&A, but they don't list amortization and the depreciation. The second report that we wanted to talk about in this segment is the balance sheet. Now, in comparison with the income statement, the balance sheet reports the financial condition of the firm representing all the things that it owes-- that it owns rather, meaning things of valu-- and all that it owes, obligations to pay somebody, at one point in time. And there are really two categories. There's assets and liabilities. We can think about assets as something the firm owns, something that has value, and we can think about the liabilities as an obligation to pay someone. Or another way to think of it is, as represented here, a claim against the assets. Now, two things important to remember about balance sheet, that assets always equal liabilities. And then the data that is represented on the balance sheet is always representing the book value. It's whatever was paid for the particular item. It does not record the market value. Now, there are certain assets on the balance sheet that we in the supply chain are going to care a lot more about, and that's going to be things such as inventory. I just want to recognize that there are different ways of valuing inventory. One's called LIFO. One's called FIFO. We'll address that in another segment. Let's look at a balance sheet as an example. In this particular case, we see that the assets are listed on the top, and we see the liabilities listed on the bottom. Sometimes you'll see them listed next to each other, assets on one side. Then you'll see liabilities on the other side, kind of like the balance sheet. You want to make sure you have a balance. Something's wrong. Right side is in balance with the left. We see again assets on the top, liabilities on the bottom, and then at the bottom, let's look at this. And in Intel's particular case for 2014, they list $91 billion worth of assets. Well, guess what? The amount of liabilities is equal. Remember, assets always equals liabilities. And then we see also that there's a current assets and a current liability segment within each. Now, just as we were saying, let's talk about the assets and the particular components of the assets. There's really two parts of that. There's current assets, and then we have the long term assets. The current assets are those assets, those things of value, that the firm has that can be converted into cash within the next accounting period, usually considered a month for the purposes of paying against the current obligations. And that would include things as we see here, cash, accounts receivable, inventory, and then you have other smaller elements in there. But primarily it's cash, accounts receivable, and inventory. Longer term assets are those things that have long term value associated with the firm and are not readily converted into cash. That will include a factory that a company might own, or property, or big pieces of machinery and equipment. It also includes things like goodwill, which we mentioned earlier, or any other kinds of fixed assets equipment that the firm may have. On the other side of the assets is of course the liabilities, and these, as we mentioned, similarly, they have current liabilities. Then they have long term liabilities, but there's another element and that's called the equity. Similar to the assets, the current liabilities are obligations to be paid in the next accounting period. Of course, that would be accounts payable. You purchase something from a supplier. You need to pay them, and then you're going to usually have to pay them within the next period. And then there's a couple of other smaller items that would be included in the current liabilities. Long term liabilities includes the long term debt. You have a mortgage on your house. Well, similarly, firms have mortgages. They have long term loans that they have to pay, not due in the next period, but in the future periods. And then this other element in the liability side of the balance sheet is equity, and that really represents the ownership of the firm. And it starts off with paid in capital. This is cash received from organizations, individuals as initial investments in the firm, and then the value of the firm increases or decreases depending on the retained earnings that are added to or deducted from by the net income of the firm. And we'll talk about that connection in just a moment. Let's talk about supply chain transactions that affect both the income statement and the balance sheet. Let's assume a company sells a product for $10,000 in cash, and the product cost $2,000 to produce and deliver. How will this impact the income statement and the balance sheet? Well, let's look at the income statement impact first. Well, first, we'd see a revenue increase of $10,000, because the company has sold the product for $10,000. That is recognized as a sale. There's a cost of $2,000, and that's reflected in part of the cost of goods sold. And then the net, of course, of that is that you have $8,000. $10,000 minus the $2,000, and you get $8,000. Now, on the balance sheet, we see that the cash current asset would increase by $10,000, because the company sold the product but they got cash. If they sold the product and it was going to be paid next month, that would show up as a current asset of an accounts receivable. But in this case, it's cash, because we see the monies immediately. The inventory would reduce by $2,000, and the retained earnings would increase by $8,000. That's the connection between the income statement and the balance sheet. The change in the net income is reflected as the change in the retained earnings. Let's look at that as in an example using Amazon from 2014. Again, a change in the retained earnings from one balance sheet statement to another is equal to the net income over this time period between those two different time phases. And that is the connection between the income statement and the balance sheet.
