If the current ratio is greater than one, but less than two, this is often considered a healthy range for the current ratio. And if the current ratio is above two, this is considered a potentially unhealthy range, possibly suggesting that the business is using its working capital inefficiently. In this case, the business may have too much inventory on hand compared with what is necessary to serve the business. This, of course, depends on the business model and go to market approach. So the ratio is more of an indicator that warrants deeper analysis. Now a more stringent ratio is called the acid test. The acid test uses a slightly different formula for calculating the current ratio, including only cash, accounts receivable, and short term investments in the numerator. It does not include inventory. This essentially assesses the ability of the company to pay off its current liabilities by using only current assets that are cash or that can be quickly converted into cash. Because it would likely take some time to convert inventory into cash, inventory is not included as asset in the acid test. Working capital performance is also measured in terms of working capital turnover. This is another ratio which gives an indication of how the company is able to generate sales using the working capital. This ratio helps answer the question, how effectively is a company using its working capital to generate sales. We calculate working capital turnover by dividing the sales for a particular period of time by the working capital. In general, a higher working capital turnover is preferable because it means that the company is able to generate more sales with the same amount of working capital. We've established that working capital is important for the business for several reasons. Of course, it's an indicator of the efficiency of the business, but also an indicator of the financial health of a business. Basically, can they pay their bills. Well given that, let's go over some options for the company to consider for actively managing their working capital. One option is to collect the receivables quickly. This may seem like an obvious pursuit, but it carries additional weight when put in the context of proactively managing the working capital. Some firms are very aggressive in collections and manage them by creating terms and conditions to provide incentives for their customers to pay them on time and even early. It's not uncommon to hear of a term called 2% net 10 days. And that means that the company would give a 2% discount on the outstanding balance if the customer pays your bill within 10 days of receipt of the invoice. Another way to manage the working capital is to stretch the payables out as long as possible. But there is a risk to taking this approach because this could damage the relationship with suppliers. It may also affect the suppliers willingness to supply and their ability to respond to critical needs. And worse, it may damage their supplier's ability to operate. So if an organization intends to extend the payable cycle with its suppliers, it may be better to structure those arrangements in advance with the supplier so that the supplier is not surprised and put into a potentially problematic flow situation. Companies can also manage the inventories to lower levels, so long as it isn't enough to provide the service that the business needs to serve their customers. Avoid cutting inventory to reduce working capital requirements without first assessing the impact of those cuts on service level. Another alternative is to consider converting current liabilities into long term liabilities. Now this doesn't make those liabilities go away, but it does take the obligation out of the current time frame. The supply chain manager has the potential to impact a number of the components of working capital. And the first and most significant impact will come from managing the inventory, just as we've mentioned. As I just suggested, managing the inventory levels down makes sense but only in the proper context of the business situation and the business objective, as well as the supply chain strategy. For example, if a company competes on the basis of having a high service level, then it makes sense for the company to maintain high inventory levels as well as responsive supply chains. All the same, the supply chain manager is responsible for making sure that the inventory policy is consistent with the business date and for managing the inventory levels efficiently and effectively. The supply chain manager also has an impact on two other primary components. And those are accounts receivable and accounts payable. By working with suppliers and customers, maintaining working relationships with each, and by structuring the terms and conditions of sale with each, the supply chain manager can directly affect the cash flows coming from customers and flowing to suppliers. This slide shows a few selected ways that supply chain managers can impact working capital through different supply chain practices and policies, some of which we've just described. On the left hand side of the slide, we see a list of practices or potential actions. The column on the right list advantages and then disadvantages from using that practice or policy. In the first example, we see extended payment terms from suppliers. This means that the company would arrange to pay the suppliers longer than the standard payment term. That helps working capital by reducing the amount of cash required to pay the supplier in the near term. But the supplier will probably increase their pricing at some point in order to offset the higher cost that they have to serve your company. On the second line we see discount for early payment by customers. This helps working capital by increasing the cash component and reducing the accounts payable component. But the company will receive less than the full revenue from the sale. If the company finances raw material or work in process for suppliers, this will raise the working capital requirements because the company's likely financing the materials with short term debt. On the plus side, it will more likely assure supply from that small supplier. So we can see from some of these examples that there is an impact on working capital as well as an impact on operating business from these different practices. Well that makes sense. Business operations will impact working capital and vice versa. So it is helpful to think about these in advance and select the practices that work best for the company and the financing plans. Let's talk about working capital requirements. How much working capital is necessary to properly operate the business. Well, enough working capital is enough to serve the customers, to deal with some variation in cash flows, and to operate the business overall. Keep in mind that when we say operate the business, this means operating at the target service level and therefore maintaining the requisite inventory to deliver that service level. The company should be considerate of variations in cash flows and therefore one should understand the timing of the operational cash flows. Start from the time of purchasing materials that the company would use to produce and sell the product, consider the time from the purchase of materials to the time of payment to the suppliers, and then consider the time from the product sale to the time it takes for the customer to pay for the product. These three time-driven development are components of the cash cycle, which we will speak about in another segment. Let's talk about the key points. Working capital represents the resources that are utilized to operate the business and keep it functioning. Positive working capital is generally better than negative working capital, but it really depends on the business model of the company and of the timing of the cash flows to suppliers and from customers. Remember, it's possible to operate with negative working capital. The supply team manager plays a key role in managing working capital because the supply chain has control of inventory as well as influence on accounts receivable and accounts payable, two other important components of working capital. If you have any questions, comments, or suggestions, please use the discussion area and I look forward to working with you in another segment. Thank you.

[lecture_08_09_cash_to_cash_cycle]
Hi. Welcome to the supply chain finance module. In this segment, we will focus on the cash to cash cycle, also known as the cash conversion cycle. Our objective for this segment is to introduce you to the concept of the cash to cash cycle. I'll also explain the mechanics of how to calculate the cash to cash cycle, and we'll review some of the ways to interpret that cycle. The cash to cash cycle is known by several terms, including the cash conversion cycle, the cash cycle, and the operating cycle. It is a liquidity measure, meaning that it provides an indication of the ability of the company to pay its bills using the cash, or other assets that can be quickly converted into cash. The cash cycle expresses liquidity in terms of the amount of time, measured in days, that a company uses to sell its inventory, collect its receivables, and pay its accounts payable. In simplest terms, the cash conversion cycle is the amount of time that it takes a company to use cash to buy materials, make a product, sell the product, and then get paid by the customer. This is how much time the company's cash is tied up in the operation of the business. The shorter the total cash cycle, the less amount of time that cash is needed. This is a good thing, because that means that the company can convert its assets into cash more quickly, making it more capable of meeting its financial obligations. As I just mentioned, the cash conversion cycle considers the time it takes for three specific cash cycles. First, consider the days of inventory outstanding. This is the number of days that it takes for the company to convert inventory into a sale. Next is the days of sales outstanding. This is the number of days that it takes the company to get paid by the customer. Lastly is the days of payables outstanding. This is the number of days that it takes the company to pay its suppliers. We calculate the cash conversion cycle by adding the days of inventory outstanding to the days of sales outstanding, and then subtract in the days of payables outstanding. Basically, we add the amount of time that we need to use the money-- making inventory, selling the product, and waiting to get paid-- and then we deduct the amount of time we get to use suppliers' money-- that is, the accounts payable. Let's look at each of these elements to fully understand each. The days of inventory outstanding is calculated by dividing the average inventory by the cost of sales-- also known as cost of goods sold, or COGS-- and then multiplying that by 365. The DIO answers the question, how long does it take the company to convert the inventory into a sale. In the formula we use cost of sales, or COGS, because inventory is maintained on the assets at cost. And the cost of sales indicates how much was sold over the year. When you compare the cost of sales over an annual period with the amount of inventory on hand, you get an indication of how long that inventory was on hand before it was sold, hence the amount of time that the cash was tied up. Now which is better, a high DIO or a low DIO? And what are the pros and cons? In general, a lower DIO is better, but it varies by industry. It is best to understand the reasons for the high or the low DIO. However, a firm may choose to have a higher DIO, recognizing the need for a longer production cycle, and maybe a longer sales cycle for custom products, for example. This illustrates a few ways to calculate the DIO. Basically, the same components, but arranged in a slightly different way. The main thing to point out here is that the average inventory should be used. This will require looking at two different balance sheets to understand how the inventory may have varied over the sales time period. The days of payables outstanding is calculated by dividing the average accounts payable by the cost of sales, and then multiplying that by 365. The DPO answers the question, how long does it take the company to pay its suppliers. In the formula, we use the cost of sales, or COGS, because materials are recorded as inventory assets at cost. We look at the average accounts payable, because that tells us how much material was purchased, on average, over the time period. Just as we had to calculate the average of for the DIO calculation, we must similarly calculate the average accounts payable for the DPO. We ask again, which is better, a high DPO or low DPO? And what are the pros and cons? In general, it's better to have a higher, or a longer, DPO, because that means you're enjoying the benefit of holding the cash longer before you pay the supplier. But there is a downside to longer DPO, if this is damaging the relationship with the supplier. Or even worse, damaging the supplier's ability to perform by extending their payment to them. So one really needs to be considerate of the impact of extending payments on your suppliers, lest you force them into unappealing situations. The days of sales outstanding is calculated by dividing the average accounts receivable by the total sales, and then multiplying that by 365. The DSO answers the question, how long does it take the company to get paid by its customers after it makes a sale. In the formula, we use total sales, because in this case, we are referring to sold product, where the sale is recorded at the revenue, or sales value of the sale. We look at the average accounts receivable, because that tells us how much sales revenue was due, but not yet received. And just as we had to calculate the average accounts payable for the DPO calculation, we must similarly calculate the average accounts receivable for the DSO. We ask again, which is better, a high DSO or a low DSO? What are the pros and cons? In general, a low days of sales outstanding is better, because that means it takes your company fewer days to collect from customers. That means you'll get your cash faster, and makes your working capital more liquid. However, a high DSO might indicate that the firm is providing financial support to its suppliers, which might not be a bad thing if this is purposeful, and useful to lock in suppliers. Alternatively, it could mean that the company is selling to less credit worthy customers, in order to make their sales. We just don't know. Further research really is necessary to understand it. Let's look at a comparison of the cash conversion cycles in the automotive industry in 2014. I pulled the data that we see here from the annual reports of Toyota, General Motors, Tesla, and Honda. This data comes right off the income statement of the balance sheet, and you too can quickly conduct this kind of analysis. It's a very simple calculation that provides a lot of insight, and an interesting comparison across an industry. And perhaps raises lots of questions. Let's look at the calculations in particular, and let's start with days of inventory outstanding. DIO is calculated by taking the average inventory, dividing it by the cost of sales, and then multiplying it by 365. For Tesla, we calculate the average inventory by adding the starting inventory to the ending inventory, and dividing that by two. This means adding $340,355, plus $953,675, dividing, again, by two, which gives you the average of $647,015. We divide that average by the cost of sales, which is $2,316,685. Multiplying that by 365, to arrive at 101.9 days. You can go through each of these cases to calculate the DIO, the DSO, and the DPO, and the total the cash to cash cycle for each of these companies. This would be a good exercise, and give you some good practice in this. Looking at the data overall, we can see that firms have very different approaches that affects their cash conversion cycle, as well as their working capital. A primary distinction between these companies is that GM, Honda, and Toyota sell through a traditional franchise dealership model, so they are not selling direct to the consumer. Tesla operates with a company owned store model, and for some of their products, the customer will pay some money upfront, in advance of the actual delivery of the product. Most of the other automotive companies have financing arms that fund the purchase of the vehicle by the consumer. One would need to consider these figures and these facts in light of those distinctions. Overall, we can see that GM has a negative cash cycle, which means that they can sell a product and get paid before they have to pay their suppliers. Looking at their DPO, it's long by comparison with DSO and DIO, and their days of sales outstanding is very short. This may also tell us more about their relationship with their dealers, and that they are getting paid by the dealers quickly. Toyota, by comparison, has over a three month cash cycle, with a long days of sales outstanding. This may indicate that Toyota is giving their dealers a lot longer to pay for their purchases. Honda is more balanced all around, with each, DSO, DIO, and DPO taking around two months. 50 to 60 days. Tesla, the new automotive company, has an interesting model, based on these figures. It takes them a long time to sell a product, over 100 days, but they collect from customers in about two weeks. And they also extend payments to their suppliers to nearly three months. This may make sense for a very high-end product being sold direct to consumers. Let's look at the cash cycle graphically, which will give us a different perspective, considering the cycle along a timeline. The timeline starts where materials are purchased, raw materials or finished goods. And then that ownership is transferred to the purchaser. The DIO represents, therefore, the time between when raw material purchases are made and the time when the finished product is sold to the customer. Again, how long it takes to convert that material inventory into a sale. The next part is the days of sales outstanding. Recall that DSO represents how long it takes from the time you sell that product, to the time you can actually get paid. When we calculate to cash to cash cycle, we add the DSO and DIO together, because that's how long it is for the company, from the time they make a commitment to purchase material to the time they collect the payment from the sale of a finished product. But that's not the total time that the company needs to fund the operation, because from that total, we can deduct the time that the company has to pay the supplier. When the company pays the supplier, it's really the first time that the company needs to outlay cash for the production of a product. And so we subtract the DPO, or the days of payable outstanding, to arrive at our cash to cash cycle. What this tells us is that the cash to cash cycle provides some input into the timing of working capital requirements. If the company can adjust the timing of even one of these different events-- the timing of the sale, the terms of payment to supplier, or from the customer, the timing of the purchase of materials from the supplier. All of these, or each of these can have a meaningful impact on the amount of time that the company needs to fund its operation, as well as the amount of capital that is required. Let's talk about our key lessons. It's important that we understand the components of the cash conversion cycle. We should also recognize that these are important levers for the company to use to proactively manage how much financial resources are required to operate the business. Those of us in the supply chain can play a role in this, by actually understanding the components, and working to adjust them to the favor of the company-- by developing favorable terms with suppliers and with customers, which would affect the DPO and the DSO. And by reducing the sourcing and production cycle time, which would reduce the DIO. Finally, the cash conversion cycle can provide useful input to the timing of working capital requirements, adding a time dimension to the working capital requirements that was not there before. If you have any questions, comments, or suggestions, please use the discussion area, and I look forward to seeing you in future sessions. Thank you.

[lecture_09_00_introduction]
Our first lesson this week focuses on cash flows. We're going to talk a lot about cash flows this week. The different types of cash flows inside of a firm, the magnitude and the timing of cash flows. And most importantly, we'll learn how to describe our supply chain designs in terms of cash flows. So we'll start by introducing, again, revisiting the concept of a supply chain being comprised of three types of flows, physical, information, and financial flows. And we're really going to focus on these financial flows throughout this whole week because they form the basis of evaluating our supply chain designs. The decisions that are made inside of a firm regarding budgeting, where to put budgets, as well as the way that the firm describes how it's creating value for investors are all based on financial flows. And most specifically, what we'll get to toward the end of the lesson are free cash flows. These are the basis for evaluation. So no matter which metrics you use to come up with your designs or describe them within the supply chain organization, we will need to boil them down to free cash flows in order to compete for resources and budget with other groups inside the firm. So it's an important process. So we're going to get to free cash flows at the end. On the way we'll start with a brief review of corporate finance. For those of you familiar with corporate finance, it'll make sure that we're on the same page in terms of the terminology we're using. For those of you less comfortable with finance, it may help you focus on the most relevant concepts that we're going to cover this week and identify where you may want to bone up a bit more on those concepts. Then we'll dive into different types of cash flows. We have to first describe how we're going to estimate the cash flows in the future that are going to be based on the designs we're coming up with. And these are the projected cash flows. Then we'll learn how to distinguish which of the cash flows are specifically relevant to our initiatives, the designs that we're coming up with in our supply chain, and make sure we're only counting the ones that are relevant. And then we'll combine these into the free cash flows. And we have a framework and a structure for you to do this and show some examples of how to take supply chain designs and turn them into free cash flows. We'll close this lesson with a bit more discussion of working capital. Working capital is something that almost every one of our supply chain initiatives is going to have an impact on. And it's often difficult to think about how this capital becomes a cash flow. So we'll spend some time talking about that as well. All right, let's get started.

[lecture_09_01_financial_flows_and_evaluation]
We begin this lesson by considering the financial flows in our supply chain, and start to understand how we'll use financial flows to evaluate various supply chain design options. So, as we've seen before, the supply chain is comprised of three flows. There is the physical flow of goods that go from suppliers to our firm and that we then turn into finished goods that we supply to our customers. In return, our customers pay us for those finished goods, and then we pay suppliers in the form of money for the goods that they send us. To enable those physical and financial flows to happen, we also have information flows, or data, to communicate across the supply chain. So we've seen this before. We've also seen how the supply chain can be comprised of multiple firms. So instead of manufacturing our goods ourselves, we could contract it out to a contract manufacturer. Instead of running our own transportation fleet, we could also use a third-party logistics provider to do our warehousing and/or our transportation. And so the physical goods may actually not even flow inside of our firm at all. It may not enter our facilities. It may flow completely through third parties. However, the financial flow almost always occurs through our firm. The customers will pay us for the goods that are being provided, and we will then pay our suppliers, as well as the service providers that we contract, in our supply chain. Now, the design of the supply chain is not only about how we're going to make the physical flows move across organizations but the financial flows can happen differently as well. So, for example, I've added some different arrows here as an alternative to paying suppliers directly. We could instead pay more to our contract manufacturer and then have the contract manufacturer pay the suppliers. And the design of how these flows happen across the supply chain, as well as the timing of those flows, will have an impact on the value that we're creating and therefore on how valuable our supply chain designs are. So we'll be also considering the financial flows and the design of those in addition to the physical flows when we consider our supply chain. Beyond the flows between parties in our supply chain, we also have some flows inside of our company, inside of our firm, that we have to consider. And those are our investments we're making internally in our supply chain. So, first of all, before we make an investment, we have to have money, or capital. The primary source for capital for a firm is from its stockholders, or equity investors, and those stockholders purchase stock in our firm to give us capital. The firm may then contract out with debtors, banks, or bond holders, to raise additional capital to expand that pool of capital that can be used for investments. And there's a contraction contractual agreement that we'll pay back those debtors. For example, if you borrow money from a bank you'll have a loan contract where you have to pay them back in a certain period of time. There's nothing not a contractual agreement with our investors, our equity investors, but we hope we'll be able to make enough money to pay them back. How are we going to do that? Well, again, we're going to be taking this capital and investing it inside of our firm, inside of our operational assets. And those investments are things that the supply chain professionals in the organization will be involved in. We may not be deciding how to raise the capital-- down here, the allocation of debt and equity-- but we are definitely involved in determining how to invest it in the operational assets because we are the ones that are helping to design the supply chain where those assets are going to be deployed. In addition, we'll manage those assets and the partners in our supply chain to generate future cash flows. And, hopefully, by managing the supply chain well, our revenues will be greater than our expenses. And, therefore, we will make money. If we cover more than our expenses and the amount of money required to invest in our assets, we will make money. And if we make enough money we will then be able to fulfill the promise we've made to our equity investors in paying them a dividend. Now, the comparison to how well we're doing is really a comparison that our stockholders will make versus other options they have. So they could invest their money elsewhere, in financial assets, and then create a portfolio of options that will give them future returns. So their investment in our firm is a part of their portfolio that they're investing in. And, given our ability to provide the returns in the form of dividends to our investors, we will be attractive to investors or not compared with other assets they can make. So that's the basic proposition, is we're trying to do a good job managing our supply chain and our operations, which is the primary way that you create value in a firm. So when you look more broadly at the financial flows in the supply chain, and it's not just the flows we saw earlier that aligned with the physical flows, but also we have some internal flows inside of our firm in the form of operational assets. So those asset investments, we have the expenses we pay to our suppliers and service providers, we hopefully will get enough revenues to cover both of those. And, if we do that, we will then also have to pay some taxes. So these are the basic financial flows that will occur in the supply chain that we have to consider when making decisions about our designs. I want to give a few examples of some of the investments we can make inside the firm. And these are, again, components of our supply chain design. So, for example, we could add a new warehouse to be closer to our customers and provide them better service, and then hopefully drive more revenue. We could also invest in more inventory, put more stock on the shelf, give our customers more selection. And, therefore, they'll make more purchases. And, again, drive more revenue. We could add a production line and expand our manufacturing capacity, and produce more goods. And, again, drive more revenue. And you're seeing a theme here. We could also invest in a truck fleet and maybe provide better service than what our contract carriers could provide, or we can do it more efficiently. And, in this case, maybe not drive more revenue but reduce our costs by having our own fleet rather than contracting it out. So hopefully our investments will either drive more revenue or will reduce the ongoing expenses we have in our firm, and, therefore, allow us to make money, have positive cash flows. Our firm may be in a situation where it wants to reduce investments, where they may need capital and don't want to go raise it from debt holders. And, therefore, they want to reduce the investments that are required in the firm to free up capital. In that case, we may be doing some of the opposite of the investments. And that would be, instead of adding a warehouse, we'll close a warehouse. And, instead of adding inventory, we're going to reduce inventory. Instead of expanding our production and transportation, we may outsource it to other firms so that we don't even have to invest in assets at all. We don't have to own a fleet of trucks at all. We could outsource it to a transportation provider. Again, we'll pay them more in terms of expenses, but we won't have to have that operational investment in the assets. So there's a lot of decisions that are made whether we're going to invest or divest in assets in our firm. And then that raises the question, well, who decides which investments or divestments to make, and how do they do it? Well, who decides basically comes down to, who has the money? The corporate finance people are the managers of the money. They're the ones that raise the money, and therefore they determine how it's allocated. However, there's a nice quote from a very standard financial textbook used in corporate finance classes that emphasize that it's more than the finance specialists that are involved in these decisions, both in terms of not only in evaluating which ones are good, but also generating them. How do we come up with ideas for investment proposals? And this is the ongoing responsibility for all managers throughout the organization, not just the finance people. And so our ability to generate these supply chain designs is going to be key in coming up with good ideas for positive investments in the firm. But we will also need to understand how they will be evaluated. And that's what we're going to focus on during this week. I'll give you a little glimpse into how that evaluation process works as a pathway of where we're going. And that's how-- this answers the question, how do they decide what to invest in? There's usually a pretty standard process that corporate finance uses that begins by first estimating all of the relevant cash flows that are involved in this particular investment. Once we've estimated all of the cash flows, we calculate what's called a figure of merit to come up with a value of the particular investment over all. And then we compare that figure of merit, or that value, with some kind of an acceptance criterion. So if we look at this part of the larger diagram we saw earlier, the key to evaluating an investment is by starting by looking at all of these cash flows, here, and turning that into some kind of a figure of merit. And once we've combined them all together-- including the initial investment, here, that happens-- we combine all of these cash flows in the initial investment to come up with a figure of merit. And then we compare this with a criterion. And once we've compared it with that criterion we can make a decision. And that's usually a pretty transparent process inside many firms and that the corporate finance people will do in a process called capital budgeting. This process will also often be applied toward projects, which may not be an investment in assets-- physical assets-- but may require our firm to expend some money in terms of human resources, for example, to conduct a project. So your supply chain design may not require any physical assets, but it may require time from staff. And that decision of whether to allocate budget for that staffing and those human resources may follow a similar process in terms of evaluation. So to sum up this segment, I want to close with one more quote from that finance textbook that was mentioned earlier that describes this broader process and the importance of the different aspects. So the calculation of the figure of merit, which we mentioned was a second part of the evaluation process, requires an understanding of the time value of money and a basic understanding of algebra. And we'll talk about those in the next lesson. But it's often more difficult-- even though algebra may be tricky-- it's often more difficult to determine what are the relevant cash flows with a particular given investment. And that's what we'll focus on in this lesson one, here. We'll spend a lot of time talking about different cash flows in our supply chain, which ones are relevant, and how we assemble them together to create the basis for calculating our figure of merit, which is what we'll discuss in more detail in lesson two.

[lecture_09_02_quick_review_of_corporate_finance]
Before I dive into the specifics of determining the relevant cash flows, we're going to do a quick review of corporate finance to make sure we're using common terminology. And also, introduce the financial statements again to provide a framework for us to consolidate these cash flows. So the financial statements we're going to use going forward to consolidate all the relevant cash flows are the balance sheet and the income statement. You may be familiar with these, but I want to go through again quickly to make sure we're using the common terminology, and to also highlight which aspects of the balance sheet and income statement we're going to be considering. So the balance sheet is a snapshot of the firm's value at any given time. It consists of assets, liabilities, and then whatever's left over is the shareholders' equity or the net worth of the company. So in terms of assets, the things we're going to consider, at least in current assets that are current year, are going to be our supply chain designs that we're going to consider are the accounts receivable and the inventory. There are the things that our supply chain design will affect, and that will be consumed in the current year. These are current assets. We also have some things that will be used over several years, over future years, the property, plant, and equipment. We are going to consider things like warehouses or manufacturing equipment, trucks, and so forth. And we'll also have to consider the depreciation of those assets over time. So that's the primary assets we're going to look at. In terms of liabilities, there's again current and long term liabilities. And the ones that our supply chain design will mostly affect is how we pay our suppliers in terms of accounts payable. Finally, in terms of shareholders' equity, that's what's left over the end. We do want to understand retained earnings, because that's going to be the critical value that we're creating in our firm. But we'd like to break that down further. And that's what the income statement does for us. So the income statement describes the performance between those snapshots on the balance sheet. And it explains why retained earnings has changed over time. It breaks it down for us very nicely. And most of the components of the income statement are things we're going to consider in our supply chain design evaluations. That starts at the top line, the revenue or sales. We'll use the term revenue. And that's the amount that's net the markdowns or discounts we may provide to incentive the customers to buy things. We also then subtract that as a separate line item, the cost of the goods sold, the material costs, the direct labor costs. These are direct cost related to any particular product that we sell. And we will use the term cost of goods sold or COGS. Subtracting those gives us our gross income. We then subtract operating expenses that can't necessarily be attributed to one particular product, but are accumulated as operations across all products. And we'll use the term operating expenses. Although a lot of times this is referred to as selling, general, and administrative expenses, or SG&A. So we will definitely consider these operating expenses. And then subtracting the operating expenses from the gross income gives us our operating income. There's another flavor of operating income, and we'll distinguish them in the minute here. But when we take out the depreciation for the longer term assets that we're going to incorporate in our supply chains, we definitely want to incorporate that depreciation into our analysis because it'll affect our income. And therefore the cash flow is related to the income that we generate. Amortization is of non-tangible goods. So we probably won't have much non-tangible goods in our supply chain. So it's mostly depreciation. And then finally, there's things-- the interest, other non-operating expenses. Obviously we wouldn't include non-operating expenses. Interest is incorporated in a special way. And we will describe that in terms of evaluating the supply chain design that's incorporated in the discounted cash flow analysis that we'll talk about in the future. So it's directly a cash flow we're going to estimate. We're going to implicitly incorporate that in our analysis. We will consider the income taxes though because that is a cash flow that's relevant dependent on how well we perform up top. And then obviously we will ignore the extraordinary items. And that gives us our bottom line income. So those are the main components. Most of the income statement we're going to incorporate. Just again for terminology, to distinguish the two types of operating income, EBIT is this part that's earnings before interest and taxes. The interest and taxes below here. So EBIT subtracts out the earnings that come before that. And right above that is the earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization. So again, to distinguish those terms, we'll use the terms in a bit later on as well. Also, I want to quickly review the difference between short and long term expenses. The short term expenses are things that will be included in this year's income statement. At the operating expenses, as we mentioned, is the term we're going to use. The longer term investments are called capital expenditures, or CapEx. And this is for assets that have a useful life beyond this current year. Therefore, we wouldn't incorporate all of the costs as an operating expense for this year's income. So we instead report it on the balance sheet. But yet, we depreciate it over time. And that depreciation will affect our income statement as well. And there's also possibly a salvage value at the end that we may want to incorporate in any investments we make in capital expenditures. Now some expenses could be either a capital expenditure or an operating expense. A good example is information technology which often changes quickly, and therefore could actually only have a useful life of one year. So for example, some companies may replace computers every year. In that case, it may be appropriate to consider it an operating expense. And there is some choice that accountants can make in doing this, whether you capitalize an expense and make it a capital expenditure, or whether you can incorporate it as an operating expense. And this will change our income and our tax burden. And let me give you a little example why. So let's assume our firm-- including everything else except for our computer purchase-- makes $5,000 a year. And we have a $3,000 computer. So we're investing $3,000. So if we make it a capital expense, a CapEx, then we will depreciate that over three-- let's say it have a useful life of three years. We'll depreciate it over three years. And that gives us then an income of 4,000 each year. But if instead we wanted to make it an operating expense, then that 5,000 over the three years is the same. But our expenses are all incorporated in that first year. The full cost of the computer to give us a $2,000 profit. And then there's no cost in the future years to give us 5. So the total in the end ends up being the same $12,000, but how that accumulates in terms of our income or reporting, if we capitalize it, we show more income this first year and less in future years. But it also changes the tax burden because it we'll show more income here. So therefore, we'll pay more taxes. So that's how that decision affects the cash flows. And a firm will often have rules and practices for this. We won't get into the details of whether it should be a capital expense or an operating expense, because you'll have to check with the accountants in your firm for any particular situation. Finally, the other aspect we want to consider and make sure we're on the same page is working capital and how we consider working capital. So the general definition of working capital is current assets minus current liabilities. But the part we're going to really focus on is this cash to cash cycle, the accounts receivable, the inventory, and the accounts payable. These are the parts of the supply chain design that we really affect. The other parts of working capital are not things that we affect completely in our supply chain design. So it's good to remember that the working capital-- the requirements we have for working capital are actually an investment. We have to finance these things. So we'll finance the accounts receivable-- or AR is an abbreviation-- because we're basically enabling our customers to pay later. Therefore, we have to carry the capital to finance that. We also obviously have to have the capital for inventory in our firm before it's purchased. So we're investing in these things. We get some relief in terms of our working capital requirements from our suppliers who allow us to pay later in terms of the form of accounts payables, or AP, which is an abbreviation for accounts payable. And so the net working capital requirement is the accounts receivable plus the inventory minus the accounts payable. That's a term we'll use for working capital. And we're really not going to consider these other aspects when we're looking at working capital investments. Now our supply chain design will definitely be able to influence these requirements by enabling us to collect payment as quickly as possible by preserving our customers well. Obviously by keeping our stock levels as low as possible without hurting our revenues, we can improve our capital situation. And if we delay paying suppliers as long as possible, that will help things as well. So there are aspects of working capital that we will influence on our supply chain design. And we want to consider how we incorporate those investments in evaluating our design.

[lecture_09_03_projected_cash_flows]
OK, let's dive into cash flows themselves. And one of the aspects we're going to have to think about with cash flows is how we project them into the future. Why is that? Remember our investment valuation is considering how we're going to invest in assets to generate future cash flows. So, we need to then estimate what those cash flows will be in the future in order to determine if this investment is actually worthwhile. So, cash flows are money that comes in and out of our firm and it happens over time. It's a combination of cash inflows, which are considered positive inflows into our company, and cash outflows where we spend money and it's negative-- cash is leaving the firm and it's a negative number. Here's a list of the kinds of things that we will consider in our projected cash flows. And it might be good for you to take this list, pause the video for a moment, and think about whether each of these is an inflow into the firm or an outflow out of the firm. And maybe you could mark each one with a plus sign if it's a positive in inflow and a negative sign if it's an outflow. OK. Hopefully you had a chance to think about the different types of cash flows and consider whether they're inflows or outflows. We'll start with the first one. Original investment is going to be an expense-- we're spending money upfront to invest-- so money is leaving the firm and it will be an outflow. Increased revenues, which we hope will get a significant amount to offset the investments, will be inflows into our firm-- we're getting money into the firm so it's positive. Another kind of increase is we could have increased costs that go with the particular initiative. And an increase in cost means that more money is leaving the firm, and therefore it's an outflow. On the flip side, if we can reduce costs, which hopefully we can do with our supply chain designs, we can reduce the amount of cash that's leaving the firm, and therefore that's a net positive or an inflow into the firm. By avoiding spending we're having an inflow into the firm. If our initiative actually has a diminishing impact on revenues then there's less money coming into the firm and that's a negative cash flow. At the end of the project we may have some assets that remain and have some residual value and we can sell those assets, and therefore a salvage value of any assets at the end will give us a positive cash flow-- money coming into the firm. And on the flip side, at the end of the initiative we may have some close down costs, which would be an outflow. So, you can see there's a mixture of positive and negative cash flows and they happen over time. So, one way we draw these pictures, the positive cash flows being arrows upwards and the negative cash flows being arrows downwards, could look like this. So it's a series of cash flows-- positive and negative-- they happen at various times-- it looks a little bit messy. So, one thing we're going to have to do is group these or aggregate these cash flows into tidier buckets, and we need to define some time periods then. We're going to aggregate the cash flows, add up all the cash flows over a certain time period. Those time periods are often either monthly, quarterly, or annually-- on a yearly basis. And so given the nature of our supply chain designs are typically a little longer term and a lot of the capital budgeting that happens in the firm ends up being over a longer period of time or an annual budgeting process will often have buckets of annual time periods. And for most of the exercises in this week we'll consider annual time buckets. Now common practice assumes that the cash flows occur at the end of the projected period. So the income statement transactions, the things that happened throughout the year-- the revenues that come in, the material cost we spend, the COGS, the other operating expenses we have-- those would accumulate throughout the year-- throughout the time period. And therefore we would account for those, add them all up and account for them, at the end of the period or at the end of the year. Balance sheet transactions are a little different because they could happen more immediately. And so therefore you want to try to group them around the annual time bucket when they happen. And generally one of the things that you want to consider is that they could happen immediately or very soon to immediately after the project begins. So obviously an initial investment in some kind of assets might happen right away but we could also have, for example, a reduction in inventory. And that could happen very close to time zero because when we make our first purchase order we purchase less to replenish our inventory, and therefore we reduce that working capital right away. So, you can have some cash flows and period zero but generally we'll move them to the end of the time period in which they occur. So for our example here, those blue arrows could be grouped a little bit more tidily into time buckets that are these black arrows that are at the end of the time period, we add them together. And you can take out your ruler if you want, I think I measured them pretty well, so that the arrows should add up in a cumulative way that the two positive ones here add up to be this larger one. And the positive and the negative offset each other to give us a net of positive cash flow. And so there's offsetting positive and negative cash flows over time and we group them all into the end of the time period. So these two are grouped into this one and these three are all added together into this one. If we did this in terms of numbers, again, this is fairly representative-- I measured the arrows so hopefully the numbers should be right-- but if we had different cash flows over those periods we add them all up together and get our cumulative cash flows. And this is what we'll use for our projections-- an easier number that adds up all the various cash flows that happened throughout the period. The next question is how long should our time horizon be? How many periods should we include in our analysis? And this depends, oftentimes the decision makers will want to include the time period when there's the most dynamic periods of activity-- when things are changing-- and wrap it up with some kind of a steady state when the cash flows are stabilized in some way. However, if you go too long waiting for that steady state, the uncertainty in being able to project or estimate what those cash flows will be-- it increases over time, so we're less confident of the cash flows that are in the future. So you don't want it to be too long. There's no right answer and the decision makers involved in the particular decision will have to come to some agreement on this. But once you decide the time period you're going to have to figure out how to wrap the project up, or the initiative up. And there's two ways to do this. One is you actually consider that all of the activities end up in a salvage cash flow. So for example, if you have assets at the end you're going to sell those and have a positive cash flow of those sales. You're going to recover the working capital that you may have deployed in the project. Or you may have costs to close things down and you have to have those costs at the end. And you try to account for all the costs that would wrap up the project in a salvage value. The other option is to consider that the initiative you've implemented is something that will continue indefinitely and in such a way where you can estimate the value of the cash flows that comprise a perpetuity. And we'll talk more about what a perpetuity is later, but this is just a way of considering that the cash flows may happen indefinitely and we can we come up with a value to estimate that at the end of our time horizon. One final consideration is inflation. Our cash flows that happened initially in the first year may increase over time with inflation. And we have to think about how we incorporate that. There's two ways to do it. The most common way is people consider nominal cash flows, which is incorporating inflation in the prices and the cost directly. So for example, if you have a revenue and you in period one, two, and three-- let's say these are years-- and we have a 3% annual inflation, then our revenue in period one may be 100 but in period two would be 103 and in period 3 would be 106-- I think a little bit more than that, 106.1. So our revenues would keep going up nominally with the rate of inflation. Now some of the cash flows don't go up over time. So for example, if we had depreciation that would be maybe 10 in year one it would stay 10 throughout because if we have a straight line depreciation. So, there may be a mixture of different inflation rates. Some of them not having inflation at all for the different components. And your salaries may go up at different rates than your raw materials, et cetera. So it can get a little complicated to incorporate all these different inflation rates. To simplify that, you may take inflation out completely and use what we call real cash flows. The flip side is that some of these cash flows that do remain constant over time have to be-- depreciation being one, it's a flat line number-- have to be reduced according to the rate of inflation to make it equivalent. This gets a little complicated as well. And in the end, once you decide whether you're going to use nominal or real cash flows you'll have to use a discount rate in doing your analysis. And we'll describe more about that in the next lesson, but the discount rate has to match with the choice of real or nominal cash flows. You can have a real discount rate or a nominal discount rate. In general, I'm giving an introduction to the inflation but I'm also going to say to simplify our exercises we're going to ignore inflation. It's something you'd want to consider in a particular initiative, but to make them the concepts a little easier to understand and the math little easier to follow we're going to ignore inflation in the exercises that we conduct this week.

[lecture_09_04_relevant_cash_flows]
So we just discussed projected cash flows. We're now going to talk about relevant cash flows. What makes a cash flow relevant? Why is this important? Let's come back to our overall evaluation process for an investment or a project. And we noted that, in the project cash flows, we're talking about how to estimate the future cash flows or project them into the future. But we have to make sure also, in this first step, that all the cash flows we are projecting are relevant to the particular investment or project that we're evaluating. So we asked the question, what is a relevant cash flow? We have two principles to help us answer that question. The first principle seems pretty obvious. And that's the cash flow principle that only cash flows where money moves are relevant. So if there's a cash flow where money's not moving, it's not relevant. It kind of seems self-evident that, when there's a flow, there's money moving. But we'll see it can be difficult to assess that at times. The second principle is with or without. So we're only going to consider cash flows relevant if they are different with the investment than without the investment. Or if we're looking at a project, with the project or without the project, any cash flows that differ between those two are relevant and any of the ones that don't differ are not relevant. Let's talk more specifically about these principles. The cash flow principle-- again, money has to move to be counted. So it's either money that's going can be received by the firm or it's a cash inflow or paid out by the firm as a cash outflow. But if the money's not moving in or out of the firm, we don't count it. So some examples of things that are not cash flows are accrual accounting. Accrual accounting may register income when it's earned, when we make a sale. But cash hasn't moved yet. So it's not a cash flow until the cash moves. Some other costs that we will incorporate into our financial statements, like depreciation, are also not cash movements. There's not cash moving with depreciation. The cash related to depreciation all moves when we purchase the asset upfront. Depreciation is a cost where there's no cash movement in the future. In addition, we have to think about whether cash is moving in and out of the firm. So if there is internal budgeting transactions that are equal and it's just internal and there's not a change in cash flows, then that's not relevant. It's not a cash flow. So for example, a headcount transfer where somebody is removed from one group and added to another-- so say we are able to do our work more efficiently in our supply chain group and we can reduce headcount. If that person is then deployed to a different group in the firm, then there's no cash flow related. Because the salaries are still being paid by the firm, even though our internal budget may go down. The flip side is, if we are able to identify employees who are being freed up in another group and use them on our projects, we won't have to incorporate their salary in our project cash flows either. So that internal budgeting is all within the firm. Cash is not moving in or out of the firm. Therefore, it's not a relevant cash flow. The with or without principle is really focused on whether things are incremental and incremental to a particular project or investment, not the overall cash flows in our firm, the overall revenues or costs. And we're not talking about before the project or after the project. But actually, since we're always looking at projecting future cash flows, it's not before or after. But it's better to think about it as two worlds, with or without the project. So we have two directions. We're here in the present. If we're looking forward into the future, if we have time going forward, then we have a with scenario and a without scenario. And what we're looking at is, in the with scenario, what cash flows we have. In the without scenario, how are they different? And we're measuring the cash flows that are different between those two scenarios. And so, the incremental cash flows are the cash flows with the project minus the cash flows without the project. So we'll subtract these two, get this difference to determine the relevant cash flows. And therefore, because we're talking about with or without the project or the investment, obviously, the cash flows are going to be directly related to that project. And they're going to be incremental. Also this is an example of things that are not cash flows, future revenues or costs that will accrue regardless of whether we make this decision to invest or to implement a project. So it won't, again, be all the revenues in our firm but any incremental revenues from implementing a particular initiative. Things that are also not cash flows are overhead costs. Even though they may be operating expenses-- like rent and utilities or supervisor salaries-- if they don't change as a result of implementing the project, with the project, then they're not a cash flow. Even though we may think that by taking inventory out we won't have to use as much utilities in a warehouse, it's not necessarily the case that, with or without the project, we would change our utility consumption. Let's think about these principles in a few different cost categories to get more specific. We talked about depreciation earlier. And again, depreciation is related to an asset that's been purchased already. Therefore, the cash flow happened when we purchased the asset. The full cash flow for the cost of that asset happened earlier. But the accountants will depreciate this over time and incorporate them in our income statement over time. But there's not money moving. Even though we show it in the income statement, there's not actually money that's moving when those depreciation amounts are incorporated in the income statement. However, the depreciation will reduce our income on the income statement. And therefore, it will affect our taxes. We'll have less income as a result of that depreciation, therefore less taxes. And taxes are relevant cash flow. So it's important to incorporate depreciation into our calculations, because of the tax impact. But we'll have to add it back in and make sure that we don't account for the actual depreciation as a cash flow in our analysis. And we'll show you how to do that in a future segment. Another type of accounting cost that we have to consider is overhead. We just set that overhead costs are generally not going to be relevant. Because with or without, they may not change. And they may not be incremental. However, there are situations where overhead could be associated with the scale of the firm. And by adding a new product line, we may have to actually add some headcount to manage that from a marketing perspective or a product perspective. Or if we add equipment, we purchase new equipment, we may need to add some special expertise in terms of maintenance or add more maintenance time. And we can really directly link it to with or without. So you have to make sure the overhead expenses. Although in general, overhead may not be affected by with or without, there may be situations where, as we grow enough, we can identify a specific cash flow difference. Sunk cost is another kind of cost we have to think about. And it's tempting to include these, because it's money that's spent. You've spent the money. But we can't recover it. So it's spilled milk in one sense. And the sunk cost should be ignored. In other words, we don't cry over spilled milk. It happened in the past. We can't change what happened in the past. Therefore, we should only look to the future and the cash flows that happen in the future. This seems pretty obvious. But it can be tricky to implement. And there's an example I'm going to give you of a government contractor in the past. They were seeking a federal guarantee for a loan, because they wanted to continue developing a new major technology. They'd spent a lot of money developing this new technology and needed more money and more capital to keep the project going. And there were two arguments that were made in that situation. One is, it's foolish to abandon a project, because we've already spent $1 billion. We should keep going. Whereas, the other side is, we shouldn't continue a project that we can't get a decent return on. If we can't finish the project-- we've spent $1 billion-- we shouldn't continue it going forward. So which argument is correct? Hopefully, you realize that this is sunk cost and both of the perspectives are wrong. The sunk cost is irrelevant. And especially when you have a large amount of money-- like $1 billion-- it's tempting to incorporate that in your analysis. But it happened in the past. It's not a future cash flow. Therefore, it's not relevant. Cash isn't going to change with or without any future decisions. Opportunity costs are another tricky cost to consider in applying these principles. So an opportunity cost is any cash flow from an alternate option that we will forgo, because of our decision to invest or to implement a project. And we have to have a clear difference. So let's illustrate this with a couple examples. So let's say we have some land that we are planning to sell. That sale will proceed if we don't build on it. So we want to add a warehouse. There's some land that we own. We're deciding whether we build the warehouse or not. And the cash from that sale of the land that would happen if we don't build is an opportunity cost. Another type of opportunity cost is some capacity that may be idle currently, but it has a planned use. So maybe we have a factory line that's down. But somebody's planning to use in the future. Or some warehouse space that's free, but there's a planned use for it. So do we account for the cost of this space or not? It's idle space. Therefore, we think maybe we shouldn't incorporate that. But do we incorporate this opportunity cost or not? The answer is opportunity costs should be included in the evaluation. Again, thinking the with or without principle-- in terms of the land sale, if we implement our warehouse and build our warehouse, with the warehouse we will not sell the land. But without the warehouse-- because they're planning to proceed forward in selling the land-- we're going to forgo the sales revenue. So therefore, it would be relevant cost. Likewise, idle factory lines or warehouse space that has a planned use-- it may be idle now or may be empty warehouse space. But there was a plan to put a new product line either in the factory or in that space. If you forego that plan because of your decision to implement your project, then that opportunity cost of that plan is something we have to incorporate. It's a relevant cost, even though it's not something that's currently happening. The key in all this is there has to be a plan in place. It's something that you're going to clearly forgo this because you would proceed to sell the land or because you would plan to use the idle space. So the key is the intention. It has to be already planned and that we're now going to forgo this. One kind of opportunity cost that is a bit special is any capitol that we free up through our projects. That capital could be reinvested. And there could be an opportunity cost for those investments. But the capital-- the actual capital that we can free up-- is something that we don't incorporate in our analysis, because of the nature of how we do our discounted cash flow analysis. And we'll get to that in the next session. It's implicitly incorporated in how we do our analysis. Therefore, the capital the we are able to free up-- we don't consider any opportunity costs of investing that capital. Let's finish this segment by considering a specific example and seeing how we can determine what cash flows will be relevant. So it's a very simple scenario. We have a fictitious company named White Nile. They're an online retailer. And they are considering a new warehouse. They want to build a new warehouse to provide better service for their home delivery for their retail operations. It might be good now to stop the video and take out a piece of paper and write down some of the cash flows that you think would be relevant. And consider the supply chain design options that you've come across in this course thus far. And think about what kinds of relevant cash flows of costs and revenues and investments and so forth. What cash flows would be relevant in this scenario? OK, hopefully you had some time to take out a piece of paper and write down some ideas for relevant cash flows. And I'll walk through a few of them. You may have thought of some other ones. But I'll walk through a few of them and see if we came up with some of the same ideas. Well, the first one's probably pretty obvious. We're implementing a new warehouse. Therefore, with the project we have a warehouse. Without the project, we don't. So a new warehouse is clearly incremental. There's going to be cash spent. There's a cash flow happening. So it's a relevant cash flow. So you may have thought the warehouse. We also have to think about, there's also equipment that goes in the warehouse. And both of these things would be a capital expenditure. In addition, if you're thinking about better service, hopefully better service made you think that we can also generate some revenues from this. There should be a reason for adding a warehouse. And with better service, we should hopefully be able to serve our customers better, make them happier, and they'll buy more from us. So more revenue-- with the warehouse, with better service, we have to account for only the revenue that would be as a result of the better warehouse. So in the region where we provide better service, that's relevant revenue. In other regions served by other warehouses, that revenue would not be relevant. Because with or without the project, that stays the same. Also, in adding our warehouse, we have labor. Now, labor is an overhead expense. It's an operating expense. It's considered overhead. And we know that overhead can or cannot be considered relevant, depending on with or without. But this is a pretty clear case. We're adding a warehouse. With the warehouse, we need labor. Without the warehouse, we don't need that labor. So it's a pretty clear incremental change, in terms of overhead and labor for the warehouse in this situation. We also to fill the warehouse with inventory. And again, with the project, we have to add warehouse into this warehouse. Without the project, we don't have inventory. And again, we don't have information about the other warehouses or other inventory initiatives. But just for this warehouse, we have to consider the incremental inventory required to fill it up. And that would be working capital investments. And finally, one other thing you may have thought of is transportation cost. This is related to the design of our network. By having an additional warehouse, we can now-- in terms of delivering landed cost to our consumer, our customer-- we can have longer segments of our transportation in full loads to the warehouse. So we could use our own fleet, for example, fill our trucks up, and be very efficient. And then, we'll reduce the distance we're traveling from the warehouse to the customer, where it's going to be less efficient. Perhaps, we are hiring a third-party with small delivery vehicles to deliver. And that'll be a higher cost-per-mile. So we're reducing the distance for those less efficient shipments to the customer. And therefore, overall, we should lower our transportation costs, which is a lower operating expense. And it would be relevant because, with the warehouse we make this change, without, we don't. So hopefully, this example helps you think about what cash flows are relevant or not. We'll use this example again later on. And we'll put some numbers to it to see how this particular situation could be evaluated.

[lecture_09_05_free_cash_flows]
In this segment, we're going to consider another kind of cash flow, and that is a free cash flow. And we'll show how free cash flows help us pull everything together in evaluating our supply chain initiatives. So let's start by asking the question, what are free cash flows? This doesn't sound intuitive, because there's obviously no such thing as free cash. But we will show how cash is freed up by considering this overall investment process that we discussed earlier. So the value proposition of the firm is to invest money internally in its operational assets to generate future cash flows that exceed the investments and then free up cash. And that process-- at the end, we hopefully will free up enough cash to be attractive to our investors. So this will combine the relevant cash flows that are in red-- relevant to a particular initiative-- and it's also projected over time. We may have several periods. As we talked about, there may be several future periods of cash flows to do to consider that are projected in the future. So combining those projected and relevant cash flows and capturing the net of those over time in periods 1, 2, 3, et cetera, we get that pot of free cash flow that's created through this particular initiative. And we're trying to make sure that that cash that's freed up is attractive to our investors. And primarily, we're actually really considering our equity investors. Because the decision to take on debt is a separate decision we want to consider in a different way than we consider our investments and initiatives. So a definition here-- the free cash flows are the funds that are available to the investors, specifically the equity investors, after the firm invests its capital and pays taxes on the profits that come from the investments. So to consider how we're going to get to the free cash flows, we want to make sure we're capturing all of the relevant cash flows that are projected over time in our supply chain. So here again is a picture of our supply chain with the financial flows. And so the kinds of cash flows we want to consider over all are the revenues that come in from customers, the cost of goods sold for those revenues, the direct raw materials we pay to suppliers, the direct costs for those as well as direct costs inside of our firm in terms of labor and equipment et cetera that are dedicated to particular goods. We also have general operating expenses that happen across the firm to generate the revenues that we need to consider. The revenues minus all of our relevant expenses will give us another relevant cash flow, and that is the taxes we have to pay on the profits that come out of that. And finally, we also have investments in capital expenditures and the working capital requirements to run our firm. So these are the main categories of the relevant cash flows that we want to consider overall in our supply chain initiatives. And we'll see that several of these come from the income statement. And the bottom ones here are income statement. And the ones up here, the capital expenditures and the working capital, are from our balance sheet. And so in bringing all these different items together in our supply chain initiative, it's going to be helpful to use incremental financial statements to capture all these projected relevant cash flows. And incremental is important, because it's going to be, again, incremental to this particular project with or without. And we're going to look at it over time in a projected way. So let's start by looking at the incremental income statement. And we've seen this before in the earlier segment, the basic aspects of the income statement. And basically, everything on the income statement is going to be relevant except for interest expense, because that's related to that financing decision, which is something we don't want to mix in with our investment analysis. Obviously, we wouldn't want to consider non-operating expenses in our supply chain, and we wouldn't want to consider extraordinary items. But we still want to incorporate income taxes. So in keeping just these most relevant cash flows for our investment decision, we see a slightly simplified income statement. And we see that once we get our EBIT here at the bottom and subtract our income taxes based on that EBIT, we have a new term called NOPAT-- Net Operating Profit After Taxes. And that'll be a useful term in calculating our free cash flows. And another way of defining this is being the EBIT minus the taxes we pay on the EBIT, or 1 minus the tax rate times the EBIT. Now remember, depreciation, we've incorporated it here, but remember, it's not really a cash flow. It's not really relevant cash flow, because cash is not moving. It doesn't satisfy that first cash flow principle. Cash is not moving. We've needed to incorporate it here to get our income taxes correct, but we should take it back out, because it's not a cash flow. So the bottom line-- the relevant cash flows from the NOPAT, this new term we came up with, is our after tax income. And then we've added back in the depreciation that we used to calculate our income taxes correctly, but it's not really a cash flow. Moving on to the balance sheet, again, here's a picture of our supply chain. We've already accounted for revenues, operating expenses, and cost of goods sold as well as taxes on our income statement, and that leaves us with our capital expenditures and our working capital. And so we're going to look at the incremental capital expenditures and working capital from our particular initiative at hand. And for each period in the future projected forward in period t over time, we will calculate the capital expenditures and the net increase in working capital requirements. Combining the income statement and the balance sheet together, again, here's the income statement portion of the NOPAT plus the depreciation amortization. That's a positive cash flow, because it's income coming in. The balance sheet-- these are both stated in terms of cash outflows-- the expenditures that we're making in capital expense as well as the net increase in working capital requirements from our balance sheet. And using the reformulation of NOPAT relative to EBIT here and putting it all together, we see the formula for our free cash flows. Now, this will be calculated over several periods over time, free cash flows over several periods. And so it'll be helpful to program this formula into a spreadsheet and calculate it over the various periods we're going to consider. And we can see here again to get our NOPAT, it's our revenue minus COGS. So we have the signs over here-- minus cogs, minus operating expenses, minus depreciation amortization, then calculating our income tax based on the EBIT, subtracting that out to give us our NOPAT. Adding back in our depreciation, and then subtracting our balance sheet items, our incremental balance sheet items of capital expenditures and the working capital investments needed. The bottom line will give us our free cash flows that are again projected over time over these various periods. So these are the relevant projected cash flows pulled together into the term free cash flow. So to demonstrate this, let's go back to our example of White Nile. This is the online retailer we considered in the previous segment. They're adding a new warehouse to provide better home delivery service. And we've talked about some of the relevant cash flows-- that being obviously the new warehouse and the equipment that goes inside of it. That's the capital expenditure. We hopefully will get more sales from that better service in that region-- it's not going to be all of the revenue in our firm, but the incremental revenue that comes from the warehouse in that region. We're also going to have warehouse labor we talked about, but we may want to generalize this to be more of a warehouse operations. Labor is one part of our warehouse cost, but we may have utilities and other costs of running a warehouse. So we'll make this a more general term of warehouse operations costs. We talked about how we could lower transportation costs by having the warehouse and therefore consolidating loads into larger loads in bound.
