[lecture_09_07_key_points]
Let's take a moment to review some of the key points from this lesson. So the lesson talked a lot about cash flows, different types of cash flows, different timing of cash flows, different magnitudes of cash flows, and hopefully you're beginning to understand why it's important to be able to describe your supply chain designs in terms of cash flows. And the main reason is because the evaluation of any initiative we have in our supply chain is going to be based on the future free cash flows it creates. And this is for internal budgeting, and to get money for investments to undertake our initiatives. It's also important for communicating to our investors the value we're creating for them, so no matter which KPIs you're using to describe your supply chain performance, you should also be able to boil it down to future free cash flows. One of the difficult steps in doing this is determining which of the cash flows are relevant for our particular supply chain initiative, and we have fortunately two principles to use in guiding that. And the first is that cash must flow-- cash has to move, money has to move, in order for it to be relevant. And two, that it must be incremental regarding our project. With our initiative or without our initiative, we should be able to determine which of the incremental cash flows between those two worlds, with and without. We then will need to be able to consolidate those future cash flows that are relevant into free cash flows. And again, this is because the free cash flows are the basis for evaluation for our investors, for the investor value creation, and also business professionals are used to looking at these incremental income statements and balance sheet numbers, but also the free cash flow template we have effectively confounds or combines the various different line items in terms of magnitude and timing. So it gives us a structure to pull everything together. It's also important not to forget the balance sheet. Where really it's a lot easier to think about the income statement and the impact of our supply chain on revenues and expenses, but the balance sheet is critical because capital expenditures, while infrequent, are high magnitude, and because the working capital is almost always a part of our supply chain design, especially the inventory part, but also the payment terms of payables and receivables can be affected. And then being able to turn the net changes in that working capital requirement into cash flows is a bit tricky, but it can make or break our design initiative. And finally, this lesson is not meant to make you an expert in finance or accounting, but it enables you to start the discussions and engage with your colleagues in accounting and finance to make sure your numbers are correct. But the important thing is that you are defining and initiating the process to define the basis for the investment decisions and the budgeting decisions that are being made inside the firm. So we've finished with lesson one, this first step in investment evaluation. Lesson two will focus on these next steps of turning those relevant free cash flows into a figure of merit and comparing it with the criterion to make a decision. So in a nutshell, we've been able to take our supply chain designs, with both our physical and our financial flows, and take those financial flows and turn them into cash flows-- here at the bottom line is free cash flows. And the next lesson helps us turn those cash flows into decisions. All right, well, as always with these lessons, practice problems are really the best way to learn this and especially to understand the cash flows, which ones are relevant. Practice problems will really help you think through that. [lecture_09_08_introduction]
Welcome to lesson two in the second week of supply chain finance. We're going to talk about discounted cash flow analysis. In the last lesson, we talked about cash flows that happen in our supply chain. We're now going to analyze and then make decisions. So if you remember from the last lesson where we left off, we talked about investment evaluations, and we learned how to estimate the future cash flows and the relevant future cash flows. We now need to focus on how to calculate a figure of merit and compare it to an acceptance criterion in making a decision. So basically, we've taken our supply chain and turned that into cash flows here. And now we need to take those cash flow projections and turn those into a decision about our supply chain design. And we're using very standard financial techniques to do this. So we'll start by introducing a few figures of merit and acceptance criterion as illustrations, and they're ones that can be useful. But they don't incorporate something called the time value of money, the fact that today's money is worth more than money in the future. So we want to incorporate that by looking at a discount rate. We'll discount any future cash flows by a rate to determine what their value is today. That's the fundamental basis for the key figures of merit we'll use in discounting cash flow analysis, net present value, and internal rate of return. That's the fundamental approaches we'll use to analyze the cash flows. We'll also talk about terminal value. At the end of the projected cash flow horizon, there may be future cash flows that are still going to create value. And we'll talk about how to turn those into something called the terminal value and incorporate them in our evaluation. And finally, we'll look at inventory holding costs, which is an important parameter in designing supply chains, and we will make sure that we're incorporating all those inventories holding costs appropriately in our discounted cash flow analysis. There's a lot of material, so let's get moving.

[lecture_09_09_figures_of_merit_and_acceptance_criteria]
In this segment, we finally get to discuss figures of merit and acceptance criteria. And why do I say finally? Well, in considering our investment evaluation process, we've been focused entire lesson one on this first step of estimating the relevant cash flows. And that's a difficult step, but we now get to consider these figures of merit and acceptance criterion in order to make decisions about these investments. So what is a figure of merit? It is basically a single number that when you boil down the investment, in terms of its economic value, we can boil it down to one number. And then this number can be compared with the criterion that's established by the firm in order to make that decision. It's important to note here that this is established by the firm. This is something each individual firm will determine, based on its discussion with its investors. There's no global criterion that you would compare it against. In addition, there's several different options for figures of merit that we can use and then the associated criteria that go with them. So we're going to begin in this segment by considering a couple of simple back of the envelope options and then move on later in the lesson to consider some more advanced ones. In order to introduce these figures of merit, we're going to have an example here. And in this case, we've got an energy component manufacturer called Plata Solar. I don't know if you've noticed my naming conventions for companies. They're all based on river names that are not Amazon, because that one's already taken by a big company. So Plata Solar is considering new automation equipment to reduce its cost per setup in its manufacturing process by $1,000 for each setup. Plata currently makes 13 setups each year, the tax rate is 30%, and I would assume to use straight line depreciation in this example. So given what we learned in lesson one, you should be able to determine the relevant cash flows, and then we can move on and finally consider whether the investment is worthwhile or not. So why don't you maybe pause for moment, pull out your template on free cash flows, and determine the relevant cash flows for this example. And then we'll move forward. All right, so if you've taken this example and considered the relevant cash flows, you might have come up with these projections. So we have our equipment upfront. This automation equipment cost $30,000, so that shows up in our net capital expenditures. And it'll show up as an investment in this first year down here. We have 13 setups each year, each costing us $1,000, and we're saving that. We're reducing it per setup up of $1,000. So 13 times 1,000 gives us the $13,000. And it's recurring. Each year we have setups we're reducing over that three year period. There's no salvage value at the end. So we don't have any, you know, any salvage at the end of this process. We do have depreciation, though, of the equipment. And it depreciates down to 0. So it's $10,000 each year. And so that will affect our-- reduce our EBIT, therefore, reduce our taxes, which are relevant cash flow. And we get our NOPAT here at $2,100 each year. And we add back in that depreciation, because it's not a cash flow. So at the bottom line, we have return of $12,100 for each of the three years following our investment, and a quick back of the envelope assessment, at least we bring back over $36,000 on this $30,000 investment. It's at least worth considering. So let's keep these free cash flows here at the bottom and consider our first figure of merit. And that's the payback period. Payback period is fairly intuitive, and it's what it sounds like. It's the time until the cumulative cash flows pay back and are equal to our initial investment. So if we look at this example here, we can kind of guess that it's already, you know, $24,200 over the first two years, it's but yet $36,000 at the end of this year, so it's sometime between year two and three. And we're going to have to compare that with a criterion to see if the payback period is less than a certain cut-off point. So let's assume within Plata Solar that our payback cut-off is three years, and we can calculate the payback period. Since, so we get $12,100 each year, we can just divide the $30,000 investment by $12,100, and that gives us 2.48 years. Since 2.48 is less than 3, which is our criterion, we would say, this is a good investment. We can accept this investment. It would be a good investment based on the payback period. So, in considering this payback period, it's pretty straightforward to understand. It's very simple. It gives you a rough measure of the risk of the investment, because if things payback sooner, they're less risky. So if we reduce our cut-off, we'll take less riskier investments. So that could be something to consider. However, it doesn't consider the timing of the cash flows. So let's consider that we had in our firm, for the same $30,000 investment, there may have been some other options we could have done to invest in assets. And maybe they would have returned $24,200 in the first year, in this case here, and then $12,100 in the third year, or maybe that $24,200 came in the second year. Each of these would be equivalent to what we have in our automation equipment example, because we get $24,200 in the first two years and the same rate in the third year, so they will all be 2.48 years of the payback period. However, we would really prefer to get this cash up front, because we could reinvest it in other assets in our firm, or even put it in the bank and get some interest. So this is actually a preferred option among these three. In addition the payback period does not consider cash flows that happen after the payback period. So for example, if we had yet another investment of $30,000 that could have returned the $12,100 each year like ours-- our automation equipment-- but then had an additional $100,000 at the, beyond the payback period, that may be even more attractive than any of the other options presented here. And it also has the same payback period of 2.4 years, even though that investment may be much more attractive. So there are some weaknesses in the payback period, even though it's very simple to calculate. Another back of the envelope simple figure of merit is a return on assets. Return on assets is a very common ratio that's considered when doing financial analysis of a firm. And that's a simply taking the net income of a firm and dividing by its total assets. And the acceptance criterion here would be is if we should invest if the return on assets is greater than the target return. So our investors may be expecting 15% return on assets each year. So if we were then to calculate the net income, we would first take from our free cash flow template our EBIT minus the depreciation and taxes to give us the net income, which, in this case, ends up being the same as our NOPAT. So that net income, the numerator, is $2,100 each year. Our total assets will be our investment in the equipment, which is $30,000 upfront, but is depreciated over time, so as we depreciate it, those total assets go down. As a result, the return on assets the first year is 2,100 divided by 30,000 gives us 7%. But in the future years, as our assets are depreciated, our return on investments goes up. Averaging it over the three years, we get 12.8%, which, in this case, is less than 15%. So if our investors are expecting 15% return on assets, we would not invest in this equipment. So one weakness of the return on assets is that, if you remember, depreciation is not really a cash flow. So net income incorporates this, but we don't have an adjustment to take it back out, and it's not a relevant cash flow. So it's something we probably shouldn't consider in our investments. In addition, this return on assets figure of merit also suffers from the same issues with timing. And so let's give an example here. What if the timing of the cash flows changed? And this is the base example here on the right-- up top. What if we had the same magnitude in 39 setups, and the same net income as a result of $6,300 over the three years. But instead of having them even, 13 each year, we do 12, 12, and 15. So there's more setups in the third year. What happens is that means that our return on assets goes down significantly in the first two years but is very large in the third year, and, on average, is now 15%-- over 15%. So if we just adjusted the cash flows, this would actually say it's a good investment using the return on assets, even though we're actually postponing the returns to a much later time period, which, as we discussed earlier, we'd rather get cash earlier so we could reinvest it than to get it later. And, in fact, if you flip it around and put those 15 set-ups in the first year and our large cash flow initially, which is what we would in general prefer-- to have cash earlier-- our average return on assets goes down. So the return on assets also suffers from not considering the timing of cash flows. It is a positive figure of merit to consider, because it is very linked to the financial statements and the ratios that investors may be using to analyze our firm, but it doesn't consider cash flows. It's based on accounting calculations, for example, depreciation in this case. So it isn't really considering the relevant cash flows, and it doesn't consider the timing of cash flows. Notice that both of these figures of merit have the weakness of not considering the time of cash flows. So we're going to consider figures of merit going forward that do consider the timing of cash flows. And in the next segment, we'll talk about the time value of money.

[lecture_09_10_time_value_of_money_and_discount_rate]
In the last segment, we considered how some of the figures of merit that we might use do not consider the timing of cash flows very well. So in this segment, we'll talk a little bit more about the time value of money and why that's important and talk about a process called discounting and the discount rate to deal with that. So if we look at what is the time value of money, we have to start with a question saying, why is today's cash more valuable than tomorrow's cash. What is the value of money today versus tomorrow? And as we discussed in the last segment, one reason is that if we have cash today, we can invest it and get further returns. So there's an opportunity cost of waiting until tomorrow because we forgo those investment options. In addition, there is the impact of inflation that over time as our purchasing power goes down because of inflation, we would rather have money today and buy things today than wait until tomorrow when the prices go up. Finally, there's a risk component that receiving cash today is better because things may happen between now and tomorrow and the receipt of cash is not guaranteed. So we don't really formally consider inflation, as we mentioned, in the first lesson in these analyses. And we don't get as far as it really addressing risk formally in this week, but there are other important factors, although we will only consider the opportunity cost in determining the time value of money. So how do we consider this in our analysis? We're going to calculate what we call the present value of our future cash. So we're going to take tomorrow's cash and turn it into a present value. And therefore, cash flows that happen at different times in the future can be compared on the same basis of the present value. And we'll do that using a discount rate. And we'll introduce what that is. So, the present value. How do we think about the present value? Well, let's start by thinking about something maybe we may be more familiar with, and that is the future value. So FV stands for future value. And PV is present value. So if we have a present value of money and we invest it at a growth rate of r, then we take that present value times 1 plus r, and then we raise that to the exponent n, to determine the future value. This is a concept called compounding. So you may be familiar with this. For example, if you put money into a certificate of deposit at a bank, and they say they guarantee to pay you 5% return, then you would put 0.05 in for r, and you'd multiply your present value, the money you invest now, times 1.05 raised to the number of periods-- years in this case, if they're doing 5% annually-- the number of years in the future, and determine what you will get back on that certificate of deposit, depending on the duration of that certificate. So that's compounding. If we simply divide both sides by 1 plus r to the n power, we will-- it's simple algebra to come up with a different equation that helps us calculate our present value. That's taking our future values and dividing that by that 1 plus r to the n. This is a process called discounting. We're discounting the future cash flows into present values. And so the r now is, instead of a growth rate, it's considered a discount rate. Now it's important that you make sure that the time period you're using for the number of periods, as well as for the growth rate or the discount rate in the period, are using the same time unit. So typically, in all the analyses we'll do in this lesson we'll focus on annual periods and annual rates. So we see that with the present value formula, we now incorporate the magnitude of the future of the future cash flows as well as the timing of the future cash flows, the number of periods in the future. There's one other variable that really will affect the calculation of our present values, and that's this r, the discount rate. This will be an important assumption in calculating the present values to compare different options. And this rate is determined based on considering this fundamental investment process that we described earlier. So remember our debt investors and our equity investors give the firm money. We invest it and get future returns and then return it to them. So the debt investors have a certain rate that they have agreed upon with the firm, and it's a formal contract put in place that we will pay them back. For example, a loan at a certain rate over a period of time. The rate that the stockholders, or the equity investors, expect depends on what options they have in terms of investing in other financial assets. So they will consider the risk of investing in our firm versus other risks they have in their financial portfolio and then determine an appropriate rate. Combining these rates together helps us determine what is the appropriate discount rate for our firm because the discount rate is based on our investors' expected rate of return when they give us their money. An example of how to do this is something called the weighted average cost of capital, or WACC. And I'm not going to describe this in detail. But it basically balances this equity rate, which has a higher risk because they get paid after the debt holders, and the debt rate, which is going to be different and a little bit lower because it has lower risk for the debt holders because they're paid before the equity investors if there's any liquidation. So combining those together will give us a weighted average cost of capital. Now, one important note here. We mentioned before in our net operating profit after taxes, or NOPAT, that we didn't incorporate the interest expense when calculating our taxes. There is a benefit of paying interest because it reduces our tax burden and taxes are a relevant cash flow. But the way that it's suggested is by adjusting the debt rate by that tax amount. And so it separates the cash flows for financing from the cash flows of operations, which is what we're really focusing on when we're considering that investment in operational assets. So the WACC is one example, there are other ones. For example, a capital asset pricing model, CAPM. There's different approaches for determining the discount rate, and this will be set by finance. However, our role in supply chain is to, once the discount rate is given, make sure that we're giving a good return on the discount rate on the operational assets, that we're providing those future cash flows to give a proper rate of return. And if we do that, if we at least meet the discount rate of return, the expectation from the expected rate of return for our investors, then our stock price should remain constant. We're meeting their expectations. If instead, we exceed the expectations, our returns are higher than that rate, then more investors will want to invest in our company and the stock price will go up. Consequently, if we fall short and we don't provide that proper rate of return, then our investors might sell, our equity investors might sell their stock, and our stock price would go down. So our role in supply chain and providing an appropriate return on the operational assets really has a strong impact on the overall performance of the firm. And in some ways, this is why this is also called the hurdle rate. This hurdle rate of the discount rate sets the hurdle by which we have to run our operations to exceed the expectations of our investors. So we now have all the components that are in place in order to conduct our discounted cash flow analysis, which is really what we're going to focus on in this lesson. And we'll talk about that in the next segment.

We invest if the future value of our cash flows is greater than the investment up front. So the NPV is greater than zero. [lecture_09_12_internal_rate_of_return]
In this segment, we consider yet another figure of merit for investment decisions. I told you there were several. And this one is called the internal rate of return, or IRR. So to motivate the IRR-- which is not so easy to say, is it-- let's consider the previous segment we had, the net present value, when we showed how it's really useful because it considers the time value of money. It's based on relevant cash flows. And it quantifies the value being created. So these are some good attributes for a figure of merit. But it's dependent on picking the right discount rate. So instead, it might be nice if we could determine the range of rates that make the investment worthwhile. And therefore, if we can say that our investors' expectations safely fall within that range, then we feel comfortable moving forward with the investment. And that's exactly what we're trying to do with the internal rate of return, or IRR. So this figure of merit, IRR, is the discount rate that gives the net present value equal to 0. So it's the rate you'd plug-in to get an NPV equal to 0. Let's draw a little example here to illustrate this. So if we have our discount rate-- and it looks like this doughnut, doesn't it? Discount rate. You can't read my writing very well. But you know what I'm saying there, discount rate on the x-axis. And we have our NPV, NPV, net present value on the y-axis. For low discount rates, the net present value will be higher for a given set of cash flows. So the lower the rate, the higher the value of NPV. And it would go something like this, that it would go down over time, as long as we're making a traditional investment of up-front money and then future cash flows that are positive. So the point at which the net present value-- this is 0. So the point at which it crosses 0, this is our IRR, internal rate of return. Now, remember that for a net present value greater than 0, so for anything above 0, we would invest. So therefore, anything between a rate of 0 and IRR in this range, as long as our target rate of return is in that range, then we would invest in that. We would make that investment for the cash flows that, given their present value that's positive, between 0 and IRR. For any target rate that's above the IRR, we would have a negative net present value. Therefore, we would not invest. So this basically gives us our criteria for acceptance, given the IRR, or figure of merit. And that is, we should invest if the IRR is greater than the target rate of return. Or in other words, as long as the target rate of return falls between 0 and IRR, then we would make the investment and feel comfortable doing that. So to calculate this IRR-- I keep saying that. It's kind of tough to say-- we would set net present value equal to 0. So net present value is again this formula. We would plug IRR in as our variable and solve for IRR to figure out what rate gives us a net present value of 0. Now, this looks pretty complicated. And in fact, it is. To solve for IRR in this equation is not straightforward because there's no algebraic formula. There's no closed form equation, no algebraic formula, that we can use to determine IRR. We can't just plug values in, and it will give us a value of IRR. So it will try different values, different rates, and determine the one that actually gets closest to the net present value equal to 0. That's a future decision. So I'm going to assume we would make that in the future. And instead, assume that we would return back to the status quo. And therefore, inventory would go up if we did not further invest in more equipment. But let's see if that inventory reduction up front-- which reduces our upfront cost, but also-- 20,000 is less than the 30,000 before, but also reduces our future one. Is that earlier cash flow going to make a difference? So again, plugging in the NPV for 1.5: 15. 15% is the investor's expectations. Years one and two are the same. Year three is lower. But our upfront investment is also lower. And the result gives us-- let's hear a little drum roll-- a positive 1,052. So we would actually invest. But it's better to free up that $10,000 for three years. It really makes this initiative worthwhile.

So those longer term values we want to make sure we capture as well. So basically we're thinking about two time spans when we're doing our cash flow projections. We have through period t where we specify unique cash flows through period t. Anything beyond period t, we would have to assume that they are stable cash flows beyond that period into the future and then calculate a value of those stable cash flows. So basically you're defining a period t in which there are some unique aspects. Once you get to that point and they're stable, then we'll try to summarize all of those future cash flows with the terminal value. So the terminal value is also called residual value. It's the present value of all the cash flows that occur after the projected period or beyond period t. There are a couple questions. First of all, how long? There are two options to consider here. One is a perpetuity, which estimates the value of stable cash flows that continue indefinitely or infinitely. The other is estimating stable cash flows for a definite period time into the future. The second question, is how stable? We can consider a situation where those stable cash flows don't grow at all or they could be stable but growing in a linear way. Now we're not going to assume--the safer assumption in our calculations is to assume no growth and we're going to go with that. Trying to assume what the growth would be in the future requires more thought and so we're not going to focus on that here and we're going to take the more conservative approach and assume for all of our exercises, that there's no growth. But we do want to capture that value well into the future with a perpetuity or an annuity. So the question is, how do we calculate that value? So beginning with the perpetuity it's in some ways a concept because it's saying that you're going to theoretically receive cash forever and it's difficult to think about something going to infinity but in mathematics we do this often, we have what we call an infinite series. And in this case, it's an infinite series of a cash flow c that keeps happening in period one, two, all the way out to infinity. We can't even write this out because we can't write an infinite number of terms but it's a mathematical series that we can calculate easily the value and that is because it converges to the value of c over r. So the cash flow divided by the discount rate gives us the value of a series of cash flows that would happen forever into the future. So it's actually a lot simpler than you might think. An annuity, it's the same kind of thing, you're having a fixed sum c that comes each year but it's for a specified number of years. So in this case again you get this value c all the way through period t but then it stops, it doesn't go to infinity. To calculate that it's a little bit trickier but actually we can use the perpetuity to help do this, so if we assume that we have a perpetuity beginning in period one so basically if we say in the present value that in period one it begins and we have another one in period two the way out to infinity, that value is c over r. So that's starting at time zero, the present. If we had a perpetuity beginning starting at period t, so all the cash flows beyond t so we have t plus 1 and t plus 2, and so forth on to infinity, the value of that perpetuity at time t would also be c over r but the value in today's terms at time zero, would be c over are discounted by that period t to be the time zero. Now using those two assumptions we can calculate the value of annuity because an annuity is exactly all those cash flows that happen between now and t, so if we subtract this minus the discounted value here, we get the exact value of this annuity in between. We can also use a spreadsheet function The spreadsheet function present value pv you have to input the rate r, the number of periods, t, and the payment, c. And that will calculate the value of an annuity. It actually though calculates the value you would pay to purchase the annuity, so the present value of an annuity is actually a negative of the present value function. It's a bit of a quirky thing with a spreadsheet that it's used for a lot of calculations for retirement and so forth, where they're saying how much would you pay to buy an annuity? In this case we're looking to estimate the present value of the annuity so we have to do a negative value of that. So let's do a simple example. Let's assume c equals 10. The value of a perpetuity and let's assume we have a discount rate of 10%, actually let's say c equals 100 and we have a 10% discount rate. Our perpetuity value would be equal to 100 divided by 0.10 equals 1,000. Simple calculation. For an annuity, considering algebraically we could type it into Excel. And again we have our rate of 0.10, we have 10 periods in the future let's assume it's still a cash flow of 100. You'll see that that present value is a lot lower than it is for perpetuity because in paying out the annuity, you also utilize the capital and it goes down to 0. So you don't need as much capital up front to keep the payment going forever. So it's a lower present value than the value of a perpetuity. So let's apply this to White Nile. It's something worth pursuing but it's not a very strong proposition. Let's change now to assume that we're going to have the value continue in perpetuity. [lecture_09_14_inventory_holding_cost]
We close lesson 1 on supply chain cash flows by considering specifically the working capital cash flows in the supply chain. We'll close this lesson by also looking at working capital, and specifically inventory, and considering the concept of inventory holding cost, which is used extensively in designing the physical flows in our supply chain. We'll see how that maps to the financial flows of our discounted cash flow analysis. To begin, what are the potential costs of holding inventory? Well, first of all, there's the capital that's tied up in the inventory, and that's the opportunity cost of capital. Rather than buying inventory, we could utilize that capital to generate returns in other ways in our firm. There's also operating costs. When we have inventory, there's going to be inherently warehousing costs, special equipment that's needed, obviously labor to handle it and keep track of it, and perhaps even some direct costs if we have to dispose of things at the end or hire somebody to do that on our behalf to scrap the inventory. Finally, there's another category of relevant cash flows or costs that are related to holding inventory that deal with lost revenue. So inventory over time can become obsolete or it can depreciate in real market value-- not as a depreciation of a fixed asset, because this is not a fixed asset, but it can actually lose market value. In the future, some of our items may not be as valuable, because consumers may not pay as much for them. They may deteriorate, such as fresh goods or anything with a shelf life. There may be shrinkage from theft or from damage. And finally, there could even be costs of insuring against lost revenue. These costs on the bottom, the operating and lost revenue costs, we'll lump together and call non-capital holding costs. The inventory holding cost is often turned into rate-- a rate that's a percentage of the inventory cost of the value of the inventory. And that inventory holding cost rate is used in making many inventory management calculations. Examples include economic order quantity or setting safety stock. So using that inventory holding cost rate helps us design the physical flows in our supply chain. That rate is, again, as I mentioned before, a percentage of the inventory value. And there's no standard rate. It could be as low as 5% of the value of the inventory, or it could be as high as 50%. And it could vary by different products in your firm. For example, some of them may be more perishable than others or may lose value quicker than others. But a common rule of thumb that's used in the industry is 25% if you don't know any different. And why does this rate vary so much? Well, part of it is actually very easy to calculate-- the capital cost part of this, the rate of the capital cost, is also the discount rate. So the opportunity cost of capital is exactly our discount rate of our firm. But the non-capital costs-- those operating costs and especially the lost revenue potential-- these are all estimated. And so that value, that percent, can vary widely. This inventory holding cost rate is often used to calculate cost savings when you make an inventory reduction. So here's an example. If you were to reduce inventory by 100,000 and your holding cost is 25%, of which 10% of this is capital cost and 15% is non-capital costs-- operating and lost revenue-- you might see someone calculate the savings of that inventory reduction as being 25% or 0.25 times 100,000, and that's 25,000. The question is, how would we incorporate such a holding cost in our discounted cash flow analysis? Is all of that 25,000 when you calculate it using the inventory holding cost rate-- is it relevant? And does it happen one time, is it an annuity, or is it a perpetuity? Well, first of all, in terms of relevant inventory holding costs, the capital cost is not a cash flow that's relevant for discounted cash flow projections. That inventory reduction is captured fully in the net change in working capital. If we reduce inventory, we reduce our working capital requirements, and that captures the amount fully. And the opportunity cost of the capital of the returns that would come from raising that capital by reducing inventory upfront is implicitly included in the discount rate. However, the non-capital costs-- those operating and lost revenue costs-- are relevant and projected cash flows. They're things that you want to definitely incorporate when you're coming up with your projections. So effectively, when you reduce inventory, you get that working capital cash flow upfront to reinvest, and you avoid operating expenses or lost revenue in the future, and so you'll increase your net operating profit after taxes over time. The question is, how long will that non-capital inventory holding cost be in place? Is it one time, an annuity, or a perpetuity? Well, it's not one time, because as long as the inventory reduction remains in place, the non-capital cost savings will affect our incremental income statement in each of those periods. If the reason is temporary, such as remember in the Plata Solar example, there was an automation equipment that would have to be replaced-- the inventory level is low as long as long as that automation equipment is in place, but as soon as it goes away, then the inventory returns to the current level. In that situation, the non-capital cost savings are applied as long as that inventory reduction is in place and go away when the inventory returns. But if the inventory reduction is permanent and you make a policy change and set inventory levels differently for the future as long as you can foresee and there's no reason they would go away, then that upfront working capital, that cash that's freed up, it implicitly generates returns in perpetuity. It will continue to be available to generate returns at the discount rate. And if that's the case, then the non-capital costs should also be applied in perpetuity, because as long as the capital is freed up and the inventory reduction remains in place, then those non-capital costs are also being avoided. Let's reconsider our example for Yellow River. Remember, this is a long-term policy change where the SKU, the Stock Keeping Unit, is taken away, because it's a slow-moving product. And we see that-- remember that the revenue goes down, and therefore, the cogs are reduced. But the net effect is we're going to have less profits, but we're reducing our working capital by a significant amount. And if we projected this in our free cash flows, we see that our net operating profits after taxes are down by 26,000 each year, but we get 240,000 in capital freed up initially. Is this a good initiative? Well, looking at it over three years, it's a very good initiative. Because you can see, even if you discounted these future cash flows, they would be somewhere around 60,000, 70,000. That's definitely far less than the 240,000 we get up front. But remember, this is going to be in perpetuity, because you reduce the Stock Keeping Unit now and it won't come back. Therefore, all of the sales for that particular product are going to be gone in perpetuity. So instead, you need to apply it this way. And then assuming our discount rate of 10%, that perpetuity of lost profits or less profits becomes a terminal value of negative 255,000. Adding that at the end of our period t-- period 3, in this case-- we can see that there's a much bigger negative impact over the long term from reducing the Stock Keeping Unit. Now let's see if it's going to more than offset the cash we get upfront. We now need to calculate net present value to determine if that's the case. You might want to pause and do that really quickly. OK. So the net present value-- if you type in 10% of the cash flows that are here in the future, and then we add in-- instead of subtract out, we're getting money up front-- we add in the 240,000 at the end. That gives us a net present value of negative 15,000. So in that case, we wouldn't invest. It doesn't pass the net present value test. If you did the IRR of the whole set of cash flows there, you'd get a value of 12.57%, which is greater than 10%. Which if we applied the rule from before, the IRR being above the discount rate says we should invest. So what's going on here? Well, actually-- and I note at the bottom here, if you didn't round off and use these numbers on the template here and you had a spreadsheet that was calculated in the gross margin exactly, these are rounded numbers. You might get these numbers down below. So what's really going on here is that the previous criterion was used for investments, and we're not making an investment. In fact, we're getting money upfront. We're divesting to get the 240,000 upfront. We're divesting from this Stock Keeping Unit to get money upfront. So while the MPV still calculates the value correctly, the IRR gives us a different interpretation. Because effectively, we're raising capital and paying it back with less profits in the future. So it's not good to raise capital at 12% if our discount rate is 10%. If we're normally raising capital with an expectation of getting 10% returns, we shouldn't raise capital at 12%. And you can see this by the curve. Before, our investment curves all looked like this, where the lower the discount rate, the higher the MPV, because we could more than exceed the ability to raise capital at that rate. But in this case, since we're raising capital, if we can get money at 5% or even 10%, that's better than if we generate capital by reducing the Stock Keeping Unit, which is effectively giving us money at 12%. So it's a little different when you're not making an investment to apply the IRR rule. However, in both situations, net present value being above or below 0 gives us the right decision of whether to act or, in this case, not to act. Now, we are missing one component. Remember, we have those non-capital costs that aren't explicitly accounted for here. We have considered the lost profits, but we have some savings. And so let's assume our non-capital holding costs are even a small rate of 5%. Before, we had an example at 15%. Let's just assume a 5% non-capital cost of holding, those warehousing costs, the lost revenue potential that comes from that. Then applying that, we see the 180,000 of inventory times 5% reduces our operating expenses by 9,000. Now our reduction in profit is only 19-- or actually, if we see, I added a decimal point down here-- 19.3000. So instead of 25,000 26,000 less profits each year, it's less. Therefore, our perpetuity now becomes 192,000, and therefore, the evaluation cash flow is not quite as negative. And if we calculate the net present value, it now becomes positive. And it says in this case, it is worth acting. When we incorporate those non-capital inventory holding costs to the capital we free up upfront and in general the capital for not only the inventory, but also the receivables and payables here. This ends up being a good way to generate money for our firm. And in fact, we are raising capital effectively at a 1.55% rate, which is really good compared to the 10% that we are promising to our investors now. So you can see the perpetuity helps us calculate the long-term impact of certain initiatives, such as a SKU reduction or inventory reduction. And it also-- we see an example of how sometimes inventory reductions that free up capital upfront, the net present value still calculates our value correctly, but the IRR is interpreted a little bit differently.

[lecture_09_15_key_points]
So I know this has been a relatively long lesson. I've introduced you to a lot of financial concepts and tools. But I'm hoping that these will be important for you in your supply chain professional practice, because they really help you to capture the value and analyze the value of the different supply chain designs you're coming up with. Let's go over a few key points to help pull that knowledge together. First of all, this lesson focused on how to assess the cash flows you came up with based on knowledge from the previous lesson and determine figures of merit to guide your decisions, whether it's investments or other supply chain designs that may include cost reductions or capital reductions. And the fundamental value we use, the fundamental figure of merit, is the net present value. It tells us the exact value of the initiative based on the cash flows we've projected for a given discount rate. Now, the weakness, the one weakness of net present value, is that it considers a given discount rate and given cash flows. So to mitigate the risk of picking the wrong discount rate, we use IRR, Internal Rate of Return, which tells us the range of discount rates that give us a net present value that's positive and, therefore, a good decision. And the wider that range, and especially the further it is from our current assumption around a discount rate or the return expected by investors, the more robust that decision is. In addition, we've assumed that all of our cash flows are deterministic. And they may not be. So the payback period, even though it doesn't incorporate time value of money, does give you a sense of how risky a project is, because if you get your cash back sooner, you don't face the risk of the future not playing out like you planned. And by using these analysis approaches, you're really evaluating decisions based on the approaches of modern business, that these techniques grew in popularity from 4% in the 1950s to over 90% in the late 1980s. So you've now learned the techniques that are used by almost every business in making decisions. And in fact, a very common financial textbook says that it's not an exaggeration to say that these discounted cash flow analysis approaches that you've learned are the backbone of modern finance and even modern business. Modern business decisions are really driven by appropriate discounted cash flow analysis. And the reason that's the case is because discounted cash flow analysis actually quantifies the value we're creating for our investors based on making good investments, based on determining appropriate capital reductions, even if they are offset by negative cash flows in the future, and by assessing the value created by cost savings. And importantly, it allows us to combine all of these things together into one projection and calculate the value based on net present value. It also helps us to really capture the full value of inventory reductions. We saw in the last segment there are some weaknesses in applying an inventory holding cost to calculate savings. But by using discounted cash flows, we can really understand the true value of the capital we're freeing up to invest. And in doing so, we're really using the IRR, internal rate of return, to determine the effective rate that we're generating money at for our firm. So we're freeing up capital. And that capital acquisition-- it's not really acquiring it, but we're freeing it up in our firm-- if it's lower than our cost of capital, that means that's a better way to generate money than to go out to investors or to banks or equity investors to further put money into the company. So that inventory reduction that frees up capital, the IRR and net present value, these discounted cash flow techniques, really help us estimate the true value of that capital reduction. And also, in addition to the capital reduction, there can be a perpetuity of non-capital costs, operating expenses that hit the income statement and increase our net operating profits, because we have less cost. So make sure to capture all of these values when you're making an inventory reduction. Now, there are some key aspects of discounted cash flow analysis we didn't cover. We talked about inflation not being something that we incorporated directly. That is an important thing to consider. And you need to understand that nominal cash flows need to go with nominal rates, and real cash flows with real rates. But you'll need to look elsewhere to see exactly how to apply that. We didn't have time to cover it. In addition, we didn't talk about perpetual growth. We talked about the importance of perpetuities. But we assumed they were going to be flat. Perpetual growth has another set of assumptions you'll have to be careful in making. But it could create more value for your initiative. A big thing we didn't incorporate is risk. We assumed everything is deterministic-- all the cash flows are exactly what we say they're going to be into the future. And there is definitely a whole lesson's worth of material on how to effectively incorporate risk in discounted cash flow analysis. Finally, we focused on evaluating one initiative or one investment. But when you're having several projects, and especially if capital is limited, which it often is, the ranking approaches and the determination of how you ration that capital to different projects merits a lot more teaching, because there's a lot of different aspects you need to incorporate. In addition if you have mutually exclusive options-- if you can do a or b, but not both, especially if they have different time horizons-- there's also some additional considerations you want to make when applying discounted cash flow analysis. So we didn't cover them here. You may need to look elsewhere for some of those specific aspects if you're going further with this knowledge. But I wanted to tie this back to the supply chain design that we've been teaching throughout this whole course, and that the objective functions often used in supply chain design don't properly quantify the value created. They're really good at capturing revenues and costs, whether it's cost of goods sold or a sell in general or administrative expenses. But oftentimes, they ignore taxes, including them important tax shield that comes from depreciation of fixed assets. And they often ignore the time value of money, which is usually a little more complicated to incorporate those into the design approaches. And so just realize that we're not not necessarily capturing all the relevant cash flows and not incorporating the time value of money. In addition, they often don't capture the balance sheet very well. It'll mix things like a full fixed cost of capital exponential, like a warehouse, with short-term costs and revenues in the income statement into one value. And also, the inventory holding cost, as we saw in the last segment, oftentimes savings from an inventory reduction, is determined by calculating-- of applying the inventory holding cost rate to the inventory reduced. And we saw that it's better to capture that with more clear assumptions than discounted cash flow analysis to capture all of the relevant cash flows and the perpetuity of benefits. However, these objective functions may lead to very appropriate designs. They're there for a reason. They're appropriate for designing the supply chain. Just at the end, once you've done your design, go back and use your discounted cash flow analysis approach, as the knowledge you have now to quantify the real value that's being created for a firm's investors. And therefore, you get credit for all of the value you're creating through your design efforts. So in practicing these, obviously there's practice problems. And using the discussion, it's very important to get a lot of practice, because these techniques are fairly new and we haven't covered them extensively in the rest of the course. And we're not going to visit them again. So definitely practice doing this. This can be, hopefully, a very useful part of your professional career going forward, because now you have the ability to count all of the huge pile of cash that you're creating in your supply chain designs. Good luck.

[lecture_10_00_defining_supply_chain_finance]
Hi, this is Jim Rice. Welcome to the supply chain finance module. This lesson, we're talking about defining supply chain finance. Our objective here is to introduce you to the concept of supply chain finance, and define the difference between supply chain financial analysis and supply chain finance. We will also review the principles that made this an emerging and important issue for organizations. And as often is the case, we're not intending to make you supply chain financing experts in this particular content. So supply chain finance, I would argue, has taken on a new meaning. And up to this point supply chain finance, that term has been used as a very broad term. It basically means what we think of as a supply chain. We think about it if we looked at it through a financial lens. So what are the financial impacts coming from a supply chain transaction like the sale of inventory, or the investment in a certain supply chain capability? What is the financial performance impact of some of these different transactions? That's how it's been used, very broadly speaking. But now it has a very specific meaning, this term supply chain financing. It refers to the method to provide liquidity to buyers and sellers. It's not new. Payment terms such as net 30, or 2%, 10 net 30 have existed. I'll explain that in a subsequent segment. The concept is not new but the term is new. And the extent to which it's being used is new, also. We've got to understand what liquidity is. And I'll use the definition borrowed from our colleagues over at Investopedia. They say liquidity is the degree to which an asset or security can be quickly bought or sold in the market without affecting the assets price. Well, I like to think about that as basically the ability for the organization to take some of their assets-- in particular, an asset would be a receivable that they have from a buyer which has an obligation to pay them-- basically to convert that quickly into cash. Now other assets they may want to convert into cash would include inventory and other materials that they might have. Liquidity is really important because you need cash to operate your business. And this was an issue-- has become an issue now-- because as a result of the global financial crisis of 2008, credit is just not available to organizations in the same way as it was previously. It's not just coming at a higher cost, sometimes the credit, the availability of cash, is just not available. And so organizations are looking for different ways to reduce the amount of capital that they need-- the cash or working capital-- that they need. One way for buyers to do that is extend their payments. And those payment terms have been extended from three to 30 days up to 150 days, pretty long time. And so what that means is that suppliers have to operate during that time frame, up to 150 days, without getting paid by that particular customer. Well, that means they need cash, cash to operate, and again that's why liquidity is important. That leads us up to supply chain finance, so better understand it. And it's the set of solutions that allow a company to finance its own working capital. This is a definition offered by my colleagues at Polytechnic [INAUDIBLE]. And an important point to note here is that it often leverages the role of supply chain relationships with other plays and supply chain. Basically if a small supplier collaborates with a larger customer, and the larger customer can make an arrangement with the bank-- to say, hey, you know what? I have a supplier who needs cash. Can you help them out? And basically trade on the credit worthiness of the larger customer that may have better credit terms. Well, then that small supplier gets the benefit out of that. There are many different benefits. And just to be clear, when we talk about financing, we're talking about the-- according to our friends at Investopedia-- the act of providing funds, the funds for activities, whether they're making purchases or investing. And this often involves third parties. And then supply chains are financing or funding their operations, specifically funding their payables, and sometimes they're funding their inventory. Again, to allow them to operate. Because you've got to have cash to pay your suppliers, to pay your utilities, to pay your employees. Liquidity in a cash conversion cycle-- worthwhile talking a little bit about the cash conversion cycle. It's one way to look at the requirement for a working capital, and how that capital is utilized across three different ways it's used. It's used to pay suppliers. It's used by customers when they're paying their suppliers. And of course, it's used to invest in materials, and labor, and salespeople to produce and sell a product. So when we think about a cash conversion cycle, we think about the time it takes from the time that organization puts one unit of money in as an investment, to when they finally get that investment returned-- and usually with the sale of product. And that cycle time is called the cash conversion cycle. For organizations to reduce the time it takes for them to get that money back, they take a variety of different actions that could include reducing the cycle time for producing a product, selling it more quickly, slowing down payments to suppliers that reduce the working capital, but also arranging for faster payments from customers. Let me give you an example that might be a helpful illustration here. We see a number of companies in the consumer products industry from 2016. All this data is from their annual reports. We've broken out their cash conversion cycle into the days of inventory outstanding, the days of sales outstanding, the days of payables outstanding. Many of these organizations, we can see here, are operating at what's called negative cash conversion cycle. And what that means is they're using OPM, or other people's money, to fund their operations. So let's look at the case of Nestle. They're paying in 147 days. So it takes 147 days for them to pay their suppliers. Well, they're using that money to fund their operation. And let's just look at this-- the days of inventory outstanding. That's basically the time it takes for a company to produce a product and then to sell it. And then maybe that takes them 68 days, so they're using 68 of those 147 days of their payments, they're using them for that. Which basically means, overall, that Nestle has been sitting on somebody else's money for almost 30 days. Pretty good deal. But you can see a look at Kimberly-Clark, they have to fund their operation for upwards of 19 days. So that's actually pretty good, but still they need to have cash to operate for 19 days. If you do this for other organizations you'll see a variety of different ranges but this gives you a little bit of an idea of how the cash is used when people-- one way to look at working capital and how it's employed by organizations. Key points here, that payment terms have increased. Credit has become more difficult, which makes it much more difficult for suppliers to operate with these long payment cycles. Therefore, they have the need to finance some of these assets that they have-- inventory, or receivable-- in order for them to actually operate, to fund their business, to pay their bills. Now supply chain finance is a specific term that is now being used to describe that process where firms reach out to finance their operations. And the cash conversion cycle provides a useful illustration for how different companies can use capital. If you have any questions, comments, or suggestions, please use the discussion forum. I look forward to seeing you in a future segment. Thank you.

[lecture_10_01_supply_chain_finance_basics]
Hi, this is Jim Rice. And welcome to this segment of the supply chain finance module. We're focused on supply chain finance basics. This segment we're going to be focused specifically on explaining how the supply chain finance works. And I'll use one example to illustrate-- it'll be one of the most common methods-- it's called factoring. And there are a couple of variations there, we'll talk right about that. Now there are two options for financing. Generally speaking, not just about supply chains-- generally speaking, two options for financing-- debt and using it. And you can use debt or equity. Debt is basically a loan. Equities are basically when you sell part of whatever you have to raise cash. And when we apply these options to the supply chain most of the time the firms are using debt or loans to provide that liquidity, or the cash to the firm. Sometimes it doesn't entail the sale of an asset-- and the asset in most cases are going to be a receivable from the buyer, that is an obligation to pay from the buyer, and usually that occurs at a deep discount. In this case, example, I'm going to go over today, is going to be factoring. And that's where they are selling the receivable. Again there are many variations and each has its own advantages and disadvantages. Let's just go over basically how supply chain works. Take the case of a raw material purchase. And it all starts when the customer places an order on the supplier. And the supplier then goes through the process of buying the raw materials, producing the product, and then ultimately they deliver-- at time t equals zero. Well, at time t equals zero, two things occur. The customer-- a clock starts if you will, on the obligation for the customer to pay the supplier. And the supplier actually gets an obligation to pay a certain amount. In this case we've identified that as an amount y, at a certain time. And that times it t equals x, we haven't defined that particular time. And so that's the basic way it works for a purchase and sale. Now a seller, if they don't want to wait these x days they can consider other options. And let's talk about one particular option called factoring. Now again, if the seller does not want to wait the seller can include and involve a third party. And in this particular case I've suggested it's a collection agency. Whatever that particular third party is they're called the factor. And so the factor is going to purchase the obligation to pay from the buyer. They're going to purchase that from the supplier. And so this collections agency, the factor, is going to get this obligation to pay y at time x. And they're going to collect that from the customer. So in exchange for that though the collections agency gives cash-- money-- and a certain amount to the supplier. And in that particular case, it's going to be whatever that amount is less some percentage. We don't know what that percentage is. It varies based on a number of different factors. So that's basically how factoring works. But there are a couple of different options. Factoring has two specific options. One is where you have factoring with recourse and factoring without recourse. It's very simple. In the case of factoring with recourse the factor can go back to the seller to collect if the buyer fails to pay. And factoring without recourse is the case where the factor has no option to go back to the seller to collect. They can only collect from the buyer. Let's look at how that works out using this example as I portrayed before. We have a third party, the factor, in this case, the collections agency. They've made arrangements to buy that obligation to pay y at time x from the supplier. And their intention is to collect from the customer. But the customer fails to pay. In this case when they have factoring with recourse they go back to the seller, and the seller has an obligation to pay the factor, and they repay that factor. Of course, the terms, how much, and timing-- that's subject to the agreement that is created between the seller, and the agency-- and the factor. Now factoring without recourse is the same process as we've outlined before. The factor buys that obligation to pay. But when they go to collect and the customer fails to pay, they don't have any other options. So broadly speaking, there are many options to finance receivables. Factoring is a common approach. And some of the other options are a function of a couple of different factors, whether you want to borrow against it, or you want to sell that receivable. The timing of the payments is an important factor-- who bears the risk is very important because that also determines the rate that someone might appreciate or enjoy-- and the use of different kinds of collateral, and also who initiates the arrangement. So each of these have different impacts on net operating working capital, which is of course one of the key objectives when one organization goes to finance their different components of the supply chain. Let's just note that it's really important to be specific about the terms that you're using and the details. Because factoring is, as I have described here, it sometimes goes by different terms. And so what you really have to do is be very clear about what are the specific terms of the agreement. So key points here is that companies have two basic options to finance receivables. You can either take a loan against the receivable or they can sell it. And factoring is a very common approach to financing receivables. And when factoring sellers use a third party basically to sell their receivable in order to get cash. And there's two options, factoring with or without recourse. And those of course, have some bearing on what the costs are, and what the risks are. And finally, be very clear about the specific financing arrangements and terms. If you have any questions, comments, or suggestions, please use the discussion forum. And I'll see you in the next segment. Thanks.

[lecture_10_02_supply_chain_finance_options]
Hi. Welcome to the supply chain finance module. In this lesson, we're going to talk about supply chain finance options. My objective in this particular segment is really to explain a number of different options that exist for the firms to finance their supply chain, and that's specifically focused, of course, on providing liquidity in the supply chain. We're going to consider the benefits of supply chain financing, and we're not going to make you experts, though. There exist many different options. We've talked about factoring, but you can see that there are a lot of different possibilities. We're going to talk about each of these. There are also some advanced options which involve the use of information technology. But beware, these terms mean different things to different people. There are many different ways to structure these arrangements. So be careful to understand the specifics of each and every one of these when you're using these terms and hearing others use these. Start with invoice discount. This is initiated by the seller. There's not a third party involved. And the seller basically offers a lower price to the buyer in exchange for early payment. So instead of paying a certain amount of y at time t equals x, what we see is the seller's offering the buyer to pay them 2% less than the invoice total if they pay in 10 days. And their other option is to pay 30 days. The timing and the amount is set by the seller. And the buyer gets to choose whether he or she wants to exercise that option or not. Financing a loan, also known as the invoice discount plus, is basically where the seller uses the receivable, which was an asset, as collateral for the basis of a loan from a bank. It's initiated by the seller, a third party is involved, and the seller again uses the invoice as collateral for a loan with a third party. And so we have the bank makes a loan of a certain amount, y, and then the seller is obliged to make payments of y plus some percentage. And if the seller defaults, then they get to use the collateral as their asset. And again, that's collateral, that's backing up the loan. Now important in this is that the bank uses the seller's credit risk to set the rate. They're only looking at the supplier in this case, or the seller. They're not looking at this customer to set that rate. Forfeiting is very similar to factoring without recourse, except it's used for exports and imports. It's initiated by the seller. And a third party's involved, typically [INAUDIBLE] an organization that has some expertise in export and import. And basically the seller is selling the receivable to a third party at a discount. And the third party buys that, they collect from the buyer. And if the buyer fails to pay, third party takes a risk. And they cannot collect from the seller. Now forfeiting is I think a little more different, has some more complexities of course, because in the export-import process you have materials going across borders, much more administration, more compliance. A lot more parties, more risk involved. And so you'd see that the risks, the compensation is going to be higher for those who are exercising this kind of arrangement. Reverse factoring sounds like factoring, but it's quite different. It's initiated by the buyer. And the buyer also initiates the financing operations, which in this case is a loan. Remember in factoring, the factoring was a sale of the receivable. A third party is involved through the buyer. Typically it's a bank that the buyer is familiar with. And the seller has the option to use the financing options offered. Now a third party basically offers to finance a receivable. But what's interesting here is that they're using the buyer's credit risk. So not the supplier, but the buyer's credit risk. If this buyer happens to be a bigger company, and they're doing a lot of business with that particular bank, that's good news for the supplier, because that means that they're going to have a lower cost loan. Because for the bank, it's a lower risk loan. Now let's talk about dynamic discount. This is basically an invoice discount, with changing terms. In the previous example of invoice discount, there was a fixed term. In this case, it's basically the same thing. But what's different here instead of the offer of say, 2% net 30, the seller might go to the buyer and say, look, we really need cash. So we're going to give you a deeper discount, but you've got to pay us even sooner, five days. Or some different arrangement-- 1% [? 20, ?] meaning 1% discount if the buyer pays within 20 days. So basically it's something that's negotiated. And of course, there's going to be a tradeoff. You're basically getting paid less. If you're the seller, you're getting paid less. But you're getting paid earlier. And inventory financing is quite different. In this case, the firm is not trying to finance a receivable, but instead they're trying to finance inventory. It's initiated by the seller, a third party is involved, and basically the seller arranges to get a loan or line of credit in order to purchase materials. This is often used when the seller has to build inventory in advance of a peak demand season, and where they may not have the adequate resources to invest in all the raw materials necessary to build that inventory. One important distinction here is that the third party is using the seller's inventory as collateral. Invoice auction is actually very similar to factoring without recourse. Let me explain. It's initiated by the seller, but third parties are invited. And the seller arranges an auction process to solicit bids for the invoice. So instead of with factoring without recourse, going to one party and making [INAUDIBLE] an agreement, they're basically holding an auction saying anybody can bid on this and the highest bidder wins. The only distinction is that here they're using an auction process to find that third party. Now keep in mind that the firm may not need to create their own auction process. There's existing auction platforms that they could use. And then you have to keep in mind that sometimes, you may have a customer who says, you are not allowed to sell my invoice. So I'm only going to pay you. And again, that would be subject to the contractual agreements between the buyer and the seller. And that may limit their options for financing. Now as I suggested earlier, there are some advanced options that involve the use of information technology that allows the firm to use automated processes that reduces the uncertainty that might actually make the cost of operations lower, may provide more information, which helps them have more informed risk assessments. And if they're more informed risk assessments, that means that firms may be able extend more favorable rates to the sellers when they're trying to finance that supply chain. Ultimately it's proactive use of IT for faster and better decision-making. Now there are some other options to finance the supply chain. These are non-financial, basically operational. And it's using things like consignment inventory, where the customer will utilize suppliers inventory, but not pay for it until they actually use it. So they'll keep it on hand, but then they'll have it ready and available. But that's basically where the capital requirement's pushed onto the supplier. Direct shipments, that allows an organization to not depend on intermediary inventory locations. And that would tie up capital. If you can ship direct to the customer, then you don't have to worry about that and there's less capital tied up. And then of course there's various forms of collaboration, [INAUDIBLE] [? managed ?] inventories, sales and operations planning, collaborative planning, forecasting, and replenishment and continuous replenishment process. Each of these allows the organization to potentially get in better alignment with their partner, and then that may help them reduce the amount of inventory necessary to maintain service levels. The benefits of supply chain, there are many potential benefits. A primary one for our purposes is the possibility of reducing in operating working capital. But overall, it also includes a possibility to lower cost operations. You may have for the buyers, they may get discounts. For the sellers, they may get lower cost loans. If there's an aggressive use of information, the cycle times might be faster. If the cash comes to you faster, than you may be able to operate faster. You may be able to reduce the cash conversion cycle. And then for those in thinking about their supply chain, may make their supply chain more robust. Because if you're collaborating with your upstream or downstream partner, you're basically making sure that they're going to be a more healthy supplier and more capable to supply your needs when you need them. And then of course there's benefits to third parties who are providing those loans, because they're getting more loans. That's desired by them. Key points, companies have many options to finance the supply chain, driven by a number of different factors. We've talked about many of them, and ultimately all of these provide the potential to reduce net operating working capital, including some operational methods as well. And then, of course, there are potential benefits for each party involved in these transactions when an organization is using supply chain finance. If you have any questions, comments, or suggestions, please use a discussion forum and look forward to seeing your future segment. Thanks.

[lecture_10_03_analyzing_financial_performance]
Let's begin with a brief introduction to this lesson on analyzing financial performance. First of all, we see the money at the bottom of the screen, and we have to ask the question, who cares about a firm's financial performance? In other words, who's going to be the audience for the analysis that we're going to talk about in this lesson? Well, clearly the investors will care about financial performance because they want to get a good return on their investment. There's also executives within the firm who are setting strategies and making key decisions, and they want to think about the implications of those decisions and strategies on the financial performance so they can keep the investors happy. But even the employees of the firm who may not be making decisions about these strategies would care about the financial performance, especially if it affects their bonus. So some further questions then, how do you analyze financial performance? And how do you measure it? How do you turn the analysis into metrics that you can monitor over time? And specifically for this topic that we're looking at with supply chain management, how do you connect it with financial analysis and measurement, and how does that affect your approach to managing supply chains? We have four lessons. We're going to go over this topic very briefly. We're going to start with the drivers of shareholder value. What's really driving the value for those investors? Secondly, we're going to look at ratio analysis. It's a particular way that you conduct analysis in looking at financial performance. There's a specific framework around a particular set of ratios that we're going to discuss. It's called DuPont analysis. And finally, we'll close with the topic, return on invested capital, which is yet another kind of ratio. This is very short and meant to give you a brief overview, but hopefully it gives you an idea of some of the key aspects in looking at financial performance of a firm.

[lecture_10_04_drivers_of_shareholder_value]
Let's begin our lesson on financial performance by thinking about the drivers of that financial performance or of shareholder value. And let's start right at the top. What are the two goals of the CEO? Well, the first one is taken from the title of the segment. And that is that the CEO must provide shareholder value and meet some of the drivers of that value. The second thing is the CEO has to do this without going to jail. Obviously, there's a legal way of driving value and there are illegal ways of doing that, as Bernie Ebbers found out in 2005. In fact, it's interesting. He got 25 years in prison for this for $11 billion of fraud. So we're going to talk about ratios in a moment here. But maybe there's a ratio for the illicit way of driving shareholder value of a little over two years for every billion of fraud you commit. But on a serious note, we are going to try to think about the drivers of shareholder value. And I think that these are really boiled down to three things-- revenue growth, operating margin, and asset productivity. We'll talk about them more in a moment, but the nice thing about these three things related to the overall objective of this course is that supply chain professionals can affect all three of these. And they should be always on the radar when thinking about our supply chain strategies. Now, where do they come from? Let's start with the balanced scorecard. Kaplan and Norton wrote a lot about this. And this is the paper in 1996 where I pulled this quote. And they say that the measures of the scorecard must be linked.
