There must be a causal link between those measures of the scorecard and financial objectives. So they're arguing that no matter what we're looking to measure or analyze our performance of our firm, we should find a way to link them to financial objectives. And in doing that, they found that there's really three themes to those strategies that lead to financial objectives. One is revenue growth, which is a direct mapping to what we just talked about. Cost reduction is very linked to operating margin. And asset utilization is another way of thinking about asset productivity. In fact, later on in 2001, they published what they call a strategy map based on the balanced scorecard. And you can see at the top, shareholder value is right there at the top of that map. And right below that are the three components-- revenue growth, cost structure or operating margin, and asset productivity. So you can see that the strategy linked to the balanced scorecard is driven by these three components. Of course, the consultants have to have their own version of this. They take a good academic concept and make it their own with a little bit of different language. At the top is shareholder value. We see revenue growth right here, broken down into both the sales volume you have, as well as the prices for the things you're selling. Operating margin, thinking about both direct and indirect costs. And asset efficiency or asset productivity, which is broken down into the fixed assets of property and equipment and then the working capital, which comprised of inventory, payables, and receivables. Capital. They have a fourth component called expectations. I'm not sure what that's about. We're going to focus on these three components, which are numbers that we can draw from the financial statements and do our analysis. If you also want to see how Deloitte maps all of their consulting services to these three components or four components and shareholder value, you can download it at this link here, at least as of the recording of this video, it worked. So if you want to take a further look into how Deloitte sees all of their consulting linking to shareholder value, you can do that. Of course, when you have three components, three drivers in a business school, you need to make a triangle. But the triangle here is really important to emphasize that these drivers-- there's tradeoffs when you're focusing on these different drivers. And thinking about some supply chain examples. For example, we can think about driving revenue growth, but having better quality products. So we're going to have higher prices for better quality products. But to do that, we may have to buy better quality components and cost of goods sold. And we have to make sure that the tradeoff between these is appropriate so that we're driving value overall. We could improve one and decrease the other-- actually, the operating margin went down by having higher quality goods. So we need to make sure that that tradeoff is effective. On the other side, asset productivity. We could think about building additional warehouses to be closer to customers. That would drive sales volume. More sales would happen because we're closer, providing better service, but our productivity would go down because we have more assets involved in doing that. So again, we have to think about the tradeoff for those two and make sure that the net is going to provide value. And on this third dimension, we could talk about operating margin and say we'd like to have good operating margins, and therefore, we're not going to take any markdowns or discounts. But if the product isn't selling very well, that could mean that we have inventory build up, and we don't get rid of our inventory. And so our asset productivity is not very good. So we have to be careful in thinking about when do we take markdowns to make sure that our asset productivity is-- we're considering that, the productivity of our working capital, and may have to sacrifice some margin to do that. So you can see some examples of tradeoffs here. We can also use this to position where our strategies ares. Our strategy is focused on revenue, on operating margin, on asset productivity, or a balance of those. And position where we want to go with our supply chain relative to these financial measures. This provides a basic framework for driving financial performance that we can link to in our supply chain management.

[lecture_10_05_ratio_analysis]
The primary type of analysis of financial performance is something called ratio analysis. And it's exactly what it sounds like-- we compute ratios of different things and of different numbers. And those numbers come from the financial statements. We have our income statement here, our balance sheet. And hopefully you're familiar with the terms in these two statements, and we'll use them throughout this segment to pull different parts of the financial statements into ratios, which is simply a numerator over denominator. So it's not complicated math, but how you form the numerator and denominator to effectively tell you something is the key. So we'll pull things from the income statement into the numerator or the denominator. We'll pull things from the balance sheet into both of those as well. It may be on the liabilities and equity side. It could be on assets. They're all going to come together into different ratios that tell us different things about the firm and give us different clues as to its financial performance. Let's start by thinking about some ratios that capture the overall measure of financial performance. And let's start by thinking, is there a single metric-- if we could boil it down to one metric-- that reflects the firm's financial performance for investors, what would that be? And, is there a different metric for executives inside the firm? A single metric that will tell you, how well is the firm performing financially? Well for investors, especially equity investors, they will want to know, what is the return on their equity? Fairly straightforward. And it's calculated by saying, what is the net income of the firm divided by the equity in the firm? And it gives you a ratio of that return on investment, specifically the equity investment. Executives may want to look more broadly than just the equity, and they want to consider the return on the assets that are deployed to create profits. And so, you'll then have return on assets, which is equal to the net income divided by total assets. The assets are used to produce income. How well are we doing that? Is the return sufficient to meet the goals of all the investors, including equity and debt? So we have some high-level measures. We may want to think about some more specific measures, and let's align these with our different drivers of shareholder values. So, first of all, our revenue growth. This is pretty straightforward. We may want to calculate the sales growth from one period to another. So if we have period T, period T minus 1. We divide those two, and subtract that once we get a percent. So let's say we had 100 last period, and we have a 110 this period. We divide those two. We get 1.1 minus 1 equals 0.1, and we might write that as 10% growth from one period to another. You may want to look at the compounded growth over a longer period of time than one period, and you can do that with the second formula. So revenue growth, you might also want to divide this into the growth in terms of sales volume, the actual amount of sales you have in units. You may want to look at the price per unit. You may even look at something-- in retail sector, they look at something called same store sales. So there's some more specific ways to look at sales growth, but it typically boils down to some kind of a growth from one period to another. Operating margin-- it's always looking at, what is the income related to sales? How much of the sales that we're making, or the revenue that we're making, is coming to the bottom line in terms of income? Well, actually, we're going to look at not only the bottom line, which is net income, but at different points in the income statement we may want to pull out a different margin. So our operating margin and our gross margin tell us different things about our ability to create profit from sales. So we'll capture different types of margin by pulling out different types of income from the income statement. In terms of asset productivity, there are a lot of different assets that are used. At a high level, you want to look at our total assets that say, what is our sales divided by our total assets? How are we using our assets to drive revenue? We may want to look more specifically, especially in supply chain, at, how is inventory being productive? So we'll look at the-- we would look at average inventory, and we want to look at COGS. COGS is the cost of goods sold because inventory is booked, in the accounting books, at the cost of goods sold. And so, we want to use that number rather than the sales number, which will also include our markup for the final price. So we'll use COGS as a good indicator for inventory turnover. And average inventory, if you remember the balance sheet, is a snapshot in time. So we can capture a snapshot in time, but we may want to see, well, the inventory might have changed from one period to the next. So we can take an average over the different periods to get a rough approximation. So if inventory went up quite a bit, we may want to take the average inventory of the two periods as a representation. And remember, COGS is going to be representing the cost of goods sold during that period. So the average inventory from the beginning and the end of the period and the COGS during the period was what we would use for inventory turnover. We can do the same for accounts receivable. Accounts receivable, we want to compare that with the turnover of sales that are made on credit. We didn't make-- we may make cash sales, as well. And cash sales are not going to contribute to accounts receivable. So we want to distinguish sales made of credit and see, how often are we turning that over compared to our ability to collect? On the payable side, again, because we're paying the cost of goods sold for things, we want to compare the COGS with the average accounts payable. And again, just like inventory, the accounts payable and the accounts receivable, you may want to average two different snapshots of the balance sheet on the ends of the period, for which we're capturing the COGS. A final way to look at asset productivity is to think about the inventory, accounts receivable, accounts payable, in terms of days. How many days of typical sales-- or in the case of inventory and accounts payable, COGS-- how many days is it taking-- how much days of inventory do we have? How much days of accounts payable do we have? This is actually just the inverse of the turnover. So these two right here are just going to be the inverse of each other. But they are also, oftentimes, more intuitive thinking about it in terms of days. And in fact, if you calculate the days for each of those, you'll get what we call the cash conversion cycle. Let's give an example here to try to work through this. This is an example where we're pulling one income statement, so we're not capturing two. So we'll just use the snapshot in time number for the-- I'm sorry-- for the balance sheet. And the income statement is over the last period. So we don't have the balance sheet from the previous period before the beginning of the income statement. We'll just use the snapshot in time for the balance sheet to be easier. And we'll calculate these six things. You might want to stop the video now and make these calculations yourself, and then check the work. OK so moving on, return on assets. What's our definition of return on assets? Well that's equal to our sales-- actually, not sales-- we don't want to look at sales. We want to look at our return, which is the net income. And we want to divide the net income by our assets, which is total assets down here. So that would be equal to-- we have our net income up here-- 936 divided by 11,300. And if we do that calculation, pull up a calculator. We get, if we round it off, we get 8.28%. So that's our return on assets for the firm. Specifically now, what is our asset turnover? So that's an overall measure. For our asset productivity, we want to look at asset turnover. So here, we're looking at total assets. Now it'll be in the numerator, right? No, actually, it's still going to be the denominator. We're going to look at sales now over total assets. How much-- how often are we turning over our assets in terms of sales? Producing sales from our assets. So that's going to equal to 14,000 divided by-- that's our sales up here at the top-- divided by our total assets, which is still 11,300. And if we do the calculation there, that ends up being 1.24. So basically, we turn our-- we created sales a little bit more than equal to our assets each year, 1.24. We turn it over a little bit more than once per year. What about our inventory? How fast are we turning that over? So here we want to look at-- in the bottom, we're going to have a good inventory. How quickly are we turning over inventory? And on the top, remember, we're not going to use sales because the inventory is booked at cost of goods sold. So we'll put COGS up here. So we'll draw from COGS up here, which is going to be equal to 11,000 divided by our inventory, which is in the balance sheet down here at 2,800. And if we divide those, we get 3.9, rounding up 3.93. So we turn our inventory over more often than our assets in general, and that's because we have some fixed assets and other things as well. So we would expect to turn the inventory over faster. And whether this is good enough or not depends on what our industry is and what our competition is. But we can get an idea that we roughly turn our inventory over four times a year. Let's move on to the next three, and these are all related to the margin. And we'll start with our gross margin, which will take the gross profit up here and divide by our sales. In fact, all these are going to be divided by sales. We're going to normalize our different levels of income by sales. Let's look down here-- equals 3,000 divided by 14,000, which gives us 21.4%. So roughly 21% of the sales we make comes into gross profit. What about operating profit? So for this one, again, we're going to have sales in the denominator. But our numerator is going to be our operating income, which EBIT is a good indicator-- is the right number to use for operating income. I don't like using EBITDA. I like to do things that includes depreciation and amortization. So EBIT over sales, which is equal to 1,800 divided by the 14,000, which gives us a number 12.9%. So you can see we have a lot of SG&A expenses here that are eating into our profits. Our gross profits, our gross margin, is 21%. By the time we get to the operating margin, we're down to 12.9. What about the bottom line? So now we're looking at net income over sales. And here we have-- pull net income here-- 936 divided by 14,000, and that gives us a number of 6.69%. So you can see, by the time we get to the bottom line, our margin has dropped. Right? Because we have interest and taxes in there to eat away at those profits. So you can see at different levels of the income statement-- and everything in our margins comes from the income statement-- we can see how profitable we are. And again, we might want to compare this with the competitors in our industry, and see how well we're doing. In fact, in general-- so here's some advice about ratio analysis. There aren't any correct values for the ratios. The ratios are meant to give you an indication of what's happening in the firm, but you have to understand them in the context in which the firm is competing. So you may want to look at industry averages overall, or, actually, specific competitors. And see how well you're doing compared to the competitors you want to be stronger than. And in fact, this is why the ratios are useful because the-- many of the ratios are dimensionless. And this means that the turnover doesn't have a dimension with it, it's just a number. A margin doesn't have a number with it. It allows you to compare companies of different size, and then be able to not worry about the size of the company. So one company may have a lot more profits than another, but the dimensionless numbers allow us to be able to compare easily across. The other thing you want to do in looking at ratio analysis is look at things over time. Both within a firm-- how well have their profits been going up, or down, or their turnover? Are they getting better returns? And, are they doing better than the competition over time in achieving of those returns? Because you can look at maybe that there's an economic, a broader economic issue that's causing the industry to not do as well. But, relative to the competitors, are we doing well? So you need to develop a framework of different ratios to think about these different aspects because not one single ratio, like a return on equity or return on assets, doesn't tell you much of what's going on. You break it down into some more specific ones-- we've mentioned a few-- and you may even go further, like I mentioned with sales growth having even more specific ratios, depending on what you're looking for. Because in the end, these ratios don't tell you exactly what's happening, but they give you really good clues that can help you tell an interesting story about the financial performance of a firm.

[lecture_10_06_dupont_analysis]
In this segment, we're going to discuss a particular kind of financial analysis called DuPont analysis. And I'm sure you're wondering, why is it called DuPont analysis specifically? Well, it comes out of the DuPont company. In fact it comes-- specifically it's a return on investment formula that was developed by a DuPont explosives salesman back in 1914. His name was Donaldson Brown. And he used it in DuPont and then later on he became the CFO at General Motors. But by the time he was deploying it widely at General Motors, it was already known as the DuPont formula. You can see him here as a young DuPont salesman, and later on as a very wily CFO of General Motors. And I would encourage you to read through this paper in the Accounting Historians Journal about the influence of this salesman, as he developed a lot of financial measures. Well his formula is actually pretty simple. Three letters, very much like the theory of relativity, E equals mc squared. You have to know E, m and c stand for. But in our formula, what do r, t, and p stand for? R is the rate of return on the capital invested when you invested capital, return on investment. And it's broken down into two parts: the turnover of invested capital and the percentage of profit on sales. And those, in terms of our thinking of ratio analysis, it's really in essence a combination of two ratios. So writing it in our ratio analysis terminology, we have the return on investment or return on assets here, is net margin times asset turnover, where our net margin is the net income divided by sales, our asset turnover is the sales divided by total assets. And obviously if we multiply them together, sales cancel out and you get net income over total assets, which is your return on assets. So it's a very straightforward and simple formula, but it helps us think about two key drivers of our financial performance. Coming back to our drivers figure from earlier, you can see the operating margin here on the left, which is our net-- net margin is a little different type of margin, but operating margin is the key to that. And asset productivity, thinking about asset turnover as a key measure ratio of looking at that. And this is a trade off of that part of the triangle that we presented earlier. And in fact, it can articulate two kinds of strategies. You can take a profit path toward return on assets or a turnover path, but you end up at the same destination, return on assets. And you can think of different industries that may focus on one or the other. In fact, the industries that have a lot more value added, like manufacturers that create the products and add a lot of the products, love higher margins and may not have as good of turnovers. And the retailers and wholesalers who sell those products may not have as good a margin, so they have to turn over the product, turn over their inventory a lot faster. So there's different paths, but they might end up with the same return on assets. Just different strategies given their role in the supply chain. I want to also introduce another term here: GMROI. It's often pronounced "Jim Roy." And it stands for Gross Margin Return On Inventory investment. It's similar to DuPont, but it's a little more narrow. It's focusing on gross margin, not the net margins, so looking at just sales and cost of goods sold, and what is the gross margin. And then the inventory turnover, not the overall turnover of assets, but the inventory turnover specifically. And you can see when you multiply your gross margin, gross profit over sales, times inventory return, which is sales over inventory, the sales cancel out. You get gross profit over inventory, which is the gross margin return on inventory investment. And it's narrower than DuPont, and it's focused more on evaluating inventory decisions and their impact on profitability. And this will come to play in a lot of supply chains. For example, at retailers specifically, you can think about two different paths here. You think about grocers, grocery stores having very low margins, right? So they have to have high turnover in order to get a good return on that inventory. On the other extreme, you may have jewelry stores that have very high margins, but they want to always be able to make sure they make a sale and have plenty of inventory, so their inventory turnover maybe low. And the net effect is that they may have very similar GMROI even though they have very different strategies of having a profit path or a turnover path. So you can see that playing out in a more narrow aspect in a particular industry. In fact you can even think about, even in the same type of industry like clothing sales, you can think of a Macy's-- in the US we have Macy's, is a very high end retail store will have higher margins, maybe not as fast of a turnover. And you have the other extreme is a T.J. Maxx. It's another retailer but they have much lower margins, lower prices, so they have to turn things over faster. So you can see this trade off, profit path and turnover path across industries. Within an industry of retail, and even within a part of a retail sector, you're having those different paths toward the same type of return on investment. I want to close by talking about the fact that return on equity is actually the focus of most DuPont analysis now. Even though initially when it was used in DuPont it was return on assets, the first two components here. We've now added financial leverages, the third part of this, to lead to return on equity. And you can see here, again with the first two components are the same, net income over sales, sales over total assets. They cross out to give us, put these two together, we get ROA, Return On Assets. But then we go further adding the financial leverage, which has their assets over equity. Then the assets cancel out, and when we get these together we get our return on equity. It brings in a third component, and it looks at the role of taking on debt to be able to drive returns for the equity investor. And so our value driver triangle needs a foundation. You know, when thinking about the value for shareholders, is that financial leverage can be incorporated to really drive value. It's a key component here. But that also can bring in some risks. There's more risk when you take on debt, so thinking about that foundation of leverage and risk. And in general we need to be thinking-- when we're thinking about driving value, we need to think about the risks we have in our supply chain as well in terms of the overall value we're providing to our shareholders. Or the returns we give them, the risks we expose them to, and giving them a good combination for their investment. So that gives you DuPont analysis as a way of looking at different paths towards the same objective of return on assets, or more broadly return on equity. And you can also be more narrow to focus on particular parts, such as gross margin and inventory.

[lecture_10_07_roic]
This segment has an obscure title, ROIC. It is an acronym. And I'll leave you in suspense a little bit as to what it stands for. I'm going to motivate this by talking about some overall measures of financial performance, some ratios that are typically used. Return on assets, which is our net income over total assets, and return on equity, which is the net over total equity. And these measures can have some flaws, especially when there's a lot of debt in a firm. So we've got-- I'm going to use a simple example here. We have company D, which refers to debt. They have a lot more debt than company E, which is all equity financed. So they have the same amount of liabilities in equity overall, and in fact, the same number of assets, same amount of assets. And in fact we're keeping the EBIT the same as well. So in terms of their ability to create EBIT based on assets, it's the same. But their ability to create income depends on their structuring because the interest expenses can change the income. So let's do the calculations here and show how this works out. So looking at ROA, we have company D, we have company E. So the ROA for company D is the net income of 60 over the total assets, which was 2,000, which is equal to 3.0%. The return on assets for company E, their net income is 168 divided by same assets, 2,000. That's going to be equal to 8.4%. So better return on assets for company E because they have more net income, lower return on equity. So in this case, we still have the same return for both of these. So 60 here, and 168. The thing that's changing here is the denominator of equity for company D, which has a lot of debt, only has 200 in equity. So that gives us a return on equity of 30%. And for company E, all of that is equity. So that's 2,000. So we already have that, that's equal to 8.4%. So you can see that for the equity-based firm, return on assets and return on equity, if it's all equity financed, is the same: 8.4%. But because of the high leverage of company D, the return on assets tells a very different picture than return on equity. A lot of the return on equity is because of the ability to finance. And with only 200 in equity they can finance and get a lot more assets and create out of the same EBIT. But that's going to be riskier because the debt holders will be paid first. So it's a very different perspective between ROA and ROE, depending on the debt structure of the firm. And when we're managing supply chains, we won't make that decision as to how much debt and equity to take on. But it will affect some measures that we may be using to evaluate our performance, such as return on assets. So this is where ROIC comes in. It stands for Return On Invested Capital. So this is looking at the returns on all capital for investors who are seeking returns. So we're not only going to look at equity. We're also not going to look at just assets purely. We're going to look at specifically what's being invested, where we expect it to get returns. So we're going to look at-- and we're also going to change the income statement. Because of the influence of the taxes, we're going to define something called NOPAT, which will help us deal with the fact that the income statement is influenced by the debt structure. So let's define these two firms in our ROIC ratio. The first one is NOPAT, which stands for Net Operating Profit After Tax. So it's exactly what it sounds like. Whatever's the operating profit after tax. But it specifically doesn't incorporate the effect of interest, because we're taking earnings before interest and taxes, right? But we are going to incorporate the tax rate here. So we're just trying to pull out the effect of the inventory, or the interest expense ability to change the nature of our bottom line net income. So it's basically the earnings as if-- after taxes, if it was all equity financed. We don't consider the interest expense. And we're actually taking out the tax rate here to be able to make it an equal comparison. The invested capital is defined as the interest bearing debt and equity. So anything that we have in debt where we're going to be paying interest on it, and the equity is all the invested capital upon which we're supposed to create a return. So yeah, the sum of all sources of cash on which return must be earned. But for example, we're not going to include accounts payable, which is a source of cash where our suppliers allow us to pay later, so effectively they are giving us financing, because we take ownership of the product before we have to pay for it. But they don't expect us to pay a return on that. So we're taking that out. We may want to subtract the excess cash as well, because the effect of the excess cash can be used to pay off the debt. And so that would reduce the invested capital. So that's something that is a little bit undefined. We will probably not subtract out excess cash in our example, but you may have the need to do that in practice. We also want to look at the book value invested capital rather than the market value, because we want to look at the value that was actually invested by the debt holders and equity holders, the shareholders. So that's the definition of ROIC. Let's give an example here. Here is our definition again. Our NOPAT is our EBIT times 1 minus the tax rate. So let's take an example here off to the right, this company. We have our EBIT is equal to-- so EBIT times 1 minus tax rate. And the tax rate is 40%. It's going to be 1,800 times 1 minus 0.4, which is 1,800 times 0.6, which works out to be 1,080. So that's our NOPAT. What is our invested capital? And here we're going to look at all the interest bearing debt and equity, so we go to the balance sheet and see we have long term debt. We'd have to pay interest on that. We don't use accounts payable because we're not paying interest on that. There's no return expected. So we have long term debt. And then we have stockholders' equity down here. So this is going to be equal to 4,800 plus 5,600, which is equal to 10,900. So our ROIC is equal to 1,080 over 10,400 which is 10.4%. So that's how we would calculate it. We come up with our operating profit after taxes. So we are going to incorporate taxes, but not interest expense and the tax savings based on interest expense in calculating the NOPAT. And then we look at what capital we're looking for return on, and we get a number of 10.4%. There's another term that's similar to this called RONA. And you know, it's similar to ROIC. It's also similar to another term called ROCE or Return On Capital Employed. These are all trying to do the same thing, saying what is the return on the assets that are expected to produce profit, which is very similar to the debt and equity upon which we're supposed to create returns. Here again, we're going to use the same denominator of NOPAT, but we're going to divide by net assets. And those, NOPAT's the same as ROIC. Net assets can be defined a couple of different ways, but what we're trying to do is look at the fixed assets plus any non-cash working capital. Or another way of calculating that is looking at the total assets and subtract out the current liabilities and the cash which are non-cash capital. So let's do this example with the same company using RONA. So we're going to get the same NOPAT as before. Remember that was equal to 1,080. It's our EBIT times 1 minus tax rate. And then our net assets is equal to our property plant and equipment, which is going to be down here. It's our fixed assets. Plus non-cash working capital. And that's going to be defined as our inventory plus our accounts receivable minus accounts payable, because we get financing for that. So that's our working capital definition. So it's plus inventory, plus accounts receivable, minus accounts payable. So that's going to be equal to 5,000 plus 2,800, plus 3,000 in accounts receivable, minus the 900 of accounts payable, which gives us a number of 9,900. Now if we calculate that with the other formula, net assets equals the total assets, which is down here, 11,300, minus current liabilities, which would be this 900 here, minus cash, which is up here. It gives us the same amount of 9,900. And in fact if you calculate this both ways, you should get the same numbers generally. I know that financial statements can be very different for different firms. The accountants may use different terms. But you should roughly get the same net assets either way. So what is our RONA? That's equal to 1,080 over 9,900, which is 10.9%. So you remember, ROIC was 10.4%. So it's a little different. And the difference is actually-- remember we had the-- the NOPAT the same in both. So for this one we had the NOPAT of 1,080. And we had our equity of 5,600 and our debt of 4,800, which gave us a total of 10,400. And that gives us our 10.4%. But notice if took this 10,400, the equity plus the debt, and if we subtracted out the cash, the 500, we would get the 1,080 over 9,900, just like we have for the net assets. So I mentioned before, you may want to subtract out excess cash when doing the ROIC calculation. This is an example why you may want to do that. Because if we did that, we would get the exact same number for RONA as well as ROIC. Now you may feel like, well, don't we have a standard definition for what these overall measures of financial performance should be? It would be nice. Accounting is a practice, the ratio analysis is a practice as well. Your firm will have a way of deciding on which overall performances or measures you're going to be using. But if you want to suggest one, I would suggest ROIC, Return On Invested Capital, as a good overall measure. And let's just see how it plays out with our example from before at the beginning of the segment. Remember we had company D had a very big disparity in return on equity and assets, whereas the equity-only firm had an alignment there. If we did our ROIC calculation on this, we would get-- and this is again, NOPAT over invested capital equals, our NOPAT here is going to be our EBIT for company D, it's going to be EBIT 280 times 1 minus the tax rate, which is 0.4, which will end up being 168 in our numerator. And our invested capital is simply going to be, it's easy to calculate here, debt plus equity is 2,000. 2,000. So that's going to give us a number of 8.4%. And if we do this on the other side, of course. The NOPAT, same EBIT, same tax rate. And we still have the same total liabilities and equity. So we'll get the same numbers on this side. But interestingly, the ROIC would again be in exactly the same, 8.4%. So it's giving you the return as if you were completely fully equity financed, if you are an equity finance only firm. But for a firm with a lot of debt and financial leverage, ROIC gives a truer idea of the performance of the returns on the capital that's being invested, whereas return on assets and return on equity can distort the picture. That's why I think ROIC is a better overall measure of return on the capital that's being entrusted into our firm to create returns. So, sorry for the graphics behind you. But you can see ROIC is a good measure for supply chain performance because it is not confounded by the financing strategies that we don't have control over in supply chain. Financing is going to be determined by the finance department. So it'll show basically the fundamental earning power of the firm, which is really what comes from the operations. And so that's what we would like to focus on in looking at the performance of the firm financially related to our operations in our supply chain management.

[lecture_10_08_key_points]
Let's wrap up this brief lesson with some key points about analyzing financial performance. Let's go back to the questions posed at the beginning. First of all, how do you analyze financial performance? And we've seen that through a broad set of ratios and ratio analysis is often how it's done. These ratios are based on data from financial statements, primarily income statement and balance sheet. So the math isn't challenging, but the difficulty is defining what's going to be in the numerator and what's in the denominator. And what are you trying to accomplish by looking at these different ratios? And in fact, you should look at several ratios in a context of a framework that allows you to see performance from different perspectives. These different ratios give us clues as to what's going on with financial performance. But you need several, maybe you could think about them as sensors, to see how well your overall firm is moving forward. And we want to use ratios that are dimensionless. And most of the ones in here, margin, turnover, are dimensionless because we can compare with competition. That may be companies of very different size, but we can look at the margins because they eliminate the size of the firm in terms of profitability and look at the pure ability to create profit based on sales or turnover, how much sales can be driven by the assets of the firm. It doesn't matter how big the company is. We can compare. We can also observe trends over time, both in competition and in our own firm. As our firm grows, we can say, are we maintaining the ability to capture good margin on sales? Are we maintaining the ability to get a good turnover of sales depending on the assets we're deploying, even as we get bigger over time? So can look at our firm's performance and see how we're doing on these dimensionless measures over time. And we'll have some homework assignments where you can try to practice some of that type of analysis. We didn't have time to get into that kind of competitive analysis and trend analysis in the lesson itself. How do you measure performance then, is very linked to how you analyze it. The financial metrics are often based on the ratios used in financial analysis. You'll often find things like return on assets, return on equity, hopefully return on invested capital. But there are often metrics in a firm that are based on the things that an analyst, a financial analyst, will use to analyze the firm. However, inside the firm you have different information than what's available in the financial accounting books, and managerial decisions may require that different information. So financial metrics based on income statement and balance sheet won't be sufficient to make the decisions. You may need managerial accounting or specific types of accounting like activity-based costing to give you the information you need in order to make decisions. And in fact, these financial metrics are only part of a bigger picture of information you're going to need to monitor the performance of your supply chain. But the financial metrics are important. And we should make sure that we're thinking about the analysis that aligns with the different drivers of shareholder value. You should keep financial metrics on your radar screen that are aligned with revenue growth, operating margin, and asset productivity. Those are the key things that the supply chain will do to create value for shareholders, who won't have as much influence on the financial leverage. We will have some ability to assess the risk of some of our strategies, but we're really at the core looking at this triangle up here: revenue growth, operating margin, and asset productivity. But we want to make sure we incorporate financial metrics in thinking about our performance and also guiding our strategy. This triangle can be helpful in saying, should we move more of our focus to revenue growth, or should we move to more margin, or should we stay more into asset productivity? You can position yourself and your goals relative to these financial measures to help set your supply chain strategy. Some metrics are better than others. Don't take every metric that's out there as one that you should incorporate. And for example, I think ROIC is a much better metric than return on assets and return on equity. That return on invested capital gives you a clearer perspective on how your firm is generating returns on the capital that's being deployed where the returns are expected. And the bottom line is, supply chain executives should definitely monitor a balanced set of financial metrics that align with the investors' interests but also employee bonuses. So in a balanced scorecard kind of a concept, financial metrics are definitely an important part of it. We shouldn't limit ourselves to only the metrics that look at supply chain performance and the processes that we're monitoring more closely, although those are very important to have on your balanced scorecard. But always include some financial metrics that think about the investors. And employee bonuses a lot of times are linked to financial performance, so make sure that you're incorporating the employee bonus, the financial metrics that will drive employee bonuses and drive the incentives such that they will try to create value in the best way through supply chain management by looking at revenue growth, operating margin, and asset productivity. As always, use the practice problems to learn more. Again, we'll give you some opportunities to do some more of this kind of analysis and even compare firms. Use the discussion for questions, comments, and suggestions. But hopefully this very brief lesson has given you a bit of a picture of how to analyze the performance of your supply chain, and you'll use this information going forward to design better supply chains and manage them for success into the future. Good luck in creating your piles of cash inside your firm.

[lecture_10_09_introduction]
OK. So this is the last lesson of the last week. So before we dive into it, let's look backwards. What have we done so far in this course? Well, everything was about what the supply chain is, and what's focus of the flow of information, the physical goods, and the financial. It's all about how to handle these flows, and so that's why we focused on them collectively and then each of them individually. So we start with the physical flows, move to information, design, and what we'll do in this lesson is talk about design of the organization itself. So for the physical flows, what we did is we modeled the problem. Hopefully, you're very comfortable with these problems, and you see that they build off each other, so that transportation and transhipment problems are really all about flow, while the location problems are all about nodes. So arcs and nodes, and they come together when we do a network design problem. And there's all sorts of different flavors of these problems, but we really focus on the standard form that allows us to use Mixed-Integer Linear Programs or MILPs. And hopefully you're very comfortable with MILPs at this point because we've used them throughout this entire course. But hopefully, what you've taken away or you will take away is the whole idea here that the interpretation and the implementation is more important than the actual math and that you understand what it is you're doing and understand the limitations of these types of solutions. Because they're not perfect, but they give you some good support to help you make a decision. And again, to drive this a little further, remember, these are decision support tools and the accent is on support. They're not making the decision. Then we moved to information flow. And we've covered sourcing with some strategies and methods, and we spent time using Mixed-Integer Linear Programs showing you how they're used for optimization-based procurement, which is widely, widely adopted. We covered make, and this is where you coordinate between manufacturing and sourcing and the demand in production planning. And, again, we use the same optimization tools to show how you make those decisions. We talked about bills and material, fixed horizon planning, where you have varying demand, but it's deterministic and it's widely used in production scheduling. And then we finished up there with the delivery, collaborating with customers, again, using the optimization for aggregate planning and distribution channel design introducing different ways products can flow and introducing different channels both closed loop supply chains as well as e-commerce. And they all have a role. And what's really interesting, this is very much evolving. So what's being done today, this year, might be very different a year from now, so there's a lot of flux here. And then we finished up with Sales and Operations Planning, S&OP, which is something that every company does. We went into financial flow. And remember, all we're doing is translating so you can speak the language of the CFO. And so we introduced you to income sheets, balance sheets, or income statements rather, balance sheets, cash flows. I know it's a lot here, but you need to have a working knowledge of some of this. It's probably worth, if you really want to get involved and move up in your company, to get more knowledge, a little more into finance and accounting. We tried to give you enough so you can understand and do some basic translation. And we focused on these five areas, and hopefully, you're fairly comfortable with them because activity-based costing is widely used in every company. Working capital is something you really need to understand because that cash-to-cash cycle time is something that every company is looking to shrink. Discounted cash flow analysis is going to help you make decisions for investments. Supply chain finance is an up-and-coming technique that a lot of large companies are using to work with their different suppliers. And then the financial analysis, the DuPont model, that's another way of helping you make investment decisions. So what we're going to do in this lesson, well for the remainder of this lesson, we're going to answer this one question. How should a supply chain be organized? And we're going to focus in on what's the structure look like, how has it changed over the last couple of years. And then the big question that every company asks, what and if, do I centralize or decentralize? What are the functions, the tasks, the processes that I keep in the headquarters? And what do I keep at low to the field? And there's a lot of debate, in fact, a lot of companies move back and forth, back and forth. Centralized this year, then two years later, they decentralize and go back and forth. But we want to talk and give you a framework to make that decision. Then tied to that is how do you measure the performance of these systems and these processes. So we'll talk about the basic primary processes and supply chains and then how to measure them. We'll talk a little theoretically about this, how do you measure performance of any process and then build on that, how to build metrics and metric systems. So that's what we're going to do for the rest of this lesson. I hope you've enjoyed it so far, but let's get started.

[lecture_10_10_supply_chain_organization_design]
So let's talk about the supply chain organization. Where does it fit into the larger corporate structure? Well, we know what falls into the function-- all these different activities, from outbound transportation down to order processing and everything in between. And we've talked about these individually. We've talked about purchasing. We've talked about transportation planning, MRP systems, sales, new product launch. All these we've talked about mainly individually. But the question is, who controls them, and how do they fit into that larger corporate function? Well, traditionally, it used to look something like this, where you'd have the President or the CEO or someone, and you'd have a level of people reporting to him or her, and they would have major functions. There was a sales and marketing, manufacturing, and maybe operations, finance. There might be some other ones I didn't list, like human resources and other things. But these are the three main that contained pieces of supply chain management activities. You see for finance, they had a lot of the things dealing with inventory control, which is all about the money, and managing the information systems. Manufacturing had all the things in supply chain management that dealt with getting product into production, including MRPs, inbound transportation, scheduling and production. And then marketing usually held all of the supply chain logistics-- getting the finished goods to the customer. This includes demand forecasting and planning, customer sales, finished goods, warehousing, new product launch, outbound transportation, all of those things. Now, about 20 years ago, things started blending a little bit. And what happened is there was a little merging of activities on the inbound side under materials management all about the logistics flow-- into the plant-- and the physical distribution-- all of the flow out from the plant to the customer. So we started seeing consolidation of different activities so you could do tradeoffs internal, and you didn't have to go between the higher level function. So it was still siloed, but there was a lot more tradeoffs being done within the inbound and the outbound. This evolved even more to where the logistics-- or this could also be supply chain, depending on the company-- it all got grouped into its own organization. And a lot of times, you see it like this, where it's a peer function along with the sales and marketing, manufacturing, and production, as well as finance. And you see all these activities fall within. Now, sometimes, you might even see that this product-- this gets thrown in as well, but it depends on the company. There's a lot of flexibility now. There isn't one organizational structure that all companies use. It really depends on a lot of different things. So what we've seen now is there's different hybrids of organizations for supply chains. Sometimes, you see it as we just showed where you have a separate function-- so logistics is a pure function along with finance, human resources, manufacturing, sales, and marketing. Or you could have it as a program where it's a separate entity, and it's something that reports into the CEO. Or what's more common-- you see it in what's known as a matrix organization. Now, why is it called a matrix organization? Well, what I have here is that the CEO has its major function-- sales and marketing, manufacturing, finance. But also reporting to them are logistics and human resources. However, there's a little bit of a difference here. Finance owns everything dealing with the finance of the firm, manufacturing. The head of manufacturing-- whether it's a VP or the Chief Manufacturing Officer, Chief Operations Officer, whatever-- owns all of the activities that happen within that function. Same thing with sales and marketing. It's called a matrix, though, because logistics now will have authority on what's known as project authority. And so for example, if they're working in the Finance department over here, they might be involved in some inventory control. So you'd have a logistics personnel within the finance company. So what does this mean for the individual? They've got two bosses. One is a dotted line this way. And different companies arrange how this is set up a little bit differently. It's known as a shared service, where logistics is important to every one of the functions, so it's shared across those functions. For here in manufacturing, you might have someone running the MRP, might be running inbound transportation. And so what we're finding is that you have teams of people acting where they have two bosses. One is the main function, and then also that shared service, that logistics. So when you come up and design something like this, there's different tradeoffs that you're looking at. One is strategic versus operational. Does the logistics and supply chain belong at the higher level? Does it sit at that same peer level as manufacturing and finance and all the other major functions? The second tradeoff you want to determine is where do the activities occur. Do they happen within its own function, logistics, or do a lot of activies occur within those individual functions? And so you want to understand where the actual work occurs. And then finally, the biggest one is whether you want to make the decision making process centralised or decentralised. And essentially, there's three questions here-- which activities in supply chain are best administered and controlled from a central headquarters location, from the chief, or which activities are best administered and controlled from more regional or decentralized locations? What's best to do centrally for everything within the company? What's best to do at all the different regions that might be slightly different from each other? And then how is the coordination made between regions, countries, business units, channels, divisions, et cetera? Because centralized, decentralized, it's not just geography. You have different business units. You have different products. So it could be that two facilities are right next to each other, but they're managed very differently-- because, for example, they're making totally different products, serving totally different markets. So why would I have the same supply chain serving both of them? So these are the types of tradeoffs and decisions you make when you're trying to design your supply chain organization. And what we'll do next is focusing on this major decision of what do I centralize and what do I decentralize.

[lecture_10_11_centralized_versus_decentralized]
So let's dive just a little deeper into the centralized versus decentralized decision-making process. So there are pros and cons. And these are really two extreme solutions. For the centralized option, that's where-- here's my corporate headquarters, and let's say I have these different business units, or different divisions in different regions across the world. And the idea is that I will make all the decisions at that central location. And these could be deciding how much inventory to stock, what to procure, who to buy from for transportation services, all the different rules and policies. So the benefit of doing it up here at this central location is that we leverage economies of scale. I might be able-- for procurement, for example, be able to buy, get a high enough volume where I get a larger discount since I'm buying for one, two, three, four different regions in this case. It allows me to harmonize policies and practices so I can establish best practices, things that make sense for my industry, for my company and strategy. And I can roll those out and make sure that everyone is using, say, the same forecasting technique and being in parallel process for this. So everything is harmonized and standardized along the best practices as I deem fit for my entire company, and it also allows for global or optimal solutions. So I might be able to find a solution that is best for the entire company, not just for one or two of the individual divisions because I'm making the trade-off decisions centrally. Now, the other option here is just the opposite, where I don't make any decisions centrally. I make them all locally. This is decentralized. So the decision of who to procure from, how much inventory to stock, how to run my operations, demand planning, all of those things are done locally for my individual business unit or division. What are the benefits here? Well, the decision-making is closer to the customer because chances are I'm facing a different customer here versus, say, over here. It might be that this division is selling to large retailers. This is selling to manufacturers or to other companies in my own industry. So they might be facing different customers. So by allowing me to make decisions individually I can be closer to my customer and make decisions that are more appropriate for them. It also allows me to reflect local cultures and customs because these could be across the world in totally different geographies. It allows for optimal solutions within that region. If I come up with a centralized one, yes, it's perfect-- or it's optimal, rather, across the company. But as we've seen in a bunch of different cases, that might mean that this division pays the price so that this division has better service or lower costs. Overall it might be better, but the benefits are not always shared evenly across all the divisions. By doing it decentralized I'm trying to optimize for each region by itself, and it allows the business units to act autonomously. Sometimes it's very hard to run things always centralized. You tend to be further away from the action, a little further removed from the decision-making process. So you've got these two extremes, and no one does dramatically one or the other. It's usually a hybrid between the two. But let's talk a little more about it. So here we've got global operations because most companies have operations at least in two countries, if not two continents. And so you have a supply chain that looks something like this. So you've got to ask yourself, if I have a bunch of things happening in Southeast Asia, that's probably going to be very different from my operations in Latin America. I might want to be managing them very differently. The currencies change differently, the standards, the cultures are all very different. Same as if I'm in North America versus Africa. So the geographies is a natural way to decentralize some decision-making. Maybe there are certain things I want to do on the ground in each of the regions and maybe centralize some other decisions. Well, it's not just geography, though, because we can look at some of these other large companies. And here's just a sampling. And so you can't imagine that all of the decisions are being made centrally. You look at a company like Tata, which operates in over a hundred countries, has dozens of companies, and they make things from cars to tea to perfume to bikes to watches. So chances are the individual business units, the strategic business units, are going to be a little closer to their different individual customers and be able to make some decentralized decisions that might be better than one centralized authority. Same thing with General Electric. They make things ranging from aircraft engines to railroad engines, household appliances, consumer financing. They're all over the place. Same thing if you look at a company like BASF. They have over 70 different strategic business units across the world, and they produce things that sell to construction industries, to agriculture, to electronics, paints, papers, plastics, and they sell to almost every company you can imagine. Even a company that tends to be in the same space, consumer packaged goods, like Unilever and P&G, they have very different products that they're selling, and they sell in different channels, different distributors, and they have very different characteristics. A company like Starbucks, which you would think, well, just selling coffee, they're actually very different in that they have retail operations. They sell into grocery stores. They also have manufacturing operations. And they're also moving further upstream in their supply chain, so different parts of the company will probably operate a little differently. Not all decisions are made centrally. They might be made decentrally depending on the geography, the type of customers you're selling to, the business units and very many other factors. So we had a thesis a couple years back here at MIT where some students looked at this and they wanted to understand, OK, we're not going to have everything centralized or everything decentralized. The question is, which decisions-- which functions do you tend to centralize and which do you tend to decentralize? What makes sense? And so what they did is they looked at all the different types of decisions you have to make and they broke them into three classic groupings- strategic, tactical, and operational. And if you look at the strategic decisions being made, these are the things that take about a year to implement, very high impact, high risk. If you think of network design, those kind of things. So they bucketed two main examples here, long-term capacity planning and sourcing decisions, because those have long-term effects. In the tactical bucket, these are things that get affected monthly or quarterly, have a kind of a medium risk factor. And you put in transportation planning, inventory planning, short-term demand, manufacturing planning, and internal improvements. And in the operational, these are the daily, weekly kind of activities. It's order fulfillment, order management, shipment handling. So if you look at these-- and you can see the impact goes from high to low, and the number of decisions go just the opposite. These decisions are made every day. So there's a ton of those operational ones, very few of the strategic ones. But what they're trying to figure out is through a survey and discussions and interviews with executives where do you make these decisions? And so what they came up with is a decision-making-- they kind of mapped the process. And, again, they have these three buckets- strategic, tactical, and operational. And they grouped the companies that they interviewed and surveyed into four categories- consumer packaged manufacturers; they're in blue, industrial products manufacturing; they're in that dark green; light green is logistics service providers; and then retailers are this kind of salmon color. And so what we have here is there are different questions we're asked, and as the number gets higher for each of those segments that means more centralization. And as a number gets lower it tends to be a decentralized. And so you can see what the big takeaway is, more strategic kind of decisions tend to be centralized. You centralize your sourcing. Economies of scale fits in there a lot, long-term capacity planning because you're balancing across all the different regions. At the other extreme, the operational decisions, or fulfillment, or management, shipment handling, mainly the outbound shipment handling, these are things closer to your individual customers, so it makes sense to be much more decentralized, closer to your customers, to make those decisions. Now, there was also a survey that was done about a couple years later by PricewaterhouseCoopers. What I like about it, it took these different activities and surveyed a bunch of companies and asked what percentage-- or whether you manage this globally, centrally, or regionally, decentrally. And you can see the different tasks, and they're sequenced-- or they're sorted by going from high to low where new product development is something that tends to be managed globally because it has really high impact. And as you go down, you see that it's a decreasing amount that is managed globally, strategic procurement, supply chain centers of excellence, those are all over 60% of the company say this is what we do centrally, we manage those globally. Now, the center of excellence, this is a typical organization within a company that establishes best practices, standard processes, best ways of doing something for that company. It's a way to ensure a standard level of quality and performance across a very wide ranging organization. The other interesting one right after that is S&OP. This makes sense, right, because this is something that helps me balance all my supply and my demand across my whole company. So we're seeing more and more companies move S&OP into something that is run globally because what it's doing, it's not just balancing between my production and my sales team, it's the entire companies because there's oftentimes a lot of shared resources. And, for example, two organizations in different business units might use the same common transportation. So therefore, the strategy that one entity takes might impact what's available for the second. So the S&OP process is a way for a company to balance those decisions and make those trade-offs across the different units. Now, as you see this number get smaller and smaller, things get closer and closer to operational type tasks, inbound and outbound logistics, customer service, warehousing, things like that. So the big takeaway here is that you're focused on more strategic things tend to be centralized. Things that are more operational and focused to the customer can be more decentralized and done individually in individual regions and business units. Now, what we're seeing more and more is across these large companies with multiple business units or strategic business units, like I said, some companies might have 50 or more of these. And so every strategic business unit is like its own company. There's usually a president, and they have P&L responsibility, profit and loss. And so they very much have that authority within their business unit. However, they all have shared services in terms of logistics. And so what we set up here is similar to what you do in a company in between, say, finance and marketing and manufacturing, but here it's much more formalized. So you might have someone who reports directly in to the business unit president but has this dotted line to the head of logistics. So someone who's serving two bosses-- and it's a challenging position, but it makes sense because they have to make sure that what they do helps the business unit but also has to adhere to the standards that the logistics function needs to adhere to, needs to enforce. So usually what happens here is you create the service agreements or some kind of rules of the road between the head of this logistics and each of the business units because the logistics services they provide to, say, Strategic Business Unit One, might be entirely different from what they provide here for Business Unit Two. Let's say this Business One might be selling to industrial customers, to other manufacturers, so that means big-batch deliveries on a schedule, can't shut a production line down but then might have other product's going to a retailer and that's much more on a fixed schedule, much more seasonality due to sales. And so they might have very different structures set up, different ways of forecasting, managing inventory, managing transportation, and everything in between. So what these service agreements usually need to dictate are what decisions are made locally within the business unit and what decisions get to be made centrally, in this case by the logistics organization. Who do the teams report to? How does that reporting structure work? How do we balance trade-offs between the strategic business units? These guys might use, for example, common transportation or production facilities. It makes sense for them to share facilities if it leads to better operational efficiencies. So someone has to balance the use of those and that's what the shared service is there for. How do you run the sales and operations planning process across these business units? And how do you charge for a fund to shared service models? A lot of times what happens is the logistics company will charge an internal cost to the business unit so they can make a decision whether to use the internal logistics or go outside. So this structure, this hybrid structure, is very, very common in large organizations. And it is something that works very well. But what it requires is that there has to be an arrangement or an agreement, a service-level agreement between the logistics function, the supply chain function, and each of the different business units.

[lecture_10_12_supply_chain_processes]
So now, let's dive a little deeper into supply chain processes. We're not going to go too deep here. We're going cover this in much more detail in SC3X. But let me just give you an overview. So everything in the supply chain can be viewed as a process with inputs that get transformed through some process into some type of outputs. And so this is a classic way of viewing supply chains. Every process within a supply chain fits this, as well. So for order fulfillment, you think of the flow unit-- the thing that's moving and being processed-- are orders. You take as inputs the receipt of an order. And the output is a delivery. For production, you take receipt of the raw materials or components. You turn those into a completed product. For outbound logistics, you take as an input the finished goods at the additive manufacturing, and you turn those into a delivery to a customer. Procurement is everything from a purchase order converted into receipt of materials. And customer service, arrival of a customer to its departure. That's the life cycle of the process of that customer service. New product development, from when you start until you launch the product. And then, you can think of the cash-to-cash cycle time, which is really the expenditure of funds all the way to collection of revenue. So you look at that whole cash-to-cash cycle time can be viewed as a process metric for looking at the transformation of inputs into outputs. In fact, one of the first models we looked at in one of the first lectures of this whole course was looking at the SCOR model-- the Supply Chain Operations Reference model-- by the Supply Chain Council. And this encapsulates everything within this supply chain and breaks it down into a handful of different tasks-- sourcing, making, delivering. These are all the execution. There's a planning phase that goes above that. How do I plan for each of those? There's return for any return activities. And then, all the other activities that enable these things. But what this shows is that I'm converting things and I'm processing through. And then, I have the return cycle, as well. So it's also a process view. We also saw a process view of supply chains as it goes from the customer all the way back to the initial supplier. We showed this view as well. And we dived just a little deeper into it to give you some flavor. So each one of these cycles, how the customer orders from the retailer, that's really a consumer. How the retailer orders from its distributor. How the distributor orders from the manufacturer. The manufacturer orders from its supplier. And this cycle can go on and on and on. Now, what happens in each one of these cycles is up here, let's say, the supplier in that cycle-- in this case, it's the retailer-- has to market the product it has available. If this is a coffee shop, it has the coffee urns out there. If it's a bookstore, it has the books out there. If it's an online, an e-commerce, retailer, then it has things out on its website. So somehow, you're marketing your product. Same thing down here for your supplier. You're marketing your product for the manufacturer, whether it's in a catalog, website, however. Then, in the process, the buyer will place an order. Again, if it's a retailer on an e-commerce site, then I go and I click on the web site. I might go and place it actually physically if I'm at a physical bookstore. For down here, I might trigger an order through my production planning. So I place an order somehow. The supplier receives that order. It might be immediate. It might be longer-term. They fill the order somehow. Sometimes, if it's a bookstore, a physical book store, I'll give you that book right away. If it's online, then I set that up to be fulfilled and sent to you later. The buyer eventually receives that order. And then, we hope they pay for it. So each of these cycles consists of separate little processes. And they all have the same format, but they differ a little bit in the details. And they differ not just as I go in the different order cycles with customer replenishment, manufacturing, or procurement, but it also differs by industry, by company, by their strategy. And so all that I wanted to get across in this very quick discussion is to view supply chains as an input-output process. And it changes. The inputs and the outputs depend on where I am in my supply chain, the type of industry I am in, and my strategy of my company itself.
exercise:
Course context:
This course explores how to design and manage the physical, information, and financial flows that connect organizations across the supply chain. Using analytical frameworks, mathematical models, and real-world applications, you will learn to make strategic decisions related to supply chain network design, supply chain finance, supplier management and procurement, production and material requirements planning, distribution, Sales & Operations Planning (S&OP), and process and organizational design. By the end of the course you will be equipped to optimize supply chain networks, apply financial analysis to supply chain decisions, and lead cross-functional initiatives that balance operational excellence with strategic business objectives. This course is part of the MITx MicroMasters® Program in Supply Chain Management.

Syllabus:
CTL.SC2x Supply Chain Design

Program:
Part of the MITx MicroMasters Program in Supply Chain Management.

Course Description:
Supply Chain Design: SC2x explores how to design and manage the physical, information, and financial flows that connect organizations across the supply chain. Using analytical frameworks, mathematical models, and real-world applications, you will learn how to make strategic decisions related to network design, supply chain finance, supplier management, production planning, distribution, and organizational design.

Learning Objectives:
- Design and optimize supply chain networks
- Apply financial analysis to supply chain decisions
- Develop supplier management and procurement strategies
- Plan production and material requirements
- Coordinate Sales & Operations Planning (S&OP) processes
- Improve cross-functional communication and organizational performance

Prerequisites:
There are no formal prerequisites. However, because SC2x is a quantitative course, learners benefit from familiarity with algebra, spreadsheets, and introductory concepts in statistics, probability, and optimization. Learners seeking additional preparation may wish to complete Supply Chain Analytics: SC0x and Supply Chain Fundamentals: SC1x first.

Estimated Effort:
8–12 hours per week for 12 weeks.

Intended Audience:
Professionals and lifelong learners interested in supply chain management, operations, logistics, and business analytics.

Course Schedule:
New material for each week is released on Wednesdays at 15:00 UTC. Graded assignments are due on Wednesdays at 15:00 UTC on the specified deadline.
- Week 0 — Course Overview & Logistics (starts Jun 17, 2026)
- Week 1 — Basic Supply Chain Network Design (Jun 24 – Jul 8, 2026)
- Week 2 — Advanced Supply Chain Network Design (Jul 1 – Jul 15, 2026)
- Week 3 — Production Planning (Jul 8 – Jul 22, 2026)
- Week 4 — Sales & Operations Planning and Distribution Strategies (Jul 15 – Jul 29, 2026)
- Week 5 — Prep Week (Jul 22, 2026) — no graded assignment; review for the Midterm
- Week 6 — Midterm Exam (Jul 29 – Jul 31, 2026)
- Week 7 — Supplier Management (Aug 5 – Aug 19, 2026)
- Week 8 — Supply Chain Finance I (Aug 12 – Aug 26, 2026)
- Week 9 — Supply Chain Finance II (Aug 19 – Sep 2, 2026)
- Week 10 — Supply Chain Finance III and Organizational Design (Aug 26 – Sep 9, 2026)
- Week 11 — Prep Week (Sep 2, 2026)
- Week 12 — Final Exam (Sep 9 – Sep 11, 2026)

Key Dates:
Course starts Jun 17, 2026; Financial assistance application deadline Jul 8, 2026; Enrollment / Verification upgrade deadline (Certificate Track) Jul 22, 2026; Course ends Sep 11, 2026; Certificates available Sep 18, 2026.

Weekly Topics:
- Week 1 — Basic Supply Chain Network Design: Introduces supply chain design and reviews key concepts from SC1x. Formulate and solve network flow models using spreadsheets, then explore the Facility Location Problem, including its formulation and solution as a Mixed Integer Linear Program (MILP).
- Week 2 — Advanced Supply Chain Network Design: Combine the Facility Location problem with network flow models to formulate and solve larger network design problems including facility capacity, level of service, and inbound/outbound transportation. Cover modeling multiple products, echelons, and periods; discuss flexibility and robustness.
- Week 3 — Production Planning: Move from network design to working with internal and external manufacturing partners. Present the Fixed Planning Horizon model; introduce Bill of Materials (BOM), Material Requirements Planning (MRP), and Distribution Requirements Planning (DRP) systems.
- Week 4 — Sales & Operations Planning and Distribution Strategies: The S&OP process is presented and discussed, along with different distribution strategies and how e-commerce has affected the design of distribution networks.
- Week 5 — Prep Week: Catch up and review; addresses practical concerns of running a network design project, in preparation for the Midterm.
- Week 6 — Midterm Exam: Assesses understanding of concepts and methods covered in Weeks 1–4.
- Weeks 7–10 — Supplier Management and Supply Chain Finance: Procurement strategy and optimization-based procurement; accounting fundamentals and cost accounting; cash flows and discounted cash flows; supply chain finance, financial performance, and organizational/metric design.
- Week 12 — Final Exam.

Assessment & Grading:
The course separates a learning space (videos, quick questions, and practice problems with immediate feedback; weekly Graded Assignments as formative assessments, with solutions released after the due date) from an assessment space (the Midterm and Final Exams, which evaluate individual mastery; no solutions or individualized feedback are provided for exam questions). To pass the course and earn a Certificate, you must achieve a final course grade of at least 60%. Grade weights:
- Weekly Graded Assignments: 20%
- Midterm Exam: 35%
- Final Exam: 45%

Enrollment Track Access:
Graded Assignments for Weeks 1, 2, and 3 are available to both Audit and Certificate Track learners. Graded Assignments for Weeks 4, 7, 8, 9, and 10, the Midterm Exam, and the Final Exam are available only to Certificate Track learners.

Graded Assignments are released with each content week and may be completed any time before the deadline (deadlines are firm; no extensions). Point values are indicated per question; most questions allow two submission attempts; only the last submission is graded; you must click Submit for each answer. A green/red indicator shows correctness after submission, and official solutions and explanations are released after the deadline. Assignments remain available for review afterward.

Exams: the Midterm and Final together account for 80% of the final grade. They are open-book (you may consult course videos, slides, Key Concept Documents, and your own notes) and timed.
