[lecture_01_00_introduction_to_network_models]
We're going to start our discussion in designing the physical flow in the supply chain by introducing network models. So let's start with some common terms. There are many different types of networks that we're going to see. But they all consist of the same two things-- nodes and arcs. Nodes are simply points. And they also sometimes are called vertices. And so these could be distribution centers, customers, regions, plants, whatever. For our purposes, they'll be probably actual facilities. But they can be more abstract things and other network problems. Then you're going to have arcs, or edges, or links. And these are the things that connect the different nodes. And so we can see, this could be a road network. This could represent flows. This could represent how cash is used on different projects. You can make it represent so many different things. That's the beauty of networks. Now, a network then or a graph-- sometimes, you see graph theory and network theory-- it's just a collection of nodes and arcs. And there's a whole large domain here. If you're really interested, you can look into network models. We're going to focus on them specifically in the supply chain space. So there's some problems that you're going to see more often than others in supply chain that use networks. Let's talk about them briefly. And you've seen some of these before. First one, shortest path, so this is the problem if I want to go from node i to node j-- let's say that's New York and the j is Los Angeles-- so I have a road network. So the shortest path problem is finding what is the shortest, least cost, usually distance, through the network. What's my path from i to j? And so this is pretty much-- we use this every day. If we use any kind of mapping tool or software, either on our phone or in our car. So this is a classic algorithm. It's kind of the root of almost every other algorithm that we're going to talk about. Then you've got the traveling salesman problem. And what the traveling salesman problem does-- let me just clean this up just a little bit, there we go-- so I've got these nodes still and these arcs. And what the traveling salesman problem says, let's start at one node. Let's call that the home. And what I want to do is find a path-- or a tour rather that visits each node once and only once and then returns home. So let's say I'm going to go here. Then I'm going to go there. And I want to find the tour that is the shortest distance. And this is a problem that's been around for hundreds of years, seems trivial, but can be quite complicated. And so this is known as the traveling salesman problem. Then you have another problem called vehicle routing. Let me clean this little network up again, erase this. And so what this is, it's a little bit different, but it's very much related to the traveling salesman problem. Let's say I have a distribution center or a source node. So this is where a bunch of supply or physical product is. And then I have a bunch of locations I need to deliver to, whether these are stores, customers, whatever. So I've got this source node, the square, and I want to visit each of the demand nodes. And so I want to find the shortest tours-- and this is each vehicle, because I have multiple vehicles-- and what tour will they take to visit all the different sites to deliver their product and return. So what you have is a bunch of vehicle tours, or vehicle routing problem. So it's the least cost, usually least distance, tours for vehicles to distribute all the product that's demanded from a single source node. Now, the problem we're going to focus on the most in this lesson and the next several lessons is known as the min cost flow problem. And so what that consists of is a little bit different. So we're trying to find the least cost flows of a certain product or a commodity through our network that satisfies demand at certain nodes. So I have certain demand nodes, call these customers. And I want to flow it-- maybe I have some distribution centers here, and I have some plants or sources. So I have product that goes from the plants to the DCs. And then each DC can go to each separate customer. This plant can also go here. And this DC could also distribute here. This plant could also go here. You see, I have all these different flows. And so I want to figure out the lowest cost solution from my source nodes to my ultimate demand nodes in the least cost manner. So we'll talk a lot more about this problem. But as you see, as you get into this deeper, you'll see they all are very much related, and they're all tied to networks. So let's talk about our approach in this module. Here are some things we're going to do. Three things we will do when we talk about network modeling. First, our whole focus here is on the insights. I want you to understand what are the trade-offs involved with design, and especially, in this case, design of the physical flow. What are the trade-offs? How do I quantify them? What are the decisions that I'm making? I want you to understand the common approaches that are used in practice and each of their strengths and weaknesses. Second thing we're going to do is we're going to formulate math programs for optimization. We're going to solve these design problems using optimization tools. And we're going to formulate them into something called a math program. Now, there are other ways that we can solve or design a physical flow of a network. I could use "seat of pants," for example, which means my experience. I know that I always want to have a distribution center here in Atlanta. I always know I want to have a certain structure, kind of based on experience. And you'd be surprised, or maybe not surprised, how often that happens. You can also use other techniques, like heuristics. So if you know your domain is of a certain volume, I know I need that over x number of distribution centers. You have rules of thumb that can be applied. You could also use simulation. So I have my demand nodes out there. And I simulate what the demand is going to be. And I test out different network structures, number of distribution centers, how I flow. And the simulation will tell you which one is better or worse. It will never tell you what to do or suggest a new solution. It'll only evaluate and rate the solutions or trials that you give them. But what we're going to focus on is the most common method. And like I said, it's using optimization. So I'm going to show you how to formulate a math program, not to solve an individual problem, but solve a class of problems. And that's the critical thing, because what you're going to see is there are classes of different problems. And no matter how the data changes, you're solving the same problem. So this will also open the door for you to understand the literature a little better. So I'm going to be a stickler on the notation, because it's important, because it provides clarity in making sure that you understand the problem. Third thing we're going to do is we're going to solve these things in spreadsheets, and specifically Excel and LibreOffice. Why do we use spreadsheets? They're the easiest tool to learn with. Everyone has access to them. They're all there. However, no one-- no one that I've ever run into uses spreadsheets to do network design. We use them for many other things in practice. But for supply chain network design, the problems are too complex, too sophisticated. So people always use some kind of specialized software package. I don't know any company that uses a spreadsheet. However, we're going to solve the exact same problems using spreadsheets. So it's just going to be a slightly smaller scale, because again, I'm here to make sure you understand the insights. So something we're not going to use-- or we're not going to do, we're not going to dive deep into optimization theory. We don't have the time. And I didn't set the prerequisites for this class. It can be a very deep literature. It's a whole field. So there are other courses and references available for those that are interesting going deep. We're going to focus on applying optimization within spreadsheets to solve these math programs, to mine for insights. That's what we're going to be doing in this course. So what's happening the rest of this lesson? We're only going to look at two problems. We're going to look at the transportation problem, which is kind of the simplest, network flow problem out there. We're going to work an example. We're going to formulate the model. We're going to solve it in a spreadsheet. Then we're going do the same thing for the next level of problem, called the transshipment problem. We're going to work an example, formulate the model, solve it in a spreadsheet. And then we're going to talk a little bit about, OK, how do you do this modeling approach? So that's what we're going to do for the rest of the lesson.

[lecture_01_01_the_transportation_problem]
Let's get started with the simplest network flow model transportation problem. So let me introduce the problem we're going to work with, SandyCo. So here's the problem background. SandyCo is two facilities. These plants mine, clean, and sort sand for use in cement, children's play boxes, small beaches, whatever. They distribute this sand from their two plants to three different customer regions, where it is packaged and sold. So each plant has a specific maximum weekly supply of available sand, each region has an expected minimum weekly required demand, and the cost to distribute a ton of sand differs between each plant and each region pairing due to the distance and other factors. Very simple problem, and the objective is how much sand should SandyCo ship from each plant to each region each week to meet the demand at the lowest planned distribution cost? It's pretty straightforward. So how do I start this? I always like to draw a picture. And being in the United States, Western culture, I always go left to right. Don't know why, but I would have raw materials come in on the left, finish up at the right. So I'm starting with two plants, Plant 1 and Plant 2, and I've got three customer regions named Region 1, 2, and 3. Now, connecting these are my arcs, and these are the connections from each plant to each region. And so the objective here is to minimize the flow, so I need some notation, so let's get those out there. I've got these plants, I's, and the regions or J's, so my flow is going to be in IJ, from I to J. So these indices are simply subscripts I'm going to use to keep track of things because things will be a little complicated as we introduce more complexity to the model. So I always have some input data. I have available supply of sand from Plant I. I'm going to call that SI, and so this is S1. I have a certain amount of sand available there. S2 here, and I have a certain demand at each region. So I'm going to have a D1, a D2, a D3, and I need to make sure that I don't exceed the supply coming out of each plant and that I meet the minimum, at least, required demand at each customer region. Now, let me give you some notation, make sure we understand. S_I equals available supply of sand from Plant I in tons-- it's always good to give the units-- and this sign means for all I's in the set S, and the set S is essentially, I'm going to circle them, all my supply nodes. D_j, demand for sand in region J in tons-- good to give the units-- for all J's in the set D, and these are my demand nodes. And now I have this other input data that I need, the cost for sending sand from Plant I to region J. And this is dollars per ton, and so it's just a straight variable cost. And it's for all I's and J's, so that's the cost. So C11, C11 is the cost of sending material from Plant 1 to Region 1. Pretty straightforward. So my decision variable is this x_i,j-- how much am I going to flow on each one of these arcs? And so to solve this, I'm going to need some input data. So we'll talk about what the pros and cons and how you get this data, but let's assume we have it right now. So I know my supply at Plant 1 is 100 units or 10 tons. Plant 2 has a slightly bigger capacity, 125. My demands for 1 are 25 and it's 95 tons and 80 tons, and I said this is tons per week. And then I have my costs. Costs for moving from Plant I to Region 1 is a $250 per ton. From 2 to 3 is $460 per ton. So you have all the information you need here. So let's think about what are we trying to do? I'm going to try to minimize all the flows because this is the only thing I'm deciding, this x_i,j. Everything else is given to me. It's input data. I want to find each of the x_i,j's to minimize the total cost. What's the total cost going to be? So the objective of any math program is to find an extreme solution. Extreme solutions are minimums or maximums, so you're going to either minimize or maximize this function subject to meeting some constraints. And then you have demand constraints. And these simply say that everything flowing into a certain demand node has to at least be equal to the demand that's required there, the D's. And all my flow has to be 0 or greater. So this is the granddaddy of flow problems, the transportation problem. We then made it a little more complicated, because that's what we like to do. And I added what's known as hubs, or transshipment points. Let me just circle those. So now, we have sources. We have sinks, or demand nodes. And I have transshipment hubs, or mixing centers. And the only difference between the transportation problem and the transshipment problem is that I have to add in these flow conservation constraints for any of the transshipment hubs. So this is simply saying what goes in must be what goes out. So what goes in for A has to be equal to what goes out. What goes in to B has to be equal to what goes out. And it's very logical. You need this to enforce a network structure. Now, a lot of limitations-- these are the simplest problems in the world, even though they're complicated the first time you go through. But we had very simple cost structures. We only had variable cost on the arcs. There were no fixed arc costs. There no node costs. In fact, I assumed that you're going to use every single one of these. I didn't have you decide which plant to use, or which DC, or give you the choice of whether to open one or not. We ignored that and said, you're going to use everything that's there. The question is, how much are you going to use it. We had a single commodity. I assumed that the sand you produced here at plant one was the same one desired at each of the customer locations. There isn't specific commodities or product types. I assumed that they're all fungible. What I produce in one can flow to anywhere-- single commodity. I also assumed a deterministic solution. My demand is a constant. It's an input. I didn't allow any variability of the demand, of the supply, or the flow. This gets more complicated-- and we'll talk about this later on-- but for the first cut, we assumed very deterministic solutions. Also we assumed everything was uncapacitated. I had no capacity constraints here at my distribution centers or at my plants. I just had a supply limitation. But you could think, it'd be a little more complicated if I said, you know what, this distribution center or packaging center A can only handle a certain level. And then that would force things to happen. And we'll get into that. Same thing with arcs, it might be that this arc can only take a certain amount of volume. We assumed total uncapacitation. There is no capacity limits. Infinite can flow. And so you can kind of see the path we're going to do. We're going to reduce some of these limitations, loosen some of these assumptions. And the models get a little more complex, but they get a little more realistic. We also did something that's really fundamental. And you'll use this throughout the course and probably beyond in your career. And that's the whole idea of formulating a model. And there is a classic process for this. The first thing you have to do-- and these are kind of common sense-- read and understand the problem thoroughly. 90% of the time, if you have a problem, you didn't read the problem that carefully. And in this class, and in other ones, if English is not your first language, read it twice, because a lot of times, the wording is critical. Once you understand that, figure out, OK, what are the things I'm trying to decide? What are my decision variables-- what the spreadsheets call changing variables? What are the things I'm trying to decide? Then you say, OK, what is the thing I'm trying to minimize or maximize? What is the objective function? Am I minimizing cost? Am I maximizing profit? What am I trying to do? Then you say, OK, what am I subject to? What are my constraints? I need to make sure everything coming out of a supply node doesn't exceed supply. Everything going into a demand node has at least be the required demand. I need to make sure that for transshipment hubs, what goes in, what goes out-- all the different constraints. And then you identify upper and lower bounds. For this, we only had a lower bound on the decision variable, zero. You couldn't be below zero. But there might be cases where you need to have a certain level, a minimum. And then you solve it. It's very important that you understand your assumptions and limitations. There is often a naive assumption that these complex models are perfect. And they are not. They all have assumptions. They all have limitations. They're models. They are never reality. They're abstractions of reality. And as I move closer and closer to reality, it gets more complicated. And we're building in more assumptions. So the most important takeaway from this is be cognizant and recognize the assumptions you're making in any model that you put together. All right, that's it for this lesson. So please use the comments for any questions. If you have suggestions, let us know. Use a discussion forum. And I'll let you look at Dexter, wondering how he can optimize his way to his own ball through the fence.

[lecture_01_05_facility_location_models]
OK so we've discussed network flow models and we've shown how to formulate them and solve them. In all those models though, we assumed that every facility that was there was used. Now we're going to restrict that problem a little bit and focus in on the decision of which facilities to use, which facilities to open. So this whole lesson is about facility location models. So we're going to start very simple in this first segment-- in this lesson-- and we're going to start talking about continuous single facility problems. By continuous, what I mean is you're on a Euclidean Space and I can place a facility anywhere within that space. So I just want to find the optimal space and it's a single facility, which means I'm only looking for one location and we're going to talk are two different ways to do this. One is known as the Weber problem and that's finding the minimum weighted distance from each of the points that I have and the other is known as center of gravity, which comes from physics. And we'll explain these and we'll talk about the strengths and weaknesses of each of them. Then we're going to go and look at a slightly different approach. Instead of having a continuous set of options to pick from, where you can have any kind of location, you're going to have a set of candidate locations and sometimes this is called a network facility location problem. And we're going to solve this using our old friend the mixed integer linear program which we started talking about in previous lessons, and we're going to show how to formulate it and we're going to show how to solve it in spreadsheets. And then we're going to expand it to multiple locations because we want to talk about the trade-offs-- because there are a lot of trade-offs involved with having a single facility, two facilities, three-- and you're going to have trade offs between the costs-- different types of costs-- as well as level of service and we'll express those and show those, formulate the problems and solve them. And then finally we'll wrap it up, talking about sensitivity analysis using these models because as we've tried to get across, the benefit of these models is not necessarily just to come up with an answer, you know, the answer's six, the real benefit is that it allows you to explore the options in the robustness of a solution. You can explore other potential solutions so it really gives you an idea of understanding the problem better-- that's the real benefit of using these models. All right so let's get started.

[lecture_01_06_continuous_location_models]
Let's start with the simplest location model, the single location model on a continuous surface. So the problem objective of this is to find one location that minimizes the transportation costs from this point to K different destination points. This is also known as the Weber problem, named after Alfred Weber, who first formulated this problem in the early 1900s. So the idea here is, I'm on a Euclidean Space. So it's a tabletop. I can go directly from point to point. There's no underlying network. The center location could be anywhere on the space. So think of a tabletop, and I'm putting one cup anywhere on that table. And the transportation cost is calculated as the distance multiplied by the volume or the weight. So if I have something that's 10 units going 2 miles, that would be 20 unit miles-- same thing as 20 units going 1 mile. So it's the transportation cost, distance times the weight. All right, so here's what the formulation would look like. It should look kind of familiar. I'm minimizing something, and I have my locations. Each location is subscripted k. So I'm saying I'm trying to minimize this function, and the minimized distance is going to be z. So z is equal to the summation for all the points k within this larger set K. And each of those is multiplied by its weight, w_k, times the distance. And that's what this function is, D_k of x, y. So x and y are my decision variables. And this is the x-coordinate, or horizontal coordinate, and the y-coordinate for my center point. So I'm trying to minimize this distance. And my two decision variables are x and y. Those are the variables that I'm trying to find the answer to. So because I'm in Euclidean space, this distance is simply the square root of the sum of the squared differences in the x and the y directions. So this is, x_k is, for the k-th location, or the point, it's its x-coordinate. And here's its y-coordinate. And I'm subtracting off from the center location, squaring that, adding it to that squared difference of the y location-- square root. And that gives me the distance from the k-th point to the center point. So I want to find and select the x and y that minimizes this function. All right, so we can solve this in Excel. And let me give you an example. And we'll use this example for the rest of this week. The New England Root Beer Distributors-- NERD. And at NERD, we serve root beer in New England. And we sell our products currently in just three cities-- Boston, Springfield, and Providence, Rhode Island. So here we are up in Boston. Springfield is out west. And Providence is down south just a little bit. So this is our market. And each one of the cities sells a certain amount of root beer and marketing is giving that Boston sells 425 units. Call it per week, or whatever time period: Providence sells 320, and Springfield sells 220. And the question is, where should I set up my distribution center to service these three markets? Now, I've given each of the cities a coordinate. So they have an x and a y coordinate. So Providence is at x-coordinate 86, y-coordinate 40. Boston is at 100, 80. And Springfield, over to the west, is 20, 60. So these are x, y coordinates. I think it's centered down here on New Haven down here, further to the left. It'll make sense a little later. And so what we're trying to do is find where is the location that would minimize the weighted distance. So where in this triangle is the distance that minimizes the distance traveled for each one of the units, for 425 units from Boston, 320 units Providence, and for Springfield, 220 units. So where will I locate that? If you think about it, you know that it's going to be somewhere within this triangle. There's no way that you'll have a shorter distance that falls outside. And I'll let you think about that, and prove that to yourself. So let's see how we solve this in a spreadsheet. This is restating the problem. And here's my function I'm trying to minimize. And so all I've done is taken the table, and put it into a spreadsheet. You can see that column B shows the weight for each city. Column C is the x-coordinate. Column D is the y-coordinate. And column E, what I've got is the distance. Now, it's finding the distance from what? Well, it's from these x, y coordinates that I put down here in cells C6 and D6. So what this is saying is that Boston is 17.54 miles away from this location. So if I typed in different values down here in cell C6 and D6, that location would change. Column F is simply the weighted distance. I multiply it by the weight for each of the cities. And then down here in cell F6, I'm simply summing them up. Because what do I want to do? I want to find the x and y-coordinates that minimize this value, that's currently 31,179. I can do this using Solver. So what's my objective function? It's F6. It's this cell right here. I'm trying to minimize that. And what cells am I going to change? The cells that are right here in the x and y, that I have highlighted in yellow. Now, for constraints, it's a little different, because I'm solving this with a non-linear solver, because this is not a linear equation. I have to put max and mins. So I set the x-coordinate max to 200, the y-coordinate max to 200. And I'm making sure that they're not going to be negative. And so that's all that these constraints are doing. They're just putting bounds on my decision variables-- have to be greater than 0 and less than 200. But since the furthest I can be-- I'll never be more than 180 away-- so it's pretty safe. Now I'm using a non-linear solving function. Make sure you pick that. If I press Solve, it'll tell you where the answer is. It'll be 89, 66. So this is the location that minimizes, like I said, the weighted distance from each of these cities. Now we're going to talk about a different approach called center of gravity. Center of gravity is something that comes to us very intuitively-- comes from physics. And the way to think about it is, suppose that this triangle was made out of a very rigid material like plywood. And I had each of the cities, instead of the weight being units of root beer, let's say they were pounds or kilograms. So I had 420 kilograms here at Boston. I had 320 kilograms here at this location, and I have 220 kilograms here. What center of gravity is trying to do is say, OK, where would I put a center point to balance all of these? If I had this rigid board, where would I put my finger to balance them all? It's the point of balance, or the centroid. This is what center of gravity means. So how would I find that? Well, all you're going to do is take the weighted average of each of the x and y coordinates. So let's look at the x one. So I would say that the x for the centroid-- that's what this is-- going to be equal to 425, which is the weight of Boston, divided by 965, which is simply the sum of these weights, just summing them all up. So this is the percent of the volume that Boston represents times its x-coordinate of 100. I'll do the same thing for Providence. 320 divided by 965 times its x coordinate, and the same thing for Springfield. And I get a weighted average of 77. Do the same thing for the y coordinates, and I get a y coordinate of 62. So this tells me my center of gravity is that blue dot, right there at x coordinate 77, y coordinate 62. Why are they different? I bet some of you thought they'd be the same. They're different because they're doing very different things. The Weber one, the single location up here, the red dot, minimized the distance times the weight. The centroid-- what its actually doing if you look at it-- it's minimizing the distance squared times the weight. So in other words it's implicitly over-weighting the further locations. So the further it's away, it's like a seesaw. The further I am on on that seesaw, the more my weight is going to count, and not just in a linear fashion, but in a squared fashion. This is how physics works. This is what a centroid is. It's not, however, minimizing the distance times the weight. So when we use this, we want to use the Weber method. We want to use the method that minimizes distance and volume, not distance squared volume. That makes sense in physics, in the physical world. Doesn't make sense when I'm designing a network. So picking between using center of gravity and Weber method for the minimum distance weight, we want to use that Weber point. However, the Weber point's not perfect. And so here I'm showing what the average distances are. And you'll see that the center of gravity, the average weighted distance for location is higher than it is for the Weber one. However, even the Weber one's not perfect. So let's think about this. So the Weber method would say, OK, locate this right outside of Walpole, the little red dot. The problem is we don't live in Euclidean space. So we don't know what's there. There might be a swamp located there. Every location is neither feasible nor equally desirable. Also, there is an underlying network of highways and rails. If anyone's been up in this area, you know there are these certain roads that you want to be on, because they have the better access. This method ignores that. So in practice, in large-scale models, we don't really do it this way. We don't look at the continuous location. We do that to get insights, but when push comes to shove and you're actually selecting a location, you use candidates. You have candidate locations. You'd say something like, OK, I'm going to consider locating it in Woonsocket, Webster, or Hopkinton. Which one is the best selection? Rather than saying, OK, if I could locate anywhere, where should I go? That's always an insightful, kind of interesting thing to do. But in practice, most of these models use location candidates. And that's what we'll do next.

[lecture_01_07_network_facility_location]
Now we're going to look at the network facility location problem, where instead of looking on a continuous space and finding one location out of any possible location, we're going to have candidates to select from. So the objective is very similar still. We want to find the location or locations from a list of candidates, rather than a continuous space, that minimizes the total costs of delivering from this point to the n or k different destination points over a given network. So this is almost identical to the problem statement we had before. The difference is we have a list of candidates now. So how are we going to solve this? We're going to use the mixed integer linear program. And so what I'm showing you here is the formulation. Calm down. It's not that complicated. Let's break it down. We know it's a math program, because we see the objective function, and we see that we're minimizing something. We see we're subject to a series of constraints. So OK, I know where I am. So now let's look at a little more, because it shouldn't be that unfamiliar to you. Because in fact, this is the transportation problem with some extra constraints. We've still got the objective function that's minimizing the cost of the flows, the cij's times the xij's, which is what we did last time. We still have the supply constraints that's making sure that everything from the source nodes doesn't exceed what I have available there for all the I's within the supply set. I'm doing the same thing on the demand side, right? Where I'm looking for all the flow going into each of the j locations that are in the demand set, that they have to be at least as much as what was demanded. So this is the exact same formulation as we had for the transportation problem, along with our non-negativity constraints for the flows. I can't flow negative volume. The only thing we're doing is adding some constraints. So let's talk about those, and we'll start from the bottom. I'm introducing a new decision variable, this y. y_i is a binary variable. That means it can only take two values, either 0 or 1. It's not between 0 and 1. It's either 0 or 1. We use this to indicate whether to open or not open a facility. So y_i is equal to 1 if distribution center i is opened. Otherwise, it's equal to 0. And this is for all the i's that are in the supply set. OK, so that's the first one. Now, we might want to have a set of constraints that limit the number of facilities I open. And so I'm introducing two new constraints here. One is setting a minimum value p_min, which is an input value I give, and then an upper bound, p_max. So p is the number of facilities I can open, min is the lower bound, max is the upper bound. How do I enforce that? Well, I'm simply going to sum all of the y_i's. So if I sum those up, since it's 1 if it's open, 0 if it's closed, if I sum all the y_i's up, that's the number of facilities I'm opening. And so these two constraints just set the min and the max, make sure I fall within those two numbers. Pretty straightforward. All right. Now, let me go to the top, because we've added something to the objective function. And what is this? Well, I've got this y_i, right? That's the decision whether I open a facility or not. It's a binary variable. And I'm multiplying it by this f_i. And that's simply the fixed cost for opening, DC_i. So this problem that we did, that we're looking at, this could be for flow over the course of a week, a month, a year. And so this fixed cost is the cost for opening this facility for that period of time. So it could be labor. It could be lease. It could be any kind of thing that doesn't change with the amount of volume going through. So the f_i is independent of how much volume is going through. If I have one unit going through, I pay the same amount. If I have 1,000 units going through, I pay the same amount. So it's a fixed cost with respect to the volume flowing through it. So I've got two elements to my objective function now. I have these variable transportation costs that I'm circling, and then I've got these fixed facility location costs. So variable costs are based on the flow. The facility costs are based on the locations that I open. All right. We're almost there. We have one other constraint that I need to talk about, and that's this one. Because up until this point, I have two decision variables, or two types of decision variables-- the flow and the locations. And they're totally independent. If I didn't include this logical, or linking, constraint, what would happen? Well, the x's would do whatever they need to do, just like in the transportation problem. They'd solve the demand, make sure I met that and didn't exceed supply, but the y's would stay at 0. In other words, if I didn't have something connecting them together, then this would just be the transportation problem all over again. It would say, OK, all facilities are open. They're all free. So we need something to tie together the x's and the y's. So here's the same formulation. I just made it a little smaller. And I boxed the constraint we're going to talk about, this linking constraint. And so what does this mean? Well, what this is really doing is saying, we can't deliver from a distribution center unless that distribution center is open. Because if we don't do this, then the objective function, right here, is going to force it closed. It's going to say, hey, it cost me money to open it? If I don't have to, I won't. What if it's not included? Well, we talked about this. It becomes a transportation problem. If this constraint is not here, right, if we got rid of it, you essentially have the transportation problem, and there's nothing different. But it is here, and so let's talk about why it works. OK. So let's think about it, because all that it's saying is that any flows I have from i to j-- and so let's draw. I have node i, and it's going to node j. If there's any flow on here, then we want to make sure that i is turned on, that y_i is turned on. So what if xij is 0? What value will y take if xij is 0? Well, if it's 0, then I'd have 0 minus this M, and we'll talk about it, but just assume right now it's a really big number. So 0 minus a big number times y less than or equal to 0. So y could actually be any number at all, and then it would satisfy this constraint, which means that y would be forced down to 0. So if there's no flow, then it's going to go to 0. However, what happens if I have one unit of flow going through, just one unit? So now, it would be 1 minus big number M times y has to be less than or equal to 0. What value does y has to take? It has to become 1. It can't be 0 any more. It has to be turned on. So what this constraint is doing, it's forcing, by keeping it negative, it's saying if xij is positive, then yi has to be 1. Or if not, then it won't be less than 0. Play with this and see how it works. It's a nice, it's a very clever constraint. What's the best value for these M's? Well, as a general rule, you want it to be big. But technically, you don't want to make it too big, because then it slows down some of the solution time, and it takes a little more effort. So a good rule of thumb, you set the Mij greater than any value that the xij, the flow, can possibly take. In fact, it's OK, it's perfectly acceptable, to set all the Mij's to the same number. You set it equal to the sum of all the demand, right? Because the flow on each individual arc can never be greater than the total demand required. So that's one way to do it, to set a really big M. And that's why we make it just a big M. I'll talk later about how you can make that a little more finer, because it makes the solution a little faster. But it's perfectly acceptable to set the M, just to make it a really big number. Because what you really want to do is connect the x's and the y's. So what we're going to do next is put this into a spreadsheet, and we're going to solve it.

So my solution is $89,478 per week. And what DC am I opening? I'm opening up Worcester because that's a 1. The other ones are 0. In this slice of the spreadsheet I'm showing the flows from Worcester. And so you're seeing that I flow from Worcester to Boston 425 units, 12 units from Worcester to Brattleboro, 12 units, 43 Worcester to Concord, and so forth. So what the model has done, it's shown me which facility to open and how much is flowing. So this is my solution for this model. Now the question is I just opened one DC. Worcester is the optimal location amongst the five candidates that gives me the lowest total cost, fixed cost, and flow cost that I forced it to be one. Is one the right number? Should open more facilities? So that's what we're going to look at next.

[lecture_01_09_tradeoffs_with_multiple_locations]
So if we want to decide whether to open multiple facilities, we need to think about the trade-offs that are involved. So in any kind of supply chain design, what you're trying to do is find a balance between competing interests, and it's mainly costs and service. And so if I look at costs, they're not all the same. There's a whole bunch of different types of costs that I incur. Inventory costs, there could be cycle stock, safety stock, pipeline stock, all these things change depending on the number of locations I have. Facility costs obviously increase as I increase the number of facilities. It could be the amount I'm paying for property, the energy to open it, the insurance, the labor, the fixed cost involved with that. Transportation costs for moving product both into the DC and out to the customers, and the inbound and the outbound cost don't always move in the same direction. The operating or labor cost, obviously, as I increase the number of facilities, my labor cost probably increased because I have more places to staff. And the asset cost, the cost of the facilities themselves. If I have a private fleet, those assets. But then I also have different service characteristics. And so if you think of service, I think about customer satisfaction. Right? I want to make sure the customer is happy and satisfied. When they order something, they get it. I want to look at sales growth. I want to make sure that it's growing. I want to make sure the profit is growing. Increased market share, all these kind of things. The challenge is in supply chain design, this is a strategic model. And so I'm looking things one, two, three years out. Very hard to measure those. So we can also think of something like response time. Am I able to respond quicker? And that whole idea of a level of service. And that's what we're really going to focus on here. And we'll talk more in depth. But in general, what we're trying to do with the multiple locations is find the balance that improves profitability, which means keeping an eye on our costs, then that increases our customer satisfaction or our service. So let's focus in on the cost first because they don't always move in the same direction. So I've a number of costs here, and as I increase the number of DCs, as that increases, obviously the cost will increase. The warehousing or facility costs because I've got more facilities. It's almost a step function, but it actually is an increasing step function because as I open more distribution centers, I'm locating them closer to customers, which means the property value be more expensive because you're moving into denser areas. So it's an actually increasing step function. Then you also have inventory costs. Now we know that inventory cost will increase as I have more distribution centers, and it doesn't increase in a linear fashion. It's actually a square root. And so that's based on mainly the impact of the safety stock. As we add more distribution centers, we assume we're adding them closer to the customers. Right? So the outbound, the yellow, this outbound delivery cost will decrease because I'm closer. So as I have more and more, I'll be closer and closer. You see a lot of online retailers opening up more DCs closer to urban areas so they can reduce this cost and actually increase their-- improve their response time as well. But it decreases the outbound delivery cost. Interestingly, as I have more of these distribution centers, my inbound, the cost for transportation from the plants into these DC's increases. Why? One, there's more of them, but the other is I have less volume going to each of them. So it's a lower concentration. So what maybe I could have done for one or two DC's, to ship by rail or by full truckload, which is the lowest per unit cost modes, I now might have to use LTL or multi-stop truckload. So as I increase the number of DC's my inbound costs will actually increase. So you'll see that I have both increasing and decreasing costs here. So we can see where there will be an optimal location. And this is an optimal number of DCs. And so that's what we're going to do in terms of cost. But now, we've got that whole other side of service. So when I talk about level of service, it means different things to different people. It's know, it's-- I don't know any company where it's just one thing. It's usually whole array of different things. And the problem is, which levels of service can we include in the strategic model? Now previously, we've looked at tactical levels of service such as Cycle Service Level, which if you remember back from previous classes, is looking at the probability that you stock out during a replenishment time, or the probability you don't stock out, rather, in a replenishment cycle, the Item Fill Rate, the percent of the items you ordered that actually come in, and then the cost penalties for each of those. So these are well-established tactical level service indicators, but they don't fit for a strategic design because they're influenced by stocking level not the location of facilities. You'll notice in the models that we've done so far, that we'll continue doing, inventory doesn't play a role. I'm just making my decisions based on mainly facility costs and transportation costs. So we can't use these levels, these metrics explicitly, but in strategic design, what we'll try to do is use distance to customer as a proxy. And so this is the best way we can get at in measuring the opportunity that you'll be able to meet these tactical service objectives. So the closer I am to the customers, the higher the probability, the easier it is for someone to be able to meet these CSL or IFR metrics. So we're going to use the distance to customers as a proxy here. Now this seems easy, but there are many different ways to measure this. So one is, I say allowable distance. So I say no customer is allowed to be more than x, 75 miles from the DC. So I make sure that there's a maximum level. Or I could say that at least 30% of all customers need to be within 20 miles. So you have some kind of absolute allowable distances. You might also have an average distance so that the average distance for customer to DCs has to be less than 22.6 miles. Whatever. Or the weighted average, so that you favor customers or regions that have higher volumes, the weighted average has to be less than some distance. So these are different ways to do it, and these are just two ways to do it. There are many different metrics. These are very commonly used. And so how would I model these? Well, one way is to not include it in the model but report it after each run. So if I do a run, then I say, OK, what is the average distance? What is the percent of customers that are within a certain distance? So I report it. The next level would be add a constraint to enforce it to happen. And so you'd say the average distance shall not be more than 20 miles. And I'll show you how to add that in as a constraint. And then the highest level, as you go from really passive to very active, I can change the objective function. There's nothing that says I can't make one of these distance equations the objective function. Minimize the average weighted distance from DCs to customers. If I make that my objective function, then it will minimize. It'll open as many facilities as possible close to customers, and then you'll be able to find out what the cost is, buy you're no longer minimizing the cost. So what we're going to do is start with this, and then we'll go and look at the constraints. So let's get started.

So what it's saying is if there's a 1, then if I'm serving that destination from the selected distribution center, it's either in 50 miles or it's not. [lecture_01_12_key_take_aways]
OK, that was a big lesson. We covered a lot of material here. So what are the key takeaways? Well, we actually looked at solving one problem-- facility location problems. Where should I open my facilities? And we looked at in in two ways. We solved them two different ways. The first, we made an assumption that it was a continuous selection. Any place was equally likely. And so we looked at two different methods here-- the center of gravity method-- which minimized the volume times the distance squared, which is similar to what we'd see in physics-- and then Weber model, which minimized the volume times the distance. And that's what this function is for. And if you're going to use this for network design, you really want to use the Weber model, because that's what you're really worried about. You're worried about minimizing the distance traveled. This is very intuitive-- the center of gravity method-- but it really doesn't quite work for what we're looking for for network design. But in reality, well, looking at continuous selections is fun. It's interesting to see where that center would be. You don't use it in practice, because every location is not the same. They're not all fungible. Something's near a highway. Something's not. It could be that your center location is in the middle of a lake. So you really want to look at candidates. So when you look at discrete candidate selection, you're going to use a mixed integer linear program. And the trade-offs are going to be between many different costs, and subject to different level of service and other constraints. And the costs are going to be-- what we've looked at-- is outbound transportation costs and fixed facility or DC costs. This was the formulation that we used. And there are many different formulations. We'll continue to add to this and modify it. But what we had was the objective function, which is minimizing my cost, my variable transportation cost, and my fixed facility costs. I had constraints that made sure I kept within what I had available for supply. I kept and made sure my demand was met. I made sure that if I had a flow coming from a distribution center, I actually opened the distribution center. I had constraints that set the minimum and maximum number of distribution centers I could open. I introduced a series of constraints that set a minimum level of service in terms of maximum average distance. The minimum percentage of the demand within 50 miles had to be at least a certain amount. And then I had my non-negativity constraints for my continuous variables, which are the flows, the x_ij's. And the y_i's are binary, either 0 or 1. So the tool-- we use this as a decision support tool. And it informs the decision making process. It doesn't make the decision. You always want to conduct sensitivity analysis whenever you run these models. Because as you do more of these models, you start noticing some things. For example, optimization models are decision support tools. They will only suggest solutions. They shouldn't dictate a solution to you. They're deterministic. They assume perfect data. And they will do anything to save a dollar. If cost is in your objective function, you're asking this model to do anything to save that one additional dollar. So you need to do sensitivity analysis to understand, well, what did it give up to find that last dollar? Maybe it's something that doesn't make sense in reality. So they're deterministic. And we'll talk about this later, in that the data you gave them-- we assume that demand doesn't change-- that there is no variability. If I say demand in Boston for root beer is 420 units, it is 420 units. So we'll loosen that and see, OK, how do I get some stochastic or some variability built in for robuster models? We assume that the data is perfect. If I tell you the transportation cost is 3.2, it's 3.2. So the model doesn't take into consideration if my data is not perfect. And like I said, it does anything-- anything-- to reduce the cost if that's in the objective function. Your model will never include everything and have complete and perfect information. You can try to make it as realistic as possible, but there's a trade-off. As you make your model more and more complex, trying to make it more and more complete, you're making an assumption that if I get more precise, it's going to be more accurate. And that's actually not the case. Because you're making more and more assumptions as I include more data, I'm assuming that more data is perfect. So there's a trade-off there of how much you want to include in the model. Because again, this is a decision support tool. And the key word there is support. It doesn't make the decision. So the real benefit of these models, in my opinion, is for sensitivity analysis, because you can quantify and analyze the impact of doing certain things. We added, removed, changed constraints, such as forcing DC's to open or close. We haven't started playing with it yet, but we will. What if the capacity of these distribution centers change? Because maybe I want to expand an existing facility, instead of opening a new one. How do I test that? You can also do things like changing the input values. What happens if the cost changes? The distances might not change, but what if the price of fuel doubles or is cut in half? What impact will that have on my design? I could also modify level of service requirements. What if I change these? How will that impact my solution? And I can change the objective function. Right now, I only have outbound costs and facility costs. There are other things I could throw in there as well. And so changing these things allows you to see a more robust, and get a better idea of the trade-offs that you're making. So we have this somewhat complicated model. It's pretty complicated the first time through. But actually, it's very, very simple. Because this is a small list of things we are not considering. We didn't consider capacity on the arcs or nodes. We assumed that every DC could handle anything you threw at it. There's no issues with capacity on the transportation arcs. We ignored manufacturing locations. We ignored inbound transportation costs. There are no handling costs at the facilities. We assumed that all the transportation costs were variable per unit, and not fixed at all. We ignored all inventory costs. We considered one product. We all know that companies have multiple products, categories of products, or families. We ignored all that, and went to a single product. We looked at one time period. We know that what I do in February influences what I can do in March. So wouldn't it be nice to have a model that takes that into account? And we didn't dive deep, again, into the optimization models, the trade-offs it made, and the solution techniques. We're not going to cover that in this course. But some of these other things-- we'll start playing with these. We'll start saying, OK, what does this do? What does increasing capacity at a facility-- what does that do to my solution? And so we'll start playing with these more in the next set of lessons. So hopefully, you got a lot out of this lesson. Please if you have any questions, use the discussion forum for comments, any questions that you might have. In this lesson, we're going to combine the different techniques we've learned in the two previous lessons into one model. We'll take the network optimization we learned two lessons ago, and combine it with the facility location models we just learned the previous lesson and tie those together into what's known as a supply chain network design model. Now we're going to use this through an example, and it should be somewhat familiar to you. It's an extension of the New England Root Beer Distributors case we've been talking about all this whole week. So we're going to call this NERD four, and there's a case study write up attached to this video in the reference section. If you have not read that yet, please do. Let me just go over the case objectives. The objective of the case is to understand how NERD, a distributor of root beer in the Northeast of the United States, should explore and restructure its network. And there are two main things they're looking at is one, should they source from this new supplier that's in Bellows Falls? And should they reduce some of their distribution centers? Remember from reading the case, you'll remember that they grew those DCs through acquisitions. They didn't necessarily want to open up distribution centers always where they were, but they acquired a company and that's where their facilities were. So that's why they kept it. So some of the details, we know that NERD sources its product, which are barrels of root beer from a single plant in Scranton, Pennsylvania. And it ships its products by truckload carrier to five distribution centers located in Massachusetts, Rhode Island, and New Hampshire. The product is stored at these DCs, and is then distributed by a local carrier to the 12 different cities it sells-- it has markets in. And each city is supposed to be dedicated to a single distribution center, but if you read the case, you'll see that's not always the case. It doesn't always happen that way even though it's supposed to. The distribution centers vary in size, there are three large ones that can handle 1,000 barrels a week and they're in Boston, Providence, and Worcester. And then there's two smaller ones that handle 500 barrels a week in Nashua and Springfield. So this is what your network looks like. Product comes from Scranton, delivered to all five of the DCs, and then they in turn, are supposed to deliver to their primary cities. And you can see that each city is dedicated to one distribution center. However, as I said earlier, it doesn't always work like that. Sometimes, Springfield might send some cases of beer up to Brattleboro or down to somewhere else. So this is how it's supposed to go, but we'll talk more about that. So the questions we want to consider. Is it the right number of DCs? Right now, I've got five. These red ones. These red cities in Nashua, Worcester, Boston, Providence, and Springfield. Should we consider sourcing from Bellows Falls? And just so you know, this is where Bellows Falls is. That's where the other plant is. Should I ship from there to any of my DCs, all of my DCs, some of my DCs, how much? And then does this configuration, that network I have, does it provides sufficient level of service? And we'll talk more about that. And then finally, does this current configuration-- can it handle an increase in demand? What if my sale for root beer goes up? Can my network handle that? Am I building a solution that's very fragile to the existing demand? So these are the questions we want to solve. We're going to formulate a model, but the first step we need to do is figure out the data. So we need to collect some data. So that's what we'll do next.

What you need to think about for your model is to understand the impact. What will these two things, how will they impact the design? Think about fixed cost. What if I have no fixed cost, but I have a very high variable cost, or whatever the variable cost is, and it's the same in any of my facilities? What am I going to do? The first case are those facilities that are run by a third party. So you have a 3PL-- third party logistics provider-- running this. For the fixed cost, one initial thought that comes to mind is that the fixed cost is the cost of building, say, the distribution center. And that's not what we're trying to get here. That's known as a capital cost. That cost will be spread out over the life of that facility. What we're looking at is what are the fixed costs during my operating, my operating fixed costs during my time period of analysis? So for this NERD problem, I'm looking at things over each week. So you have to be careful about what costs you include. Now, we can also use capacity at the facility. Trying to limit the fixed cost by limiting the capacity creates a higher fixed cost. We're going to talk about what the operating cost, the operating fixed cost is. Those costs that don't depend on the units of flow, but get triggered each time period. For variable costs, what we're going to look at is something called activity-based costing. And we'll cover this in a future lesson. But essentially, it's mapping everything you do in a warehouse to the costs that accrue. So anything that increases in cost due to the increased number of units processed through, that's what I want to include. It could be labor. It could be the utility for power. Another way is to look at historical data and run a regression. So let's say I have a distribution center. And I look at a bunch of different weeks the last couple years. And I look at how many barrels they processed per week. And I look at the costs at each week-- labor costs, power costs, anything I can do there. And then, I run a regression. And I find the best fit line. And there, I've got my fixed cost and I've got my variable cost. And that's one way to do it with the data. There are other ways but this is a common approach. So in our problem, I'm going to have for the plants, no fixed cost but a variable cost. And you see that the new variable cost for the Bellow Falls plant, the new one, is $2 per unit per barrel. And it's $0.75 for the Scranton plant. For the distribution centers, I have both a fixed and a variable cost. And we assume that you calculated those by doing this type of regression. All right. Now, we want to talk about capacity at the facility. Capacity is a funny thing. We all know what it means, right? The engineering definition is the maximum amount that something can contain. The problem is, in supply chain, it's not that simple. We think about the capacity of a location, a facility, even a transportation link, is the maximum amount of product or material that can be processed or manufactured through a facility over a set period of time. So sometimes in practice, you hear someone saying, we're operating at 130% of capacity. In the real world, that doesn't make sense. If I have a pint glass of beer I can hold a pint of beer. Anything I pour above that, I can't have 120% of my pint of beer. It doesn't hold it. In supply chain in the facilities, it's a little trickier because it's not this hard and fast number. It's not a single number, either. Essentially, what we're trying to do with capacity, we're saying there are certain limits to the time I have that it takes to process things, or the space that I have. And the interesting thing is, those can be increased. It's not fixed, like a cup. You can't make the pint glass of beer any bigger by stretching it. You can, in supply chain. You can add labor shifts. I can add a third shift that will increase my capacity for processing material. I can improve the policies and processes that I use. If I switch from manually checking each order as it comes in versus using bar codes versus using RFID, then that might improve and speed up the time. So I might have a better policy that will increase my flow I might have better material handling equipment. If I have to load everything in by hand one box at the time, that's very slow. If I put it on a pallet, I can use a forklift. If I use belts, I can go even faster. So there's different things I can add to make it faster to go through. And then loading docks. I might be able to schedule and optimize the way I use my loading docks. Some could be for delivery, some for pickup, and I might be able to use shared ones. So there's all these different things you can do to change the capacity. In fact, you can think of it as a step function of fixed cost. So I might have a fixed cost at a certain level, assuming that there are two shifts of labor. And it will be a higher fixed cost per week if I want to go to three shifts. Because once I add that third shift, I can't suddenly let them go. I have to keep them there for a period of time. And they're there, whether they handle one package or 100 packages. So it's a fixed cost for that time period. All right. So how do I capture these? Well, there's many different ways you can do this. You can look at historically what has typically happened. What happens during peak periods? For our model, I'm going to assume a very simple capacity for the DC's, the five DC's. I'm assuming there's three big DC's in Boston, Providence, and Worcester, and two small ones in Nashua and Springfield. All right. Last thing to get. The product flows. Why do I need these? Well, I need to understand what's happening today. I can't say how much better an improvement is, a new design is, unless I can have something to compare it to. And so I need to capture what's happening today. You have to resist the temptation to remove all the outliers, and instead of capturing what actually happened, you capture what you wished had happened. You've got to avoid that temptation. You want to capture all the dirty things, and see what really happened. So in our case, here's what we've got. Oops. So in our case, here's what we've got. I've got my five DC's, and I've got each of my cities. And remember, every city's supposed to be served by one and only one DC? Well, that never happens. Look at New London. It's served by both Providence and Springfield. Worcester is served by almost every DC except Nashua. So in practice, they're not really adhering to that policy of single DC to city. So OK. I've gotten the data. What do I do next? Well, next, I'm going to build the model.

Nashua is taking care of most of the northern, because that helps me get closer to those customers. Without Nashua, you're going to have a big jump in customer service, because it's better located to those northern cities. But then you see that they're all fully used. And look at Springfield. Springfield is really tapped out. That could get you to start thinking about some other things, maybe explore what would it take to expand capacity. Can I do something there to increase my throughput at the Springfield plant? So I did six new scenarios, scenarios 5 through 10. And what I'm showing here is the baseline. Then I'm showing scenario 2, which was the optimal 3 DC, because that's what we want to compare to. Because if I let anything else be free and I let it pick, that's the one that I want to do comparisons to, because that's the lowest cost. So I compare these, and I say, OK, what happens if I open up my plant? What if I allow infinite capacity at that new plant? Remember scenario 7 and 8, I messed around with the demand to see how we handled increases in demand. That's these ones. And then I looked at keeping the same three big DC's open, and then what happened when I enforce a level of service. As you're guessing, there are an infinite number of different scenarios you can run. We'll talk more in later lessons about how you should run this process. But I wanted to demonstrate that using a very, very simple model, you can have all these different runs. And these different scenarios are testing out different policies. Whether I open more DC's, whether I open a new plant, whether I enforce a certain level of service, all of these are different scenarios. And what this model allows you to do is to put a price on it and let you rate it out in terms of all the other performance metrics that we have here. So this is the real benefit of having a model like this.

[lecture_02_05_key_take_aways]
All right. Another pretty intensive lesson here. So hopefully, you're getting a handle on things though, because we only did one thing during this lesson. We used this example of the New England Root Beer Distributors to show how to use network design models to test the different design scenarios. Previous lessons showed us how to build the models, and we played with them, and showed how the optimization worked. Now we apply them. And I tried to show through a very simple example, the three major steps. The first step, collecting the data, which is the most critical step, and the most commonly ignored step. It's the one that people gloss over because it's very hard. But you need to collect data that is representative and makes sense. The data has to be costs and capacities on the transportation arcs and the facilities. We'll talk much more about this in next week's lessons but this is always the hardest part and most time consuming part of any network design. The second thing we did, we built baselines. Why did we build a baseline? We need to make sure that our model closely resembles reality. That's why we use existing flows. A model that doesn't reflect what happens today is useless. No one will believe it in the future. So you have to make sure that the mechanics of the model and the costs of the model represent reality. And it's a great way to test your data. To make sure that the individual components of the data make sense. Make sure that the total costs look reasonable, but also make sure that the different components, the facility cost, the distribution cost, the transportation costs, make sure those buckets are also somewhat reasonable. Then we use the baseline. We optimized that to be able to compare it to the new designs. You don't want to just compare last year's bad execution to a future plan with perfect execution. You have to make it apples to apples, and that's why we created the other baselines. Because we said OK, now let's take away the poor execution because when I do future models, it's going to assume that it's executed perfectly, because we're all perfect in the future. So we need to make sure I'm comparing apples to apples and not apples to my baseline one, which is more like a rotten apple. It has all that bad execution in it. All right. The last thing we did was the fun part, and that's running the scenarios. They are so easy to run. And I showed you how to run them in a spreadsheet. And that's where you have to change cells, and that's pretty complicated. Using sophisticated software, it's very easy. And so, you can spend a lot of time running these. And as you can think about it, you could run a million different scenarios. So you have to think more about what you want to do, and I'll spend more time on this next week to talk about how you might want to run your scenarios, and how you want-- might want to organize them. Because it's very easy to get overwhelmed. But these scenarios are an excellent way to price out potential options. It's not just about getting an answer. The answer is six. Right? It's all about understanding what the recommendation is and using your common sense. Remember, it's an art and a science. We're showing the science, but you need to make sure that art comes in and make sure you-- the solution makes business sense, not just mathematical, optimal sense. But you do have to resist that temptation for analysis paralysis. You could do scenarios forever, I've been engaged in the scenario planning and other optimization based studies where we spent more than six months doing analysis of scenario, after scenario, after scenario.
