And at the same time, energy costs are lower. So they both are good for the environment and good for cost reductions. Many, many examples like these. Many companies change light bulbs or reduce the speed of trucks. They reduce the energy consumption, reduce costs, but also reduce the emission and the use of fossil fuel. So one can think about a range of procurement activities in order to make sure the suppliers are environmentally responsible. The first one is having a code of conduct. Many companies have a code of conduct for the supplier. For example, Ikea have what is called IWAY, or IKEA Way. This is a set of principles that govern who can be a supplier for IKEA. And suppliers, if they fall on one of them and don't comply with one of them, they have a certain time to make good and to improve, otherwise they stop being suppliers. Unless it's one of those very important items that there's no recovery from them. For example, if IKEA discovers that somebody is using slave labor or child labor, the supplier is cut off immediately. But this is all included in the code of conduct. Of course, the code of conduct requires regular auditing. For example, many companies, IKEA and many others are doing auditing of suppliers. Intel is trying to make sure, or has now made sure that it's not using any conflict minerals. These are minerals that are mined in the Democratic Republic of Congo through conditions of slave labor and coercion in conflict area by guerrilla group and gangs. So it's now enshrined in US law, but even before the law, Intel tried to make sure that it's not using any mineral mined in the Congo. For this, it had to go very deep in the supply chain and finally get to the smelters, which is the last point where one can audit where the conflict minerals are coming from. But audit was key to find out that it is free of conflict minerals. Many companies also train suppliers to become better. For example, Starbucks has over 100,000 suppliers of coffee. And it works with all of them to make sure that they use efficient processes, they use just enough water and not too much, and use the right minerals in the ground, and they work with the land in an efficient way. These things both reduce the environmental footprint of growing coffee, and they also actually reduce the cost or they making the farms a lot more productive, and thereby reducing the cost. Unilever does the same thing with their tea plantations and many of their other materials that they get into their products. Finally, there's supply chain transparency. There's the issue of you want to be very transparent, to show everybody that you're actually doing the right thing. So the example is Patagonia. If you go to the Patagonia site and click on a certain product, and then click on what's called the footprint chronicle, you will get a picture something like this. You will first get a picture of a map with a location of all the suppliers who contribute parts and material to the product, and where the product is actually assembled or made. And then when you click on each supplier, you'll get a lot more information on the supplier, including for example the ratio of females and males, including the language spoken, what they make here. But a whole history of their corporate social responsibility, how they treat their employees. Were there any problems that Patagonia found through the audit? Or the existing problem, how much time they have to fix this problem? So the good and the bad about each one of these suppliers. This type of transparency makes sure that Patagonia creates pressure on itself and its competitors to provide similar transparency and not to hide anything, making sure that everybody who is using Patagonia product can be sure that they're using a product responsibly produced. You can think by way of summary about the hierarchy of procurement, corporate social responsibility. We think about economic responsibility. As we mentioned at the beginning, one has to be ahead of the competition. One has to be within the confines of the law, and have to fulfill any legal responsibility. One has to have so-called license to operate, and fulfill any ethical responsibility. And finally, there are discretionary responsibility, where one might go beyond just standard ethical responsibility or provide some philanthropy on top of everything. So let's summarize this lesson. We started saying that procurement is based on more than cost. Remember the iceberg scenario, the iceberg metaphor. As you deal more and more into the item that you buy, you discover there are many hidden costs that should be taken into account. We can segment the buy into complexity versus spend. And the important quadrant there is the critical buy, that should be reduced either by substitution, simplification, or partnership. So you move the upper left corner into the lower left corner, the lower right corner, or the upper right corner through substitution, simplification, or partnership. The procurement process, we went through the process itself, but the most important part or one of the important part is that it should be followed by data collection and reporting for the next contract. So when you come to procure the same item a year or two years from now, you're not in the same position but you know more. It's important to be a good customer. You have to look internally. Ask your suppliers what you can do in order to simplify processes. Be a better customer, because this will be reflected in the price and in the service. We talk about volatility that can be handled by hedging, either financial hedging or actual hedging. We talked about options. And another way of handling volatility is to separate between the stable and volatile element, and to tie the volatile element to some index. We mentioned that capital goods require special attention, and we talked about certain issues with capital goods that one should pay attention to. We talked about the pros and cons in outsourcing, the fact that it may help you concentrate on the main business, on your core competency. It also may change fixed costs into variable costs. But on the other hand, you may create a competitor, and the business may be taken away from you. Finally, we talked about supplier CSR, and understand that supplier corporate social responsibility reflects on the company. In fact, suppliers by and large are not very susceptible to NGO attack on the corporate social responsibility processes. The companies who are susceptible are the brand holder, the OEM, because they sell to consumers and NGO campaigns usually are aimed at swaying consumers to avoid using the product of a company, even though the offenders may be deep in the supply chain. So let's stop here, and thank you for listening to this lesson.

[lecture_07_13_but_what_about_service]
In this lesson, we will talk about service. We all know that there's more than price to choosing a supplier, especially if it's not a commodity business. And in many cases, the service, innovation, reach, and other factors that we all call service are just as important, and sometimes even more important, than the price that we pay just for the item itself, or for the bare-knuckle service. So let's talk about how, in most cases, services are dealt with. So the current practice, by and large, is what's called screen and auction. For example, we're talking here throughout this lesson about tracking, so let's assume that we decide that we want to have only truck companies that are 95% or better on time. So out of all the bidders, we define a group of suppliers, just those whose service meets our minimum service requirement. And among those, we conduct an auction based on price. And this, is some sense, takes into account both service and price. However, it has an important drawback. It's arbitrary. The cutoff point does not allow for trade offs. For example, a 93% service supplier may be out. And a 94% service supplier may be in, regardless of the price. In other words, the 93% service supplier may be substantially lower in price than the 94% supplier. So is it really worth it, such big price difference for 1% level of service. So we want to do it more methodically. So how do we do it within an optimization framework? So let's go through it. Within the framework that we have used to day, what we do is we modified the prices based on service before the optimization. So we use the optimization, as we'll see in a minute, modified prices rather than the price is submitted by the bidders. Let me give you an example, it is much easier to explain to an American example than talk about it in abstract. So let's say there's a supplier who's bidding on one lane, $500. And this supplier is 97% on time supplier. And let's say the 97% is what we care about in this particular example. There's also another supply who is submitting a lower bid for $475, but he's only 94% on time. The question is, do we choose the high service, high cost supplier? Or the low service, low cost supplier? In order to determine, we have to determine how much level of service is worth. Let's assume that level of service is worth $10 per each 1% of service. Somehow we got into this determination, we found out. We'll talk in a minute how to do it. But we decided that each percent of level of service is worth $10. What we do now, we decide on some baseline. And let's standardize on 94%, even though we can standardize on any other baseline. So let's bring everything to 94%, assuming that each 1% of service is worth $10. So the 94% supplier is already had $475. We don't have to move it. But the 97% supplier bid is modified. We take $30 off because his service is 30% better than the standard service. So we look at this supplier as if his cost is $470. Now we conduct the optimization between the two. And we compare $470 to $475. And of course, the 97% supplier will be chosen. At the end, of course, we don't pay $470. We pay $500. We pay the bid that this supplier gave us. But since we decided that each 1% level of service was worth $10, we should be happy with this choice. Of course, the challenge here is to estimate the value of the level of service. And there is no easy way to do it. Usually, it's done by-- I could call it-- argument. You can call it discussion between sales people, marketing people, operating people, plant people. They all come together. And through this discussion, they have to decide on the value of level of service. So it's something that comes from the outside in the optimization. In this segment we will talk about system attributes or system constraint. So the first order of business is to explain what they are. And it can be explained through an example. Recall that the capacity constraint that we had before were item level conditions, or item level constraint. What does it mean? We had the condition that on a particularly lane, a particular carrier can carry only x loads. I think carrier one a to b could carry only five, or whatever. This is an item level constraint because it's a particular origin destination pair on a particular item up for bid. There could be also system level constraint. For example, the vendor may say, I can give you only so many trucks, or I can serve only so many lanes, or I can do only so much business with you. So you now you have to find the best solution, the cost-minimizing solution, subject to the fact that you meet the carrier demand, or you meet the carrier constraint. So this is now a system constraint. And a system constraint, there are many types. It's not the one-- and we'll give some example. We'll talk about it more as we go along here. By and large, they require the introduction of more elaborate mathematical constraints of the optimization. We always represent this condition through constraint, because we want to meet this condition or this attributes of the problem, so we introduce a constraint and make sure that they get the minimum cost subject to the fact that this condition is met. So we introduce this as a constraint into the optimization. Another example of system condition is vendor preference. Here, the vendor does not put up the condition, we as the auctioneer, or as the procurement department, put certain conditions. [lecture_07_16_economies_of_scope_and_combinatorial_auctions_part_i]
As I mentioned in the introduction to this lesson, one of the challenges in procurement optimization is to take advantage of economies of scope. And this can be done with what's called combinatorial auction or combinatorial procurement, and this is the subject of this particular segment. So let's talk about economies of scope first. In many cases, the bid for one item depends on whether the supplier knows that they will get other items. They may have joint cost in the production of certain things, so it's not that this supplier will give us lower costs if we get more of the certain items. This is simply based on economies of scope, and suppliers, as we saw before, can give us some volume discount. This is not volume discount. This is getting discount or getting low price on both items if the supplier gets to supply both. It can be 2, 3, or 100 items, but they are related somehow, and the cost to produce them are related. So the supplier may get, for example, to sell two types of cereal that are made together or a navigation system and cruise control that can be made in one system. And in that case, the supplier will give us a better price because its costs are lower. So these are not false economics. This is not pressing the supplier or anything like this. This is the supplier costs are lower because the production cost depend on doing these two items together. It is particularly evident in trucking transportation when you think about truckloads. Truckloads is taking full loads from origin to destination. Here the transportation product is lane, origin destination. This is what we've talked about throughout this lecture. So the cost are of two types. There's direct cost and connection cost for the truckers, for the transportation carrier. The direct costs are the costs that are tied to the distance going from A to B. Its the drivers' salaries, it's the gas, it's the tire wear, all the cost that are directly tied to the movement. Connection costs are different. The connection costs are the cost of what happens at the destination. For example, does the driver have to wait there for three days until he get the next load? Does the truck have to move empty out of there, which costs money and nobody pays? So these are some of the connection costs. The connection costs create lane cost dependency or economies of scope. Because if I just bid on A to B not knowing what's going to happen in B, is different than if I get an A to B knowing that you also give me B to A or B to C. Because now I know the truck doesn't have to wait there, I don't have to go empty, and I can give you a better price because it costs the trucker or less, so the trucker can give a better price. So the problem is that the standard auction process or the standard procurement process for transportation is that the shippers, the auctioneers, evaluate each lane bid by itself, which means the carrier are bidding on hundreds or thousands or tens of thousands of lane. The shipper, which is the auctioneer, may look at all the bids from Boston to Detroit and choose the minimum subject to some constraints or the combination of the best service and best price. But to simplify this, let's not talk only about price. So basically the shipper ranks all the bids on a particular lane and chooses the best one or the least cost one. It doesn't work for the carriers because the carriers are trying to build a network. They need to get the equipment back home. They need to keep moving. So there's a disconnect here that we'll explain further. So the current practice in transportation, in most cases, it starts with information exchange. The shippers gives aggregated volume estimate by lane, by origin, region, system, based on last year and some forecast of what will happen last year and ask the carrier to bid on this. The carrier submit lane rate, which sometimes a per mile rate or per move between A to B, a charge that much or between A to B, a charge so much per mile, same idea. And then what happened? There's assign mechanism. It's what I mentioned before, lane-by-lane analysis. For each lane, the low bids win. It's basically done through a spreadsheet. So let's continue with an example that we use throughout this lesson. So this is the simple network with four lanes, A to B. We call A to B, B to C, C to A, and C to B, and C to A. And these are the bids of the carriers. That's actually the same table that we had before. And you look at this and it's clear that the carrier 1 will meet A to B, C to A, and C to B, and carrier 2 will win B to C. So this will be the winning bids, and if we look back on who is winning what, carrier 1 is winning A to B, B to C, and C to A. Carrier 2 winning B to C. That's a problem because there are two very good trips here. One trip is if one carrier can win A to B, B to C, and C to A, it can get the equipment back to maintenance, it can get the driver back home, or if a carrier win, B to C and C to A. In this situation, none of the carriers won this good tour, this good type of business. That's the disconnect between the carrier and the shipper here. To solve this, what we use is what's called combinatorial bid. And with combinatorial bid, the following takes place. We let each carrier bid not only on each lane separately, but on combinations. So in this particular network, possible package includes the following. The first one is bids on each lane separately. Package number 1 is A to B. Package number 2 is B to C. We call it the package just to keep the nomenclature. But we also let carriers bid on the combination of lanes. So carrier can say, I can give you a good price if you give me A to B and B to C and even better price if you give me A to B, B to C, and C to A. I can give you a good price B to C. So now we get lots of bids for all these combinations. Now, the problem is the complexity. The complexity is that there's a large number of bids. Each carrier, let's say we have several 100 lanes or 7,000 lanes. Instead of submitting just number of bids similar to the number of lanes or smaller than the number of lanes, it's submit that plus many of the combinations. So we can have million bids submitted by each carrier. Then we have also overlapping bid. What does it mean overlapping bid? Let me give you an example. Let's say we have a lane going from A to B. So one carrier bids A to B, the same carrier bids A, let's say C to A to B. Another carrier bids going to A going to B going to somewhere else coming back. That's just one carrier. There's another carrier submit I'm going from this point to here and here and there and then coming back. So which carrier gets what? There are so many overlapping bids, it gets really, really difficult. And the large number, of course, doesn't simplify anything. So how do we deal with it? Turns out there are certain mathematical structures that are very good for dealing with this. So let's arrange, let's show how it is done in a spreadsheet. In actuality, it's not done in spreadsheet. It's done in special software, but for this particular example, it can be done with a spreadsheet. So let's arrange all the bids in a table. How do we arrange it? So this is the package number here. These are the lane. And a 1 here says that this carrier is bidding on this lane. So package 1 includes a bid on A to B, and the price is $500. Package 5 include a bid on A to B and B to C, and the price is $975. You see the $975 is less than bidding separately for A to B and B to C because if carrier 1 is bidding separately for A to B and B to C, he does not know if he'll get both of them. But if he bids on a combination, he can provide a better price because he knows that he'll get both of them. By the same token, you see, bid number 8 includes B to C and C to B. This is the round robin, B to C and C to B. And bid number 9 is A to B, B to C, and C to A. This is a tour that goes back to the origin. And unlike bidding these three separately, which cost $1,500, the carrier, in this example, is bidding $1,325, so they're bidding less. Same thing. Carrier 2 is also, there's a 1. If there's a bid, the price is here at the bottom and we have the same number of bids. Add to this now the number of loads assigned to a particular package. So this is the variable that we have, the number of loads assigned to a particular package, and this is the cost data on the lanes. So what we have here is, if this 1, this means that there's a bid here. This is the number of loads. It's coming through here, and this is 10 times $500 will be the $5,000 here. So we have here the bid of this particular. So if carrier 1 gets package 1, they'll get 10 loads on this number of loads. If it will be 10 at a cost of $500 each, their bid is $5,000. So let's see how each one of these works. So just repeating what we said. So there's a 1 in the table, in the cell, in this case in package 5. So if A to B is included in package 5, there's a 1 here and 0 otherwise or nothing. The cost of package 5 by carrier 1 is $975. In addition, the number of loads assigned to carrier 1, package 5 is here. This is the variable that will change as part of the permutation, but we start with less then number of 10 as a starting condition. This is the loads that carrier 1 carries from A to B, and this is the total cost of assigning 10 loads to package 1 of carrier 1. Here we have actually the objective function. This is total cost. It's the sum of all the cost along here, along the bottom row. And of course, this is the sum of the loads. We have to have this column equal to this column. So we have to have at least 10. The constraint here is least 10 on every one of these lanes, just like before. So now we get to the optimization itself. So in the optimization, we are trying, this is the objective function. So this is the C11 times C5. So this is C5 times C11. It's simply the number of loads that carrier 1 is getting under package 1. This is the number of loads that carrier 1 is getting under package 7. This is the cost, the amount of money that carrier 1 is getting under package 1 if package 1 is assigned to it, and this is the sum which is the total cost which we are trying to minimize. And this, of course, again, is the constraint that having 10, we want to have 10 in each row. Now, we get to the optimization. So we optimize-- U19 is exactly here. And we want to change the C11 to T11 are here. Everything here is integer, and you see here that we contend here that U15, U18, which is this, is equal to this. So now we optimize this. You see this is just what I said. And these numbers are these numbers here are the decision variables, and we impose the constraint that we have 10 in each row. So now we look at a solution. We optimize it. We solve this, minimize this. And this is not easy to do by inspection. You can do it, but it's not as easy. You see that this is the solution. We see that carrier 1, the round robin here, was carrying A to B, B to C, and C to A. A to B, B to C, C to A. In carrier 2 and also carrier 1 was getting this one. So carrier 1 was getting everything. But most importantly it got its round robin that it really wanted. Now, this is the solution. This is what carrier 1 is getting.

We can have conditions on the combinatorial bid. For example, we want to use minimum two carriers and each carrier should get at least 10. We don't know where, but must get at least 10 throughout the system. What we add here is this variable here. This is the sum. 90 is the sum of all. This is the sum of the loads that carrier one is getting. This 90 is the sum of the loads that carrier two is getting. So this is the sum of C11 to K11. You see C11 is here, to K11, which is here. And this is the sum of L11 to T11 from here to here. So each one of them is the sum of all the loads that the carrier is getting. And we can put it to any number, want to have some minimum number, so whatever. In this case I think we put it to 10. So we make sure that each of the sum is greater than or equal to 10. So this is simply an additional constraint. You see that K12, which is this, and T12, which is this here, are greater then 10. All the other conditions are the same. So this is what we just said. So we go ahead and optimizing it, and we get a different solution in this case. You see that still carrier one is getting its package that it wants, but carrier two is also getting some reasonably good packaging. For example, it gets B to C and C to B, what's called a continuous move. And the assignment is different. You see here, by the way, that the solution here is higher. We had 18,000 before. It's 18,125 because we just impose the constraint that each carrier has to get at least 10. And you see how the numbers here are distributed across the various packages. So the solution looks now something like this. Carrier one gets five on C to B and gets its package. Carrier two gets C to A and A to B, which is a continuous move, and also one of the good packages. So with this, let's explain the process. This is a more complex process than just regular auction or regular procurement. So you'll need to explain to the supplier. In many cases, companies that we dealt with are having a supplier conference, more than one sometimes, and explaining how the process works. And especially for small suppliers, you need to help them. In fact you may want to even identify the packages in their request for bids that you are submitting because large carriers will have operation research analysts who can find out through the data what are good bids and what good packages. Small carriers you may want to identify when you talk about transportation carriers. It does require a specialized software for the optimization. The problem structure is called set covering. And it can be solved for very large problems. But you need specialized software. This is something for a good-sized problem. It is something that is solved iteratively. It is not solved, of course, by a spreadsheet. And it takes a while to get to a good solution. You test various condition with various optimization runs. Just like we said before, you can test all kinds of conditions, add to the combinatorial nature of the problem anything that you want. You can add a level of service. You can add my brother-in-law has to get at least a million dollars, anything that you want. Or we can deal with only so many carriers or at least that many carriers or a constraint that the order sees from every facility, we want to have at least two carriers, one primary and one secondary. All kinds of conditions take place. And these conditions are imposed after the bidding data is in. So you first get the data. Then you impose all those conditions and run the optimization. Now as we said before, one of the issues that's not easily determined-- and determined as we said through negotiations. Remember when we talk about the level of service, we said that you had to determine how much level of service is worth to you. Turns out that in many cases it's not easy to determine. And by successive runs, you run with different types of level of service, different values. And people can say OK, this is what it means when the level of service is $10 per 1%. This is what it mean when it's $5 or $15 or whatever. And they start seeing the implication of different values on the level of service. By way of summary, let's say the following. A procurement auction is always an optimization process. You always try to find the best, the minimum, the lowest, the best service, what have you. The choice of the winner of course can be based on various criteria and can be subject to many conditions. Each one of these criteria and conditions can be modeled as a constraint. And you can have a combination of them. You don't need to test them one at a time. You may want to test what happens when I do a conspiratorial auction or when I introduce a level of service, take into account level of service, and also give a million dollars to my brother-in-law and also make sure that 30% of the carriers are women-owned businesses or whatever. So these are actual conditions that people are using in practice. And they usually use a combination of them. In all cases the solution will never be as good as running it with no conditions at all. When you introduce a constraint, the solution would be more expensive. But it means that it is worth for you. But you also know how much it is worth for you. By modeling it this way you know, for example, that having 25% minority carriers-- if you run the solution without any condition and then you run with 25% minority carriers, the difference in the value, the extra money that you have to pay for having 25% minority carriers is the extra that you pay for meeting this condition. So now you know how much this condition costs you. Now you know how much it may cost you to deal with high level of service carriers as opposed to any carrier. So all these introduce light and introduce more understanding into the process. You can model the level of service tradeoff explicitly. Do you want to have high cost, high service carrier? Do you want to have low cost, low service carrier? And finally combinatorial auctions that we did in the last segment can account for bidders' economics of scope. So you can take into account the actual economics of the supplier. Not only your economics are taken into account by the optimization, but you take into account the economics of the supplier who can give you better prices if they can be guaranteed that they are getting a combination of items that they're bidding on. So let's stop here. Thank you very much.

[lecture_08_00_components_of_financial_statements]
Hi, and welcome to the supply chain finance module. In this segment, we'll focus on components of the financial statements, specifically the income statement and the balance sheet. Now, the financial statements of the firm include many, many different types and documents. They include those that are presented to investors, those that are presented to the general public, and those that are used internally in the firm, generated many cases by the accounting department. Those are often used for helping the business to make decisions. The ones that we're going to be speaking about today are going to be the income statement and the balance sheet. These documents can be found in the 10k. They can be found in the annual report, and they can also be found-- or at least, components of that would be found in the statement of changes in financial position. Again, there are many reports. In this segment, we're focusing on the income statement and the balance sheet. Now, the income statement really lists all the flows of cash in and out of the firm, and it's quite simple. Because there are really three components of the income statement. We have revenues. That represents all the monies that come into the firm. You have expenses. Those are the monies that go out of the firm, and then you have profit. That's what's left over afterwards. Now, revenues goes by a number of different terms. You see them sometimes as gross sales, turnover proceeds. Expenses has many different components. The main ones that we'll talk about today will be the cost of goods sold and an operating expense. And then profit goes by a number of different terms, and of course, there are variations of different ways to think and look at profit. As we see listed here, sometimes it's called income. Another segment of that or one way to look at it is gross margin. There's gross profit, again, many of the others. EBIT and EBITDA, EBIT standing for Earnings Before Interest and Tax, and EBITDA standing for Earnings Before Interest Tax Depreciation and Amortization. You'll probably hear that if you're traveling around in the financial circles. The operating expense element that is most important that we will think about in the supply chain will be the cost of goods sold. This represents all the direct cost that contribute to the production of a particular product, which basically entails the materials and the labor that are used to produce the product. It does not include what are called indirect expenses, meaning expenses that cannot be directly attributed to the production of one particular product. These would be things like distribution costs or sales force costs. These are generally speaking not included in COGS, or Cost of Goods Sold. Now, there's a variation of cost of goods sold that's called the cost of revenue, and cost of revenue, it's slightly different. And the distinction here is that the cost of revenue includes those costs beyond the production of the product, beyond the materials and the labor, and includes distribution and marketing. You'll see it represented in various ways, but most of the time you'll see COGS. Another important element of the operating expense is what's called SG&A, or Sales General Administration. There's really two components there. Those are the sales expenses. Those are the expenses that are directly linked to the sale of the product, and of course, you could imagine that would be the cost of the sales personnel, the cost for the vehicle that they might be driving, the cost for travel expenses, the cost for them using a phone, cost for promotion, advertising, all things associated with sales. The other element of SG&A is general administration. All right, those are going to be the expenses that would be the managerial expenses or any kind of cost that the firm incurs that are not directly attributable to the production of the product or the sale and distribution of the product. This would include things like utilities or include cost for insurance, or the attorneys, or the finance department. That's what's included in SG&A. There's another group of expenses called other, and this can include a lot of different components, really depending on what's important within the firm. Sometimes you'll see it as depreciation. Sometimes you'll see amortization, and sometimes you'll see R&D. It's really up to the firm what they include in other, and how they represent that, and for that matter, how it's shown in the income statement. Depreciation very directly an expense that accounts for the use of a tangible asset. Amortization is accounting for the use of an intangible asset, particularly goodwill, and goodwill is nothing more than the monies that are spent in excess of the book value of a particular asset that the firm purchases. So if a firm pays $1 billion to acquire a company and its book value is $100 million, well, they just earned themselves $900 million worth of goodwill. We can talk about that at another time, but again, this is just another potential list of an expense. Let's look at an example or two to give you an idea of what an income statement might look like. Income statement for Intel 2014, starts with net revenue, top line. We often hear the term top line. It means the revenue. It's the monies that you bring in before any expenses. We see here it's $55 billion, and of course, the other components are just as we've spoke about. In their case, they're showing the cost of goods sold, the cost of sales, and you deduct the cost of sales. Remember you would get gross margin. Below that there are a series of other expenses that represent the operating expenses. In this case, they show R&D. They show marketing general administration, and then they include amortization. They don't show depreciation in this particular case. The result is you have an income before taxes, and then the bottom line-- what's left over at the end-- is their net income. We could see something similar for General Motors. In their case, they show the cost of revenue. Remember we talked about a different way to represent the cost of goods sold. This includes distribution costs, but here we see they list their expenses. And in their expenses they similarly include R&D. In their case, they have SG&A, but they don't list amortization and the depreciation. The second report that we wanted to talk about in this segment is the balance sheet. Now, in comparison with the income statement, the balance sheet reports the financial condition of the firm representing all the things that it owes-- that it owns rather, meaning things of valu-- and all that it owes, obligations to pay somebody, at one point in time. And there are really two categories. There's assets and liabilities. We can think about assets as something the firm owns, something that has value, and we can think about the liabilities as an obligation to pay someone. Or another way to think of it is, as represented here, a claim against the assets. Now, two things important to remember about balance sheet, that assets always equal liabilities. And then the data that is represented on the balance sheet is always representing the book value. It's whatever was paid for the particular item. It does not record the market value. Now, there are certain assets on the balance sheet that we in the supply chain are going to care a lot more about, and that's going to be things such as inventory. I just want to recognize that there are different ways of valuing inventory. One's called LIFO. One's called FIFO. We'll address that in another segment. Let's look at a balance sheet as an example. In this particular case, we see that the assets are listed on the top, and we see the liabilities listed on the bottom. Sometimes you'll see them listed next to each other, assets on one side. Then you'll see liabilities on the other side, kind of like the balance sheet. You want to make sure you have a balance. Something's wrong. Right side is in balance with the left. We see again assets on the top, liabilities on the bottom, and then at the bottom, let's look at this. And in Intel's particular case for 2014, they list $91 billion worth of assets. Well, guess what? The amount of liabilities is equal. Remember, assets always equals liabilities. And then we see also that there's a current assets and a current liability segment within each. Now, just as we were saying, let's talk about the assets and the particular components of the assets. There's really two parts of that. There's current assets, and then we have the long term assets. The current assets are those assets, those things of value, that the firm has that can be converted into cash within the next accounting period, usually considered a month for the purposes of paying against the current obligations. And that would include things as we see here, cash, accounts receivable, inventory, and then you have other smaller elements in there. But primarily it's cash, accounts receivable, and inventory. Longer term assets are those things that have long term value associated with the firm and are not readily converted into cash. That will include a factory that a company might own, or property, or big pieces of machinery and equipment. It also includes things like goodwill, which we mentioned earlier, or any other kinds of fixed assets equipment that the firm may have. On the other side of the assets is of course the liabilities, and these, as we mentioned, similarly, they have current liabilities. Then they have long term liabilities, but there's another element and that's called the equity. Similar to the assets, the current liabilities are obligations to be paid in the next accounting period. Of course, that would be accounts payable. You purchase something from a supplier. You need to pay them, and then you're going to usually have to pay them within the next period. And then there's a couple of other smaller items that would be included in the current liabilities. Long term liabilities includes the long term debt. You have a mortgage on your house. Well, similarly, firms have mortgages. They have long term loans that they have to pay, not due in the next period, but in the future periods. And then this other element in the liability side of the balance sheet is equity, and that really represents the ownership of the firm. And it starts off with paid in capital. This is cash received from organizations, individuals as initial investments in the firm, and then the value of the firm increases or decreases depending on the retained earnings that are added to or deducted from by the net income of the firm. And we'll talk about that connection in just a moment. Let's talk about supply chain transactions that affect both the income statement and the balance sheet. Let's assume a company sells a product for $10,000 in cash, and the product cost $2,000 to produce and deliver. How will this impact the income statement and the balance sheet? Well, let's look at the income statement impact first. Well, first, we'd see a revenue increase of $10,000, because the company has sold the product for $10,000. That is recognized as a sale. There's a cost of $2,000, and that's reflected in part of the cost of goods sold. And then the net, of course, of that is that you have $8,000. $10,000 minus the $2,000, and you get $8,000. Now, on the balance sheet, we see that the cash current asset would increase by $10,000, because the company sold the product but they got cash. If they sold the product and it was going to be paid next month, that would show up as a current asset of an accounts receivable. But in this case, it's cash, because we see the monies immediately. The inventory would reduce by $2,000, and the retained earnings would increase by $8,000. That's the connection between the income statement and the balance sheet. The change in the net income is reflected as the change in the retained earnings. Let's look at that as in an example using Amazon from 2014. Again, a change in the retained earnings from one balance sheet statement to another is equal to the net income over this time period between those two different time phases. And that is the connection between the income statement and the balance sheet. If we look at Amazon's balance sheet from 2014, we see they had retained earnings of $1,949,000, but the prior year, they had a higher amount of retained earnings, $2,190,000. So that effectively is a change, a drop, of $241 million. Well, if we look at the income statement, what are we going to see? Well, we're going to see-- in the income statement, we'll see a drop of $241 million. Again, the net income, the change in the net income, is reflected as a change in the retained earnings. Let's talk about key points. The income statement provides a summary of the flows in and out of the firm, so all the cash that comes in and all the cash that goes out over a period of time. And this really reflects the performance of the firm in terms of its ability to generate income. And of course, the difference between their ability to generate income and produce a loss is their ability to make sure that they bring in more revenue versus the expenses that they have. The balance sheet really gives them a snapshot, just a picture in one point in time, of the assets that the firm has and all of the obligations that the firms have at one particular point in time. Together, these two statements provide a useful but not a complete assessment of the performance of the firm. If you have any questions, comments, or suggestions, please use the discussion area. And i look forward to seeing you in future sessions. Thank. You.

[lecture_08_01_role_of_accounting]
Hi. Welcome to the Supply Chain Finance module. In this segment, we're going to talk about the role of accounting. Now, the accountants in the firm play a number of different roles, and some of the decisions that they make do indeed affect those of us in the supply chain. But let's talk about the fundamental roles that the accountant plays. First and foremost, they are recording the transactions of the business. Meaning any time that there's money that comes into the firm or goes out of the firm, they're recording that in some transaction. When there's material that comes into the firm or goes out of the firm, they're recording that as a transaction. Now, they use those transactions, and those are the source material for a number of different reports, including financial reports that serve investors in attempt to provide investors with an objective perspective of the performance of the firm. Tax accountant reports basically provide a list to the IRS of the performance of the firm representing their financial obligations to the IRS. And then lastly, there are internal reports called product cost reports that management uses to help make decisions about which products to invest in an other investments that the firm should make to improve the performance of the business. Now, all of these are prepared consistent with basic accounting concepts, but keep in mind that there are choices that the accountant can make with regards to some other transactions. There are limits and there are legal guidelines, of course, that he or she must follow. Now, the annual report is one of the more popular or common reports we might think of and there are a number of different components, including some of the financial statements such as the income statement and balance sheet, there are notes in financial reports which provides some detail to the numbers in the aforementioned financial reports. It's very important to go look at those and understand those if you really want to understand the numbers on the income statement, the balance sheet, and the statement of changes and financial position. And then there's the chairperson's comments, and this generally provides the leader's assessment and explanation of the performance of the firm and usually an optimistic assessment of the future that the firm faces. Lastly, there's a statement from a public accounting firm, and auditing firm, and their statement really does not offer an opinion on how well the business is performing. Only it suggests that the reports repaired in compliance with generally accepted accounting principles, and that the reports are a fair representation of the numbers that they were given as the accounting firm. Now, tax accounting and financial accounting are quite different and there are different objectives as we had just described. It's important to understand the distinction. The financial accountants and the financial reports are going to attempt to provide a fair representation of the performance of the firm, and this is primarily first for investors and stockholders. Compare that with the tax accountant's objective, and the tax accountant's objective is to minimize the tax liability and to defer the payment of taxes as long as possible. Of course, that's better for the firm. It's better for the firm to reduce its financial obligations and to delay payments it has to make of any kind. Now, understandably, with those two different objectives and those different sets of reports, you can recognize that this is quite complex. And as such, the tax code permits organizations to actually maintain two sets of books. So it's completely legal to do that. Now, let's understand a little more why that's legal. Within United States, the tax code permits firms to depreciate capital assets. That's assets that have lasting value longer than one accounting period. Generally speaking, it's more than five accounting periods. And the tax code also permits organizations that have capital assets to depreciate those using a different schedule, meaning that there's a different rate of depreciation. Now, we'll talk about what depreciation is in a moment. But again, it's legal for organizations to have two sets of books-- one for accounting and more for tax reporting. Now, let's talk about depreciation. What is that? Well, it's an expense to the firm. So as we think about the income statement, you have a revenue line which represents the monies that come in, and you have expenses. Expenses are those that are costs to the firm, and then the resultant of deducting the expenses from the revenue out of a profit. So within the expenses there are often-- not often, but most of the expenses are cash expenses, like for materials and labor and such. But depreciation is an unusual expense. It's a non-cash expense. What it is, it's a recognition that a capital asset that a firm might have, let's say a piece of machinery that they paid $100,000 for and that's going to last 10 years, well each year, there's a value, there's a cost for using that. And that cost is represented as a depreciation. So it's basically an estimated cost of using that asset. Then in recognizing and recording a depreciation expense, each year, the capital asset is reduced by the exact amount of the expense. Let's go over an example to better understand this. So let's assume that a firm buys a machine for $10,000 and they figure that this is going to last 10 years. And therefore, the annual depreciation expense is going to be $1,000 per year. Assuming a 10-year life, so we have to understand how long that capital asset will last, the value of that asset at the end of its life-- in this case we're assuming it's a zero residual value-- and then also the rate of depreciation, which is how much of that is depreciating each year? And so in this case, we're looking at what's called the straight line method, meaning each year the depreciation is the same as the next year. So again, with $10,000 cost over a 10-year period, that means $1,000 each year the depreciation expense is recorded, and that asset is depreciated by that exact amount each year. So after year 1, the asset would be valued at $9,000 and there would've been a $1,000 expense recorded in that year 1. In year 2, there would be another $1,000 expense recorded, and then that asset would be recorded as being valued at $8,000. Now, the depreciation rate changes based on what the firm thinks of how long their the material is going to last and the value associated with its use in each year. That's for the firm to decide. Now let's go through an exercise here, and the challenge is to calculate the depreciation expense for the value used of a truck each year given these facts. It cost $60,000, it will last 5 years in terms of its service life, and at the end of its service life, it will have a $5,000 salvage value. Let's assume that each year the depreciation expense is going to be the same. What is that annual depreciation expense, and what is the impact on taxes? Well, let's look at that. Here are the facts as we've just described them. Important-- the first thing we have to do is understand what's the total amount we're going to depreciate? In this case, it's going to be the purchase cost of $60,000 less the remaining value at the end, which is $5,000. So how much is going to be depreciated is $55,000. Now, over what time period? We said a 5-year time period. OK, 5 years service life. So that means we take this $55,000 and we divide it by the service life, and that will give us $55,000/5, or $11,000, which represents the annual depreciation expense. So what does that mean in terms of the impact on taxes? Well, what it means is that each year, the firm is reporting an $11,000 expense that they didn't have before, which means there's $11,000 less of revenue that they have the report. And therefore, they report a higher expense. It means their net is going to be lower and therefore their income is going to be reported lower even though they're actually going to have that cash on hand. And with the lower income, that means their tax obligation is going to be lower, as well. Let's talk about key points. As suggested, the accountants in the firm are the ones who are reporting all the comings and goings of cash and materials within the firm. And this serves as the basis for all of the different reports that accountants prepare, whether it's for investors, the IRS, or management. We spent some time talking about depreciation, and this is the recognition of the value used of capital assets. And it's important to understand that because within the supply chain, many of the capital assets are used by the supply chain. It's important to understand the actual quality and condition of those assets. Recognize how old, how new they are, and whether that capital is being replenished, the capital equipment is being replenished or not. If you have any questions, comments, or suggestions, please use the discussion area. I look forward to seeing you in future sessions. Thank you.

[lecture_08_02_accounting_concepts]
Hi. Welcome to the supply chain finance module. In this segment, we're going to be focused on accounting concepts. Accounting concepts are the basic set of concepts to provide guidance to the accountant in carrying out his or her duties recording the transactions of the business. The comings and goings of the cash and the materials in and out of the firm. Keep in mind that although accounts are dealing with discrete numbers, the process of recording a transaction is not always an exact science. There are choices that the accountant must make, with guidance from management, when recording a transaction. The big choices include whether to categorized an expenditure as a period expense or as an investment and capital asset, how the firm allocates overhead in their costing system, and the relative conservative or aggressive choices when recording inventory. There are a number of different basic accounting concepts. We'll go through 10 of the primary concepts right now. These are not all the core concepts. Most of the relevant ones. We're going to go in some greater depth for some more so than others, because some of them are more meaningful and more important to the supply chain than some of the other concepts. The dual aspect concept is also known as a duality principle. This refers to the basic convention in accounting where total assets always equals total liabilities, where liabilities includes short-term liabilities or shown here as current liabilities, long-term liabilities, and equity. So there are different components within liabilities. So when we see a balance sheet with the assets on one side, and the liabilities on the other, the bottom line-- total assets and total liabilities are always going to equal each other. Now, you may have heard about the double entry accounting system, and that refers to the recording process that requires two entries for each transaction, which ultimately helps keep the assets equal due to liabilities. The accounting period concept merely states that the usual time frame for accounting analysis is a one-year time frame. Therefore an income statement reports the income generating capabilities of the firm for a one-year period. That being said, all businesses will conduct analyzes on the business performance in smaller increments of time-- quarterly, monthly, weekly, and even daily in some cases. The conservatism concept suggests that the accountant should adopt a conservative position when faced with a choice in recording a transaction. For example, the accountant should recognize transactions that may decrease equity when they are reasonably possible, and recognize transactions that may result in increases in equity only when they are reasonably certain. Let's take an example. If a food company has $100 million in finished goods inventory that is held beyond the expiration date, the value of the inventory would certainly come under question. It's highly likely that that inventory suffered a permanent loss of value, and the only marketable use may be for animal feed. That market value would likely be much lower than the cost of materials on the books, which is $100 million. So the firm should choose the lower of cost or market and revalue the inventory down to the market value of the inventory. That would be a conservative approach. Now, keep in mind that organizations should not be revaluing their inventory frequently. This should only occur with dramatic reductions in the evaluation, and it should be done rarely. Otherwise the investors will start to question the accuracy of the financial reports if the organization keeps changing the evaluation method or the evaluation of their different assets. One more example may help. Let's say a supplier submitted a quote to a customer, and the customer called back to say that their bid was very appealing, and that they will likely make the purchase. Should they record that as a sale? No. As encouraging as that call would be, the supplier should not record that as a sale, not until they receive a firm order, preferably in writing, from the customer. The accrual concept suggests that the accountant should record transactions when there is a relevant event, but not necessarily when cash is exchanged. This simple example here illustrates this. If a company sells $2,500 in furniture for custom furniture and receives a balance in full at the time of the custom order-- let's say on April 1-- but the company ships a product on June 15, well, there are two different ways to record that transaction. If we use the accrual method, the accountant will record June 15 as the date of the sale, because that's when the furniture was shipped to the customer. Whereas using the cash method the accountant would record April 1 as the date of the sale. Keep in mind that in almost all cases, the accrual method is used only in rare cases as a cash method used for-- and that's typically by smaller firms. The rest of the concepts I'll share are straightforward and warrant a brief explanation. The cost concept means that in transactions, the accountant will record the cost basis, meaning that the accountant will not consider the market value of an asset. This prevents manipulation of the asset value that could occur if the accountant chose to use a subjective market value. For supply chain professionals, we should recognize that inventories are recorded on a cost basis, not what the market value is, meaning that the value of inventory on hand could be higher or it could be lower, depending on changes in the marketplace for those particular materials. The materiality concept means that the accountant should record all transactions that have the potential for impact on the business, but should ignore those that are trivial in nature. For example, if an organization purchases office products-- let's just say USB flash drives-- for $200, these should be treated as period expenses, even if the flash drives are going to provide value to the firm for more than the one-year accounting period. Yes, one could record the flash drives as an asset, because they're going to be providing value for more than a year, but the cost of keeping track of the flash drives in the accounting system and then the potential impact of this on a financial report-- that's really negligible. So this purchase really should be treated as an expense to the firm. Basically, they accountant should not invest resources in tracking immaterial items that will not impact the financial reports either way. The consistency concept states that the accountant should be consistent using the same method for recording transactions. For example, an organization should not be using LIFO inventory valuation method for one kind of inventory asset and a FIFO inventory valuation method for another. These are different Inventory valuation methods that could result in very different measures reported for the very same material. The entity concept is among the most simple. It means that the accounting is performed for an entity and not for individuals. Obviously, some of the same principles may apply for one's personal financial management, but the accounting reports that we're discussing are for our purposes are focused on the business entity. The matching concept indicates that when costs are incurred, they're associated or matched with the revenues that are associated with them, and then they are treated as expenses in the same period of revenue received. So if a company spends money in June on materials that are used in a product that is sold in October, those costs would be matched with the revenues and recorded as expenses in October. Put another way, when an organization purchases raw materials to produce a product, all those costs are accumulated in the inventory valuation of a particular item and only recognized as an expense in the period when the product is sold. Lastly, the money measurement concept suggests that money is the common measure. The accountant will record only items that are expressed in monetary terms. It seems simple, but we will often find cases where organizations want to quantify things like market share, or competitive advantage, or innovation. All important to a business, but they are not measurable in monetary terms, and for purposes of financial reporting, they are not, therefore, recorded. As suggested, all these principles provide the underpinning for some of the fundamental choices that the accountant has to make regarding product costing, depreciation method, and asset expense. These choices will be addressed in a separate session. Let's talk about key points. I suggested that basic accounting concepts guide the accountant for reporting, and this is going to provide them with the insight as to how to treat the various and sundry transactions of the business. We know that the accountant does have choices, and we'll see how the sum of those very same transactions can result in very different financial reports, depending on the choices that the accountant makes, again, in conjunction with the business. If you have any questions, comments, or suggestions, please use the discussion area. I look forward to working with you in a future segment. Thanks.

[lecture_08_03_asset_or_expense]
Hi, welcome to the supply chain finance module. In this segment, we're going to talk about capital asset or expense when it comes to recording transactions. As I have suggested in prior segments, accounting professionals have a number of choices when recording certain transactions. One in particular is the area of product costing and the choices are how to allocate overhead and how to categorize expenses. Additionally, the accountant can choose among different appreciation rates and also decide which expenditures will be treated as period expenses, or as capital asset purchases. Keep in mind that the accountant ought to be making these decisions with the guidance from the business but keeping consistent with generally accepted accounting principles, also known as GAP. In this segment, we will address the latter option of asset or expense categorization. Now, some expenditures can possibly be treated as either a period expense or a capital asset. If treated as a period cost, it means recording the expenditure as a cost for that accounting period, which increases reported costs on the income statement and lowers taxable income. If the expenditure is treated as a capital asset, it means that the funds expended would show up not as an expense on the income statement, but as an asset on the balance sheet. Choosing the capital asset option can make the income statement look better because there would be lower expenses and, therefore, higher income. But that also comes with an obligation to pay more taxes. The real question for the accountant and the businesses is what's the best way to fairly represent the transaction? If the purchase will provide the business with lasting value to the firm, it may be more accurate record the purchase as a capital asset which would reflect higher assets for the firm compared to recording a transaction as an expense. Let's look at a few examples to show how this would affect the income statement and balance sheet. And keep in mind that many of the assets are those that will be used in the supply chain. In this instance, we see that the firm purchases a machine for $25,000 on December 31st with the intention that it would be used as a prototype. This could be treated as an expense or as a capital asset purchase. Now, let's walk through this. In this particular case, the prototype purchase is being considered as an expense. Well, apparently the assumption by the business would be that it's useful in their first year and beyond that it does not have lasting value to the firm. Well, in that particular case, in year one there is no impact on revenue but there is a change in expense, because that $25,000 would be recorded as a period expense and listed as $25,000 part of the operating cost. Now, this results in a negative $25,000 impact on net income. Now, on the balance sheet in your one, there are two offsetting transactions. Because it was paid for in cash, there is a drop in the current asset cash balance of $25,000. This is offset on the other side of the balance sheet with a drop in the owner's equity of $25,000. Now, because this was recognized as an expense in year one, there are no other transactions to record in year two. Now, let's look at this same purchase and treat it as a capital asset. Perhaps, the logic here would be that the machine while only a prototype could provide long term value to the firm by helping the firm understand its use and the potential development of future products by the company. So one could make that argument. Now, let's walk through this particular case. On the income statement in year one there is no impact on revenue, operating cost or profit or net income because there are no expenses to report and there's no impact on revenue. On the balance sheet in year one, however, there are again two offsetting transactions. Again, because it was paid for cash, there is a drop in the current asset cash balance of $25,000. This is offset in the long term asset category by an increase $25,000. Again, because we are now adding that $25,000 prototype as an asset and, therefore, we record it as an asset on the books. Now, there is no impact on liabilities or equity in your one. Now, in year two we take into consideration depreciation. Now, you may say, wait a minute-- hold on, not so fast, here. Why don't we include depreciation in year one? Well, in this case, the purchase was made on the last day of December-- December 31st-- and it would not be reasonable to charge a depreciation expense for the entire year when there was only one day of possible use out of 365 days. This is, of course, assuming that their fiscal year is the same as the calendar year. Charging one day of depreciation expense for such an item would fall into the category of an immaterial transaction. But in your two, there should be a depreciation expense recognized. Now, in this case, it appears that the firm has decided that the prototype would provide value for a five year period. Why? Well, because we know that the asset is worth $25,000 and if they're depreciating $5,000, well, they're doing that over a five year period. Now, as we see here, again, assuming that would be using the straight line depreciation method. So in the income statement of year two, we'd see this depreciation expense of $5,000 which results in a decrease in the netting income of $5,000. Now, on the balance sheet in your two, once again there are two offsetting transactions. There is a decrease in the long term asset value $5,000. Why is that? Well, because this is reflecting the reduction in the value of that prototype. It started out being worth $25,000, now, after we take into account the $5,000 expense for use of that prototype in your two, it is now worth $20,000. So this reflects a reduction in the value of that prototype on the books. This is offset on the other side of the balance sheet with a drop in the owner's equity of $5,000 as well. So the same transaction-- a purchase of a prototype for $25,000-- can result in very different financial records, depending on the assumptions and treatment decisions made by the accountant, again, in conjunction with the business. So let's talk about key points. Certain expenditures can be considered capital asset purchases or purity expenses. And this reflects, of course, one of the choices that the account has, again, in conjunction with the business leadership-- I keep saying that because it's important. The accountant must work hand in hand with the business leaders to make consensus decisions about what the proper treatment is for these different transactions. And as we've just seen in this example, depending on the method used for recording those transactions, you get very different results. And as supply chain managers, we need to understand the possible ways that the financial reports are recording and reporting the assets as well as the expenses of the business. If you have any questions, comments, or discussions, please use the discussion area. And I look forward to working with you in a future segment. Thank you.

[lecture_08_04_inventory_valuation_methods]
Hi. Welcome to the Supply Chain Finance module. This segment will focus on inventory valuation methods, specifically last in, first out and first in, first out methods, more commonly known as LIFO and FIFO. Now, to understand the importance of inventory valuation, we only need to start to understand the importance of inventory. Inventory is fundamental to business because it is what is used to serve customer demand. Now, inventory is also used as a buffer to demand and supply variation, and it also enables immediate response and often sustained response in the aftermath of some disruptions. So inventory enables a business a serve its mission. Now, inventory is one of the primary assets our supply chain managers are charged with managing, and as such, it is critically important that we as supply chain managers understand those assets in order to best manage them and utilize them to serve the business needs. Now, if you're going to manage these inventory assets, you need to be able to keep track of those assets. In order to keep track of them, you actually have to measure them. Now, how does one measure inventory? Your first thought may be to say that, well, maybe we should measure inventory in terms of units because most of the time, customers buy finished goods and products in units, right? While that is true in most cases, it won't due to measure units on a financial report. Let's recall the money measurement concept, which tells us that money is the common measure. Therefore, we measure inventory in monetary terms. And so instead of counting units, we're going to be counting inventory in terms of money. Now, there are a number of different methods used for accounting for the cost of inventory, and we see a handful of them here, including several different averages, simple, weighted, and moving average, for example. But the ones we're going to be speaking about are the last two methods listed here, which are the last in, first out or LIFO and the first in in, first out inventory valuation methods. Before we explain LIFO and FIFO further, let's understand how the sale of a product is recorded, the transaction that involves inventory valuation. Whenever the company sells a product it, receives revenue or the promise of revenue, a receivable. In order to record that transaction, the accountant has to match the revenue of that product sale with the cost of the product. What is the cost of the product? It is whatever has been built into the inventory valuation up to that point in time. And for the products that are purchased and resold, the LIFO and FIFO method are two suitable options for inventory valuation. Depending on the method chosen, that cost will vary depending on which inventory valuation methods we use. But a central concept for you to understand is that with some of the inventory valuation methods, the product flows are different than the financial flows. If we use the last in, first out method, we use the cost of the items that were purchased most recently, meaning the last received in. And then when we're using the first in, first out method, we're using that cost of the products that were purchased originally, or the oldest products on the books. We'll talk more in detail about that in just a moment. But importantly, you must understand that the physical product flows are not exactly the same as financial flows, meaning that what happens in the factory or warehouse is not exactly what we record in the financial books. In the financial books, we want to keep track of which items came in first and which items came in last because they're going to be recorded at different costs. In the factory, all the same materials are treated as being the same, meaning that they are not used in the order received but in order of convenience. That's most of the time. Assume that a factory worker receives 10 units of a particular item on one day, and the following week receives another 10 units. All of those units are going to a bin. And the first one that the worker pulls out are not necessarily going to be the ones that were purchased earlier. In many cases, it's a random grab. And in most cases, the order of the use of those materials doesn't impact the quality or performance of the product. So while the factory and warehouses use materials of the same type that were received at different times independent of the actual receipt of the materials, the financial books record the use of the materials in actual order of receipt. Let's start with last in, first out inventory valuation. LIFO is a valuation method used mainly in the US because there are some specific tax laws that permit accounting for inventory values that effectively defer the payment of taxes until a later point in time. Using the LIFO or last in, first out method, the accountant uses the most recent cost of inventory when matching the revenue of a sale with the cost of the inventory. That means that even though the company may have inventory on hand that was purchased at a different, usually lower, cost, the accountant matches the revenue using the most recent cost of inventory. In most cases the cost of materials increases over time, and therefore, using LIFO, it is possible that the inventory remaining on the books will be worth more than actually what is recorded. Or put another way, the inventory on hand would have a higher replacement value than what is represented on the financial books. So oftentimes inventory valuations using LIFO may be undervalued. So keep in mind that if we buy 100 units of a particular raw product or raw material on January 1st and we buy another 100 units on March 1st, but we use only 50 units, if we're using the last in, first out method, we're using the cost for 50 units from the March 1st purchase, which means that the items that remain in inventory-- all 100 units from January and 50 units from March-- will be valued at the purchase price at those particular times. Presumably, the items purchased in January were purchased at a lower cost, again assuming that the raw material purchase prices increase over time. Let's contrast LIFO with first in, first out inventory or FIFO valuation. Like LIFO, it is a valuation method, but it is used broadly-- not just in the United States, but around the world. FIFO uses the oldest cost or the cost associated with materials that were purchased at the earliest point in time when matching the revenue from a sale with the inventory cost. What that means is that the materials that remain in inventory on the books are those with costs that were incurred most recently. Put another way, the costs that remain in inventory are the costs that are associated with the most recent purchase. What does that mean to the inventory valuation? It means that inventory valued using FIFO will likely be valued at market replacement costs or the cost for us to go out in the marketplace right now and replace that inventory. Because effectively, the inventory on hand, at least on the books, is what was purchased most recently at market prices. Now, in use, financial reports will be different depending on which one of these methods are used. Using the LIFO method will likely report a higher cost than FIFO because the costs that are being matched with the revenue are the costs from the last in or most recent purchase. This assumes that material costs are increasing over time. You've heard me say that a few times. It's important to keep that in mind. Well, when you have a higher cost associated with the inventory, that means you'll have a lower profit to report. And with a lower profit, then you, of course, will have a lower tax obligation, at least in the current period. When using FIFO, it's the exact opposite. FIFO reports a lower cost associated with this sale because it assumes the materials that are associated with that sale are the ones that were purchased at the earliest point in time. Again, with our assumption that material costs increase over time, those costs would be lower, which means that there will be a higher profit reported. That, then, means that there will be a higher current tax obligation. Let's go through an example to illustrate this. Assume that a company buys a raw material for a product, and it buys it at three different points in time-- January 15th, February 1st, and March 1st. And at each different purchase point, they buy 20 units. But note that the cost of unit-- each unit increases over time. So on January 15th, the cost was $5 per unit. On February 1st, the cost was $10 per unit. And on March 1st, the cost was $15 per unit. Now, let's also assume that the company sells 30 units at $20 each on April 1st. Using the FIFO method, the revenue for the firm is $600 having sold 30 units at $20 each. Now let's look at the cost using FIFO. With FIFO, we use the cost of the first in materials, so we need to identify the cost of the first 30 units. The first 30 units purchased includes materials purchased on January 15th-- that's 20 units-- and some purchased on February 1st. So we use 20 units from January, which were purchased at $5 each, and then another 10 units from the February 1st purchase, which were purchased at $10 each. That gives us a total cost of $200 and a net income of $400. Now, assume that there's a 20% tax rate, and therefore, the tax in that particular sale is $80. Given that, what is the value of the remaining inventory? Well, as we see here, we can calculate that. There are 10 units remaining from the February 1st purchase at the $10 price, so that's 10 units at $10, or $100. And then there's 20 units remaining at the $15 price from the March 1st, purchase or $300. The value of the remaining inventory is therefore $400. That's the $100 plus the $300. There is no inventory in the books remaining from the January 1st purchase. Those items were already used for the sale of the 30 units. Let's compare that with the LIFO method. Using the LIFO method, we have the same revenue of $600, but our costs are going to be different because we're going to use the different valuation method. Now, with LIFO, we used the cost of the last in materials, so we'll need to identify the cost of the most recently purchased, or last in, 30 units. The last 30 units purchased includes materials purchased on March 1st-- would be 20 units-- and some purchased on February 1st. So we use 20 units from1 March, which were purchased at $15 each, and then another 10 units from February st purchase, which were purchased at $10 each.
