That gives us a total cost of $400 at a net income of $200. Well, look at this. What a stark contrast. We have different net incomes reported-- $400 for FIFO and $200 for LIFO. And we have different tax obligations-- $80 for for FIFO and $40 for LIFO. And then interestingly enough, the value of the remaining inventory is recorded as being quite different-- $400 versus $200. Keep in mind that we still have 30 units remaining in each of these different valuations, just that they are valued at different purchase costs. With FIFO, we see a higher reported net income and a higher tax obligation in the near-term, as well as a higher valuation of the inventory. In contrast, with LIFO, as we've suggested earlier, the inventory may actually be undervalued as compared to the FIFO inventory. So let's talk about key points. There are different ways to value inventory, and we discussed two of the more popular methods of LIFO and FIFO. LIFO uses the most recent cost of inventory to match revenue from sales, and this typically results in lower-stated profits, again, assuming material costs are increasing over time. As we have seen from the example, therefore, the remaining inventory can be undervalued. FIFO uses the oldest cost of inventory to match revenue with sales, and this typically results in a higher potential profit, but it does incur near-term term tax obligations that you don't have with LIFO. As a result, the remaining inventory, using a FIFO system, is more likely to be associated or closely associated with the current market value or current replacement cost of that inventory. Considering that the impact these two different valuation methods have on financial statements, it is important for supply chain managers to become familiar with the inventory valuation method being used in the business in order to fully appreciate the assets on hand and how those assets, inventory, are being used in the financial reports and to serve the business. Just using our example, we observed that two inventories with the exact same number of units could have financial valuations with one being twice as large as the other. If you have any questions, comments, or suggestions, please use the discussion area, and I look forward to seeing you in the next session. Thanks.

[lecture_08_05_connecting_transactions_to_financial_statements]
Hi, welcome to the supply chain finance module. This segment, we'll focus on connecting supply chain transactions to the financial statements. In the world of the supply chain manager, we are often focused on the tangible aspects of our work, inventory management, forecasting, distribution planning, capacity planning, and how the design of the supply chain can create competitive advantage. These are indeed important for the supply chain manager, but today's supply chain manager needs to be able to communicate what we do in the supply chain to those in leadership positions. And we need to be able to do so using a language that leaders understand. That is the language of finance. And so this segment, we'll make a connection between all those things we do on the supply chain with the financial statements, so that we as supply chain managers can effectively communicate with leadership regarding the impact of our work on the business in financial terms. We start with two basic but important financial statements, the income statement and the balance sheet. We must also consider other factors relating to the accounting, including financial accounting, tax accounting, managerial accounting, the core accounting principles, and the inventory valuation methods of LIFO and FIFO. Altogether, the statements and the accounting methods set the foundation of knowledge for identifying how supply chain activities impact the balance sheet and the income statement. To start off, we first consider one fundamental transaction that involves the supply chain, the sale of finished goods. We'll use this as an example to understand how these different transactions impact the financial statements. The case is a simple one. A company sells a printing system for $10,000 in cash. The product cost $2,000 for the company to produce it. What is the impact on the income statement? Well, because they sold the product for $10,000, the income statement would reflect an increase of $10,000 in revenue. When a product is sold, the revenue is matched with the costs associated with the product being sold. In this case, the costs associated with the product is $2,000. And therefore, $2,000 of costs are recognized, and costs increase by that amount. You may wonder where those costs were recorded up to that point. Until the sale of the product, those costs were included in the valuation of the inventory, which was maintained as a current asset on the balance sheet. Now, as a result of these two changes, then that income would increase by $8,000. So that accounts for the impact on the income statement. But what about the impact on the balance sheet? Well, because the printing system was sold for $10,000 in cash, the current asset cash account would increase by $10,000. And as previously noted, because we sold the product and it carried a cost of $2,000, the current asset inventory account would decrease by $2,000. Because that inventory was removed, and the costs associated with selling that product was matched with the revenue. Therefore, we have a net increase on the asset side of the balance sheet of $8,000. Well, that comes from $10,000 minus the $2,000, and that gives you the $8,000. Remembering that assets always equal liabilities where liabilities include short term liabilities, long term liabilities, and equity, the liabilities side must also show an increase of $8,000. And that's where we see that this increase of $8,000 increase in retained earnings. Another way to illustrate this is to show both the income statement and the balance sheet at the same time attract the changes coming from this very same case. This helps us understand how the transaction of sale works this way through each of these different statements. Again, it's a graphical representation. We're going over the same material. Let's start with the sale. We sell a product for $10,000. Here again revenue is increased by $10,000. We're getting $10,000 in cash, so our current asset of cash account goes up by $10,000. Well OK, we recognize the cost of goods sold for the products that we sold. In this case, the cost of the system was $2,000, so there's our $2,000 increase in costs. Well, but at the same time in the balance sheet that $2,000 cost was matched with the revenue, and that takes out $2,000 from the inventory current asset account, the current asset inventory account that is. With that impact here of course, shows the convergence of these activities, these transactions. On the income statement, we have $10,000 minus the $2,000, and that gives us an $8,000 increase in profit. On the balance sheet, we see a net increase in assets of $8,000, mentioned that before. And then, of course the corresponding increase on the right side of the balance sheet-- with the liabilities under the total liabilities-- with an increase of $8,000 in owner's equity. Now, we've only talked about one transaction or one type of transaction, the sale of a product. But how do we represent the impact of other supply chain initiatives on the financial reports? For example, how would improving operational performance impact the financial statements, such as improving the cycle time, making faster deliveries, or increasing inventory terms? Well, this slide provides a line item for a number of different supply chain changes and offers a linkage to various financial reports and elements in the financial reports. Let's start with order cycle times we see up here, and I'll walk through each of these different items. If the company improves the order cycle time, the production and delivery cycle is shorter, meaning that your cash cycle is going to be shorter, too. You're going to get your cash sooner. Well, therefore your accounts receivable will likely be smaller, because you're receiving the cash more quickly than you had otherwise. As a result of the faster order cycle time, the company will not need as much inventory, and therefore the faster order cycle time may result in lower capital costs, which means lower cost and higher net income. Let's think about this. If the company reduces the order cycle time, that usually means that the warehousing operation has to handle the product fewer times, because they're not storing it, and pulling it out, and storing it again, and moving products around in the warehouse, which means that the warehousing costs would be lower. That shows up in the income statement as a reduction in cost. Now, because the company has improved the order cycle time, you're able to get the product to market more quickly than your competitor. And you have therefore created some competitive advantage. How does that competitive advantage impact the financial statements? Well, having a competitive advantage means that with all things being the same, the customers would prefer your product to your competitor's product. In the marketplace that means that with a competitive advantage the company can choose to sell more product at the same price as their competitor, which would increase their market share. Or the company can choose to sell their product for a higher price, which would increase their profits. In both cases, the company would increase their revenues by virtue of the actions that they've taken, either from more unit sales or from the same unit sales but at a higher price. Let's look at some of the other supply chain initiatives that may impact the financial reports. If the firm improves their ability to deliver on time orders, that means that the company will likely have higher customer satisfaction ratings. And therefore, the company may enjoy some additional competitive advantage. Just as we've discussed, the competitive advantage can finds its way to increase the revenue on the income statement. With an increase in on time orders, delays are reduced, which probably entails lower transportation cost, because there's no embedded cost for delays or surcharges for delays in those transportation cost. This shows up in the income statement as a reduction in cost. Now, if the company's able to produce and ship products and deliver them as ordered-- meaning exactly as what the customer's ordered-- that means the company has a higher fill rate, which means getting their cash faster. Because they're not arguing with the customer about what product was sold and whether they got the right stuff. They got the right stuff, and therefore their bills are going to be submitted, and they'll be paid within their particular cycle time, which practically speaking means the company's cash is not tied up in those silly arguments between companies about whether they got the right product or not. Now let's also look at accurate billing. If the company can improve billing accuracy, that means reducing the invoicing errors, which means the company will likely have lower order management costs. And again, that shows up in the income statement as a reduction in cost. And then lastly, improving inventory turns can impact the balance sheet and the income statement. With an increase in inventory turns, the requirement for inventory drops, meaning that less inventory is required to support the same level of sales. And therefore, one would expect that you would see a lower current asset inventory account. And similar to some of the other improvements, with less inventory, there's less handling, which means lower costs. And possibly with less inventory the company may not need all of the existing distribution centers. Well, if that happens, then-- and the company were elected to actually eliminate a distribution center, then the impact would show up as a reduction on the balance sheet in the long term asset, plant property, and equipment account. So as we see, with each of these initiatives, they find their way to the income statement and the balance sheet. Let's look at the same supply chain initiatives and their impact on the financial statements graphically. We'll do this quickly just to give you an idea, another kind of look at the same assessment. An improvement in service level creates competitive advantage that can lead to market share gains and price premiums, which lead to higher revenues. And we see how these hit the income statement and resulting increased profits, which then show up on the balance sheet in retained earnings. Lower cost from improved order management, less handling, and lower capital cost shows up in the income statement as reductions in cost and the associated increase in profits. Lower inventory could result in lower cost in the income statement and increases in cash and reduction in inventory levels on the balance sheet. Cycle time improvements can create competitive advantage, which we have seen how that impacts the statements. It also impacts lower inventory levels, which we have just discussed, and it could also make for lower capital costs, which impacts the profits on the income statement. Finally, inventory turns can affect the balance sheet by requiring less current asset inventory on the books, requiring lower working capital. So we see here there are lots of connections from the supply chain to the financial statements. So let's talk about key points. We've shown we can connect supply chain transactions and the impact of initiatives directly to the income statement and the balance sheet. This is incredibly powerful. This allows a supply chain leader to communicate directly with the company leaders in the primary language of business. That is in financial terms. There's great power available to the supply chain managers who are able to do this, recognizing that this ability derives for understanding the foundation of accounting principles, practices, and application of these to the supply chain domain. If you have any questions, comments, or suggestions, please use the discussion area. And I look forward to seeing you in the next session.

[lecture_08_06_cost_accounting_cost_systems]
Hi. Welcome to the supply chain finance module. This segment will focus on cost accounting systems. Now, in this segment, the objective is not to make you an expert. Instead, I want to introduce you to some fundamental concepts relating to cost accounting systems, which are also referred to as cost systems. You'll learn that there are different methods used to track cost, and that there are some trade-offs and limitations associated with the different choices that are made for recording costs. Let's start with cost accounting. Cost accounting serves a different objective than financial accounting and different than tax accounting. The objective for cost accounting is to measure costs that will enable the firm to understand their business operations. And it enables the firm to make better decisions, informed with proper information about the costs associated with conducting their business. Cost accounting creates reports and conducts analysis for use by managers within the firm. The analyses are not expected to be used externally. They are used to inform and motivate management action. Therefore, the information that will best inform the management and provide decision support really ought to be tailored for each firm, depending on their situation and their objectives. Keep in mind that for cost accounting, the accountants do not have to follow the Generally Accepted Accounting Principles, because it's not important to make comparisons with external parties. Any comparison of product costs with external companies' product costs would first need to be studied to make sure that the measures are comparable. There are many different systems that exist to track costs and allocate overhead costs. The traditional cost system sets a standard for the cost and then compares the variance from that standard when assessing performance. They often use one factor to allocate the costs, the overhead cost. And that could be labor hours, machine time, or machine hours, or a variety of other different factors. Activity-based cost systems use cost drivers that are based on activities and the resources consumed in performing those activities in order to allocate overhead costs. This will be covered in greater depth in a separate session. There are other cost systems, such as Lean accounting and marginal costing, but we won't spend time talking about those systems here. It would help to outline all the different types and categories of costs, so we start with fixed costs. Now fixed costs are those costs that do not vary with the production of volume-- volume of production. Examples include the cost of monthly mortgage payment for the plant, and the cost of the monthly insurance bill. For these costs, it doesn't matter how many units you make. That monthly payment will be the same regardless of how many products are made. Variable and semi-variable costs are costs that do vary with volume. As an example, the cost of materials, well, that will vary depending on how many products you make. If you make a lot of products, you need a lot of materials and your costs will be higher than if you produced fewer products. A semi-variable cost is one that varies in part. One example is the cost for commissioned salespeople. Many salespeople are paid a base salary, which is fixed, and then they get paid a variable part, which is a function of, how many products did they sell? So that is an example of a semi-variable cost. Direct costs refer to costs of materials and labor that can be directly attributed to the production of a specific product. These are the costs that are included in the cost of goods sold, as we have discussed in previous segments. Now in contrast to direct costs, indirect costs are those that cannot be attributed to the production of a specific product. Cost accounting systems use various methods to take these indirect costs and allocate them to products in order to have a more complete understanding of the total cost for the company to deliver a product to the marketplace. This is referred to as overhead allocation. I just mentioned overhead allocation. For companies that operate manufacturing facilities, it is useful for those factories to understand not just the direct cost of producing a product, but also the total cost of producing their product. This would therefore need to include not just the material and labor costs directly associated with the product, but also those other factory costs that cannot be directly associated with a specific product. This could include the cost of quality control, maintenance, supervision, and utilities in a factory. This introduces the overhead allocation challenge. It is difficult to allocate overhead, allocate indirect costs when there is no direct relationship between the use of the cost-creating resource and the product. How can the company choose to allocate the overhead costs in such a way that provides management with an informed and accurate assessment of the cost to produce a product or serve a customer? This indeed is the challenge for the cost system. Now, most companies use what are commonly referred to as traditional cost systems. Traditional cost systems allocate costs to products using a standard. And the allocation is usually done on the basis of how much labor is used in the production of the product. There are a variety of ways that traditional systems allocate overhead. The fundamental approach is that the indirect costs are allocated by assigning a percentage of the indirect costs to each product or cost object based on a previously agreed standard. Let's use an example. Assume a factory has $100,000 in indirect costs and that factory produces 5 products in the following volumes that we see here, and with each product using a certain amount of labor hours, different labor hours for different products. How much of the overhead should be allocated to the different products? Well, if we use volume to allocate the overhead, well, then we'll use a percentage of the volume produced as a percentage of the overhead allocated to each product. So we see that the total volume of units produced is 38,000. So if we're looking at Product A, we'd say, well, the percentage of overhead allocated if we're using volume as a basis would be 5,000 over 38,000. Well, let's see that played out in an allocation using volume. Sure enough, we see 5,000 divided by 38,000 multiplied by the $100,000 overhead cost. And we see that the portion that Product A would absorb would be $13,158. Of course, we can see that played out through each of these different items. Now, instead of allocating on the basis of volume, what would the case be if we allocated using labor hours? Well, in that case, we will use a percentage of the labor hours used by each respective product as a percentage of the overhead allocated to each product. This results in very different overhead allocations. Let's walk through the math here a little bit. Let's look again a Product A. 200 labor hours consumed in Product A. Well, that as a percentage of the total 2,100 labor hours, that's 200 over 2,100 times $100,000. The allocation of overhead in that case is $9,524. That's a pretty big difference we see between those two. But if we look at some of the other numbers, wow, look at this one, Product E. We see a huge difference. So it really does matter how the accountant makes the decision on how to allocate the overhead, because it can have meaningful impact on the product, how the product costs are represented. Now, 50 years ago, when labor represented a large percentage of the product cost, using labor as a basis for allocating overhead seemed to make some sense. Today, labor costs represent a smaller part of the total product cost, with more costs coming from the indirect category, such as the cost to sell and distribute the product. As a result, allocating overhead on the basis of labor sometimes provides a representative cost, but not always. In order to really make an assessment of whether it's representative, one must give due consideration to the specifics of the business, particularly when choosing the allocation method, of course. And that is considering, well, how important is labor as a factor? Does labor represent a large percentage of the product cost? If so, then it might be representative. Is using standard costs, does that make sense if there's a high degree of complexity? Probably not. Standards do not accurately represent the complexity of production systems. And then, of course, one has to consider the production volume, to make sure there are no abnormalities that might surface if you are allocating the overhead cost over very low volumes. An important concern for the business leader is whether the cost system is accurate enough. It may be possible to increase the accuracy, but it may also require an abnormally high cost to get that improved accuracy. Is the benefit worth the added cost? Therefore, a key challenge in choosing the cost system and then the allocation methods that will best serve the internal needs of management to provide them with better data and information for their business decisions. Actual cost systems, we use actual costs for the reporting and planning purposes. But this is a little misleading, since actual cost systems often include some element of standard costs, as we can see here. It is possible to have an actual cost system that reports the cost of materials using actual quantities but a standard price. Why not use the actual quantity and price? Well, this data is not always readily available and accessible when the product cost reports need to be prepared, and so some standard data is used. As we see here, there are several variants of this actual cost. The company may choose one of these depending upon what provides the most accurate and representative assessment of their costs. Here we see the actual quantities used here. We see here the actual prices are used here. And in this particular case, actual quantities and actual prices are used. But again, that's difficult to have that, because the cycle time to get that data and information is usually pretty long. Now, risks exist with using this kind of a system. If the standard rates used are too low or too high, the result will be inaccurate cost estimates. In either cases, where too much overhead is allocated or absorbed by the product or not enough overhead is allocated, well, this is a problem because the business would be making decisions based on inaccurate information. An example can help illustrate this. Let's take the case of a company that makes bicycle tires. And there's a budgeted overhead of $50,000 each month. That's what they anticipate in their overhead costs. Additionally, each month there's a budgeted volume or production of 25,000 tires. OK. So based on this, what is the planned overhead allocation per tire? Well, $50,000 divided by $25,000 gives you $2 per tire. But what happens when the actual overhead costs were lower, at $48,000, and the actual volume was a little higher, $26,000, well, what you get is over-absorption. The amount of overhead allocated to the product would be $52,000, compared to the actual overhead of $48,000, meaning that the cost system would have added another $4,000 to the cost of the product that should not have been added, at least in the reports they're providing. That makes it tough for management to make proper decisions when the cost system is not accurate. Let's talk about key points. Cost accounting provides important information to management regarding the costs of the business. In our discussion, we spoke only of allocating costs of products. But the cost accountant may also be tasked to identify costs to serve various customer segments, for example. The same principles apply. This is an important process intended to support management decision-making. Traditional cost systems do not always serve that objective well for product cost structures today, and we will see in other sessions other methods to provide more accurate cost reporting. If you have any questions, comments, or suggestions, please use the Discussion area, and I look forward to seeing you in the subsequent session. Thank you.

[lecture_08_07_activity_based_costing]
Hi. Welcome to the Supply Chain Finance Module. This segment will focus on activity-based costing, also known ABC or ABC accounting. Our objective in this segment is to introduce you to the activity-based cost accounting, to review the principles and use of ABC, and make you aware of the ABC method. But we're not going to make you an ABC expert. That would require a lot more time and experience. Hopefully, you'll get enough so that you can be conversant on the issues and recognize the importance and the power of an activity-based system. The first question to ask is, why activity-based cost accounting? Well, traditional methods of allocating overhead worked for times where labor comprised a large part of the product cost. Using volume or the amount of labor hours used in a product as a proxy for the allocating the smaller overhead costs made sense. And it seemed to provide adequate product cost information for management. Today, things are different. Generally speaking, today labor represents a smaller percentage of the product cost. And material and overhead costs represent a higher percentage of the total product cost. In this situation, volume or labor hours would not serve as a good proxy for assessing the consumption of overhead costs. Therefore, it's important to recreate a system that provides a more accurate allocation of overhead to the product costs. Otherwise, management will have inaccurate assessments of the product cost, which may lead to poor product investment decisions. Let's go over a simple example. Assume a children's book company makes two products. One's a standard story book. And another product is the same story book that has been tailored in story and design to a specific child. The actual cost to physically produce the product would be approximately the same. But the customized book would require additional software, product design, additional content creation from writers, and would require a different distribution system to deliver the product to the child. In fact, the company offers free shipping for custom orders. So they're not charging for the additional cost to distribute the product. In the most recent month of operation, for example, the company produced and delivered 2,000 books. 1,900 of them were standard, and 100 were custom. Now the company, to offset the additional costs associated with the custom book, they have added $10 to the sales for a custom product. The overhead cost of distribution center operations are $2,740, although this includes some costs that are specific to standard books, such as the LTL shipping costs, and some costs that are specific to custom books, which would be the book prep and the direct shipment cost. Now using the information provided, if the company allocates the DC costs to each book based on volume, which would be a traditional measure, then the overhead cost per book is $2,740 divided by 2,000 units, or $1.37 per book. This spreads all of the costs evenly across each unit. But of course, as we know, the standard and custom books require different activities that have different costs. We see the custom books used $1,475 of DC activities specific for custom books and $65 in pooled activities, meaning that custom book activities consume $1,540 in resources. Now if we allocate those costs to the custom books, considering that there's 100 books, that results in a $15.40 per unit allocation for custom books. So that means the overhead, using an activity-based system, would be allocated at $15.40. Well, this is more than $14 higher than what would have been charged using a traditional system, or the $1.37 per book. Additionally, that $15.40 per unit allocation is, of course, more than $5 higher than what the company is recovering in cost through their $10 price increase. So this provides very misleading information. If the business leaders use the traditional allocations method, it's going to lead them to mistakenly believe that their custom book business is very profitable. Again, this is a very simple example to help illustrate how a change is necessary in many instances in order to provide more accurate information to business leaders. Well, as the example suggests, ABC is a cost accounting system that entails allocating overhead costs to cost objects based on the amount of resources that those cost objects consume. And the cost objects consume the resources in various activities. Now in activity-based costing, cost drivers are used to determine how much of the resources are consumed. Cost drivers take into consideration the activities that are involved in producing a product or service and the resources that are used in those activities. Ideally the cost driver would serve as a good proxy for the consumption and use of the resources in question. Now I've used the term cost object here. And I want to explain. A cost object can be a product, a service, a customer, or any other segmentation that a management team desires in order for them to better understand that particular situation. Again, keep in mind, this goes back to the concept that cost accounting systems are there to serve the internal needs and decision support needs for business leaders. In an ABC view, costs come from activities. To understand ABC, consider that any product is going to be created using a variety of activities. Therefore the total cost of producing that product would be the total cost of all the activities involved. Put another way, each product that the firm creates and offers is considered in terms of a bundle of activities. Now ABC is especially useful when there's a lot of variety and complexity associated with the production of products. This is the case because with ABC, one can identify costs coming from specific activities that offer a level of detail that the traditional methods of allocating them on the basis of labor hours or machine hours or volume just doesn't permit. When dealing with complexity, I would argue that more detail will provide more accuracy, which will enable better decisions. As a result of this improved accuracy available using ABC, activity-based costing has become one of the more important methods for getting accurate information to support business leaders in their product and service decisions. Let's follow the flow using an activity-based cost system from resources-- you see here, think about the indirect costs that represent the resources that are consumed-- to the activities-- we see four activities outlined here-- to the products, services, and customers. In this case, the cost object we're thinking of as a series of three products, but it could be, as we suggest before, services or customer segments. Now look, if you go online, you can find lots of examples in a variety of different ways to characterize the ABC flow. But they're all going to basically suggest this general flow. This is a simple one. Of course, it's not the only one. So what are the basic steps to ABC? As I had mentioned on the previous slide, if you go online, you can find many examples or a variety of steps for ABC. This is just one set. But you'll find that they are fairly similar. Let's walk through each of these briefly. The first step is to identify all the relevant activities. Usually the cost to describe this is coming from specific functions-- manufacturing, distribution, logistics, order management, order service, et cetera and so forth. But with activity-based costing, functions are not generally used. The activity represents a collection of tasks that sometimes include activities from or tasks from different functions. We describe the activities with a specific format. We describe them in terms of a verb followed by a direct object. This should be describing how the work is done, not necessarily by function or department. Now the second step is identifying the resources consumed for performing the activities. This is done through interview and observation. And it can get tricky because some resources are used in multiple activities. Note that when we're doing an activity-based cost analysis, we do not include unused capacity. The ABC method allocates the costs that are actually used in the production and delivery of the product. Now if the company elects to have excess capacity, that excess capacity is not considered a relevant cost that ought to be borne by a product. Otherwise the product cost would be artificially inflated because they would be reflecting the cost of providing the product and service and the cost of carrying the excess capacity. Those require different analyses. In step three, we start with the resources that were used, which were identified in step two. Now you know the resources used, identify the costs for those resources and then collect those into cost pools by activity. The next step is to determine the cost drivers. Really, what we're looking for is the best measure of the output of an activity, a single measure. So for example, if the activity is ship product, perhaps the best measure would be the number of orders shipped. Or if the activity is store packages, maybe the best measure would be the number of packages stored. But it could be the number of storage runs made. So it really depends on the situation. From our example, we see that the cost drivers are the number of tests associated with the test raw materials activity. Or the cost driver is the number of boxes when we're looking at packaging materials. Or the number of CAD hours when we're looking at design layout. Or the number of items shipped when you're looking at the ship items activity. In step five, we calculate the cost driver rate. This is very simple. We take the costs that were associated with the activity, and we divide it by the cost driver. From our example, we would divide the cost of test raw materials activity and divide it by the cost driver, which we know to be the number of tests. So if the test raw material activity generated $300 in costs, and there were 50 tests conducted, then the cost driver rate would be $300 divided by the cost driver of 50 tests for a cost driver rate of $6 per test. The last step is to trace the costs to the cost objects using the cost driver rates that we just identified. What we really want to do is identify how much of each activity is consumed by a particular product. So let's look at Product C from our example and determine how much of the overhead costs should be allocated to Product C. From the graphic, we can observe that Product C uses only two activities, package materials and ship items. So we see only two of those particular activities associated with Product C. Considering the facts here-- there are 50 boxes of Product C and there are five shipments of Product C-- we can calculate the cost driver rates to be $3 per box and $7 per item shipped. Therefore the total cost to allocate to Product C would equal the sum of 50 boxes times $3 per box and five shipments times $7 per item shipped for a total of $185. This approach would allow us to get a better assessment of cost for each of these different products based on the activities that they were used to create that service. Product A uses all four activities, so its cost allocation may be higher than the allocation for Product C that uses only two activities, unless the volumes for Product C are much higher than that for Product A. It really depends on the amount of use and the cost driver rates. Let's talk about key points. Hopefully, this has illustrated how an activity-based cost system can provide much more accuracy in the allocation of indirect costs especially in environments with a lot of complexity and large expenses, where there's lots of different indirect and support resources, with multiple products and customers or custom requirements. The system is designed to capture great detail. But of course, it does require additional effort and data. Activity-based cost systems are less important when a product cost has a high labor component or when it's a relatively simple environment where there's a single product or a limited diversity of products or customers or processes or geographies. ABC systems can provide a lot of detail to management that enables them to make product or customer investment decisions with the necessary accuracy. But an important question to ask is, how accurate does our cost reporting system need to be? It's a challenging question that is up for the management to decide. And if you have any questions, comments, or suggestions, please use the discussion area. I look forward to seeing you in the next segment. Thank you.

[lecture_08_08_working_capital]
Hi. Welcome to the Supply Chain Finance module. In this segment, we will focus on working capital. Our objective in this segment is to introduce you to working capital and identify the role that supply chain managers play in assisting in the managing of working capital. First, what is working capital. Well qualitatively, working capital is a basic measure of both the company's efficiency as well as its short term financial health. One way to think about it is that it's the amount of financial resources that are used to conduct daily business. And it includes current assets such as accounts receivable, cash, and inventory. Tracking working capital is therefore important to best understand how the company's resources are being used to serve its business objectives. Now that is rather general, so a more detailed description is wanted. Quantitatively, we calculate working capital by subtracting the current liabilities from the current assets. More often than not, working capital is presented in terms of a positive monetary figure. When the figure is positive, it represents a net amount of financial resources or capital that's required in order to operate the business. And it also indicates that the business has enough current assets to continue operations. If the working capital figure is negative, that could be a good or a bad indicator. In the worst case, a negative working capital figure indicates that the business does not have enough current assets on hand to pay their accounts payable and short term debt obligations. If the company cannot convert other assets into cash, then the business will not be able to satisfy their short term obligations and they run the risk of losing credit or worse, they run the risk of bankruptcy. Now in some cases, a company is able to operate on negative working capital. How can that be. Well this can occur when a company can collect payments in advance of the time that they need to pay their suppliers. Meaning that they're using the money from customers to fund the operation of the business. This can work for subscription businesses or businesses where the customer pays upfront for a product that is produced and delivered at a later date. Now the primary components of working capital are accounts receivable, inventory, accounts payable cash, short term investments, and short term debt. Now we managers and leaders in the supply chain can have an impact on these factors. That is-- accounts receivable, inventory, and accounts payable. But not so much on these factors, short term investments and short term debt. And we'll see over the course of our time together what managers might be able to do to deal with accounts receivable, inventory, and accounts payable. Sometimes working capital is measured in terms of a ratio. And one of the more common ones is called the current ratio. The current ratio is calculated by dividing the current assets by the current liabilities. If the current ratio is less than one, the business has negative working capital, meaning that the current assets are less than the current liabilities. And that means that the company cannot pay their current liabilities with their current assets. If the current ratio is greater than one, but less than two, this is often considered a healthy range for the current ratio. And if the current ratio is above two, this is considered a potentially unhealthy range, possibly suggesting that the business is using its working capital inefficiently. In this case, the business may have too much inventory on hand compared with what is necessary to serve the business. This, of course, depends on the business model and go to market approach. So the ratio is more of an indicator that warrants deeper analysis. Now a more stringent ratio is called the acid test. The acid test uses a slightly different formula for calculating the current ratio, including only cash, accounts receivable, and short term investments in the numerator. It does not include inventory. This essentially assesses the ability of the company to pay off its current liabilities by using only current assets that are cash or that can be quickly converted into cash. Because it would likely take some time to convert inventory into cash, inventory is not included as asset in the acid test. Working capital performance is also measured in terms of working capital turnover. This is another ratio which gives an indication of how the company is able to generate sales using the working capital. This ratio helps answer the question, how effectively is a company using its working capital to generate sales. We calculate working capital turnover by dividing the sales for a particular period of time by the working capital. In general, a higher working capital turnover is preferable because it means that the company is able to generate more sales with the same amount of working capital. We've established that working capital is important for the business for several reasons. Of course, it's an indicator of the efficiency of the business, but also an indicator of the financial health of a business. Basically, can they pay their bills. Well given that, let's go over some options for the company to consider for actively managing their working capital. One option is to collect the receivables quickly. This may seem like an obvious pursuit, but it carries additional weight when put in the context of proactively managing the working capital. Some firms are very aggressive in collections and manage them by creating terms and conditions to provide incentives for their customers to pay them on time and even early. It's not uncommon to hear of a term called 2% net 10 days. And that means that the company would give a 2% discount on the outstanding balance if the customer pays your bill within 10 days of receipt of the invoice. Another way to manage the working capital is to stretch the payables out as long as possible. But there is a risk to taking this approach because this could damage the relationship with suppliers. It may also affect the suppliers willingness to supply and their ability to respond to critical needs. And worse, it may damage their supplier's ability to operate. So if an organization intends to extend the payable cycle with its suppliers, it may be better to structure those arrangements in advance with the supplier so that the supplier is not surprised and put into a potentially problematic flow situation. Companies can also manage the inventories to lower levels, so long as it isn't enough to provide the service that the business needs to serve their customers. Avoid cutting inventory to reduce working capital requirements without first assessing the impact of those cuts on service level. Another alternative is to consider converting current liabilities into long term liabilities. Now this doesn't make those liabilities go away, but it does take the obligation out of the current time frame. The supply chain manager has the potential to impact a number of the components of working capital. And the first and most significant impact will come from managing the inventory, just as we've mentioned. As I just suggested, managing the inventory levels down makes sense but only in the proper context of the business situation and the business objective, as well as the supply chain strategy. For example, if a company competes on the basis of having a high service level, then it makes sense for the company to maintain high inventory levels as well as responsive supply chains. All the same, the supply chain manager is responsible for making sure that the inventory policy is consistent with the business date and for managing the inventory levels efficiently and effectively. The supply chain manager also has an impact on two other primary components. And those are accounts receivable and accounts payable. By working with suppliers and customers, maintaining working relationships with each, and by structuring the terms and conditions of sale with each, the supply chain manager can directly affect the cash flows coming from customers and flowing to suppliers. This slide shows a few selected ways that supply chain managers can impact working capital through different supply chain practices and policies, some of which we've just described. On the left hand side of the slide, we see a list of practices or potential actions. The column on the right list advantages and then disadvantages from using that practice or policy. In the first example, we see extended payment terms from suppliers. This means that the company would arrange to pay the suppliers longer than the standard payment term. That helps working capital by reducing the amount of cash required to pay the supplier in the near term. But the supplier will probably increase their pricing at some point in order to offset the higher cost that they have to serve your company. On the second line we see discount for early payment by customers. This helps working capital by increasing the cash component and reducing the accounts payable component. But the company will receive less than the full revenue from the sale. If the company finances raw material or work in process for suppliers, this will raise the working capital requirements because the company's likely financing the materials with short term debt. On the plus side, it will more likely assure supply from that small supplier. So we can see from some of these examples that there is an impact on working capital as well as an impact on operating business from these different practices. Well that makes sense. Business operations will impact working capital and vice versa. So it is helpful to think about these in advance and select the practices that work best for the company and the financing plans. Let's talk about working capital requirements. How much working capital is necessary to properly operate the business. Well, enough working capital is enough to serve the customers, to deal with some variation in cash flows, and to operate the business overall. Keep in mind that when we say operate the business, this means operating at the target service level and therefore maintaining the requisite inventory to deliver that service level. The company should be considerate of variations in cash flows and therefore one should understand the timing of the operational cash flows. Start from the time of purchasing materials that the company would use to produce and sell the product, consider the time from the purchase of materials to the time of payment to the suppliers, and then consider the time from the product sale to the time it takes for the customer to pay for the product. These three time-driven development are components of the cash cycle, which we will speak about in another segment. Let's talk about the key points. Working capital represents the resources that are utilized to operate the business and keep it functioning. Positive working capital is generally better than negative working capital, but it really depends on the business model of the company and of the timing of the cash flows to suppliers and from customers. Remember, it's possible to operate with negative working capital. The supply team manager plays a key role in managing working capital because the supply chain has control of inventory as well as influence on accounts receivable and accounts payable, two other important components of working capital. If you have any questions, comments, or suggestions, please use the discussion area and I look forward to working with you in another segment. Thank you.

[lecture_08_09_cash_to_cash_cycle]
Hi. Welcome to the supply chain finance module. In this segment, we will focus on the cash to cash cycle, also known as the cash conversion cycle. Our objective for this segment is to introduce you to the concept of the cash to cash cycle. I'll also explain the mechanics of how to calculate the cash to cash cycle, and we'll review some of the ways to interpret that cycle. The cash to cash cycle is known by several terms, including the cash conversion cycle, the cash cycle, and the operating cycle. It is a liquidity measure, meaning that it provides an indication of the ability of the company to pay its bills using the cash, or other assets that can be quickly converted into cash. The cash cycle expresses liquidity in terms of the amount of time, measured in days, that a company uses to sell its inventory, collect its receivables, and pay its accounts payable. In simplest terms, the cash conversion cycle is the amount of time that it takes a company to use cash to buy materials, make a product, sell the product, and then get paid by the customer. This is how much time the company's cash is tied up in the operation of the business. The shorter the total cash cycle, the less amount of time that cash is needed. This is a good thing, because that means that the company can convert its assets into cash more quickly, making it more capable of meeting its financial obligations. As I just mentioned, the cash conversion cycle considers the time it takes for three specific cash cycles. First, consider the days of inventory outstanding. This is the number of days that it takes for the company to convert inventory into a sale. Next is the days of sales outstanding. This is the number of days that it takes the company to get paid by the customer. Lastly is the days of payables outstanding. This is the number of days that it takes the company to pay its suppliers. We calculate the cash conversion cycle by adding the days of inventory outstanding to the days of sales outstanding, and then subtract in the days of payables outstanding. Basically, we add the amount of time that we need to use the money-- making inventory, selling the product, and waiting to get paid-- and then we deduct the amount of time we get to use suppliers' money-- that is, the accounts payable. Let's look at each of these elements to fully understand each. The days of inventory outstanding is calculated by dividing the average inventory by the cost of sales-- also known as cost of goods sold, or COGS-- and then multiplying that by 365. The DIO answers the question, how long does it take the company to convert the inventory into a sale. In the formula we use cost of sales, or COGS, because inventory is maintained on the assets at cost. And the cost of sales indicates how much was sold over the year. When you compare the cost of sales over an annual period with the amount of inventory on hand, you get an indication of how long that inventory was on hand before it was sold, hence the amount of time that the cash was tied up. Now which is better, a high DIO or a low DIO? And what are the pros and cons? In general, a lower DIO is better, but it varies by industry. It is best to understand the reasons for the high or the low DIO. However, a firm may choose to have a higher DIO, recognizing the need for a longer production cycle, and maybe a longer sales cycle for custom products, for example. This illustrates a few ways to calculate the DIO. Basically, the same components, but arranged in a slightly different way. The main thing to point out here is that the average inventory should be used. This will require looking at two different balance sheets to understand how the inventory may have varied over the sales time period. The days of payables outstanding is calculated by dividing the average accounts payable by the cost of sales, and then multiplying that by 365. The DPO answers the question, how long does it take the company to pay its suppliers. In the formula, we use the cost of sales, or COGS, because materials are recorded as inventory assets at cost. We look at the average accounts payable, because that tells us how much material was purchased, on average, over the time period. Just as we had to calculate the average of for the DIO calculation, we must similarly calculate the average accounts payable for the DPO. We ask again, which is better, a high DPO or low DPO? And what are the pros and cons? In general, it's better to have a higher, or a longer, DPO, because that means you're enjoying the benefit of holding the cash longer before you pay the supplier. But there is a downside to longer DPO, if this is damaging the relationship with the supplier. Or even worse, damaging the supplier's ability to perform by extending their payment to them. So one really needs to be considerate of the impact of extending payments on your suppliers, lest you force them into unappealing situations. The days of sales outstanding is calculated by dividing the average accounts receivable by the total sales, and then multiplying that by 365. The DSO answers the question, how long does it take the company to get paid by its customers after it makes a sale. In the formula, we use total sales, because in this case, we are referring to sold product, where the sale is recorded at the revenue, or sales value of the sale. We look at the average accounts receivable, because that tells us how much sales revenue was due, but not yet received. And just as we had to calculate the average accounts payable for the DPO calculation, we must similarly calculate the average accounts receivable for the DSO. We ask again, which is better, a high DSO or a low DSO? What are the pros and cons? In general, a low days of sales outstanding is better, because that means it takes your company fewer days to collect from customers. That means you'll get your cash faster, and makes your working capital more liquid. However, a high DSO might indicate that the firm is providing financial support to its suppliers, which might not be a bad thing if this is purposeful, and useful to lock in suppliers. Alternatively, it could mean that the company is selling to less credit worthy customers, in order to make their sales. We just don't know. Further research really is necessary to understand it. Let's look at a comparison of the cash conversion cycles in the automotive industry in 2014. I pulled the data that we see here from the annual reports of Toyota, General Motors, Tesla, and Honda. This data comes right off the income statement of the balance sheet, and you too can quickly conduct this kind of analysis. It's a very simple calculation that provides a lot of insight, and an interesting comparison across an industry. And perhaps raises lots of questions. Let's look at the calculations in particular, and let's start with days of inventory outstanding. DIO is calculated by taking the average inventory, dividing it by the cost of sales, and then multiplying it by 365. For Tesla, we calculate the average inventory by adding the starting inventory to the ending inventory, and dividing that by two. This means adding $340,355, plus $953,675, dividing, again, by two, which gives you the average of $647,015. We divide that average by the cost of sales, which is $2,316,685. Multiplying that by 365, to arrive at 101.9 days. You can go through each of these cases to calculate the DIO, the DSO, and the DPO, and the total the cash to cash cycle for each of these companies. This would be a good exercise, and give you some good practice in this. Looking at the data overall, we can see that firms have very different approaches that affects their cash conversion cycle, as well as their working capital. A primary distinction between these companies is that GM, Honda, and Toyota sell through a traditional franchise dealership model, so they are not selling direct to the consumer. Tesla operates with a company owned store model, and for some of their products, the customer will pay some money upfront, in advance of the actual delivery of the product. Most of the other automotive companies have financing arms that fund the purchase of the vehicle by the consumer. One would need to consider these figures and these facts in light of those distinctions. Overall, we can see that GM has a negative cash cycle, which means that they can sell a product and get paid before they have to pay their suppliers. Looking at their DPO, it's long by comparison with DSO and DIO, and their days of sales outstanding is very short. This may also tell us more about their relationship with their dealers, and that they are getting paid by the dealers quickly. Toyota, by comparison, has over a three month cash cycle, with a long days of sales outstanding. This may indicate that Toyota is giving their dealers a lot longer to pay for their purchases. Honda is more balanced all around, with each, DSO, DIO, and DPO taking around two months. 50 to 60 days. Tesla, the new automotive company, has an interesting model, based on these figures. It takes them a long time to sell a product, over 100 days, but they collect from customers in about two weeks. And they also extend payments to their suppliers to nearly three months. This may make sense for a very high-end product being sold direct to consumers. Let's look at the cash cycle graphically, which will give us a different perspective, considering the cycle along a timeline. The timeline starts where materials are purchased, raw materials or finished goods. And then that ownership is transferred to the purchaser. The DIO represents, therefore, the time between when raw material purchases are made and the time when the finished product is sold to the customer. Again, how long it takes to convert that material inventory into a sale. The next part is the days of sales outstanding. Recall that DSO represents how long it takes from the time you sell that product, to the time you can actually get paid. When we calculate to cash to cash cycle, we add the DSO and DIO together, because that's how long it is for the company, from the time they make a commitment to purchase material to the time they collect the payment from the sale of a finished product. But that's not the total time that the company needs to fund the operation, because from that total, we can deduct the time that the company has to pay the supplier. When the company pays the supplier, it's really the first time that the company needs to outlay cash for the production of a product. And so we subtract the DPO, or the days of payable outstanding, to arrive at our cash to cash cycle. What this tells us is that the cash to cash cycle provides some input into the timing of working capital requirements. If the company can adjust the timing of even one of these different events-- the timing of the sale, the terms of payment to supplier, or from the customer, the timing of the purchase of materials from the supplier. All of these, or each of these can have a meaningful impact on the amount of time that the company needs to fund its operation, as well as the amount of capital that is required. Let's talk about our key lessons. It's important that we understand the components of the cash conversion cycle. We should also recognize that these are important levers for the company to use to proactively manage how much financial resources are required to operate the business. Those of us in the supply chain can play a role in this, by actually understanding the components, and working to adjust them to the favor of the company-- by developing favorable terms with suppliers and with customers, which would affect the DPO and the DSO. And by reducing the sourcing and production cycle time, which would reduce the DIO. Finally, the cash conversion cycle can provide useful input to the timing of working capital requirements, adding a time dimension to the working capital requirements that was not there before. If you have any questions, comments, or suggestions, please use the discussion area, and I look forward to seeing you in future sessions. Thank you.

[lecture_09_00_introduction]
Our first lesson this week focuses on cash flows. We're going to talk a lot about cash flows this week. The different types of cash flows inside of a firm, the magnitude and the timing of cash flows. And most importantly, we'll learn how to describe our supply chain designs in terms of cash flows. So we'll start by introducing, again, revisiting the concept of a supply chain being comprised of three types of flows, physical, information, and financial flows. And we're really going to focus on these financial flows throughout this whole week because they form the basis of evaluating our supply chain designs. The decisions that are made inside of a firm regarding budgeting, where to put budgets, as well as the way that the firm describes how it's creating value for investors are all based on financial flows. And most specifically, what we'll get to toward the end of the lesson are free cash flows. These are the basis for evaluation. So no matter which metrics you use to come up with your designs or describe them within the supply chain organization, we will need to boil them down to free cash flows in order to compete for resources and budget with other groups inside the firm. So it's an important process. So we're going to get to free cash flows at the end. On the way we'll start with a brief review of corporate finance. For those of you familiar with corporate finance, it'll make sure that we're on the same page in terms of the terminology we're using. For those of you less comfortable with finance, it may help you focus on the most relevant concepts that we're going to cover this week and identify where you may want to bone up a bit more on those concepts. Then we'll dive into different types of cash flows. We have to first describe how we're going to estimate the cash flows in the future that are going to be based on the designs we're coming up with. And these are the projected cash flows. Then we'll learn how to distinguish which of the cash flows are specifically relevant to our initiatives, the designs that we're coming up with in our supply chain, and make sure we're only counting the ones that are relevant. And then we'll combine these into the free cash flows. And we have a framework and a structure for you to do this and show some examples of how to take supply chain designs and turn them into free cash flows. We'll close this lesson with a bit more discussion of working capital. Working capital is something that almost every one of our supply chain initiatives is going to have an impact on. And it's often difficult to think about how this capital becomes a cash flow. So we'll spend some time talking about that as well. All right, let's get started.

[lecture_09_01_financial_flows_and_evaluation]
We begin this lesson by considering the financial flows in our supply chain, and start to understand how we'll use financial flows to evaluate various supply chain design options. So, as we've seen before, the supply chain is comprised of three flows. There is the physical flow of goods that go from suppliers to our firm and that we then turn into finished goods that we supply to our customers. In return, our customers pay us for those finished goods, and then we pay suppliers in the form of money for the goods that they send us. To enable those physical and financial flows to happen, we also have information flows, or data, to communicate across the supply chain. So we've seen this before. We've also seen how the supply chain can be comprised of multiple firms. So instead of manufacturing our goods ourselves, we could contract it out to a contract manufacturer. Instead of running our own transportation fleet, we could also use a third-party logistics provider to do our warehousing and/or our transportation. And so the physical goods may actually not even flow inside of our firm at all. It may not enter our facilities. It may flow completely through third parties. However, the financial flow almost always occurs through our firm. The customers will pay us for the goods that are being provided, and we will then pay our suppliers, as well as the service providers that we contract, in our supply chain. Now, the design of the supply chain is not only about how we're going to make the physical flows move across organizations but the financial flows can happen differently as well. So, for example, I've added some different arrows here as an alternative to paying suppliers directly. We could instead pay more to our contract manufacturer and then have the contract manufacturer pay the suppliers. And the design of how these flows happen across the supply chain, as well as the timing of those flows, will have an impact on the value that we're creating and therefore on how valuable our supply chain designs are. So we'll be also considering the financial flows and the design of those in addition to the physical flows when we consider our supply chain. Beyond the flows between parties in our supply chain, we also have some flows inside of our company, inside of our firm, that we have to consider. And those are our investments we're making internally in our supply chain. So, first of all, before we make an investment, we have to have money, or capital. The primary source for capital for a firm is from its stockholders, or equity investors, and those stockholders purchase stock in our firm to give us capital. The firm may then contract out with debtors, banks, or bond holders, to raise additional capital to expand that pool of capital that can be used for investments. And there's a contraction contractual agreement that we'll pay back those debtors. For example, if you borrow money from a bank you'll have a loan contract where you have to pay them back in a certain period of time. There's nothing not a contractual agreement with our investors, our equity investors, but we hope we'll be able to make enough money to pay them back. How are we going to do that? Well, again, we're going to be taking this capital and investing it inside of our firm, inside of our operational assets. And those investments are things that the supply chain professionals in the organization will be involved in. We may not be deciding how to raise the capital-- down here, the allocation of debt and equity-- but we are definitely involved in determining how to invest it in the operational assets because we are the ones that are helping to design the supply chain where those assets are going to be deployed. In addition, we'll manage those assets and the partners in our supply chain to generate future cash flows. And, hopefully, by managing the supply chain well, our revenues will be greater than our expenses. And, therefore, we will make money. If we cover more than our expenses and the amount of money required to invest in our assets, we will make money. And if we make enough money we will then be able to fulfill the promise we've made to our equity investors in paying them a dividend. Now, the comparison to how well we're doing is really a comparison that our stockholders will make versus other options they have. So they could invest their money elsewhere, in financial assets, and then create a portfolio of options that will give them future returns. So their investment in our firm is a part of their portfolio that they're investing in. And, given our ability to provide the returns in the form of dividends to our investors, we will be attractive to investors or not compared with other assets they can make. So that's the basic proposition, is we're trying to do a good job managing our supply chain and our operations, which is the primary way that you create value in a firm. So when you look more broadly at the financial flows in the supply chain, and it's not just the flows we saw earlier that aligned with the physical flows, but also we have some internal flows inside of our firm in the form of operational assets. So those asset investments, we have the expenses we pay to our suppliers and service providers, we hopefully will get enough revenues to cover both of those. And, if we do that, we will then also have to pay some taxes. So these are the basic financial flows that will occur in the supply chain that we have to consider when making decisions about our designs. I want to give a few examples of some of the investments we can make inside the firm. And these are, again, components of our supply chain design. So, for example, we could add a new warehouse to be closer to our customers and provide them better service, and then hopefully drive more revenue. We could also invest in more inventory, put more stock on the shelf, give our customers more selection. And, therefore, they'll make more purchases. And, again, drive more revenue. We could add a production line and expand our manufacturing capacity, and produce more goods. And, again, drive more revenue. And you're seeing a theme here. We could also invest in a truck fleet and maybe provide better service than what our contract carriers could provide, or we can do it more efficiently. And, in this case, maybe not drive more revenue but reduce our costs by having our own fleet rather than contracting it out. So hopefully our investments will either drive more revenue or will reduce the ongoing expenses we have in our firm, and, therefore, allow us to make money, have positive cash flows. Our firm may be in a situation where it wants to reduce investments, where they may need capital and don't want to go raise it from debt holders. And, therefore, they want to reduce the investments that are required in the firm to free up capital. In that case, we may be doing some of the opposite of the investments. And that would be, instead of adding a warehouse, we'll close a warehouse. And, instead of adding inventory, we're going to reduce inventory. Instead of expanding our production and transportation, we may outsource it to other firms so that we don't even have to invest in assets at all. We don't have to own a fleet of trucks at all. We could outsource it to a transportation provider. Again, we'll pay them more in terms of expenses, but we won't have to have that operational investment in the assets. So there's a lot of decisions that are made whether we're going to invest or divest in assets in our firm. And then that raises the question, well, who decides which investments or divestments to make, and how do they do it? Well, who decides basically comes down to, who has the money? The corporate finance people are the managers of the money. They're the ones that raise the money, and therefore they determine how it's allocated. However, there's a nice quote from a very standard financial textbook used in corporate finance classes that emphasize that it's more than the finance specialists that are involved in these decisions, both in terms of not only in evaluating which ones are good, but also generating them. How do we come up with ideas for investment proposals? And this is the ongoing responsibility for all managers throughout the organization, not just the finance people. And so our ability to generate these supply chain designs is going to be key in coming up with good ideas for positive investments in the firm. But we will also need to understand how they will be evaluated. And that's what we're going to focus on during this week. I'll give you a little glimpse into how that evaluation process works as a pathway of where we're going. And that's how-- this answers the question, how do they decide what to invest in? There's usually a pretty standard process that corporate finance uses that begins by first estimating all of the relevant cash flows that are involved in this particular investment. Once we've estimated all of the cash flows, we calculate what's called a figure of merit to come up with a value of the particular investment over all. And then we compare that figure of merit, or that value, with some kind of an acceptance criterion. So if we look at this part of the larger diagram we saw earlier, the key to evaluating an investment is by starting by looking at all of these cash flows, here, and turning that into some kind of a figure of merit. And once we've combined them all together-- including the initial investment, here, that happens-- we combine all of these cash flows in the initial investment to come up with a figure of merit. And then we compare this with a criterion. And once we've compared it with that criterion we can make a decision. And that's usually a pretty transparent process inside many firms and that the corporate finance people will do in a process called capital budgeting. This process will also often be applied toward projects, which may not be an investment in assets-- physical assets-- but may require our firm to expend some money in terms of human resources, for example, to conduct a project. So your supply chain design may not require any physical assets, but it may require time from staff. And that decision of whether to allocate budget for that staffing and those human resources may follow a similar process in terms of evaluation. So to sum up this segment, I want to close with one more quote from that finance textbook that was mentioned earlier that describes this broader process and the importance of the different aspects. So the calculation of the figure of merit, which we mentioned was a second part of the evaluation process, requires an understanding of the time value of money and a basic understanding of algebra. And we'll talk about those in the next lesson. But it's often more difficult-- even though algebra may be tricky-- it's often more difficult to determine what are the relevant cash flows with a particular given investment. And that's what we'll focus on in this lesson one, here. We'll spend a lot of time talking about different cash flows in our supply chain, which ones are relevant, and how we assemble them together to create the basis for calculating our figure of merit, which is what we'll discuss in more detail in lesson two.

[lecture_09_02_quick_review_of_corporate_finance]
Before I dive into the specifics of determining the relevant cash flows, we're going to do a quick review of corporate finance to make sure we're using common terminology. And also, introduce the financial statements again to provide a framework for us to consolidate these cash flows. So the financial statements we're going to use going forward to consolidate all the relevant cash flows are the balance sheet and the income statement. You may be familiar with these, but I want to go through again quickly to make sure we're using the common terminology, and to also highlight which aspects of the balance sheet and income statement we're going to be considering. So the balance sheet is a snapshot of the firm's value at any given time. It consists of assets, liabilities, and then whatever's left over is the shareholders' equity or the net worth of the company. So in terms of assets, the things we're going to consider, at least in current assets that are current year, are going to be our supply chain designs that we're going to consider are the accounts receivable and the inventory. There are the things that our supply chain design will affect, and that will be consumed in the current year. These are current assets. We also have some things that will be used over several years, over future years, the property, plant, and equipment. We are going to consider things like warehouses or manufacturing equipment, trucks, and so forth. And we'll also have to consider the depreciation of those assets over time. So that's the primary assets we're going to look at. In terms of liabilities, there's again current and long term liabilities. And the ones that our supply chain design will mostly affect is how we pay our suppliers in terms of accounts payable. Finally, in terms of shareholders' equity, that's what's left over the end. We do want to understand retained earnings, because that's going to be the critical value that we're creating in our firm. But we'd like to break that down further. And that's what the income statement does for us. So the income statement describes the performance between those snapshots on the balance sheet. And it explains why retained earnings has changed over time. It breaks it down for us very nicely. And most of the components of the income statement are things we're going to consider in our supply chain design evaluations. That starts at the top line, the revenue or sales. We'll use the term revenue. And that's the amount that's net the markdowns or discounts we may provide to incentive the customers to buy things. We also then subtract that as a separate line item, the cost of the goods sold, the material costs, the direct labor costs. These are direct cost related to any particular product that we sell.
