And we will use the term cost of goods sold or COGS. Subtracting those gives us our gross income. We then subtract operating expenses that can't necessarily be attributed to one particular product, but are accumulated as operations across all products. And we'll use the term operating expenses. Although a lot of times this is referred to as selling, general, and administrative expenses, or SG&A. So we will definitely consider these operating expenses. And then subtracting the operating expenses from the gross income gives us our operating income. There's another flavor of operating income, and we'll distinguish them in the minute here. But when we take out the depreciation for the longer term assets that we're going to incorporate in our supply chains, we definitely want to incorporate that depreciation into our analysis because it'll affect our income. And therefore the cash flow is related to the income that we generate. Amortization is of non-tangible goods. So we probably won't have much non-tangible goods in our supply chain. So it's mostly depreciation. And then finally, there's things-- the interest, other non-operating expenses. Obviously we wouldn't include non-operating expenses. Interest is incorporated in a special way. And we will describe that in terms of evaluating the supply chain design that's incorporated in the discounted cash flow analysis that we'll talk about in the future. So it's directly a cash flow we're going to estimate. We're going to implicitly incorporate that in our analysis. We will consider the income taxes though because that is a cash flow that's relevant dependent on how well we perform up top. And then obviously we will ignore the extraordinary items. And that gives us our bottom line income. So those are the main components. Most of the income statement we're going to incorporate. Just again for terminology, to distinguish the two types of operating income, EBIT is this part that's earnings before interest and taxes. The interest and taxes below here. So EBIT subtracts out the earnings that come before that. And right above that is the earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization. So again, to distinguish those terms, we'll use the terms in a bit later on as well. Also, I want to quickly review the difference between short and long term expenses. The short term expenses are things that will be included in this year's income statement. At the operating expenses, as we mentioned, is the term we're going to use. The longer term investments are called capital expenditures, or CapEx. And this is for assets that have a useful life beyond this current year. Therefore, we wouldn't incorporate all of the costs as an operating expense for this year's income. So we instead report it on the balance sheet. But yet, we depreciate it over time. And that depreciation will affect our income statement as well. And there's also possibly a salvage value at the end that we may want to incorporate in any investments we make in capital expenditures. Now some expenses could be either a capital expenditure or an operating expense. A good example is information technology which often changes quickly, and therefore could actually only have a useful life of one year. So for example, some companies may replace computers every year. In that case, it may be appropriate to consider it an operating expense. And there is some choice that accountants can make in doing this, whether you capitalize an expense and make it a capital expenditure, or whether you can incorporate it as an operating expense. And this will change our income and our tax burden. And let me give you a little example why. So let's assume our firm-- including everything else except for our computer purchase-- makes $5,000 a year. And we have a $3,000 computer. So we're investing $3,000. So if we make it a capital expense, a CapEx, then we will depreciate that over three-- let's say it have a useful life of three years. We'll depreciate it over three years. And that gives us then an income of 4,000 each year. But if instead we wanted to make it an operating expense, then that 5,000 over the three years is the same. But our expenses are all incorporated in that first year. The full cost of the computer to give us a $2,000 profit. And then there's no cost in the future years to give us 5. So the total in the end ends up being the same $12,000, but how that accumulates in terms of our income or reporting, if we capitalize it, we show more income this first year and less in future years. But it also changes the tax burden because it we'll show more income here. So therefore, we'll pay more taxes. So that's how that decision affects the cash flows. And a firm will often have rules and practices for this. We won't get into the details of whether it should be a capital expense or an operating expense, because you'll have to check with the accountants in your firm for any particular situation. Finally, the other aspect we want to consider and make sure we're on the same page is working capital and how we consider working capital. So the general definition of working capital is current assets minus current liabilities. But the part we're going to really focus on is this cash to cash cycle, the accounts receivable, the inventory, and the accounts payable. These are the parts of the supply chain design that we really affect. The other parts of working capital are not things that we affect completely in our supply chain design. So it's good to remember that the working capital-- the requirements we have for working capital are actually an investment. We have to finance these things. So we'll finance the accounts receivable-- or AR is an abbreviation-- because we're basically enabling our customers to pay later. Therefore, we have to carry the capital to finance that. We also obviously have to have the capital for inventory in our firm before it's purchased. So we're investing in these things. We get some relief in terms of our working capital requirements from our suppliers who allow us to pay later in terms of the form of accounts payables, or AP, which is an abbreviation for accounts payable. And so the net working capital requirement is the accounts receivable plus the inventory minus the accounts payable. That's a term we'll use for working capital. And we're really not going to consider these other aspects when we're looking at working capital investments. Now our supply chain design will definitely be able to influence these requirements by enabling us to collect payment as quickly as possible by preserving our customers well. Obviously by keeping our stock levels as low as possible without hurting our revenues, we can improve our capital situation. And if we delay paying suppliers as long as possible, that will help things as well. So there are aspects of working capital that we will influence on our supply chain design. And we want to consider how we incorporate those investments in evaluating our design.

[lecture_09_03_projected_cash_flows]
OK, let's dive into cash flows themselves. And one of the aspects we're going to have to think about with cash flows is how we project them into the future. Why is that? Remember our investment valuation is considering how we're going to invest in assets to generate future cash flows. So, we need to then estimate what those cash flows will be in the future in order to determine if this investment is actually worthwhile. So, cash flows are money that comes in and out of our firm and it happens over time. It's a combination of cash inflows, which are considered positive inflows into our company, and cash outflows where we spend money and it's negative-- cash is leaving the firm and it's a negative number. Here's a list of the kinds of things that we will consider in our projected cash flows. And it might be good for you to take this list, pause the video for a moment, and think about whether each of these is an inflow into the firm or an outflow out of the firm. And maybe you could mark each one with a plus sign if it's a positive in inflow and a negative sign if it's an outflow. OK. Hopefully you had a chance to think about the different types of cash flows and consider whether they're inflows or outflows. We'll start with the first one. Original investment is going to be an expense-- we're spending money upfront to invest-- so money is leaving the firm and it will be an outflow. Increased revenues, which we hope will get a significant amount to offset the investments, will be inflows into our firm-- we're getting money into the firm so it's positive. Another kind of increase is we could have increased costs that go with the particular initiative. And an increase in cost means that more money is leaving the firm, and therefore it's an outflow. On the flip side, if we can reduce costs, which hopefully we can do with our supply chain designs, we can reduce the amount of cash that's leaving the firm, and therefore that's a net positive or an inflow into the firm. By avoiding spending we're having an inflow into the firm. If our initiative actually has a diminishing impact on revenues then there's less money coming into the firm and that's a negative cash flow. At the end of the project we may have some assets that remain and have some residual value and we can sell those assets, and therefore a salvage value of any assets at the end will give us a positive cash flow-- money coming into the firm. And on the flip side, at the end of the initiative we may have some close down costs, which would be an outflow. So, you can see there's a mixture of positive and negative cash flows and they happen over time. So, one way we draw these pictures, the positive cash flows being arrows upwards and the negative cash flows being arrows downwards, could look like this. So it's a series of cash flows-- positive and negative-- they happen at various times-- it looks a little bit messy. So, one thing we're going to have to do is group these or aggregate these cash flows into tidier buckets, and we need to define some time periods then. We're going to aggregate the cash flows, add up all the cash flows over a certain time period. Those time periods are often either monthly, quarterly, or annually-- on a yearly basis. And so given the nature of our supply chain designs are typically a little longer term and a lot of the capital budgeting that happens in the firm ends up being over a longer period of time or an annual budgeting process will often have buckets of annual time periods. And for most of the exercises in this week we'll consider annual time buckets. Now common practice assumes that the cash flows occur at the end of the projected period. So the income statement transactions, the things that happened throughout the year-- the revenues that come in, the material cost we spend, the COGS, the other operating expenses we have-- those would accumulate throughout the year-- throughout the time period. And therefore we would account for those, add them all up and account for them, at the end of the period or at the end of the year. Balance sheet transactions are a little different because they could happen more immediately. And so therefore you want to try to group them around the annual time bucket when they happen. And generally one of the things that you want to consider is that they could happen immediately or very soon to immediately after the project begins. So obviously an initial investment in some kind of assets might happen right away but we could also have, for example, a reduction in inventory. And that could happen very close to time zero because when we make our first purchase order we purchase less to replenish our inventory, and therefore we reduce that working capital right away. So, you can have some cash flows and period zero but generally we'll move them to the end of the time period in which they occur. So for our example here, those blue arrows could be grouped a little bit more tidily into time buckets that are these black arrows that are at the end of the time period, we add them together. And you can take out your ruler if you want, I think I measured them pretty well, so that the arrows should add up in a cumulative way that the two positive ones here add up to be this larger one. And the positive and the negative offset each other to give us a net of positive cash flow. And so there's offsetting positive and negative cash flows over time and we group them all into the end of the time period. So these two are grouped into this one and these three are all added together into this one. If we did this in terms of numbers, again, this is fairly representative-- I measured the arrows so hopefully the numbers should be right-- but if we had different cash flows over those periods we add them all up together and get our cumulative cash flows. And this is what we'll use for our projections-- an easier number that adds up all the various cash flows that happened throughout the period. The next question is how long should our time horizon be? How many periods should we include in our analysis? And this depends, oftentimes the decision makers will want to include the time period when there's the most dynamic periods of activity-- when things are changing-- and wrap it up with some kind of a steady state when the cash flows are stabilized in some way. However, if you go too long waiting for that steady state, the uncertainty in being able to project or estimate what those cash flows will be-- it increases over time, so we're less confident of the cash flows that are in the future. So you don't want it to be too long. There's no right answer and the decision makers involved in the particular decision will have to come to some agreement on this. But once you decide the time period you're going to have to figure out how to wrap the project up, or the initiative up. And there's two ways to do this. One is you actually consider that all of the activities end up in a salvage cash flow. So for example, if you have assets at the end you're going to sell those and have a positive cash flow of those sales. You're going to recover the working capital that you may have deployed in the project. Or you may have costs to close things down and you have to have those costs at the end. And you try to account for all the costs that would wrap up the project in a salvage value. The other option is to consider that the initiative you've implemented is something that will continue indefinitely and in such a way where you can estimate the value of the cash flows that comprise a perpetuity. And we'll talk more about what a perpetuity is later, but this is just a way of considering that the cash flows may happen indefinitely and we can we come up with a value to estimate that at the end of our time horizon. One final consideration is inflation. Our cash flows that happened initially in the first year may increase over time with inflation. And we have to think about how we incorporate that. There's two ways to do it. The most common way is people consider nominal cash flows, which is incorporating inflation in the prices and the cost directly. So for example, if you have a revenue and you in period one, two, and three-- let's say these are years-- and we have a 3% annual inflation, then our revenue in period one may be 100 but in period two would be 103 and in period 3 would be 106-- I think a little bit more than that, 106.1. So our revenues would keep going up nominally with the rate of inflation. Now some of the cash flows don't go up over time. So for example, if we had depreciation that would be maybe 10 in year one it would stay 10 throughout because if we have a straight line depreciation. So, there may be a mixture of different inflation rates. Some of them not having inflation at all for the different components. And your salaries may go up at different rates than your raw materials, et cetera. So it can get a little complicated to incorporate all these different inflation rates. To simplify that, you may take inflation out completely and use what we call real cash flows. The flip side is that some of these cash flows that do remain constant over time have to be-- depreciation being one, it's a flat line number-- have to be reduced according to the rate of inflation to make it equivalent. This gets a little complicated as well. And in the end, once you decide whether you're going to use nominal or real cash flows you'll have to use a discount rate in doing your analysis. And we'll describe more about that in the next lesson, but the discount rate has to match with the choice of real or nominal cash flows. You can have a real discount rate or a nominal discount rate. In general, I'm giving an introduction to the inflation but I'm also going to say to simplify our exercises we're going to ignore inflation. It's something you'd want to consider in a particular initiative, but to make them the concepts a little easier to understand and the math little easier to follow we're going to ignore inflation in the exercises that we conduct this week.

[lecture_09_04_relevant_cash_flows]
So we just discussed projected cash flows. We're now going to talk about relevant cash flows. What makes a cash flow relevant? Why is this important? Let's come back to our overall evaluation process for an investment or a project. And we noted that, in the project cash flows, we're talking about how to estimate the future cash flows or project them into the future. But we have to make sure also, in this first step, that all the cash flows we are projecting are relevant to the particular investment or project that we're evaluating. So we asked the question, what is a relevant cash flow? We have two principles to help us answer that question. The first principle seems pretty obvious. And that's the cash flow principle that only cash flows where money moves are relevant. So if there's a cash flow where money's not moving, it's not relevant. It kind of seems self-evident that, when there's a flow, there's money moving. But we'll see it can be difficult to assess that at times. The second principle is with or without. So we're only going to consider cash flows relevant if they are different with the investment than without the investment. Or if we're looking at a project, with the project or without the project, any cash flows that differ between those two are relevant and any of the ones that don't differ are not relevant. Let's talk more specifically about these principles. The cash flow principle-- again, money has to move to be counted. So it's either money that's going can be received by the firm or it's a cash inflow or paid out by the firm as a cash outflow. But if the money's not moving in or out of the firm, we don't count it. So some examples of things that are not cash flows are accrual accounting. Accrual accounting may register income when it's earned, when we make a sale. But cash hasn't moved yet. So it's not a cash flow until the cash moves. Some other costs that we will incorporate into our financial statements, like depreciation, are also not cash movements. There's not cash moving with depreciation. The cash related to depreciation all moves when we purchase the asset upfront. Depreciation is a cost where there's no cash movement in the future. In addition, we have to think about whether cash is moving in and out of the firm. So if there is internal budgeting transactions that are equal and it's just internal and there's not a change in cash flows, then that's not relevant. It's not a cash flow. So for example, a headcount transfer where somebody is removed from one group and added to another-- so say we are able to do our work more efficiently in our supply chain group and we can reduce headcount. If that person is then deployed to a different group in the firm, then there's no cash flow related. Because the salaries are still being paid by the firm, even though our internal budget may go down. The flip side is, if we are able to identify employees who are being freed up in another group and use them on our projects, we won't have to incorporate their salary in our project cash flows either. So that internal budgeting is all within the firm. Cash is not moving in or out of the firm. Therefore, it's not a relevant cash flow. The with or without principle is really focused on whether things are incremental and incremental to a particular project or investment, not the overall cash flows in our firm, the overall revenues or costs. And we're not talking about before the project or after the project. But actually, since we're always looking at projecting future cash flows, it's not before or after. But it's better to think about it as two worlds, with or without the project. So we have two directions. We're here in the present. If we're looking forward into the future, if we have time going forward, then we have a with scenario and a without scenario. And what we're looking at is, in the with scenario, what cash flows we have. In the without scenario, how are they different? And we're measuring the cash flows that are different between those two scenarios. And so, the incremental cash flows are the cash flows with the project minus the cash flows without the project. So we'll subtract these two, get this difference to determine the relevant cash flows. And therefore, because we're talking about with or without the project or the investment, obviously, the cash flows are going to be directly related to that project. And they're going to be incremental. Also this is an example of things that are not cash flows, future revenues or costs that will accrue regardless of whether we make this decision to invest or to implement a project. So it won't, again, be all the revenues in our firm but any incremental revenues from implementing a particular initiative. Things that are also not cash flows are overhead costs. Even though they may be operating expenses-- like rent and utilities or supervisor salaries-- if they don't change as a result of implementing the project, with the project, then they're not a cash flow. Even though we may think that by taking inventory out we won't have to use as much utilities in a warehouse, it's not necessarily the case that, with or without the project, we would change our utility consumption. Let's think about these principles in a few different cost categories to get more specific. We talked about depreciation earlier. And again, depreciation is related to an asset that's been purchased already. Therefore, the cash flow happened when we purchased the asset. The full cash flow for the cost of that asset happened earlier. But the accountants will depreciate this over time and incorporate them in our income statement over time. But there's not money moving. Even though we show it in the income statement, there's not actually money that's moving when those depreciation amounts are incorporated in the income statement. However, the depreciation will reduce our income on the income statement. And therefore, it will affect our taxes. We'll have less income as a result of that depreciation, therefore less taxes. And taxes are relevant cash flow. So it's important to incorporate depreciation into our calculations, because of the tax impact. But we'll have to add it back in and make sure that we don't account for the actual depreciation as a cash flow in our analysis. And we'll show you how to do that in a future segment. Another type of accounting cost that we have to consider is overhead. We just set that overhead costs are generally not going to be relevant. Because with or without, they may not change. And they may not be incremental. However, there are situations where overhead could be associated with the scale of the firm. And by adding a new product line, we may have to actually add some headcount to manage that from a marketing perspective or a product perspective. Or if we add equipment, we purchase new equipment, we may need to add some special expertise in terms of maintenance or add more maintenance time. And we can really directly link it to with or without. So you have to make sure the overhead expenses. Although in general, overhead may not be affected by with or without, there may be situations where, as we grow enough, we can identify a specific cash flow difference. Sunk cost is another kind of cost we have to think about. And it's tempting to include these, because it's money that's spent. You've spent the money. But we can't recover it. So it's spilled milk in one sense. And the sunk cost should be ignored. In other words, we don't cry over spilled milk. It happened in the past. We can't change what happened in the past. Therefore, we should only look to the future and the cash flows that happen in the future. This seems pretty obvious. But it can be tricky to implement. And there's an example I'm going to give you of a government contractor in the past. They were seeking a federal guarantee for a loan, because they wanted to continue developing a new major technology. They'd spent a lot of money developing this new technology and needed more money and more capital to keep the project going. And there were two arguments that were made in that situation. One is, it's foolish to abandon a project, because we've already spent $1 billion. We should keep going. Whereas, the other side is, we shouldn't continue a project that we can't get a decent return on. If we can't finish the project-- we've spent $1 billion-- we shouldn't continue it going forward. So which argument is correct? Hopefully, you realize that this is sunk cost and both of the perspectives are wrong. The sunk cost is irrelevant. And especially when you have a large amount of money-- like $1 billion-- it's tempting to incorporate that in your analysis. But it happened in the past. It's not a future cash flow. Therefore, it's not relevant. Cash isn't going to change with or without any future decisions. Opportunity costs are another tricky cost to consider in applying these principles. So an opportunity cost is any cash flow from an alternate option that we will forgo, because of our decision to invest or to implement a project. And we have to have a clear difference. So let's illustrate this with a couple examples. So let's say we have some land that we are planning to sell. That sale will proceed if we don't build on it. So we want to add a warehouse. There's some land that we own. We're deciding whether we build the warehouse or not. And the cash from that sale of the land that would happen if we don't build is an opportunity cost. Another type of opportunity cost is some capacity that may be idle currently, but it has a planned use. So maybe we have a factory line that's down. But somebody's planning to use in the future. Or some warehouse space that's free, but there's a planned use for it. So do we account for the cost of this space or not? It's idle space. Therefore, we think maybe we shouldn't incorporate that. But do we incorporate this opportunity cost or not? The answer is opportunity costs should be included in the evaluation. Again, thinking the with or without principle-- in terms of the land sale, if we implement our warehouse and build our warehouse, with the warehouse we will not sell the land. But without the warehouse-- because they're planning to proceed forward in selling the land-- we're going to forgo the sales revenue. So therefore, it would be relevant cost. Likewise, idle factory lines or warehouse space that has a planned use-- it may be idle now or may be empty warehouse space. But there was a plan to put a new product line either in the factory or in that space. If you forego that plan because of your decision to implement your project, then that opportunity cost of that plan is something we have to incorporate. It's a relevant cost, even though it's not something that's currently happening. The key in all this is there has to be a plan in place. It's something that you're going to clearly forgo this because you would proceed to sell the land or because you would plan to use the idle space. So the key is the intention. It has to be already planned and that we're now going to forgo this. One kind of opportunity cost that is a bit special is any capitol that we free up through our projects. That capital could be reinvested. And there could be an opportunity cost for those investments. But the capital-- the actual capital that we can free up-- is something that we don't incorporate in our analysis, because of the nature of how we do our discounted cash flow analysis. And we'll get to that in the next session. It's implicitly incorporated in how we do our analysis. Therefore, the capital the we are able to free up-- we don't consider any opportunity costs of investing that capital. Let's finish this segment by considering a specific example and seeing how we can determine what cash flows will be relevant. So it's a very simple scenario. We have a fictitious company named White Nile. They're an online retailer. And they are considering a new warehouse. They want to build a new warehouse to provide better service for their home delivery for their retail operations. It might be good now to stop the video and take out a piece of paper and write down some of the cash flows that you think would be relevant. And consider the supply chain design options that you've come across in this course thus far. And think about what kinds of relevant cash flows of costs and revenues and investments and so forth. What cash flows would be relevant in this scenario? OK, hopefully you had some time to take out a piece of paper and write down some ideas for relevant cash flows. And I'll walk through a few of them. You may have thought of some other ones. But I'll walk through a few of them and see if we came up with some of the same ideas. Well, the first one's probably pretty obvious. We're implementing a new warehouse. Therefore, with the project we have a warehouse. Without the project, we don't. So a new warehouse is clearly incremental. There's going to be cash spent. There's a cash flow happening. So it's a relevant cash flow. So you may have thought the warehouse. We also have to think about, there's also equipment that goes in the warehouse. And both of these things would be a capital expenditure. In addition, if you're thinking about better service, hopefully better service made you think that we can also generate some revenues from this. There should be a reason for adding a warehouse. And with better service, we should hopefully be able to serve our customers better, make them happier, and they'll buy more from us. So more revenue-- with the warehouse, with better service, we have to account for only the revenue that would be as a result of the better warehouse. So in the region where we provide better service, that's relevant revenue. In other regions served by other warehouses, that revenue would not be relevant. Because with or without the project, that stays the same. Also, in adding our warehouse, we have labor. Now, labor is an overhead expense. It's an operating expense. It's considered overhead. And we know that overhead can or cannot be considered relevant, depending on with or without. But this is a pretty clear case. We're adding a warehouse. With the warehouse, we need labor. Without the warehouse, we don't need that labor. So it's a pretty clear incremental change, in terms of overhead and labor for the warehouse in this situation. We also to fill the warehouse with inventory. And again, with the project, we have to add warehouse into this warehouse. Without the project, we don't have inventory. And again, we don't have information about the other warehouses or other inventory initiatives. But just for this warehouse, we have to consider the incremental inventory required to fill it up. And that would be working capital investments. And finally, one other thing you may have thought of is transportation cost. This is related to the design of our network. By having an additional warehouse, we can now-- in terms of delivering landed cost to our consumer, our customer-- we can have longer segments of our transportation in full loads to the warehouse. So we could use our own fleet, for example, fill our trucks up, and be very efficient. And then, we'll reduce the distance we're traveling from the warehouse to the customer, where it's going to be less efficient. Perhaps, we are hiring a third-party with small delivery vehicles to deliver. And that'll be a higher cost-per-mile. So we're reducing the distance for those less efficient shipments to the customer. And therefore, overall, we should lower our transportation costs, which is a lower operating expense. And it would be relevant because, with the warehouse we make this change, without, we don't. So hopefully, this example helps you think about what cash flows are relevant or not. We'll use this example again later on. And we'll put some numbers to it to see how this particular situation could be evaluated.

[lecture_09_05_free_cash_flows]
In this segment, we're going to consider another kind of cash flow, and that is a free cash flow. And we'll show how free cash flows help us pull everything together in evaluating our supply chain initiatives. So let's start by asking the question, what are free cash flows? This doesn't sound intuitive, because there's obviously no such thing as free cash. But we will show how cash is freed up by considering this overall investment process that we discussed earlier. So the value proposition of the firm is to invest money internally in its operational assets to generate future cash flows that exceed the investments and then free up cash. And that process-- at the end, we hopefully will free up enough cash to be attractive to our investors. So this will combine the relevant cash flows that are in red-- relevant to a particular initiative-- and it's also projected over time. We may have several periods. As we talked about, there may be several future periods of cash flows to do to consider that are projected in the future. So combining those projected and relevant cash flows and capturing the net of those over time in periods 1, 2, 3, et cetera, we get that pot of free cash flow that's created through this particular initiative. And we're trying to make sure that that cash that's freed up is attractive to our investors. And primarily, we're actually really considering our equity investors. Because the decision to take on debt is a separate decision we want to consider in a different way than we consider our investments and initiatives. So a definition here-- the free cash flows are the funds that are available to the investors, specifically the equity investors, after the firm invests its capital and pays taxes on the profits that come from the investments. So to consider how we're going to get to the free cash flows, we want to make sure we're capturing all of the relevant cash flows that are projected over time in our supply chain. So here again is a picture of our supply chain with the financial flows. And so the kinds of cash flows we want to consider over all are the revenues that come in from customers, the cost of goods sold for those revenues, the direct raw materials we pay to suppliers, the direct costs for those as well as direct costs inside of our firm in terms of labor and equipment et cetera that are dedicated to particular goods. We also have general operating expenses that happen across the firm to generate the revenues that we need to consider. The revenues minus all of our relevant expenses will give us another relevant cash flow, and that is the taxes we have to pay on the profits that come out of that. And finally, we also have investments in capital expenditures and the working capital requirements to run our firm. So these are the main categories of the relevant cash flows that we want to consider overall in our supply chain initiatives. And we'll see that several of these come from the income statement. And the bottom ones here are income statement. And the ones up here, the capital expenditures and the working capital, are from our balance sheet. And so in bringing all these different items together in our supply chain initiative, it's going to be helpful to use incremental financial statements to capture all these projected relevant cash flows. And incremental is important, because it's going to be, again, incremental to this particular project with or without. And we're going to look at it over time in a projected way. So let's start by looking at the incremental income statement. And we've seen this before in the earlier segment, the basic aspects of the income statement. And basically, everything on the income statement is going to be relevant except for interest expense, because that's related to that financing decision, which is something we don't want to mix in with our investment analysis. Obviously, we wouldn't want to consider non-operating expenses in our supply chain, and we wouldn't want to consider extraordinary items. But we still want to incorporate income taxes. So in keeping just these most relevant cash flows for our investment decision, we see a slightly simplified income statement. And we see that once we get our EBIT here at the bottom and subtract our income taxes based on that EBIT, we have a new term called NOPAT-- Net Operating Profit After Taxes. And that'll be a useful term in calculating our free cash flows. And another way of defining this is being the EBIT minus the taxes we pay on the EBIT, or 1 minus the tax rate times the EBIT. Now remember, depreciation, we've incorporated it here, but remember, it's not really a cash flow. It's not really relevant cash flow, because cash is not moving. It doesn't satisfy that first cash flow principle. Cash is not moving. We've needed to incorporate it here to get our income taxes correct, but we should take it back out, because it's not a cash flow. So the bottom line-- the relevant cash flows from the NOPAT, this new term we came up with, is our after tax income. And then we've added back in the depreciation that we used to calculate our income taxes correctly, but it's not really a cash flow. Moving on to the balance sheet, again, here's a picture of our supply chain. We've already accounted for revenues, operating expenses, and cost of goods sold as well as taxes on our income statement, and that leaves us with our capital expenditures and our working capital. And so we're going to look at the incremental capital expenditures and working capital from our particular initiative at hand. And for each period in the future projected forward in period t over time, we will calculate the capital expenditures and the net increase in working capital requirements. Combining the income statement and the balance sheet together, again, here's the income statement portion of the NOPAT plus the depreciation amortization. That's a positive cash flow, because it's income coming in. The balance sheet-- these are both stated in terms of cash outflows-- the expenditures that we're making in capital expense as well as the net increase in working capital requirements from our balance sheet. And using the reformulation of NOPAT relative to EBIT here and putting it all together, we see the formula for our free cash flows. Now, this will be calculated over several periods over time, free cash flows over several periods. And so it'll be helpful to program this formula into a spreadsheet and calculate it over the various periods we're going to consider. And we can see here again to get our NOPAT, it's our revenue minus COGS. So we have the signs over here-- minus cogs, minus operating expenses, minus depreciation amortization, then calculating our income tax based on the EBIT, subtracting that out to give us our NOPAT. Adding back in our depreciation, and then subtracting our balance sheet items, our incremental balance sheet items of capital expenditures and the working capital investments needed. The bottom line will give us our free cash flows that are again projected over time over these various periods. So these are the relevant projected cash flows pulled together into the term free cash flow. So to demonstrate this, let's go back to our example of White Nile. This is the online retailer we considered in the previous segment. They're adding a new warehouse to provide better home delivery service. And we've talked about some of the relevant cash flows-- that being obviously the new warehouse and the equipment that goes inside of it. That's the capital expenditure. We hopefully will get more sales from that better service in that region-- it's not going to be all of the revenue in our firm, but the incremental revenue that comes from the warehouse in that region. We're also going to have warehouse labor we talked about, but we may want to generalize this to be more of a warehouse operations. Labor is one part of our warehouse cost, but we may have utilities and other costs of running a warehouse. So we'll make this a more general term of warehouse operations costs. We talked about how we could lower transportation costs by having the warehouse and therefore consolidating loads into larger loads in bound. And we will also to fill the warehouse up, so we're going to have more in inventory, so it's a net increase in working capital. So to flesh out the example, I'm going to provide you some numbers here. For the warehouse, it's $5 million. The incremental revenue is $6 million. The warehouse operations, $400,000. Again, we're having lower transportation costs. We're reducing our operating expenses by $200,000 on transportation, and an incremental investment in working capital of $600,000 for the inventory. Now, there are some additional cash flows we're missing. Remember, we've tried to think about all the financial flows in our supply chain. And you may want to go back, pause for a minute, go back and think about what are the cash flows we might be missing, and then restart the video again. So hopefully, going back and looking at the cash flows in our supply chain and the incremental income statement and balance you may have come up with some additional cash flows. And here's the ones that I'm thinking are going to be relevant here. And that is, obviously we have more revenue, but with that comes cost of goods sold. We didn't account for that formally. We said we have $6 million in revenue, so we have a cost of goods sold. And we'll have to calculate that based on considering what we call gross margin. So gross margin, just to give you a quick definition, is equal to the revenue minus the CGS divided by the revenue. And that should be equal to 25%. Depreciation is another cash flow that we need to incorporate to get our taxes right. And it's a depreciation of this whole $5 million over 10 years. And we'll call it straight line depreciation, so it's the same amount each year over 10 years. We may also want to consider that with a time horizon of three years, we may have salvage value for this particular warehouse. We may not assume we're going to keep it forever. So let's assume we sell it after three years, and we're going to sell it at a book value, which would be the initial investment capital expenditure minus whatever depreciation has accumulated over those three years. And then we have income taxes to consider as well, another relevant cash flow. And we have to have some assumption about what our income tax rate is, and we'll assume it's 30%. So based on these additional relevant cash flows, see if you can think about how to calculate at least some of these items based on the numbers up here and the assumptions we've listed beside each of these, and see if you can come up with some of the numbers we'll need to incorporate in our example. OK. If you've paused the video, I hope you've had a chance to think about that. I'm going to put up the numbers here, and see if you got your calculations correct. So for our gross margin to be 25%, we have to have-- our revenue here is $6 million. And so 25% of that is $1,500,000. So revenue minus our COGS to give us 1,500,000, that means our costs of goods sold would have to be $4.5 million. For depreciation, 10 years straight line, you just take the 5 million divided by 10 to give us 500,000. That's pretty easy math, right? And finally, the salvage value is-- once we've determined our annual depreciation, our depreciation over three years is 1.5 million, so 5 million minus 1.5 million equals 3,500,000. Hopefully, you didn't spend too much time trying to calculate income tax. It's a bit tricky to do that with the numbers here. You're going to have to pull things together a bit. So I'm going to put it as to be calculated, because we're going to do that in our spreadsheet. One other assumption here is that we don't have any inflation. Remember, I mentioned that earlier in an earlier segment. In all these exercises we're not assuming there's any inflation. We'll assume that same amount continues over time. So once we put this in, we take our individual line items that we mentioned before in blue here-- here's the revenue of $6 million. And remember, there's no inflation, so it stays flat over all three years. We get our COGS in there. Notice that our transportation cost is a negative number, because it goes down. And our warehouse operations is a positive number, because that increases our operating expenses. And the net of these two together gives us an increased operating expense of $200,000. Our depreciation shows up here, and so if we take our signs over here, we can calculate this as we go. 6 million minus 4.5 million gives us a $1.5 million gross income each year. Subtracting out our operating expenses of 200,000 then gives us an EBITDA of 1.3 million. Subtracting out our $500,000 of depreciation gives us an EBIT of 800,000, and then that gives us at a 30% tax rate an income tax of 240,000. Subtracting that out, we finally get our NOPAT of $560,000. Notice I got rid of the thousands. All of the numbers here are in thousands of dollars, because it's a lot easier to see the numbers without all the zeros. So our NOPAT is our cash inflow coming in. We have to then add in the depreciation down here. So we add in the 500,000 that was the same as we had up here. So I just add it back in. Then we have our capital expenditures. And we're assuming those happen right away. So those happen in period 0, the $5 million for our new warehouse and equipment. We're also considering in period 0 that we have to invest in our inventory right away, so that's $600,000 of inventory investment, gives us a net increase in working capital investment And there's also at the end of the period-- we're assuming, again, a horizon of three years, so we're going to sell our equipment. Here's the salvage value, and it's a negative 3.5 million, because it's a negative expenditure because we're getting money back. And the same way when we sell off our warehouse-- we're going to sell off the inventory in the warehouse, and so we'll again have a negative net working capital requirement, because we'll get that working capital back at the end of the three years. So we take our NOPAT and make those adjustments, we finally get our free cash flows. And you can do the math here to see that it works out. So you can see how the incremental income statement and the adjustments from the balance sheet pulled together in the free cash flows really help us account for all of the relevant projected cash flows. And that free cash flow is really useful in making investment decisions-- a lot better than having various-- we could have tried to account for all the different line items that we listed initially as relevant cash flows, but those line items become complicated. Because first of all, we could have things that are confounded. So not in this example, but consider you had revenue growth and margin growth at the same time. It's hard to calculate both the revenue increasing as a top line number as well as the margin increasing at the same time. It's easier just to put it into the incremental income statement to confound those rather than keeping them as separate line items. It applies income tax once in an effective way. Otherwise, with line items, we would have to consider the after tax flow of every one of the line items, and it's a bit complicated there. Also, business professionals are really accustomed to reading income statements. So seeing an incremental income statement is something that they're more familiar with. And again, coming back to the beginning of our segment here, the investor value, the equity investors are really caring about the cash that we're freeing up over time. So those free cash flows are really the critical aspects that they're considering as we're making our investment decisions in our supply chain initiatives.

[lecture_09_06_working_capital_cash_flows]
So we've now covered the majority of this lesson considering the different types of cash flows we'll need to estimate when evaluating a supply chain initiative. We'll spend a little time now at the end thinking about the working capital cash flows, which tend to be the trickiest to calculate. Again, looking at our free cash flow template, we've considered the relevant cash flows that are listed here in the template. And we've considered how we can think about them as being projected over time. And we consolidate them into our free cash flows here at the bottom. Most of the items on the income statement are pretty straightforward. Revenues are accounted for as long as we've applied the cash flow principle. Again, the bottom, the capital expenditures, are most likely going to be easily estimated, because they're r investments in long-term assets that are going to be pretty easy to identify. The most challenging part is just determining what are the working capital investments in determining the free cash flows. So let's think about how is working capital considered a cash flow? The requirement is the accounts receivable plus the inventory subtracting out the accounts payable, where the suppliers give us time to pay, and then other aspects of current assets and liabilities, short-term investments, short-term debt, and cash. [lecture_09_07_key_points]
Let's take a moment to review some of the key points from this lesson. So the lesson talked a lot about cash flows, different types of cash flows, different timing of cash flows, different magnitudes of cash flows, and hopefully you're beginning to understand why it's important to be able to describe your supply chain designs in terms of cash flows. And the main reason is because the evaluation of any initiative we have in our supply chain is going to be based on the future free cash flows it creates. And this is for internal budgeting, and to get money for investments to undertake our initiatives. It's also important for communicating to our investors the value we're creating for them, so no matter which KPIs you're using to describe your supply chain performance, you should also be able to boil it down to future free cash flows. One of the difficult steps in doing this is determining which of the cash flows are relevant for our particular supply chain initiative, and we have fortunately two principles to use in guiding that. And the first is that cash must flow-- cash has to move, money has to move, in order for it to be relevant. And two, that it must be incremental regarding our project. With our initiative or without our initiative, we should be able to determine which of the incremental cash flows between those two worlds, with and without. We then will need to be able to consolidate those future cash flows that are relevant into free cash flows. And again, this is because the free cash flows are the basis for evaluation for our investors, for the investor value creation, and also business professionals are used to looking at these incremental income statements and balance sheet numbers, but also the free cash flow template we have effectively confounds or combines the various different line items in terms of magnitude and timing. So it gives us a structure to pull everything together. It's also important not to forget the balance sheet. Where really it's a lot easier to think about the income statement and the impact of our supply chain on revenues and expenses, but the balance sheet is critical because capital expenditures, while infrequent, are high magnitude, and because the working capital is almost always a part of our supply chain design, especially the inventory part, but also the payment terms of payables and receivables can be affected. And then being able to turn the net changes in that working capital requirement into cash flows is a bit tricky, but it can make or break our design initiative. And finally, this lesson is not meant to make you an expert in finance or accounting, but it enables you to start the discussions and engage with your colleagues in accounting and finance to make sure your numbers are correct. Welcome to lesson two in the second week of supply chain finance. We're going to talk about discounted cash flow analysis. [lecture_09_09_figures_of_merit_and_acceptance_criteria]
In this segment, we finally get to discuss figures of merit and acceptance criteria. And why do I say finally? Well, in considering our investment evaluation process, we've been focused entire lesson one on this first step of estimating the relevant cash flows. And that's a difficult step, but we now get to consider these figures of merit and acceptance criterion in order to make decisions about these investments. So what is a figure of merit? It is basically a single number that when you boil down the investment, in terms of its economic value, we can boil it down to one number. And then this number can be compared with the criterion that's established by the firm in order to make that decision. It's important to note here that this is established by the firm. This is something each individual firm will determine, based on its discussion with its investors. There's no global criterion that you would compare it against. In addition, there's several different options for figures of merit that we can use and then the associated criteria that go with them. So we're going to begin in this segment by considering a couple of simple back of the envelope options and then move on later in the lesson to consider some more advanced ones. In order to introduce these figures of merit, we're going to have an example here. And in this case, we've got an energy component manufacturer called Plata Solar. I don't know if you've noticed my naming conventions for companies. They're all based on river names that are not Amazon, because that one's already taken by a big company. So Plata Solar is considering new automation equipment to reduce its cost per setup in its manufacturing process by $1,000 for each setup. Plata currently makes 13 setups each year, the tax rate is 30%, and I would assume to use straight line depreciation in this example. So given what we learned in lesson one, you should be able to determine the relevant cash flows, and then we can move on and finally consider whether the investment is worthwhile or not. So why don't you maybe pause for moment, pull out your template on free cash flows, and determine the relevant cash flows for this example. And then we'll move forward. All right, so if you've taken this example and considered the relevant cash flows, you might have come up with these projections. So we have our equipment upfront. This automation equipment cost $30,000, so that shows up in our net capital expenditures. And it'll show up as an investment in this first year down here. We have 13 setups each year, each costing us $1,000, and we're saving that. We're reducing it per setup up of $1,000. So 13 times 1,000 gives us the $13,000. And it's recurring. Each year we have setups we're reducing over that three year period. There's no salvage value at the end. So we don't have any, you know, any salvage at the end of this process. We do have depreciation, though, of the equipment. And it depreciates down to 0. So it's $10,000 each year. And so that will affect our-- reduce our EBIT, therefore, reduce our taxes, which are relevant cash flow. And we get our NOPAT here at $2,100 each year. And we add back in that depreciation, because it's not a cash flow. So at the bottom line, we have return of $12,100 for each of the three years following our investment, and a quick back of the envelope assessment, at least we bring back over $36,000 on this $30,000 investment. It's at least worth considering. So let's keep these free cash flows here at the bottom and consider our first figure of merit. And that's the payback period. Payback period is fairly intuitive, and it's what it sounds like. It's the time until the cumulative cash flows pay back and are equal to our initial investment. So if we look at this example here, we can kind of guess that it's already, you know, $24,200 over the first two years, it's but yet $36,000 at the end of this year, so it's sometime between year two and three. And we're going to have to compare that with a criterion to see if the payback period is less than a certain cut-off point. So let's assume within Plata Solar that our payback cut-off is three years, and we can calculate the payback period. Since, so we get $12,100 each year, we can just divide the $30,000 investment by $12,100, and that gives us 2.48 years. Since 2.48 is less than 3, which is our criterion, we would say, this is a good investment. We can accept this investment. It would be a good investment based on the payback period. So, in considering this payback period, it's pretty straightforward to understand. It's very simple. It gives you a rough measure of the risk of the investment, because if things payback sooner, they're less risky. So if we reduce our cut-off, we'll take less riskier investments. So that could be something to consider. However, it doesn't consider the timing of the cash flows. So let's consider that we had in our firm, for the same $30,000 investment, there may have been some other options we could have done to invest in assets. And maybe they would have returned $24,200 in the first year, in this case here, and then $12,100 in the third year, or maybe that $24,200 came in the second year. Each of these would be equivalent to what we have in our automation equipment example, because we get $24,200 in the first two years and the same rate in the third year, so they will all be 2.48 years of the payback period. However, we would really prefer to get this cash up front, because we could reinvest it in other assets in our firm, or even put it in the bank and get some interest. So this is actually a preferred option among these three. In addition the payback period does not consider cash flows that happen after the payback period. So for example, if we had yet another investment of $30,000 that could have returned the $12,100 each year like ours-- our automation equipment-- but then had an additional $100,000 at the, beyond the payback period, that may be even more attractive than any of the other options presented here. And it also has the same payback period of 2.4 years, even though that investment may be much more attractive. So there are some weaknesses in the payback period, even though it's very simple to calculate. Another back of the envelope simple figure of merit is a return on assets. Return on assets is a very common ratio that's considered when doing financial analysis of a firm. And that's a simply taking the net income of a firm and dividing by its total assets. And the acceptance criterion here would be is if we should invest if the return on assets is greater than the target return. So our investors may be expecting 15% return on assets each year. So if we were then to calculate the net income, we would first take from our free cash flow template our EBIT minus the depreciation and taxes to give us the net income, which, in this case, ends up being the same as our NOPAT. So that net income, the numerator, is $2,100 each year. Our total assets will be our investment in the equipment, which is $30,000 upfront, but is depreciated over time, so as we depreciate it, those total assets go down. As a result, the return on assets the first year is 2,100 divided by 30,000 gives us 7%. But in the future years, as our assets are depreciated, our return on investments goes up. Averaging it over the three years, we get 12.8%, which, in this case, is less than 15%. So if our investors are expecting 15% return on assets, we would not invest in this equipment. So one weakness of the return on assets is that, if you remember, depreciation is not really a cash flow. So net income incorporates this, but we don't have an adjustment to take it back out, and it's not a relevant cash flow. So it's something we probably shouldn't consider in our investments. In addition, this return on assets figure of merit also suffers from the same issues with timing. And so let's give an example here. What if the timing of the cash flows changed? And this is the base example here on the right-- up top. What if we had the same magnitude in 39 setups, and the same net income as a result of $6,300 over the three years. But instead of having them even, 13 each year, we do 12, 12, and 15. So there's more setups in the third year. What happens is that means that our return on assets goes down significantly in the first two years but is very large in the third year, and, on average, is now 15%-- over 15%. So if we just adjusted the cash flows, this would actually say it's a good investment using the return on assets, even though we're actually postponing the returns to a much later time period, which, as we discussed earlier, we'd rather get cash earlier so we could reinvest it than to get it later. And, in fact, if you flip it around and put those 15 set-ups in the first year and our large cash flow initially, which is what we would in general prefer-- to have cash earlier-- our average return on assets goes down. So the return on assets also suffers from not considering the timing of cash flows. It is a positive figure of merit to consider, because it is very linked to the financial statements and the ratios that investors may be using to analyze our firm, but it doesn't consider cash flows. It's based on accounting calculations, for example, depreciation in this case. So it isn't really considering the relevant cash flows, and it doesn't consider the timing of cash flows. Notice that both of these figures of merit have the weakness of not considering the time of cash flows. So we're going to consider figures of merit going forward that do consider the timing of cash flows. And in the next segment, we'll talk about the time value of money.

[lecture_09_10_time_value_of_money_and_discount_rate]
In the last segment, we considered how some of the figures of merit that we might use do not consider the timing of cash flows very well. So in this segment, we'll talk a little bit more about the time value of money and why that's important and talk about a process called discounting and the discount rate to deal with that. So if we look at what is the time value of money, we have to start with a question saying, why is today's cash more valuable than tomorrow's cash. What is the value of money today versus tomorrow? And as we discussed in the last segment, one reason is that if we have cash today, we can invest it and get further returns. So there's an opportunity cost of waiting until tomorrow because we forgo those investment options. In addition, there is the impact of inflation that over time as our purchasing power goes down because of inflation, we would rather have money today and buy things today than wait until tomorrow when the prices go up. Finally, there's a risk component that receiving cash today is better because things may happen between now and tomorrow and the receipt of cash is not guaranteed. So we don't really formally consider inflation, as we mentioned, in the first lesson in these analyses. And we don't get as far as it really addressing risk formally in this week, but there are other important factors, although we will only consider the opportunity cost in determining the time value of money. So how do we consider this in our analysis? We're going to calculate what we call the present value of our future cash. So we're going to take tomorrow's cash and turn it into a present value. And therefore, cash flows that happen at different times in the future can be compared on the same basis of the present value. And we'll do that using a discount rate. And we'll introduce what that is. So, the present value. How do we think about the present value? Well, let's start by thinking about something maybe we may be more familiar with, and that is the future value. So FV stands for future value. And PV is present value. So if we have a present value of money and we invest it at a growth rate of r, then we take that present value times 1 plus r, and then we raise that to the exponent n, to determine the future value. This is a concept called compounding. So you may be familiar with this. For example, if you put money into a certificate of deposit at a bank, and they say they guarantee to pay you 5% return, then you would put 0.05 in for r, and you'd multiply your present value, the money you invest now, times 1.05 raised to the number of periods-- years in this case, if they're doing 5% annually-- the number of years in the future, and determine what you will get back on that certificate of deposit, depending on the duration of that certificate. So that's compounding. If we simply divide both sides by 1 plus r to the n power, we will-- it's simple algebra to come up with a different equation that helps us calculate our present value. That's taking our future values and dividing that by that 1 plus r to the n. This is a process called discounting. We're discounting the future cash flows into present values. And so the r now is, instead of a growth rate, it's considered a discount rate. Now it's important that you make sure that the time period you're using for the number of periods, as well as for the growth rate or the discount rate in the period, are using the same time unit. So typically, in all the analyses we'll do in this lesson we'll focus on annual periods and annual rates. So we see that with the present value formula, we now incorporate the magnitude of the future of the future cash flows as well as the timing of the future cash flows, the number of periods in the future. There's one other variable that really will affect the calculation of our present values, and that's this r, the discount rate. This will be an important assumption in calculating the present values to compare different options. And this rate is determined based on considering this fundamental investment process that we described earlier. So remember our debt investors and our equity investors give the firm money. We invest it and get future returns and then return it to them. So the debt investors have a certain rate that they have agreed upon with the firm, and it's a formal contract put in place that we will pay them back. For example, a loan at a certain rate over a period of time. The rate that the stockholders, or the equity investors, expect depends on what options they have in terms of investing in other financial assets. So they will consider the risk of investing in our firm versus other risks they have in their financial portfolio and then determine an appropriate rate. Combining these rates together helps us determine what is the appropriate discount rate for our firm because the discount rate is based on our investors' expected rate of return when they give us their money. An example of how to do this is something called the weighted average cost of capital, or WACC. And I'm not going to describe this in detail. But it basically balances this equity rate, which has a higher risk because they get paid after the debt holders, and the debt rate, which is going to be different and a little bit lower because it has lower risk for the debt holders because they're paid before the equity investors if there's any liquidation. So combining those together will give us a weighted average cost of capital. Now, one important note here. We mentioned before in our net operating profit after taxes, or NOPAT, that we didn't incorporate the interest expense when calculating our taxes. There is a benefit of paying interest because it reduces our tax burden and taxes are a relevant cash flow. But the way that it's suggested is by adjusting the debt rate by that tax amount. And so it separates the cash flows for financing from the cash flows of operations, which is what we're really focusing on when we're considering that investment in operational assets. So the WACC is one example, there are other ones. For example, a capital asset pricing model, CAPM. There's different approaches for determining the discount rate, and this will be set by finance. However, our role in supply chain is to, once the discount rate is given, make sure that we're giving a good return on the discount rate on the operational assets, that we're providing those future cash flows to give a proper rate of return. And if we do that, if we at least meet the discount rate of return, the expectation from the expected rate of return for our investors, then our stock price should remain constant. We're meeting their expectations. If instead, we exceed the expectations, our returns are higher than that rate, then more investors will want to invest in our company and the stock price will go up. Consequently, if we fall short and we don't provide that proper rate of return, then our investors might sell, our equity investors might sell their stock, and our stock price would go down. So our role in supply chain and providing an appropriate return on the operational assets really has a strong impact on the overall performance of the firm. And in some ways, this is why this is also called the hurdle rate. This hurdle rate of the discount rate sets the hurdle by which we have to run our operations to exceed the expectations of our investors. So we now have all the components that are in place in order to conduct our discounted cash flow analysis, which is really what we're going to focus on in this lesson. And we'll talk about that in the next segment.

And in this case, it's an infinite series of a cash flow c that keeps happening in period one, two, all the way out to infinity. We can't even write this out because we can't write an infinite number of terms but it's a mathematical series that we can calculate easily the value and that is because it converges to the value of c over r. So the cash flow divided by the discount rate gives us the value of a series of cash flows that would happen forever into the future. So it's actually a lot simpler than you might think. An annuity, it's the same kind of thing, you're having a fixed sum c that comes each year but it's for a specified number of years. So in this case again you get this value c all the way through period t but then it stops, it doesn't go to infinity. To calculate that it's a little bit trickier but actually we can use the perpetuity to help do this, so if we assume that we have a perpetuity beginning in period one so basically if we say in the present value that in period one it begins and we have another one in period two the way out to infinity, that value is c over r. So that's starting at time zero, the present. If we had a perpetuity beginning starting at period t, so all the cash flows beyond t so we have t plus 1 and t plus 2, and so forth on to infinity, the value of that perpetuity at time t would also be c over r but the value in today's terms at time zero, would be c over are discounted by that period t to be the time zero. Now using those two assumptions we can calculate the value of annuity because an annuity is exactly all those cash flows that happen between now and t, so if we subtract this minus the discounted value here, we get the exact value of this annuity in between. We can also use a spreadsheet function The spreadsheet function present value pv you have to input the rate r, the number of periods, t, and the payment, c. And that will calculate the value of an annuity. It actually though calculates the value you would pay to purchase the annuity, so the present value of an annuity is actually a negative of the present value function. It's a bit of a quirky thing with a spreadsheet that it's used for a lot of calculations for retirement and so forth, where they're saying how much would you pay to buy an annuity? In this case we're looking to estimate the present value of the annuity so we have to do a negative value of that. So let's do a simple example. Let's assume c equals 10. The value of a perpetuity and let's assume we have a discount rate of 10%, actually let's say c equals 100 and we have a 10% discount rate. Our perpetuity value would be equal to 100 divided by 0.10 equals 1,000. Simple calculation. For an annuity, considering algebraically we could type it into Excel. And again we have our rate of 0.10, we have 10 periods in the future let's assume it's still a cash flow of 100. You'll see that that present value is a lot lower than it is for perpetuity because in paying out the annuity, you also utilize the capital and it goes down to 0. So you don't need as much capital up front to keep the payment going forever. So it's a lower present value than the value of a perpetuity. So let's apply this to White Nile. Remember we had our warehouse that was going to be sold after three years at book value and we had a bit of a marginal investment. It's something worth pursuing but it's not a very strong proposition. Let's change now to assume that we're going to have the value continue in perpetuity. And let's assume also that when we estimated our warehouse operations before, we also included maintenance and so it's counting all of the cash flows that would be continuing to add value for this warehouse in perpetuity. So here's what the projections look like. Now I've had to do something, I've had to extend this out to period t. Why is that? Well because we have our warehouse coming up front and we have the depreciation that goes for 10 years and after 10 years we have a change in our cash flows so they remain stable through period 10 but it isn't stable into the period 11 because that depreciation goes away. You see? It's gone here. All the other cash flows we're assuming, continue on but that one changes. And from that point forward, from period 11 on, so after a period 10, everything is stable and we're assuming it's not going to grow. And so we can use this to calculate our perpetuity. The difference ends up being, if you notice here, our cash flows up through period 10 is a 1.60 million and 910,000 afterwards and the difference is because without the depreciation, our taxes go up because we no longer can discount or deduct the depreciation and reduce our tax burden so our taxes go up and therefore our cash flows go down in perpetuity. Now if you see here, we have our discount rate of 10%, take that 910 divided by the discount rate of r, 10%, and that gives us a perpetuity value, a terminal value, of $9.1 million. Now we add this at the end of period 10 at the end of t, so we add the free cash flow in period 10 with the terminal value to get what I'm calling our evaluation cash flows at the bottom here. So this is now the series of cash flows that we're going to use to evaluate this initiative. So you can might want to stop again and use that bottom line evaluation cash flows and calculate the net present value in IRR and see what you get. OK hopefully with the perpetuity, you're able to come up with a net present value of $4,422,000. So it is definitely higher, a lot higher, than the situation with the salvage value. And the IRR are is also much higher as well, this is a much more robust investment when we consider the perpetuity value that's created by setting up a warehouse. But notice it's not as much as the 9.1 million here because we are discounting this to present value, so you see that that's far in the future. So discounting to today's value, it doesn't add $9 million, it adds around $4 million but it's still a significant amount to make this initiative worthwhile. You may not be comfortable saying a warehouse would last in perpetuity but we can use an annuity to maybe make an assumption that's more reasonable and again assuming our maintenance is included here, let's assume for 20 years the warehouse is in place for 20 years and calculate the value using an annuity. So again, we've got our salvage value, our perpetuity value and we have our annuity now and we're assuming there's 20 periods total so it's 10 periods beyond period t. So an annuity for 10 periods beyond period t of 910 so it's 10 more periods at 10% is a little over $5 million. Again we take that terminal value, add it to their free cash flows in the last period t to get our evaluation cash flows. And again we apply the net present value and IRR to that series of cash flows. Pause, see what you come up with. You should get a value for the annuity of a little over $3 million. So you can see that by assuming it doesn't go to forever, it is less than the perpetuity, which makes sense but it's not a lot less. Even though this value went from $9 million down to $5 million it happens far in the future so the discounting makes it so that there's not as big of a difference and it's still a very robust investment to consider for our investors. So we've now showed so that there can be long term value to your supply chain initiatives and we've come up with a way to calculate that long term value and to pull it into our analysis by using the terminal value.

[lecture_09_14_inventory_holding_cost]
We close lesson 1 on supply chain cash flows by considering specifically the working capital cash flows in the supply chain. We'll close this lesson by also looking at working capital, and specifically inventory, and considering the concept of inventory holding cost, which is used extensively in designing the physical flows in our supply chain. We'll see how that maps to the financial flows of our discounted cash flow analysis. To begin, what are the potential costs of holding inventory? Well, first of all, there's the capital that's tied up in the inventory, and that's the opportunity cost of capital. Rather than buying inventory, we could utilize that capital to generate returns in other ways in our firm. There's also operating costs. When we have inventory, there's going to be inherently warehousing costs, special equipment that's needed, obviously labor to handle it and keep track of it, and perhaps even some direct costs if we have to dispose of things at the end or hire somebody to do that on our behalf to scrap the inventory. Finally, there's another category of relevant cash flows or costs that are related to holding inventory that deal with lost revenue. So inventory over time can become obsolete or it can depreciate in real market value-- not as a depreciation of a fixed asset, because this is not a fixed asset, but it can actually lose market value.
