And let's just see how it plays out with our example from before at the beginning of the segment. Remember we had company D had a very big disparity in return on equity and assets, whereas the equity-only firm had an alignment there. If we did our ROIC calculation on this, we would get-- and this is again, NOPAT over invested capital equals, our NOPAT here is going to be our EBIT for company D, it's going to be EBIT 280 times 1 minus the tax rate, which is 0.4, which will end up being 168 in our numerator. And our invested capital is simply going to be, it's easy to calculate here, debt plus equity is 2,000. 2,000. So that's going to give us a number of 8.4%. And if we do this on the other side, of course. The NOPAT, same EBIT, same tax rate. And we still have the same total liabilities and equity. So we'll get the same numbers on this side. But interestingly, the ROIC would again be in exactly the same, 8.4%. So it's giving you the return as if you were completely fully equity financed, if you are an equity finance only firm. But for a firm with a lot of debt and financial leverage, ROIC gives a truer idea of the performance of the returns on the capital that's being invested, whereas return on assets and return on equity can distort the picture. That's why I think ROIC is a better overall measure of return on the capital that's being entrusted into our firm to create returns. So, sorry for the graphics behind you. But you can see ROIC is a good measure for supply chain performance because it is not confounded by the financing strategies that we don't have control over in supply chain. Financing is going to be determined by the finance department. So it'll show basically the fundamental earning power of the firm, which is really what comes from the operations. And so that's what we would like to focus on in looking at the performance of the firm financially related to our operations in our supply chain management.

[lecture_10_08_key_points]
Let's wrap up this brief lesson with some key points about analyzing financial performance. Let's go back to the questions posed at the beginning. First of all, how do you analyze financial performance? And we've seen that through a broad set of ratios and ratio analysis is often how it's done. These ratios are based on data from financial statements, primarily income statement and balance sheet. So the math isn't challenging, but the difficulty is defining what's going to be in the numerator and what's in the denominator. And what are you trying to accomplish by looking at these different ratios? And in fact, you should look at several ratios in a context of a framework that allows you to see performance from different perspectives. These different ratios give us clues as to what's going on with financial performance. But you need several, maybe you could think about them as sensors, to see how well your overall firm is moving forward. And we want to use ratios that are dimensionless. And most of the ones in here, margin, turnover, are dimensionless because we can compare with competition. That may be companies of very different size, but we can look at the margins because they eliminate the size of the firm in terms of profitability and look at the pure ability to create profit based on sales or turnover, how much sales can be driven by the assets of the firm. It doesn't matter how big the company is. We can compare. We can also observe trends over time, both in competition and in our own firm. As our firm grows, we can say, are we maintaining the ability to capture good margin on sales? Are we maintaining the ability to get a good turnover of sales depending on the assets we're deploying, even as we get bigger over time? So can look at our firm's performance and see how we're doing on these dimensionless measures over time. And we'll have some homework assignments where you can try to practice some of that type of analysis. We didn't have time to get into that kind of competitive analysis and trend analysis in the lesson itself. How do you measure performance then, is very linked to how you analyze it. The financial metrics are often based on the ratios used in financial analysis. You'll often find things like return on assets, return on equity, hopefully return on invested capital. But there are often metrics in a firm that are based on the things that an analyst, a financial analyst, will use to analyze the firm. However, inside the firm you have different information than what's available in the financial accounting books, and managerial decisions may require that different information. So financial metrics based on income statement and balance sheet won't be sufficient to make the decisions. You may need managerial accounting or specific types of accounting like activity-based costing to give you the information you need in order to make decisions. And in fact, these financial metrics are only part of a bigger picture of information you're going to need to monitor the performance of your supply chain. But the financial metrics are important. And we should make sure that we're thinking about the analysis that aligns with the different drivers of shareholder value. You should keep financial metrics on your radar screen that are aligned with revenue growth, operating margin, and asset productivity. Those are the key things that the supply chain will do to create value for shareholders, who won't have as much influence on the financial leverage. We will have some ability to assess the risk of some of our strategies, but we're really at the core looking at this triangle up here: revenue growth, operating margin, and asset productivity. But we want to make sure we incorporate financial metrics in thinking about our performance and also guiding our strategy. This triangle can be helpful in saying, should we move more of our focus to revenue growth, or should we move to more margin, or should we stay more into asset productivity? You can position yourself and your goals relative to these financial measures to help set your supply chain strategy. Some metrics are better than others. Don't take every metric that's out there as one that you should incorporate. And for example, I think ROIC is a much better metric than return on assets and return on equity. That return on invested capital gives you a clearer perspective on how your firm is generating returns on the capital that's being deployed where the returns are expected. And the bottom line is, supply chain executives should definitely monitor a balanced set of financial metrics that align with the investors' interests but also employee bonuses. So in a balanced scorecard kind of a concept, financial metrics are definitely an important part of it. We shouldn't limit ourselves to only the metrics that look at supply chain performance and the processes that we're monitoring more closely, although those are very important to have on your balanced scorecard. But always include some financial metrics that think about the investors. And employee bonuses a lot of times are linked to financial performance, so make sure that you're incorporating the employee bonus, the financial metrics that will drive employee bonuses and drive the incentives such that they will try to create value in the best way through supply chain management by looking at revenue growth, operating margin, and asset productivity. As always, use the practice problems to learn more. Again, we'll give you some opportunities to do some more of this kind of analysis and even compare firms. Use the discussion for questions, comments, and suggestions. But hopefully this very brief lesson has given you a bit of a picture of how to analyze the performance of your supply chain, and you'll use this information going forward to design better supply chains and manage them for success into the future. Good luck in creating your piles of cash inside your firm.

[lecture_10_09_introduction]
OK. So this is the last lesson of the last week. So before we dive into it, let's look backwards. What have we done so far in this course? Well, everything was about what the supply chain is, and what's focus of the flow of information, the physical goods, and the financial. It's all about how to handle these flows, and so that's why we focused on them collectively and then each of them individually. So we start with the physical flows, move to information, design, and what we'll do in this lesson is talk about design of the organization itself. So for the physical flows, what we did is we modeled the problem. Hopefully, you're very comfortable with these problems, and you see that they build off each other, so that transportation and transhipment problems are really all about flow, while the location problems are all about nodes. So arcs and nodes, and they come together when we do a network design problem. And there's all sorts of different flavors of these problems, but we really focus on the standard form that allows us to use Mixed-Integer Linear Programs or MILPs. And hopefully you're very comfortable with MILPs at this point because we've used them throughout this entire course. But hopefully, what you've taken away or you will take away is the whole idea here that the interpretation and the implementation is more important than the actual math and that you understand what it is you're doing and understand the limitations of these types of solutions. Because they're not perfect, but they give you some good support to help you make a decision. And again, to drive this a little further, remember, these are decision support tools and the accent is on support. They're not making the decision. Then we moved to information flow. And we've covered sourcing with some strategies and methods, and we spent time using Mixed-Integer Linear Programs showing you how they're used for optimization-based procurement, which is widely, widely adopted. We covered make, and this is where you coordinate between manufacturing and sourcing and the demand in production planning. And, again, we use the same optimization tools to show how you make those decisions. We talked about bills and material, fixed horizon planning, where you have varying demand, but it's deterministic and it's widely used in production scheduling. And then we finished up there with the delivery, collaborating with customers, again, using the optimization for aggregate planning and distribution channel design introducing different ways products can flow and introducing different channels both closed loop supply chains as well as e-commerce. And they all have a role. And what's really interesting, this is very much evolving. So what's being done today, this year, might be very different a year from now, so there's a lot of flux here. And then we finished up with Sales and Operations Planning, S&OP, which is something that every company does. We went into financial flow. And remember, all we're doing is translating so you can speak the language of the CFO. And so we introduced you to income sheets, balance sheets, or income statements rather, balance sheets, cash flows. I know it's a lot here, but you need to have a working knowledge of some of this. It's probably worth, if you really want to get involved and move up in your company, to get more knowledge, a little more into finance and accounting. We tried to give you enough so you can understand and do some basic translation. And we focused on these five areas, and hopefully, you're fairly comfortable with them because activity-based costing is widely used in every company. Working capital is something you really need to understand because that cash-to-cash cycle time is something that every company is looking to shrink. Discounted cash flow analysis is going to help you make decisions for investments. Supply chain finance is an up-and-coming technique that a lot of large companies are using to work with their different suppliers. And then the financial analysis, the DuPont model, that's another way of helping you make investment decisions. So what we're going to do in this lesson, well for the remainder of this lesson, we're going to answer this one question. How should a supply chain be organized? And we're going to focus in on what's the structure look like, how has it changed over the last couple of years. And then the big question that every company asks, what and if, do I centralize or decentralize? What are the functions, the tasks, the processes that I keep in the headquarters? And what do I keep at low to the field? And there's a lot of debate, in fact, a lot of companies move back and forth, back and forth. Centralized this year, then two years later, they decentralize and go back and forth. But we want to talk and give you a framework to make that decision. Then tied to that is how do you measure the performance of these systems and these processes. So we'll talk about the basic primary processes and supply chains and then how to measure them. We'll talk a little theoretically about this, how do you measure performance of any process and then build on that, how to build metrics and metric systems. So that's what we're going to do for the rest of this lesson. I hope you've enjoyed it so far, but let's get started.

[lecture_10_10_supply_chain_organization_design]
So let's talk about the supply chain organization. Where does it fit into the larger corporate structure? Well, we know what falls into the function-- all these different activities, from outbound transportation down to order processing and everything in between. And we've talked about these individually. We've talked about purchasing. We've talked about transportation planning, MRP systems, sales, new product launch. All these we've talked about mainly individually. But the question is, who controls them, and how do they fit into that larger corporate function? Well, traditionally, it used to look something like this, where you'd have the President or the CEO or someone, and you'd have a level of people reporting to him or her, and they would have major functions. There was a sales and marketing, manufacturing, and maybe operations, finance. There might be some other ones I didn't list, like human resources and other things. But these are the three main that contained pieces of supply chain management activities. You see for finance, they had a lot of the things dealing with inventory control, which is all about the money, and managing the information systems. Manufacturing had all the things in supply chain management that dealt with getting product into production, including MRPs, inbound transportation, scheduling and production. And then marketing usually held all of the supply chain logistics-- getting the finished goods to the customer. This includes demand forecasting and planning, customer sales, finished goods, warehousing, new product launch, outbound transportation, all of those things. Now, about 20 years ago, things started blending a little bit. And what happened is there was a little merging of activities on the inbound side under materials management all about the logistics flow-- into the plant-- and the physical distribution-- all of the flow out from the plant to the customer. So we started seeing consolidation of different activities so you could do tradeoffs internal, and you didn't have to go between the higher level function. So it was still siloed, but there was a lot more tradeoffs being done within the inbound and the outbound. This evolved even more to where the logistics-- or this could also be supply chain, depending on the company-- it all got grouped into its own organization. And a lot of times, you see it like this, where it's a peer function along with the sales and marketing, manufacturing, and production, as well as finance. And you see all these activities fall within. Now, sometimes, you might even see that this product-- this gets thrown in as well, but it depends on the company. There's a lot of flexibility now. There isn't one organizational structure that all companies use. It really depends on a lot of different things. So what we've seen now is there's different hybrids of organizations for supply chains. Sometimes, you see it as we just showed where you have a separate function-- so logistics is a pure function along with finance, human resources, manufacturing, sales, and marketing. Or you could have it as a program where it's a separate entity, and it's something that reports into the CEO. Or what's more common-- you see it in what's known as a matrix organization. Now, why is it called a matrix organization? Well, what I have here is that the CEO has its major function-- sales and marketing, manufacturing, finance. But also reporting to them are logistics and human resources. However, there's a little bit of a difference here. Finance owns everything dealing with the finance of the firm, manufacturing. The head of manufacturing-- whether it's a VP or the Chief Manufacturing Officer, Chief Operations Officer, whatever-- owns all of the activities that happen within that function. Same thing with sales and marketing. It's called a matrix, though, because logistics now will have authority on what's known as project authority. And so for example, if they're working in the Finance department over here, they might be involved in some inventory control. So you'd have a logistics personnel within the finance company. So what does this mean for the individual? They've got two bosses. One is a dotted line this way. And different companies arrange how this is set up a little bit differently. It's known as a shared service, where logistics is important to every one of the functions, so it's shared across those functions. For here in manufacturing, you might have someone running the MRP, might be running inbound transportation. And so what we're finding is that you have teams of people acting where they have two bosses. One is the main function, and then also that shared service, that logistics. So when you come up and design something like this, there's different tradeoffs that you're looking at. One is strategic versus operational. Does the logistics and supply chain belong at the higher level? Does it sit at that same peer level as manufacturing and finance and all the other major functions? The second tradeoff you want to determine is where do the activities occur. Do they happen within its own function, logistics, or do a lot of activies occur within those individual functions? And so you want to understand where the actual work occurs. And then finally, the biggest one is whether you want to make the decision making process centralised or decentralised. And essentially, there's three questions here-- which activities in supply chain are best administered and controlled from a central headquarters location, from the chief, or which activities are best administered and controlled from more regional or decentralized locations? What's best to do centrally for everything within the company? What's best to do at all the different regions that might be slightly different from each other? And then how is the coordination made between regions, countries, business units, channels, divisions, et cetera? Because centralized, decentralized, it's not just geography. You have different business units. You have different products. So it could be that two facilities are right next to each other, but they're managed very differently-- because, for example, they're making totally different products, serving totally different markets. So why would I have the same supply chain serving both of them? So these are the types of tradeoffs and decisions you make when you're trying to design your supply chain organization. And what we'll do next is focusing on this major decision of what do I centralize and what do I decentralize.

[lecture_10_11_centralized_versus_decentralized]
So let's dive just a little deeper into the centralized versus decentralized decision-making process. So there are pros and cons. And these are really two extreme solutions. For the centralized option, that's where-- here's my corporate headquarters, and let's say I have these different business units, or different divisions in different regions across the world. And the idea is that I will make all the decisions at that central location. And these could be deciding how much inventory to stock, what to procure, who to buy from for transportation services, all the different rules and policies. So the benefit of doing it up here at this central location is that we leverage economies of scale. I might be able-- for procurement, for example, be able to buy, get a high enough volume where I get a larger discount since I'm buying for one, two, three, four different regions in this case. It allows me to harmonize policies and practices so I can establish best practices, things that make sense for my industry, for my company and strategy. And I can roll those out and make sure that everyone is using, say, the same forecasting technique and being in parallel process for this. So everything is harmonized and standardized along the best practices as I deem fit for my entire company, and it also allows for global or optimal solutions. So I might be able to find a solution that is best for the entire company, not just for one or two of the individual divisions because I'm making the trade-off decisions centrally. Now, the other option here is just the opposite, where I don't make any decisions centrally. I make them all locally. This is decentralized. So the decision of who to procure from, how much inventory to stock, how to run my operations, demand planning, all of those things are done locally for my individual business unit or division. What are the benefits here? Well, the decision-making is closer to the customer because chances are I'm facing a different customer here versus, say, over here. It might be that this division is selling to large retailers. This is selling to manufacturers or to other companies in my own industry. So they might be facing different customers. So by allowing me to make decisions individually I can be closer to my customer and make decisions that are more appropriate for them. It also allows me to reflect local cultures and customs because these could be across the world in totally different geographies. It allows for optimal solutions within that region. If I come up with a centralized one, yes, it's perfect-- or it's optimal, rather, across the company. But as we've seen in a bunch of different cases, that might mean that this division pays the price so that this division has better service or lower costs. Overall it might be better, but the benefits are not always shared evenly across all the divisions. By doing it decentralized I'm trying to optimize for each region by itself, and it allows the business units to act autonomously. Sometimes it's very hard to run things always centralized. You tend to be further away from the action, a little further removed from the decision-making process. So you've got these two extremes, and no one does dramatically one or the other. It's usually a hybrid between the two. But let's talk a little more about it. So here we've got global operations because most companies have operations at least in two countries, if not two continents. And so you have a supply chain that looks something like this. So you've got to ask yourself, if I have a bunch of things happening in Southeast Asia, that's probably going to be very different from my operations in Latin America. I might want to be managing them very differently. The currencies change differently, the standards, the cultures are all very different. Same as if I'm in North America versus Africa. So the geographies is a natural way to decentralize some decision-making. Maybe there are certain things I want to do on the ground in each of the regions and maybe centralize some other decisions. Well, it's not just geography, though, because we can look at some of these other large companies. And here's just a sampling. And so you can't imagine that all of the decisions are being made centrally. You look at a company like Tata, which operates in over a hundred countries, has dozens of companies, and they make things from cars to tea to perfume to bikes to watches. So chances are the individual business units, the strategic business units, are going to be a little closer to their different individual customers and be able to make some decentralized decisions that might be better than one centralized authority. Same thing with General Electric. They make things ranging from aircraft engines to railroad engines, household appliances, consumer financing. They're all over the place. Same thing if you look at a company like BASF. They have over 70 different strategic business units across the world, and they produce things that sell to construction industries, to agriculture, to electronics, paints, papers, plastics, and they sell to almost every company you can imagine. Even a company that tends to be in the same space, consumer packaged goods, like Unilever and P&G, they have very different products that they're selling, and they sell in different channels, different distributors, and they have very different characteristics. A company like Starbucks, which you would think, well, just selling coffee, they're actually very different in that they have retail operations. They sell into grocery stores. They also have manufacturing operations. And they're also moving further upstream in their supply chain, so different parts of the company will probably operate a little differently. Not all decisions are made centrally. They might be made decentrally depending on the geography, the type of customers you're selling to, the business units and very many other factors. So we had a thesis a couple years back here at MIT where some students looked at this and they wanted to understand, OK, we're not going to have everything centralized or everything decentralized. The question is, which decisions-- which functions do you tend to centralize and which do you tend to decentralize? What makes sense? And so what they did is they looked at all the different types of decisions you have to make and they broke them into three classic groupings- strategic, tactical, and operational. And if you look at the strategic decisions being made, these are the things that take about a year to implement, very high impact, high risk. If you think of network design, those kind of things. So they bucketed two main examples here, long-term capacity planning and sourcing decisions, because those have long-term effects. In the tactical bucket, these are things that get affected monthly or quarterly, have a kind of a medium risk factor. And you put in transportation planning, inventory planning, short-term demand, manufacturing planning, and internal improvements. And in the operational, these are the daily, weekly kind of activities. It's order fulfillment, order management, shipment handling. So if you look at these-- and you can see the impact goes from high to low, and the number of decisions go just the opposite. These decisions are made every day. So there's a ton of those operational ones, very few of the strategic ones. But what they're trying to figure out is through a survey and discussions and interviews with executives where do you make these decisions? And so what they came up with is a decision-making-- they kind of mapped the process. And, again, they have these three buckets- strategic, tactical, and operational. And they grouped the companies that they interviewed and surveyed into four categories- consumer packaged manufacturers; they're in blue, industrial products manufacturing; they're in that dark green; light green is logistics service providers; and then retailers are this kind of salmon color. And so what we have here is there are different questions we're asked, and as the number gets higher for each of those segments that means more centralization. And as a number gets lower it tends to be a decentralized. And so you can see what the big takeaway is, more strategic kind of decisions tend to be centralized. You centralize your sourcing. Economies of scale fits in there a lot, long-term capacity planning because you're balancing across all the different regions. At the other extreme, the operational decisions, or fulfillment, or management, shipment handling, mainly the outbound shipment handling, these are things closer to your individual customers, so it makes sense to be much more decentralized, closer to your customers, to make those decisions. Now, there was also a survey that was done about a couple years later by PricewaterhouseCoopers. What I like about it, it took these different activities and surveyed a bunch of companies and asked what percentage-- or whether you manage this globally, centrally, or regionally, decentrally. And you can see the different tasks, and they're sequenced-- or they're sorted by going from high to low where new product development is something that tends to be managed globally because it has really high impact. And as you go down, you see that it's a decreasing amount that is managed globally, strategic procurement, supply chain centers of excellence, those are all over 60% of the company say this is what we do centrally, we manage those globally. Now, the center of excellence, this is a typical organization within a company that establishes best practices, standard processes, best ways of doing something for that company. It's a way to ensure a standard level of quality and performance across a very wide ranging organization. The other interesting one right after that is S&OP. This makes sense, right, because this is something that helps me balance all my supply and my demand across my whole company. So we're seeing more and more companies move S&OP into something that is run globally because what it's doing, it's not just balancing between my production and my sales team, it's the entire companies because there's oftentimes a lot of shared resources. And, for example, two organizations in different business units might use the same common transportation. So therefore, the strategy that one entity takes might impact what's available for the second. So the S&OP process is a way for a company to balance those decisions and make those trade-offs across the different units. Now, as you see this number get smaller and smaller, things get closer and closer to operational type tasks, inbound and outbound logistics, customer service, warehousing, things like that. So the big takeaway here is that you're focused on more strategic things tend to be centralized. Things that are more operational and focused to the customer can be more decentralized and done individually in individual regions and business units. Now, what we're seeing more and more is across these large companies with multiple business units or strategic business units, like I said, some companies might have 50 or more of these. And so every strategic business unit is like its own company. There's usually a president, and they have P&L responsibility, profit and loss. And so they very much have that authority within their business unit. However, they all have shared services in terms of logistics. And so what we set up here is similar to what you do in a company in between, say, finance and marketing and manufacturing, but here it's much more formalized. So you might have someone who reports directly in to the business unit president but has this dotted line to the head of logistics. So someone who's serving two bosses-- and it's a challenging position, but it makes sense because they have to make sure that what they do helps the business unit but also has to adhere to the standards that the logistics function needs to adhere to, needs to enforce. So usually what happens here is you create the service agreements or some kind of rules of the road between the head of this logistics and each of the business units because the logistics services they provide to, say, Strategic Business Unit One, might be entirely different from what they provide here for Business Unit Two. Let's say this Business One might be selling to industrial customers, to other manufacturers, so that means big-batch deliveries on a schedule, can't shut a production line down but then might have other product's going to a retailer and that's much more on a fixed schedule, much more seasonality due to sales. And so they might have very different structures set up, different ways of forecasting, managing inventory, managing transportation, and everything in between. So what these service agreements usually need to dictate are what decisions are made locally within the business unit and what decisions get to be made centrally, in this case by the logistics organization. Who do the teams report to? How does that reporting structure work? How do we balance trade-offs between the strategic business units? These guys might use, for example, common transportation or production facilities. It makes sense for them to share facilities if it leads to better operational efficiencies. So someone has to balance the use of those and that's what the shared service is there for. How do you run the sales and operations planning process across these business units? And how do you charge for a fund to shared service models? A lot of times what happens is the logistics company will charge an internal cost to the business unit so they can make a decision whether to use the internal logistics or go outside. So this structure, this hybrid structure, is very, very common in large organizations. And it is something that works very well. But what it requires is that there has to be an arrangement or an agreement, a service-level agreement between the logistics function, the supply chain function, and each of the different business units.

[lecture_10_12_supply_chain_processes]
So now, let's dive a little deeper into supply chain processes. We're not going to go too deep here. We're going cover this in much more detail in SC3X. But let me just give you an overview. So everything in the supply chain can be viewed as a process with inputs that get transformed through some process into some type of outputs. And so this is a classic way of viewing supply chains. Every process within a supply chain fits this, as well. So for order fulfillment, you think of the flow unit-- the thing that's moving and being processed-- are orders. You take as inputs the receipt of an order. And the output is a delivery. For production, you take receipt of the raw materials or components. You turn those into a completed product. For outbound logistics, you take as an input the finished goods at the additive manufacturing, and you turn those into a delivery to a customer. Procurement is everything from a purchase order converted into receipt of materials. And customer service, arrival of a customer to its departure. That's the life cycle of the process of that customer service. New product development, from when you start until you launch the product. And then, you can think of the cash-to-cash cycle time, which is really the expenditure of funds all the way to collection of revenue. So you look at that whole cash-to-cash cycle time can be viewed as a process metric for looking at the transformation of inputs into outputs. In fact, one of the first models we looked at in one of the first lectures of this whole course was looking at the SCOR model-- the Supply Chain Operations Reference model-- by the Supply Chain Council. And this encapsulates everything within this supply chain and breaks it down into a handful of different tasks-- sourcing, making, delivering. These are all the execution. There's a planning phase that goes above that. How do I plan for each of those? There's return for any return activities. And then, all the other activities that enable these things. But what this shows is that I'm converting things and I'm processing through. And then, I have the return cycle, as well. So it's also a process view. We also saw a process view of supply chains as it goes from the customer all the way back to the initial supplier. We showed this view as well. And we dived just a little deeper into it to give you some flavor. So each one of these cycles, how the customer orders from the retailer, that's really a consumer. How the retailer orders from its distributor. How the distributor orders from the manufacturer. The manufacturer orders from its supplier. And this cycle can go on and on and on. Now, what happens in each one of these cycles is up here, let's say, the supplier in that cycle-- in this case, it's the retailer-- has to market the product it has available. If this is a coffee shop, it has the coffee urns out there. If it's a bookstore, it has the books out there. If it's an online, an e-commerce, retailer, then it has things out on its website. So somehow, you're marketing your product. Same thing down here for your supplier. You're marketing your product for the manufacturer, whether it's in a catalog, website, however. Then, in the process, the buyer will place an order. Again, if it's a retailer on an e-commerce site, then I go and I click on the web site. I might go and place it actually physically if I'm at a physical bookstore. For down here, I might trigger an order through my production planning. So I place an order somehow. The supplier receives that order. It might be immediate. It might be longer-term. They fill the order somehow. Sometimes, if it's a bookstore, a physical book store, I'll give you that book right away. If it's online, then I set that up to be fulfilled and sent to you later. The buyer eventually receives that order. And then, we hope they pay for it. So each of these cycles consists of separate little processes. And they all have the same format, but they differ a little bit in the details. And they differ not just as I go in the different order cycles with customer replenishment, manufacturing, or procurement, but it also differs by industry, by company, by their strategy. And so all that I wanted to get across in this very quick discussion is to view supply chains as an input-output process. And it changes. The inputs and the outputs depend on where I am in my supply chain, the type of industry I am in, and my strategy of my company itself.

Practice Problem 2: SteelCo
SteelCo is a small steel distributor in the Northern USA. They have two distribution centers (DCs), one in Allentown, PA, and one in Toledo, OH.
SteelCo has just received a large order from a construction contractor and will be supplying steel to four construction projects in the Northeast: one in Buffalo, one in Albany, one in Philadelphia, and one in Cleveland.
The contractor has ordered the following amounts to be shipped each week:
Buffalo: 40,500 pounds
Albany: 22,230 pounds
Philadelphia: 85,200 pounds
Cleveland: 47,500 pounds
Both DCs can each handle 100,000 pounds of steel per week.
The transport costs for a pound of steel for the different DC-construction site combinations are given in the table below.
Transport cost per pound	Buffalo	Albany	Philadelphia	Cleveland
Allentown	52	32	11	69
Toledo	45	84	76	15

Practice Problem 6: Temporary Networks
When a natural disaster strikes, normal supply chains are disrupted and many vital supplies cannot reach those affected through the normal routes. To handle the first couple of days after the disaster, many specialized Non Governmental Organizations (NGOs) work hard to secure vital supplies such as food, blankets, and medicines, and deliver these to people in the affected areas. To do so, they set up temporary supply networks, which only operate as long as needed.
Suppose you are working with one such NGO to set up a temporary network to distribute disaster-kits in the aftermath of a hurricane. You have secured kits from your central supply facility that will be flown in regularly. The kits need to be delivered once per week as long as needed to eight (8) temporary shelters, which are located a few hours drive from the airport.
To simplify the operations, you aim to set up a logistics and distribution center (DC) that controls all distribution. All incoming shipments will be transported from the airport directly to the center by the military, and from the DC you will plan how the kits are delivered to the shelters.
You are choosing between five (5) locations for the DC. The distances between the five (5) potential locations and the eight (8) shelters are shown in the table below. Download from the data spreadsheet.

DC 3	4.99	2.41	2.33	3.95	8.84	7.94	7.87	0.94
DC 4	5.58	8.8	6.32	8.54	5.15	6.06	9.42	2.16
DC 5	7.87	9.64	0.7	5.92	2.7	0.26	0.5	3.4
The weekly demand at each shelter has been estimated as follows:

Hints and Tips to solve this problem:
You need to build a facility location model in your spreadsheet, following the steps of the lessons.  To check your spreadsheet, try setting each decision variable (the flow on each of the 40 arcs and the five binary open-or-not variables) = 1.  Your total cost (assuming a $1 per mile per unit transportation cost )  should be 500,199.  If this is not the case, check your model!
This week, we have learned about different basic models, which are the foundation for more complex problems. The team noticed that this practice problem usually drives most of the current week's questions within the forum. Therefore, the following advice:
 1.- Remember to express demand and supply in the same units for your calculations.
 2.- Avoid creating your own assumptions! Use only the information provided in the problem statements, and read carefully the question to solve.
 3.- Sometimes, you may need to use "=" instead of ">=" for your demand constraint. Go to Software for SC2x - Recitation - Optimization using Excel - How to debug your model section for further details. Try to run your models with = and then with >= and observe what happens and why! This should be helpful for a better understanding of how this works for future problems.
 Have fun!
Part 1
0 points possible (ungraded)
